| **Example** | "Mobile phone must have 6-hour battery backup" | "Phone auto-detects when user is driving and switches to hands-free mode" |
| **Strategic Value** | Baseline/hygiene — must satisfy | Competitive differentiator — drives delight |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Don't write only the definitions without examples. KTU evaluators award 1 mark specifically for a relevant engineering/service example.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain Juran's six-step Quality Planning process with a suitable engineering example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of the **House of Quality (HOQ)** as a Quality Planning tool. Construct a hypothetical HOQ for a "Smart Helmet for Two-Wheeler Riders" identifying at least four "Whats" and four "Hows." *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Juran's Six-Step Quality Planning (7 Marks)**

The six steps applied to the example of an *electric vehicle (EV) battery pack design*:

1. **Identify Customers** — EV manufacturers, end consumers (daily commuters), regulatory bodies (AIS-156 standards), internal customers (assembly line).
2. **Determine Needs** — Range ≥ 200 km/charge, fast-charging ≤ 60 minutes, fire safety, 5-year warranty.
3. **Translate to Specifications** — Energy density $\geq 160\ \text{Wh/kg}$, peak charge rate $\geq 1C$, thermal runaway containment $\leq 5\ \text{minutes}$.
4. **Develop Product Features** — NMC 811 cell chemistry, liquid cooling loop, BMS with cell-level monitoring.
5. **Develop Process Capabilities** — Validate that laser-welding process achieves $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ on busbar resistance.
6. **Transfer to Operating Forces** — SOP for assembly, SPC charts on cell voltage, training of line operators.

Each step gets **≈ 1 mark**, plus **1 mark** for coherent engineering example integration.

**(b) House of Quality for Smart Helmet (7 Marks)**

| Hows → | Bluetooth v5.0 | Solar Cell Film | Impact-Absorbing Foam | Ventilation Fan |
|--------|----------------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **Whats ↓** | | | | |
| Hands-free calling | **9** | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Auto-charge while riding | 3 | **9** | 1 | 1 |
| Head injury protection | 1 | 1 | **9** | 3 |
| Comfort in summer | 1 | 1 | 3 | **9** |

- **Roof correlations:** Solar cell ↔ Bluetooth (+ve synergy via battery life); Ventilation ↔ Impact foam (-ve conflict due to openings).
- **Weights** assigned 1–5 based on customer survey.
- **Targets:** Battery standby ≥ 7 days, solar charging ≥ 0.5 W, foam passes AIS-128 impact test, airflow ≥ 15 L/min.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 4 Whats correctly listed → 2 marks
- 4 Hows correctly listed → 2 marks
- Filled relationship matrix → 2 marks
- Roof/Target/Weight discussion → 1 mark

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Lose up to 3 marks):**
> 1. Do not confuse the "Whats" with "Hows." Whats = **customer requirements**; Hows = **engineering/technical responses**.
> 2. Relationship matrix must use **9/3/1 coding** — random numbers cost marks.
> 3. The HOQ must include a **roof matrix**; omitting it loses 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Quality Planning and differentiate between *Strategic* and *Operational* Quality Planning with examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing process has the following parameters: $USL = 5.05\ \text{mm}$, $LSL = 4.95\ \text{mm}$, $\mu = 4.99\ \text{mm}$, $\sigma = 0.012\ \text{mm}$. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on whether the process is capable and what action should be taken during Quality Planning. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Quality Planning — Strategic vs Operational (7 Marks)**

- **Quality Planning** is the systematic design of products, processes, and controls to meet customer-defined quality goals.
- **Strategic Quality Planning** is long-term (3–5 years), organization-wide, sets the quality vision and policy.
  - *Example:* "Become a defect-free manufacturer by adopting Six Sigma across all plants by 2027."
- **Operational Quality Planning** is short-term (3–12 months), project-specific, translates strategy into actionable goals.
  - *Example:* "Reduce soldering defects on Line 4 to ≤ 20 PPM in Q3."

The relationship is **hierarchical**: strategy defines *direction*, operations define *execution*. Both are required; neither is sufficient alone.

**(b) Process Capability Calculation (7 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{5.05 - 4.95}{6 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.10}{0.072} \approx 1.389 \\
C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{5.05 - 4.99}{3 \times 0.012},\ \frac{4.99 - 4.95}{3 \times 0.012}\right) \\
&= \min\!\left(\frac{0.06}{0.036},\ \frac{0.04}{0.036}\right) \\
&= \min(1.667,\ 1.111) = 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** $C_p = 1.389 > 1.33$ indicates the process **spread** is acceptable. However, $C_{pk} = 1.111 < 1.33$ indicates the process is **off-center** (mean is closer to LSL than USL). This is a *planning failure* — the process must be **re-centered** on the midpoint $\mu_{\text{target}} = 5.00\ \text{mm}$.

**Quality Planning Action Required:** Re-calibrate machinery; investigate whether the off-center drift is due to tool wear, fixture misalignment, or operator bias. Implement SPC with X̄-R charts to monitor future centering.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall (Lose up to 2 marks):**
> 1. Students often confuse $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$. $C_p$ measures *spread only*; $C_{pk}$ measures *spread AND centering*. Always compute both.
> 2. Forgetting units in $\sigma$ — $\sigma$ must be in the same units as USL/LSL (here, mm).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read before exam:**

- ✅ Quality Planning is **Step 1 of Juran's Trilogy** (Planning → Control → Improvement).
- ✅ Six-step universal sequence: Identify customers → Determine needs → Translate to specs → Develop product → Develop process → Transfer plan.
- ✅ Quality Planning is **upstream** and **preventive**; it is the cheapest stage to ensure quality (Rule of Ten).
- ✅ Tools of Quality Planning = **New Seven Tools** (Affinity, Interrelationship Digraph, Tree, Matrix, Prioritization, PDPC, Arrow Diagram).
- ✅ **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the central QFD diagram with **6 walls + 1 roof + 1 floor + central matrix**.
- ✅ Relationship strengths in HOQ: **9 (strong), 3 (medium), 1 (weak)**.
- ✅ Process capability formulas:
  - $C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6\sigma)$
  - $C_{pk} = \min[(USL-\mu)/(3\sigma),\ (\mu - LSL)/(3\sigma)]$
  - **Benchmarks:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (capable), $\geq 1.67$ (Six Sigma).
- ✅ Customer needs hierarchy: **Stated → Real → Unstated → Exciting** (full satisfaction requires all four).
- ✅ Strategic QP (long-term, org-wide) ≠ Operational QP (short-term, project-specific); both are essential.
- ✅ Internal customers (next process) are **as important** as external customers — a key TQM principle.
- ✅ Quality must be **designed in**, not **inspected in** — Juran's foundational principle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Costs - Analysis Techniques for Quality Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Costs - Analysis Techniques for Quality Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Costs (Cost of Quality - COQ):**
> Quality costs are defined as the **sum of all costs incurred in the design, implementation, operation, and maintenance of a quality system**, along with the costs resulting from internal and external failures of products and services. These are expenses incurred because quality does (or does not) exist.

According to the **American Society for Quality (ASQC)**, Quality Cost is the *"cost of conformance plus the cost of non-conformance to quality requirements."*

The four universally accepted categories of quality cost were formally codified in the **ASQC/ANSI Standard Z1.7 (1971)** and refined later by **Joseph Juran** (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure model) and **Armand Feigenbaum** (P-A-IF-EF PAF model, 1956).

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Quality Costs (PAF Model)

$$COQ = C_{Prevention} + C_{Appraisal} + C_{Internal\,Failure} + C_{External\,Failure}$$

| Pillar | Symbol | KTU Term | Strategic Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Prevention Cost** | $C_P$ | Cost of Avoiding Defects | Investment in design, training, planning |
| **Appraisal Cost** | $C_A$ | Cost of Detecting Defects | Inspection, testing, auditing |
| **Internal Failure** | $C_{IF}$ | Cost of Defects Found In-House | Rework, scrap, rework delays |
| **External Failure** | $C_{EF}$ | Cost of Defects Found by Customer | Warranty, returns, brand damage |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Service Garage" Intuition

Imagine you own a **car repair garage**:

- **Prevention Costs** = Routine oil changes and engine tuning you do for customers before breakdowns happen.
- **Appraisal Costs** = Running diagnostic scans to *find* hidden engine problems early.
- **Internal Failure Costs** = Engine parts that fail *while still on your workshop bench* (you eat the cost — scrap, re-machining).
- **External Failure Costs** = Cars that *break down on the highway after delivery* (towing, lawsuits, lost trust — the most painful).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** If you double your **Prevention** spend (₹100 → ₹200), your **Failure** costs plummet (₹10,000 → ₹2,000). The total cost of quality *decreases*. This is the **ROI of prevention** — the central thesis of the PAF model.

## 1.4 The "Iceberg Principle" of Quality Costs

In Feigenbaum's classic iceberg model:
- **Visible (above water):** Scrap, rework, warranty claims — easy to measure.
- **Hidden (below water):** Lost customer goodwill, employee frustration, lost sales, brand damage — *real but rarely accounted*.

A naive accountant sees only the tip. A TQM practitioner measures the whole iceberg.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course explicitly requires students to understand that **modern quality costing extends beyond visible failure costs** to include hidden costs (lost opportunity, customer churn, morale).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of the Four Cost Categories — Exhaustive Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs ($C_P$)
Costs of activities specifically designed to **preclude** the occurrence of defects. Investment-oriented, not remedial.

- Quality planning and administration (QMS design, documentation)
- New-product review (Design for Quality, DFQ)
- Process capability studies & SPC program setup
- **Supplier capability** surveys and rating
- **Training** in quality (Kaizen, 5S, statistical tools)
- Quality improvement projects (CI teams, Six Sigma projects)
- Field trials and pilot runs
- Quality data acquisition and reporting systems

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs ($C_A$)
Costs incurred to **detect** defects — measuring, inspecting, and testing products, processes, and services.

- Incoming-material inspection (receiving inspection)
- In-process inspection (first-piece, patrol, floor inspection)
- Final product acceptance testing
- **Calibration** of test and measurement equipment (T&M)
- Quality audits (system, process, product)
- Field testing and performance evaluation
- Cost of maintaining inspection workforce

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$)
Costs arising because defects are detected **before** reaching the customer — scrap, rework, re-inspection.

- Scrap and wastage of materials
- **Rework** and re-processing labor
- **Re-inspection** of reworked items
- Material review and disposition costs (MRB)
- Failure-mode analysis (root cause investigation)
- Downtime caused by quality problems
- Design changes to correct defects

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$)
Costs arising when defects are discovered **after** the product reaches the customer — the most painful category.

- Warranty claims and field service
- Repair and replacement costs
- Handling customer complaints (call centers)
- **Returned materials** and concessions
- Liability and litigation costs
- Loss of goodwill, brand reputation damage
- Lost sales and market share erosion

## 2.2 The Classical vs. Revised PAF Models

### 2.2.1 Classical (Feigenbaum / Juran) PAF Model

$$COQ_{Classical} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

**Behavioral Assumption:** As $C_P$ increases, $C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ decreases — producing a U-shaped total cost curve with an **optimum cost point** $Q^*$.

### 2.2.2 Revised / Modern PAF Model (Crosby, Gryna, Gitlow)

> [!NOTE]
> **Philip B. Crosby (1979)** argued that *the only acceptable performance standard is **Zero Defects***. In Crosby's philosophy, the optimum cost of quality is **not at the bottom of a U-curve** but at the **lower-left corner** — i.e., when both failure and prevention costs converge toward zero.

Modern TQM therefore emphasizes the **cost of conformance** ($C_{CON}$) and **cost of non-conformance** ($C_{NON}$):

$$COQ_{Revised} = C_{CON} + C_{NON}$$

Where:
- $C_{CON} = C_P + C_A$ (doing things right the first time)
- $C_{NON} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ (cost of failure)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 3-mark definitions, 7-mark derivations, and 14-mark case-study computations.**

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning / Use | Variables | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Total Cost of Quality | All monetary | ₹ (Rupees) |
| 2 | $COQ = C_{CON} + C_{NON}$ | Revised model | Conformance / Non-conformance | ₹ |
| 3 | $C_{CON} = C_P + C_A$ | Conformance cost | Prevention + Appraisal | ₹ |
| 4 | $C_{NON} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Non-conformance cost | Failure costs | ₹ |
| 5 | $COQ\% = \dfrac{COQ}{Sales\,Revenue} \times 100$ | Cost-of-Quality Ratio (industry benchmark) | Ratio $\vert$ | \% |
| 6 | $CPI = \dfrac{C_P}{COQ}$ | Prevention-Cost Index (higher = better) | Ratio | Decimal |
| 7 | $FCR = \dfrac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{COQ}$ | Failure-Cost Ratio (lower = better) | Ratio | Decimal |
| 8 | $Q_{opt} = \arg\min\limits_{C_P} \, COQ(C_P)$ | Optimum Prevention Spend | Calculus | ₹ |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta C_{NON}}{\Delta C_P}$ | Cost Leverage Ratio (negative number, larger magnitude = better ROI) | Ratio | — |
| 10 | $\sigma_{COQ} = \sqrt{\sum_i \sigma_i^2}$ | Standard deviation of quality costs (for control limits) | Std. dev. | ₹ |

> **Benchmark values (industry averages):**
> - Excellent organizations: $COQ\% \le 2.5\%$ of sales
> - Average organizations: $COQ\% \approx 5\%$ of sales
> - Poor performers: $COQ\% \ge 15\%$ of sales

## 2.4 The PAF Curve — Mathematical Behavior

The total cost curve has two regions:

$$\dfrac{dCOQ}{dC_P} = \dfrac{d(C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF})}{dC_P}$$

Setting the first derivative to zero gives the optimum:

$$\dfrac{dCOQ}{dC_P} = 1 + \dfrac{dC_A}{dC_P} + \dfrac{dC_{IF}}{dC_P} + \dfrac{dC_{EF}}{dC_P} = 0$$

Since failure costs **decrease** as prevention rises, their derivatives are negative. The optimum $C_P^*$ occurs where the marginal reduction in failure costs just equals the marginal increase in prevention spend.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing plants** use COQ to justify Six Sigma Black Belt projects.
- **Software firms** track COQ to find defect injection vs. removal costs (IBM's defect removal efficiency model).
- **Service industries** (banks, hospitals) apply COQ to reduce transaction errors and patient safety incidents.
- **Procurement & supplier management:** COQ-based supplier scorecards (e.g., automotive industry QS-9000 / IATF 16949).
- **Lean & Industry 4.0:** Real-time COQ dashboards in MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Prevention Spend ($C_P^*$)

Assume linear approximations for cost categories around the current operating point:

$$C_A(C_P) = C_{A0} + a \cdot C_P$$

$$C_{IF}(C_P) = C_{IF0} - b \cdot C_P$$

$$C_{EF}(C_P) = C_{EF0} - c \cdot C_P$$

where $a, b, c$ are empirically determined sensitivity coefficients ($a \ge 0$, $b, c > 0$).

**Step 1 — Substitute into the COQ identity:**

$$COQ(C_P) = C_P + (C_{A0} + a \cdot C_P) + (C_{IF0} - b \cdot C_P) + (C_{EF0} - c \cdot C_P)$$

**Step 2 — Group terms by $C_P$:**

$$COQ(C_P) = (C_{A0} + C_{IF0} + C_{EF0}) + C_P \cdot (1 + a - b - c)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize this is linear in $C_P$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COQ(C_P) &= K_0 + K_1 \cdot C_P \\
\text{where } K_0 &= C_{A0} + C_{IF0} + C_{EF0} \\
\text{and } K_1 &= (1 + a - b - c)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Behavioral economics constraint:** For a meaningful optimum to exist (U-shaped curve), we need $K_1 < 0$, i.e.:

$$b + c > 1 + a$$

This says the **combined sensitivity of failure costs to prevention must exceed the unit cost of prevention** — a sanity check on whether prevention investment is worthwhile.

**Step 5 — If non-linear models are used** (e.g., quadratic failure cost), then:

$$C_{IF}(C_P) = C_{IF0} - b \cdot C_P + d \cdot C_P^2$$

Taking the derivative:

$$\dfrac{dCOQ}{dC_P} = 1 + a - b + 2d \cdot C_P = 0$$

Solving:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_P^* &= \dfrac{b - 1 - a}{2d} \\
COQ_{min} &= K_0 + K_1 \cdot C_P^* + d \cdot (C_P^*)^2
\end{aligned}$$

This produces a true **convex parabola** with a global minimum.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quality Cost Percentage (COQ%) Benchmarking

Given:
- Total Sales Revenue $S = \text{₹}\,10{,}00{,}000$
- $C_P = \text{₹}\,40{,}000$, $C_A = \text{₹}\,60{,}000$
- $C_{IF} = \text{₹}\,80{,}000$, $C_{EF} = \text{₹}\,1{,}20{,}000$

**Step 1 — Compute Total COQ:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COQ &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
COQ &= 40{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 \\
COQ &= \text{₹}\,3{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute COQ percentage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COQ\% &= \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
COQ\% &= 30\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

A $COQ\% = 30\%$ is **far above** the world-class benchmark of $2.5\%$. The failure-cost dominance ($C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 66.7\%$ of COQ) signals the organization is "**failing into profit**" — typical of companies that haven't yet adopted TQM.

**Step 4 — Compute the Prevention-Cost Index (CPI):**

$$\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \dfrac{C_P}{COQ} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \\
CPI &= 0.1333 \approx 13.33\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Failure-Cost Ratio (FCR):**

$$\begin{aligned}
FCR &= \dfrac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{COQ} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \\
FCR &= 0.6667 \approx 66.67\%
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost Leverage Ratio for Project Justification

A Six Sigma project proposes the following change:
- Increase Prevention spend: $\Delta C_P = +\text{₹}\,50{,}000$
- Resulting decrease in Internal Failure: $\Delta C_{IF} = -\text{₹}\,1{,}50{,}000$
- Resulting decrease in External Failure: $\Delta C_{EF} = -\text{₹}\,3{,}00{,}000$
- Minor increase in Appraisal: $\Delta C_A = +\text{₹}\,10{,}000$

**Step 1 — Net change in COQ:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta COQ &= \Delta C_P + \Delta C_A + \Delta C_{IF} + \Delta C_{EF} \\
\Delta COQ &= 50{,}000 + 10{,}000 + (-1{,}50{,}000) + (-3{,}00{,}000) \\
\Delta COQ &= -\text{₹}\,3{,}90{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Cost Leverage Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{\Delta C_{NON}}{\Delta C_P} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{IF} + \Delta C_{EF}}{\Delta C_P} \\
\eta &= \dfrac{-1{,}50{,}000 + (-3{,}00{,}000)}{50{,}000} \\
\eta &= \dfrac{-4{,}50{,}000}{50{,}000} = -9
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

A leverage of $\eta = -9$ means **every ₹1 spent on prevention avoids ₹9 of failure costs**. The project pays for itself **9 times over** — an extremely strong investment case.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cost Categories

| Dimension | $C_P$ (Prevention) | $C_A$ (Appraisal) | $C_{IF}$ (Internal Failure) | $C_{EF}$ (External Failure) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Timing** | Before defect | During process | Before shipment | After delivery |
| **Variability** | Predictable | Predictable | Variable | Highly variable |
| **Visibility** | High (budgeted) | High (budgeted) | Moderate | Low / hidden |
| **Customer visibility** | None | None | None | **Very High** |
| **Leverage on quality** | Highest | Medium | Negative | Most damaging |
| **Optimal trend** | **Increase** | Increase then stabilize | **Decrease** | **Drive to zero** |
| **TQM action** | Maximize | Optimize | Minimize | Eliminate |
| **Owner in org.** | Quality Engineering | Inspection | Production / QA | Service / Legal |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Multi-Year Trend Analysis

A firm recorded the following COQ data over 3 years (figures in ₹ lakhs):

| Category | Year 1 | Year 2 | Year 3 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $C_P$ | 5 | 8 | 12 |
| $C_A$ | 10 | 11 | 12 |
| $C_{IF}$ | 30 | 22 | 14 |
| $C_{EF}$ | 50 | 35 | 18 |
| **Total COQ** | **95** | **76** | **56** |
| Sales Revenue | 1000 | 1000 | 1000 |
| **COQ %** | **9.5%** | **7.6%** | **5.6%** |

**Step 1 — Year-1 to Year-3 Reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta COQ &= 95 - 56 = 39 \,\text{₹ lakhs} \\
\Delta COQ\% &= 9.5 - 5.6 = 3.9\,\text{percentage points}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Prevention Investment vs. Failure Reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_P &= 12 - 5 = 7 \\
\Delta (C_{IF} + C_{EF}) &= (14+18) - (30+50) = 32 - 80 = -48
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Leverage achieved:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{\Delta (C_{IF} + C_{EF})}{\Delta C_P} = \dfrac{-48}{7} \approx -6.86
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Pareto 80/20 check (Year 1):**

Total failure in Year 1 = $30 + 50 = 80$ ₹ lakhs = 84.2% of COQ.

Per Pareto logic, fixing the **top 20% of defect causes** (often identified via Pareto charts of defect types) will eliminate **~80% of failure cost**. The TQM mandate is clear.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The PAF Model — Block Architecture of Quality Costs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Cost of Quality COQ] --> B[Cost of Conformance Ccon]
    A --> C[Cost of Non Conformance Cnon]

    B --> D[Prevention Cost Cp]
    B --> E[Appraisal Cost Ca]

    C --> F[Internal Failure Cost Cif]
    C --> G[External Failure Cost Cef]

    D --> D1[Quality Planning]
    D --> D2[Training and Education]
    D --> D3[Supplier Evaluation]
    D --> D4[Process Capability Studies]
    D --> D5[Quality Improvement Projects]

    E --> E1[Incoming Inspection]
    E --> E2[In Process Inspection]
    E --> E3[Final Testing]
    E --> E4[Equipment Calibration]
    E --> E5[Quality Audits]

    F --> F1[Scrap and Reject]
    F --> F2[Rework and Repair]
    F --> F3[Re Inspection]
    F --> F4[Failure Analysis]
    F --> F5[Downtime Losses]

    G --> G1[Warranty Claims]
    G --> G2[Field Service Costs]
    G --> G3[Customer Complaints]
    G --> G4[Returned Goods]
    G --> G5[Liability and Lost Goodwill]
```

## 4.2 Analysis Techniques for Quality Costs — Master Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Quality Cost Data Collected] --> T1[Technique 1: COQ Reports]
    Start --> T2[Technique 2: Pareto Analysis]
    Start --> T3[Technique 3: Trend Analysis]
    Start --> T4[Technique 4: Activity Based Costing ABC]
    Start --> T5[Technique 5: Process Cost Analysis]
    Start --> T6[Technique 6: Quality Cost Index and Ratios]

    T1 --> R1[Categorize Cp Ca Cif Cef by Department and Product]
    T2 --> R2[Identify Vital Few Defect Causes 80 20 Rule]
    T3 --> R3[Plot COQ vs Time to Detect Improvement]
    T4 --> R4[Allocate Quality Costs to Activities then Products]
    T5 --> R5[Map Costs to Process Steps using Fishbone or Value Stream]
    T6 --> R6[Compute COQ percent CPI FCR Leverage]

    R1 --> Decision[Management Decision and CI Projects]
    R2 --> Decision
    R3 --> Decision
    R4 --> Decision
    R5 --> Decision
    R6 --> Decision
```

## 4.3 The PAF Behavior Curve — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Prevention Spend Cp Axis] --> Y[Total COQ Axis]

    Y --> Zone1[Zone A Under Prevention: Failure Dominates]
    Y --> Zone2[Zone B Optimum Q Star: Minimum COQ]
    Y --> Zone3[Zone C Over Prevention: Diminishing Returns]

    Zone1 --> Action1[Action: Invest in Prevention Cp Up]
    Zone2 --> Action2[Action: Hold Steady and Monitor]
    Zone3 --> Action3[Action: Re evaluate Appraisal Efficiency]
```

## 4.4 Six-Step Quality Cost Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Quality Cost Categories] --> S2[Step 2: Establish Cost Accounts in ERP]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Collect Data Monthly Quarterly]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Analyze via Pareto Trend and Ratios]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Report to Top Management]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Launch Continuous Improvement Projects]
    S6 --> S1
```

## 4.5 Quality Cost Reporting Matrix — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[ERP Cost Data]
        I2[Shop Floor Logs]
        I3[Customer Returns DB]
        I4[Warranty Claims DB]
    end

    subgraph Processing
        P1[Categorize into P A IF EF]
        P2[Allocate by Product Line]
        P3[Allocate by Process Step]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        O1[COQ Bar Chart by Category]
        O2[Pareto Chart of Defects]
        O3[Trend Line vs Time]
        O4[Cost Leverage Report]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> O1
    P2 --> P3 --> O2
    P1 --> O3
    P3 --> O4
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Quality Cost. List the four categories of quality cost as per the PAF model. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Quality Cost (or Cost of Quality, COQ) is defined as the total expenditure incurred by an organization in ensuring that products and services conform to quality requirements, plus the costs arising from failure to meet those requirements (ASQC Standard Z1.7).

**Four Categories (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_P$):** Costs of activities to *prevent* defects (training, planning, supplier evaluation).
2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_A$):** Costs of activities to *detect* defects (inspection, testing, auditing).
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):** Costs of defects found *before* delivery (scrap, rework).
4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Costs of defects found *after* delivery (warranty, returns).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Cost of Conformance and Cost of Non-Conformance. Give two examples each. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cost of Conformance ($C_{CON}$) | Cost of Non-Conformance ($C_{NON}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Meaning** | Cost of doing things right the first time | Cost of failing to meet requirements |
| **Components** | $C_P + C_A$ | $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ |
| **Nature** | Investment / proactive | Penalty / reactive |
| **Examples** | (i) Quality training programs; (ii) Inspection of incoming material | (i) Warranty claims; (ii) Scrap & rework |
| **TQM Goal** | Optimize | Drive to zero |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-internal choice means **you answer EITHER Q-A OR Q-B**, not both. Pick the one you can structure better. Marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive PAF Model Analysis

> **Q-A(a)** [7 Marks] — Explain in detail the **four categories of quality costs** with **at least five examples each**. State the relationship between them and discuss how an organization can strategically manage these costs.

> **Q-A(b)** [7 Marks] — A manufacturing company has the following annual quality-related expenses (in ₹ lakhs):
>
> | Item | Amount |
> | :--- | :---: |
> | Quality planning | 4 |
> | Operator training on quality | 3 |
> | Incoming inspection | 6 |
> | In-process inspection | 5 |
> | Final testing | 4 |
> | Scrap and rejection | 18 |
> | Rework labor | 12 |
> | Warranty claims | 30 |
> | Customer complaint handling | 8 |
> | Calibration of instruments | 3 |
>
> **Compute:** (i) Total Prevention Cost, (ii) Total Appraisal Cost, (iii) Total Internal Failure Cost, (iv) Total External Failure Cost, (v) Total COQ, (vi) COQ% if annual sales = ₹ 800 lakhs, and (vii) interpret the result.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Quality costs were first formalized by **Armand V. Feigenbaum** in 1956 and codified in **ASQC Standard Z1.7**. They represent the financial impact of quality management decisions.

**Body (5 marks — split across the four categories):**

**(i) Prevention Costs (1.25 marks):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects. Examples:
1. Quality planning and QMS documentation
2. Operator and engineer training
3. New-product design reviews (DFMEA, PFMEA)
4. Supplier evaluation and rating
5. Process capability studies

**(ii) Appraisal Costs (1.25 marks):** Costs to *detect* defects.
1. Incoming material inspection
2. In-process and final inspection
3. Calibration of testing equipment
4. Quality audits
5. Field performance testing

**(iii) Internal Failure Costs (1.25 marks):** Defects found *in-house*.
1. Scrap and wastage
2. Rework and re-processing
3. Re-inspection
4. Material review board dispositions
5. Production line downtime

**(iv) External Failure Costs (1.25 marks):** Defects found *by customer*.
1. Warranty claims
2. Returned goods
3. Field service
4. Litigation and liability
5. Loss of brand goodwill

**Strategic Management (1 mark):**
- Shift the cost mix toward $C_P$ and $C_A$ (conformance costs).
- Drive $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ toward zero via CI projects, Six Sigma, and FMEA.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Classify each item into PAF categories:**

| Item | Amount (₹ lakhs) | Category |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Quality planning | 4 | Prevention |
| Operator training | 3 | Prevention |
| Incoming inspection | 6 | Appraisal |
| In-process inspection | 5 | Appraisal |
| Final testing | 4 | Appraisal |
| Scrap and rejection | 18 | Internal Failure |
| Rework labor | 12 | Internal Failure |
| Warranty claims | 30 | External Failure |
| Customer complaint handling | 8 | External Failure |
| Calibration of instruments | 3 | Appraisal |

**Step 2 — Compute Prevention Cost:**

$$C_P = 4 + 3 = \text{₹}\,7\,\text{lakhs}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Appraisal Cost:**

$$C_A = 6 + 5 + 4 + 3 = \text{₹}\,18\,\text{lakhs}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Internal Failure Cost:**

$$C_{IF} = 18 + 12 = \text{₹}\,30\,\text{lakhs}$$

**Step 5 — Compute External Failure Cost:**

$$C_{EF} = 30 + 8 = \text{₹}\,38\,\text{lakhs}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Total COQ:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COQ &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
COQ &= 7 + 18 + 30 + 38 = \text{₹}\,93\,\text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compute COQ Percentage:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COQ\% &= \dfrac{93}{800} \times 100 = 11.625\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:**
- $COQ\% = 11.625\%$ is **well above** the world-class benchmark of $2.5\%$ — indicates poor quality maturity.
- Failure costs dominate: $C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 68/93 = 73.1\%$ of total COQ.
- External failure ($C_{EF} = 38$) exceeds Internal Failure ($C_{IF} = 30$) — the most damaging sign, since EF costs include lost goodwill.
- **Recommendation:** Aggressively invest in $C_P$ (training, design reviews) to reduce $C_{EF}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often mis-classify *quality planning* as an appraisal cost. Remember: **planning is prevention**; **inspection is appraisal**. Misclassification will lose you 1–2 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Analysis Techniques for Quality Costs

> **Q-B(a)** [7 Marks] — Explain any **four analysis techniques for quality costs** with their purpose and output.

> **Q-B(b)** [7 Marks] — A firm has annual sales of ₹ 50 crores. The total COQ for last year was ₹ 2.5 crores. After implementing a quality improvement program, the firm increased its prevention cost from ₹ 25 lakhs to ₹ 60 lakhs, and reduced its internal failure cost from ₹ 90 lakhs to ₹ 40 lakhs and external failure cost from ₹ 1.20 crores to ₹ 60 lakhs. Appraisal cost remained at ₹ 25 lakhs. **Calculate:** (i) Old and new COQ, (ii) Old and new COQ%, (iii) Cost leverage achieved, and (iv) Comment on the strategic shift.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**(1) COQ Reports (1.75 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Summarize quality costs by category, department, product, or process step.
- **Output:** Monthly/quarterly tabular reports; bar charts and pie charts.
- **Example:** Quality Manager presents a Pareto chart showing 70% of rework cost comes from 3 defect types.

**(2) Pareto Analysis (1.75 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Apply the **80/20 rule** — 80% of failure cost comes from 20% of causes.
- **Output:** A descending bar chart with cumulative % line.
- **Use:** Helps prioritize improvement projects on the "vital few" causes.

**(3) Trend Analysis (1.75 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Track COQ over multiple periods to detect improvement or deterioration.
- **Output:** Time-series plot of each PAF component.
- **Use:** Validates whether CI initiatives are delivering financial benefits.

**(4) Activity-Based Costing (ABC) for Quality (1.75 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Allocate quality costs to *specific activities* then to *products/customers*.
- **Output:** Per-product cost of quality.
- **Use:** Reveals hidden cost of low-volume or complex products; supports pricing decisions.

**(Other valid techniques — for extra credit):** Quality Cost Index (COQ% ratio), Process Cost Analysis, Benchmarking, Failure Mode Cost Analysis.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Given Data:**

| Item | Before | After |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $C_P$ (Prevention) | ₹ 25 lakhs | ₹ 60 lakhs |
| $C_A$ (Appraisal) | ₹ 25 lakhs | ₹ 25 lakhs |
| $C_{IF}$ (Internal Failure) | ₹ 90 lakhs | ₹ 40 lakhs |
| $C_{EF}$ (External Failure) | ₹ 1.20 cr = ₹ 120 lakhs | ₹ 60 lakhs |
| Sales | ₹ 50 cr = ₹ 5000 lakhs | ₹ 5000 lakhs |

**Step 1 — Old COQ:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COQ_{old} &= 25 + 25 + 90 + 120 \\
COQ_{old} &= \text{₹}\,260\,\text{lakhs} = \text{₹}\,2.6\,\text{crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — New COQ:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COQ_{new} &= 60 + 25 + 40 + 60 \\
COQ_{new} &= \text{₹}\,185\,\text{lakhs} = \text{₹}\,1.85\,\text{crores}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Old COQ%:**

$$COQ\%_{old} = \dfrac{260}{5000} \times 100 = 5.2\%$$

**Step 4 — New COQ%:**

$$COQ\%_{new} = \dfrac{185}{5000} \times 100 = 3.7\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute Changes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_P &= 60 - 25 = +\text{₹}\,35\,\text{lakhs} \\
\Delta C_{IF} &= 40 - 90 = -\text{₹}\,50\,\text{lakhs} \\
\Delta C_{EF} &= 60 - 120 = -\text{₹}\,60\,\text{lakhs} \\
\Delta COQ &= 185 - 260 = -\text{₹}\,75\,\text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Cost Leverage Ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{\Delta C_{IF} + \Delta C_{EF}}{\Delta C_P} \\
\eta &= \dfrac{-50 + (-60)}{35} = \dfrac{-110}{35} \\
\eta &\approx -3.14
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Strategic Commentary:**
- A leverage of **−3.14** means every ₹1 invested in prevention saved ₹3.14 in failure costs — an excellent ROI.
- COQ% dropped from **5.2% to 3.7%** — a 28.8% relative improvement in one year.
- Failure costs dropped from ₹210 lakhs to ₹100 lakhs (52.4% reduction), while prevention only rose by 140%.
- The firm is moving from a **"detect and correct"** model to a **"prevent and predict"** model — the textbook hallmark of TQM.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** When computing COQ% in crores vs. lakhs, **always convert to the same unit first**. Students commonly divide ₹ 2.5 crores by ₹ 50 crores without converting — yielding 0.05 instead of 5%. Loss: 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Don't forget that **appraisal cost also includes calibration**. If a question lists "calibration of instruments," treat it as $C_A$, not $C_P$. Many students lose marks here.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — memorize before every KTU exam:**

- **COQ = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$** — the master equation.
- **PAF model** = Prevention, Appraisal, Failure (Internal + External).
- **Revised model:** $COQ = C_{CON} + C_{NON}$; where $C_{CON} = C_P + C_A$ and $C_{NON} = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$.
- **Crosby's view:** optimum quality cost is at **zero defects**, not at the bottom of a U-curve.
- **Juran's view:** there is an **optimum** prevention spend where total cost is minimized.
- **World-class benchmark:** $COQ\% \le 2.5\%$ of sales.
- **Failure costs must decrease as prevention increases** — this is the cornerstone assumption.
- **External Failure** is the most damaging category — includes goodwill loss.
- **Prevention Cost Index (CPI) = $C_P / COQ$** — higher is better.
- **Failure Cost Ratio (FCR) = $(C_{IF} + C_{EF}) / COQ$** — lower is better.
- **Cost Leverage $\eta = \Delta C_{NON} / \Delta C_P$** — negative value; larger magnitude = better.
- **Analysis techniques:** COQ reports, Pareto, Trend, ABC, Process Cost Analysis, Quality Cost Index.
- **Pareto rule** for quality: 20% of defect types cause 80% of failure costs.
- **Always state units** (₹ lakhs, ₹ crores) — unit mismatch costs marks.
- **Iceberg principle:** Hidden costs (goodwill, morale, lost sales) often exceed visible costs.
- **Six Sigma alignment:** COQ reduction is the financial language of Six Sigma projects.
- **TQM mandate:** *Shift the cost mix from failure to prevention; drive failure to zero.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Total Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Total Quality Management # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a **comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy** that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving **all members** of the organization — from top management to front-line employees — to meet or exceed **customer expectations** and achieve **long-term organizational success**. Formally, as per the KTU 2024 Scheme definition: > **TQM** = A *customer-focused*, *process-centered*, *total-system*, *data-driven*, and *continuous-improvement* approach to organizational excellence. $$TQM = f(\text{Customer Focus}, \text{Process Management}, \text{People Involvement}, \text{Continuous Improvement}, \text{Data-Driven Decisions})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Must Memorize Verbatim):** > *"Total Quality Management is an integrative philosophy of management for continuously improving the quality of products and processes to achieve customer satisfaction and long-term success of the organization through the participation of all members at all levels."* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "TQM as a Recipe for a Five-Star Restaurant" Imagine a five-star restaurant. Every dish that leaves the kitchen is the restaurant's "product." The customer is the diner at the table. Now ask: who is responsible for the taste? - The **chef** who cooks? ✔ - The **waiter** who serves it warm and well-presented? ✔ - The **purchase manager** who sourced fresh ingredients? ✔ - The **cleaner** who keeps the kitchen hygienic? ✔ - The **owner** who set the quality policy? ✔ If **any one** of them fails, the customer leaves unhappy. TQM says: **quality is not the job of one person — it is everyone's job, every day, in every process.** The restaurant continuously takes customer feedback, refines recipes, trains staff, and re-evaluates — that is TQM in action. **Key insight:** Just as a restaurant cannot have one "quality department" alone produce great food, a company cannot have a separate QA team produce great products. Quality must be **baked into the culture**. --- ## 1.3 Why TQM Matters — The Modern Engineering Context In today's competitive engineering and manufacturing environment, customers have multiple alternatives. Organizations survive only when their products/services consistently meet specifications. TQM provides: | Benefit | Real-World Impact | |---|---| | **Improved customer satisfaction** | Repeat business, brand loyalty | | **Reduced waste & rework** | Lower production cost | | **Better employee morale** | Lower attrition, higher engagement | | **Competitive advantage** | Market leadership | | **Regulatory compliance** | ISO 9001, AS9100 alignment | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** TQM is **NOT** a tool, a technique, or a software — it is a **management PHILOSOPHY** and a **culture**. Students frequently lose marks by defining TQM as a "system" or "tool" — avoid this mistake. --- ## 1.4 Quality — The Foundational Concept **Quality** is the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs (ISO 8402 definition). In simpler terms: **Quality = Fitness for Use** (Juran's classical definition). $$Q = f(\text{Conformance to Specifications}, \text{Performance}, \text{Reliability}, \text{Durability}, \text{Serviceability}, \text{Aesthetics}, \text{Perceived Quality})$$ Three viewpoints of quality (widely tested in KTU exams): 1. **User-based view** — Quality lies in the eyes of the customer (subjective). 2. **Product-based view** — Quality is a precise, measurable variable (objective). 3. **Manufacturing-based view** — Quality is conformance to specifications and design. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Quality Views — "UMP"**: **U**ser-based, **M**anufacturing-based, **P**roduct-based. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ## 2.1 The Evolution of Quality Management (Historical Timeline) Quality management did not appear overnight. It evolved through four distinct eras. **This is a guaranteed 7-mark KTU question** (often asked as "Explain the evolution of quality management"). | Era | Era Name | Time Period | Key Focus | Limitation | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Inspection** | Pre-1920s | Detect defective products | Reactive, end-of-line, costly | | 2 | **Quality Control (QC)** | 1920s–1950s | Use statistical tools to control processes | Still detective in nature | | 3 | **Quality Assurance (QA)** | 1950s–1980s | Plan quality into the process (proactive) | Department-focused | | 4 | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | 1980s–Present | Organization-wide culture, customer obsession | Requires deep cultural change | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Trap:** Many students write that TQM "replaced" QA. Wrong! TQM **encompasses** QA, which encompasses QC, which encompasses Inspection. It is a **layered** evolution, not a substitution. --- ## 2.2 The Quality Gurus — Pillars of TQM (HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024) KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the contributions of the five quality gurus. Memorize these **in tabular form**. | Guru | Key Contribution | Famous Concept | Famous Quote | |---|---|---|---| | **W. Edwards Deming** | Statistical Process Control, 14 Points for Management | PDCA Cycle (Deming Cycle / Deming Wheel) | *"In God we trust; all others must bring data."* | | **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) | Fitness for Use, Pareto Principle (80/20) | *"Quality is fitness for use."* | | **Philip B. Crosby** | Quality is Free, Zero Defects concept | 4 Absolutes of Quality, Quality Vaccine | *"Quality is free; it's not a gift, but it is free."* | | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles, Cause-and-Effect diagrams | Company-wide Quality Control (CWQC) | *"Quality begins and ends with education."* | | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control (originator) | "Hidden Plant" cost of quality concept | *"Quality is a total field, not a series of techniques."* | ### 2.2.1 Deming's 14 Points (Frequently Asked in KTU Part A) Deming's philosophy centers on **system thinking** — improving the system, not blaming individuals. His 14 points (abbreviated for revision): 1. Create constancy of purpose toward improvement. 2. Adopt the new philosophy. 3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality. 4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone. 5. Improve constantly every process. 6. Institute training on the job. 7. Institute leadership. 8. Drive out fear. 9. Break down barriers between departments. 10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations. 11. Eliminate numerical quotas. 12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship. 13. Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement. 14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "Deming's 14 Principles" — the correct term is **"Deming's 14 Points"** or **"Deming's 14 Obligations"**. Marks are deducted for incorrect terminology. ### 2.2.2 Juran's Quality Trilogy $$\text{Juran Trilogy} = \text{Quality Planning} \rightarrow \text{Quality Control} \rightarrow \text{Quality Improvement}$$ This is a **universal sequence** applicable to any business problem and is often tested with 7 marks. ### 2.2.3 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality | # | Absolute | |---|---| | 1 | Quality = **Conformance to requirements** (not "goodness") | | 2 | Prevention, not inspection | | 3 | Zero defects performance standard | | 4 | Measure quality by the **price of non-conformance** (PONC) | --- ## 2.3 Principles of TQM (The 8 Universal Pillars) These principles form the **core theoretical content** of Module 1 and are tested as 7-mark/14-mark questions in KTU. | # | Principle | Engineering Explanation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Customer Focus** | Customer is the ultimate judge of quality. | | 2 | **Total Employee Involvement / Participation** | Quality is everyone's responsibility. | | 3 | **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** | Small, incremental, continuous improvements. | | 4 | **Process-Centered Approach** | Focus on the process; product quality follows. | | 5 | **Integrated System** | All departments work as a connected system. | | 6 | **Strategic & Systematic Approach** | Quality is a strategic plan, not a tactic. | | 7 | **Fact-Based Decision Making** | Decisions based on data, not intuition. | | 8 | **Communication** | Effective communication at all levels. | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for TQM Principles — "CCPI IFS"**: **C**ustomer focus, **C**ontinuous improvement, **P**eople involvement, **I**ntegrated system, **F**act-based, **S**trategic approach. (Communication is implicit in IFS.) --- ## 2.4 Dimensions of Quality — Garvin's Eight Dimensions David Garvin (1987) identified **8 dimensions of product quality**. This is a **very common 7-mark KTU question**. | # | Dimension | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Performance** | Primary operating characteristics | | 2 | **Features** | Secondary, supplementary characteristics | | 3 | **Reliability** | Probability of failure-free operation over time | | 4 | **Conformance** | Degree to which product meets specifications | | 5 | **Durability** | Length of product life | | 6 | **Serviceability** | Ease and speed of repair | | 7 | **Aesthetics** | Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound) | | 8 | **Perceived Quality** | Reputation / brand image | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic — "PRFCD SAP"**: **P**erformance, **R**eliability, **F**eatures, **C**onformance, **D**urability, **S**erviceability, **A**esthetics, **P**erceived quality. --- ## 2.5 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The "Hidden Plant" A critical TQM concept tested regularly. COQ is the **total cost incurred to ensure quality** + the cost incurred **due to lack of quality**. $$\text{Total COQ} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}$$ | Category | Examples | Type | |---|---|---| | **Prevention Costs** | Training, quality planning, supplier capability assessment | Cost of conformance | | **Appraisal Costs** | Inspection, testing, calibration of measuring equipment | Cost of conformance | | **Internal Failure Costs** | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, downtime | Cost of non-conformance | | **External Failure Costs** | Warranty claims, returns, lawsuits, lost goodwill | Cost of non-conformance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Juran's Golden Rule:** The **optimal** level of quality is reached when **Prevention + Appraisal costs ≈ Internal + External Failure costs**. As quality improves, prevention/appraisal costs rise, but failure costs fall dramatically. --- ## 2.6 The PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel) The **Plan-Do-Check-Act** cycle is the engine of continuous improvement in TQM. $$PDCA = \text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Check} \rightarrow \text{Act} \rightarrow \text{(repeat)}$$ | Step | Action | |---|---| | **Plan** | Identify the problem, analyze, develop a plan. | | **Do** | Implement the plan on a small scale (pilot). | | **Check** | Study results; compare actual vs. expected. | | **Act** | Standardize if successful, or revise and repeat. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Note:** Sometimes referred to as the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**. All three names are accepted in the answer script, but "PDCA" is the most universally recognized. --- ## 2.7 Quality Statements (Vision, Mission, Quality Policy) Every TQM-driven organization must formulate: - **Vision Statement** — Where the organization wants to be in the future. - **Mission Statement** — What the organization does and for whom. - **Quality Policy** — The organization's formal commitment to quality, signed by top management. $$Q_{\text{organizational}} = f(\text{Vision}, \text{Mission}, \text{Quality Policy}, \text{Quality Objectives})$$ > [!TIP] > **Example (Kerala Engineering Firm):** Vision → "To be the most trusted engineering solutions provider in South India." Quality Policy → "We commit to delivering defect-free products on time, every time, through continuous improvement of our processes and people." # SECTION 3 — Analytical Matrix & Conceptual Derivations ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation of TQM Components Since TQM is a **conceptual management framework** (not a numerical system), "derivations" here mean **logical decompositions** of how TQM components interrelate. This analytical framework is what KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers. ### Step 1 — The Input → Process → Output (IPO) Foundation Every organization is fundamentally an **input–process–output** system. $$\text{Inputs} \xrightarrow{\text{Processes}} \text{Outputs (Products/Services)} \rightarrow \text{Customer Satisfaction}$$ In TQM, this is extended to: $$\text{Inputs} \xrightarrow{\text{Quality Processes}} \text{Quality Outputs} \rightarrow \text{Customer Delight}$$ **Step-by-step logical transition:** 1. **Inputs** (raw materials, data, capital, human resources) enter the system. 2. **Processes** (manufacturing, service delivery) transform inputs into outputs. 3. TQM insists: quality must be **designed into the process**, not inspected out. 4. The **outputs** must satisfy or exceed **customer requirements** (both stated and implied). 5. **Customer feedback** loops back as an input, fueling **continuous improvement**. ### Step 2 — The TQM Equation Expansion Starting from the simplified TQM equation: $$TQM = C + P + C + C + I + S + F + Cm$$ Where: - $C$ = Customer focus - $P$ = People involvement - $C$ = Continuous improvement - $C$ = Communication - $I$ = Integrated system - $S$ = Strategic approach - $F$ = Fact-based decisions - $Cm$ = Commitment of top management > **Logical necessity:** Remove any one of these elements and TQM fails. This is why TQM is called a **"total"** system — it requires all components acting in concert. ### Step 3 — The Customer Satisfaction Function $$CS = f\left(Q_{\text{product}}, P_{\text{price}}, T_{\text{delivery}}, S_{\text{service}}\right)$$ Where: - $CS$ = Customer Satisfaction - $Q_{\text{product}}$ = Product Quality - $P_{\text{price}}$ = Price Fairness - $T_{\text{delivery}}$ = On-time Delivery - $S_{\text{service}}$ = After-sales Service In a TQM environment, organizations must **maximize** all four variables simultaneously — a multi-dimensional optimization problem that drives all process improvements. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — TQM vs. Traditional Quality Management This comparison matrix is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU ESE. | Parameter | Traditional Quality Management | Total Quality Management | |---|---|---| | **Scope** | Department / function-based | Organization-wide | | **Responsibility** | Quality department only | Every employee | | **Focus** | End-product inspection | Process improvement | | **Customer focus** | External customer | Internal + External customer | | **Improvement** | Reactive (problem-based) | Proactive (continuous, incremental) | | **Decision-making** | Intuition-based | Data and statistical tools | | **Supplier relationship** | Adversarial | Partnership (long-term) | | **Training** | Limited, on-the-job only | Continuous, holistic | | **Cost of quality** | Appraisal + Failure dominant | Prevention + Appraisal dominant | | **Time horizon** | Short-term | Long-term | | **Leadership role** | Top-down authority | Servant leadership, facilitation | --- ## 3.3 The Quality Improvement Spiral (Juran's Model) Juran conceptualized quality improvement as a **spiral**, not a flat cycle, because each improvement raises the bar for the next. $$\text{Level 1 (Baseline Quality)} \xrightarrow{\text{QC}} \text{Level 2} \xrightarrow{\text{QI}} \text{Level 3} \xrightarrow{\text{PDCA}} \text{Level 4} \rightarrow \ldots$$ **Step-by-step reasoning:** 1. Establish **infrastructure** for quality (quality council, teams). 2. Identify **the vital few** improvement projects (Pareto analysis). 3. Establish **infrastructure support** for each project. 4. Run each project through Juran Trilogy: **Plan → Control → Improve**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** Juran's model is the **most "examiner-friendly"** framework. It has appeared at least once in every KTU exam cycle (2019, 2020, 2022, 2023) — memorize it thoroughly. --- ## 3.4 Linking TQM to Engineering Education (Why this matters to KTU Students) As B.Tech students, you will work in companies practicing TQM. The connection: | Engineering Activity | TQM Application | |---|---| | Design (CAD/CAM) | Design for Six Sigma (DFSS), QFD | | Manufacturing | SPC, Kaizen, 5S | | Testing | Acceptance Sampling, DOE | | Project Management | Quality planning in PERT/CPM | | Service Industries | SERVQUAL gap model | > **Bottom line:** TQM is **not just management theory** — it directly affects how engineers design, produce, and deliver products. The Kerala industrial ecosystem (Kerala State Electronics Development Corporation, Keltron, etc.) actively practices TQM principles. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution of Quality Management (Timeline Flow) The following Mermaid block diagrams the four-stage evolution of quality thinking from Inspection to TQM. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[STAGE 1: INSPECTION
Pre-1920s] --> B[STAGE 2: QUALITY CONTROL
1920s to 1950s] B --> C[STAGE 3: QUALITY ASSURANCE
1950s to 1980s] C --> D[STAGE 4: TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
1980s to Present] A1[Detect defects
End-of-line
Costly rework]:::down --> A B1[SQC and SPC tools
Shewhart charts
Still detective]:::down --> B C1[Plan quality in
Systematic
Department-based]:::down --> C D1[Total culture
Customer obsession
Continuous improvement]:::up --> D classDef down fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,color:#000 classDef up fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#06a,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Deming's PDCA Cycle (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[PLAN
Define the problem
Analyze the process
Develop hypotheses] --> D D[DO
Pilot the plan on small scale
Collect data
Implement with care] --> C C[CHECK
Compare results vs. expected
Validate hypothesis
Identify gaps] --> A A[ACT
Standardize if successful
If not, revise and re-plan
Document learnings] --> P P:::planNode D:::doNode C:::checkNode A:::actNode classDef planNode fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#080,color:#000 classDef doNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa0,color:#000 classDef checkNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06a,color:#000 classDef actNode fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 TQM Framework — The 8 Principles Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Center[ORGANIZATIONAL EXCELLENCE]:::core Center --> P1[Customer Focus] Center --> P2[Total Employee Involvement] Center --> P3[Continuous Improvement Kaizen] Center --> P4[Process Centered Approach] Center --> P5[Integrated System] Center --> P6[Strategic Approach] Center --> P7[Fact Based Decisions] Center --> P8[Effective Communication] P1 --> R1[Outcome: Loyalty and Trust]:::result P2 --> R2[Outcome: Skilled Empowered Workforce]:::result P3 --> R3[Outcome: Long Term Stability]:::result P4 --> R4[Outcome: Consistent Quality Outputs]:::result P5 --> R5[Outcome: Departmental Synergy]:::result P6 --> R6[Outcome: Aligned Vision]:::result P7 --> R7[Outcome: Reduced Risk]:::result P8 --> R8[Outcome: Cultural Cohesion]:::result classDef core fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef result fill:#d6f0ff,stroke:#06a,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Cost of Quality (COQ) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR COQ[COST OF QUALITY]:::root COQ --> CONFORM[COST OF CONFORMANCE]:::up COQ --> NONCONFORM[COST OF NON CONFORMANCE]:::down CONFORM --> P[PREVENTION COST
