List any four characteristics of an effective Quality Policy. **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2024]** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A **Quality Policy** is a formal declaration by top management of an organization's overall intentions and direction related to quality, as required by **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2**. **Four Characteristics of an Effective Quality Policy:** 1. **Appropriate to the organization** — aligned with its purpose, context, and strategic direction 2. **Provides a framework for setting quality objectives** — includes measurable targets 3. **Includes commitment to satisfy requirements** — meets customer and regulatory needs 4. **Includes commitment to continual improvement** — supports ongoing enhancement of the QMS *(Additional acceptable characteristics: communicated, understood, applied, reviewed periodically, available to interested parties.)* --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Quality Policy and Quality Objectives with one example each. **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Quality Policy | Quality Objectives | |---|---|---| | **Nature** | Qualitative declaration | Quantitative, measurable | | **Scope** | Broad, organization-wide | Specific, function/department-wise | | **Time Frame** | Long-term (stable) | Short to medium-term (dynamic) | | **Source** | Top management intent | Derived from Quality Policy | | **ISO Clause** | 5.2 | 6.2 | | **Example** | *"We are committed to delivering defect-free products."* | *"Reduce defect rate to below 1% by December 2025."* | --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the concept of Quality Statements in TQM. Discuss the relationship between Vision, Mission, Quality Policy, and Quality Objectives with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **Model Answer:** **Concept of Quality Statements:** Quality Statements are formalized, top-level declarations made by an organization's top management that define its commitment to quality and provide the strategic direction for the Quality Management System. They are the **foundation of TQM implementation** and ensure that every employee understands the organization's quality goals. **Relationship Between Quality Statements:** 1. **Vision Statement (Aspirational)** - Defines what the organization aspires to become in the future - *Example*: *"To be the world's most customer-centric company"* (Amazon) 2. **Mission Statement (Operational)** - Translates the vision into the current purpose and business - *Example*: *"We strive to offer our customers the lowest possible prices, the best available selection, and the utmost convenience"* (Amazon) 3. **Quality Policy (Commitment)** - Top management's formal commitment to quality, derived from vision and mission - *Example*: *"Amazon is committed to providing a superior customer experience through continuous improvement, innovation, and operational excellence."* 4. **Quality Objectives (Measurable Targets)** - SMART goals cascaded from the Quality Policy - *Example*: *"Achieve 99.9% on-time delivery rate by Q4 2025"* and *"Reduce product return rate to below 2% by Q2 2025"* **Hierarchical Flow:** $$\text{Vision} \rightarrow \text{Mission} \rightarrow \text{Quality Policy} \rightarrow \text{Quality Objectives}$$ This cascade ensures **strategic alignment** from the aspirational to the operational level. --- **(b)** What are SMART Quality Objectives? Explain each component of the SMART framework with a relevant example for a manufacturing organization. **(7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]** **Model Answer:** **Definition of SMART Quality Objectives:** SMART is an acronym representing the five characteristics of effective quality objectives. SMART objectives translate the Quality Policy into **actionable, measurable targets** that can be tracked and achieved. **Components of SMART:** | Letter | Meaning | Description | Example (Manufacturing) | |---|---|---|---| | **S** | Specific | Clearly states what is to be accomplished, with no ambiguity | "Reduce the defect rate in Product X" | | **M** | Measurable | Includes a quantifiable metric | "...from 5% to less than 1%" | | **A** | Achievable | Realistic given current resources, skills, and technology | Achievable through Six Sigma training and process improvement | | **R** | Relevant | Directly linked to the Quality Policy and customer requirements | Aligned with "Customer Satisfaction" policy | | **T** | Time-bound | Has a clear deadline or timeline | "...by December 2025" | **Complete SMART Objective Example:** > *"Reduce the defect rate of Product X from 5% to less than 1% by December 2025, through the implementation of Six Sigma methodologies and enhanced employee training, in alignment with our commitment to customer satisfaction."* **Verification:** - **S**: Reduces defect rate of Product X ✓ - **M**: From 5% to <1% ✓ - **A**: Achievable via Six Sigma tools ✓ - **R**: Linked to customer satisfaction policy ✓ - **T**: By December 2025 ✓ **Benefits of SMART Objectives:** 1. Provide **clarity** in goal-setting 2. Enable **objective performance evaluation** 3. Facilitate **resource allocation** and prioritization 4. Support **accountability** at all levels 5. Drive **continual improvement** through measurable progress --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** What is a Quality Policy? Explain the seven essential requirements of a Quality Policy as specified in ISO 9001:2015. **(7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A **Quality Policy** is a formal statement of the overall intentions and direction of an organization related to quality, as expressed by top management. It is a **mandatory requirement** under **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2** and serves as the strategic foundation of the QMS. **Seven Essential Requirements of a Quality Policy (ISO 9001:2015):** 1. **Appropriate to the Purpose and Context of the Organization** - The policy must align with the organization's strategic direction, market position, and operational realities - *Example*: A hospital's quality policy should emphasize patient safety, not manufacturing efficiency 2. **Provides a Framework for Setting Quality Objectives** - The policy should outline the broad direction from which specific, measurable objectives can be derived - *Example*: A policy commitment to "customer satisfaction" provides a framework for objectives like "reduce complaint resolution time to 24 hours" 3. **Commitment to Satisfy Applicable Requirements** - Includes both **customer requirements** and **regulatory/statutory requirements** - *Example*: Compliance with FDA regulations in pharmaceutical companies 4. **Commitment to Continual Improvement** - The policy must explicitly support ongoing enhancement of the QMS - *Example*: A commitment to PDCA cycle implementation 5. **Available to Relevant Interested Parties** - Must be accessible to customers, suppliers, regulators, and the public - *Example*: Published on the company website and included in annual reports 6. **Communicated, Understood, and Applied Within the Organization** - Must be disseminated at all levels and translated into actionable behaviors - *Example*: Displayed in factories, included in training, and reinforced in meetings 7. **Reviewed and Updated as Necessary** - Top management must periodically review the policy for continuing suitability - *Example*: Annual management review meetings to update the policy --- **(b)** Discuss the process of developing and cascading Quality Objectives across an organization. Illustrate with an example from an engineering firm. **(7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]** **Model Answer:** **Process of Developing and Cascading Quality Objectives:** **Step 1: Derive Objectives from the Quality Policy** Quality objectives must be directly linked to the commitments made in the Quality Policy. Each policy statement becomes the basis for one or more measurable objectives. **Step 2: Apply SMART Criteria** Each objective must be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. **Step 3: Identify Resources and Assign Responsibilities** For each objective, identify: - Required resources (budget, equipment, training) - Responsible persons/departments - Accountability mechanisms **Step 4: Cascade Objectives Across Levels** Use the **Hoshin Kanri** (Policy Deployment) or **Balanced Scorecard** approach to cascade objectives from top management to the shop floor. **Step 5: Monitor, Review, and Update** Track progress through KPIs, conduct periodic reviews, and update objectives as needed. **Example: Engineering Firm — "PrecisionAuto Components Ltd."** | Cascade Level | Quality Objective | Metric | Target | Timeline | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Corporate** | Improve overall product quality | Customer rejection rate | From 2.5% to 0.8% | FY 2025-26 | | **Production Dept** | Reduce in-process defects | Defects per million (DPMO) | From 5,000 to 1,500 | Q2 2025 | | **Quality Assurance** | Increase internal audit coverage | % of processes audited | From 70% to 95% | Q3 2025 | | **Maintenance Team** | Reduce machine downtime | Hours of downtime per month | < 8 hours | Monthly | | **Individual Operator** | Adhere to standard operating procedures | SOP compliance rate | 100% | Daily | **Cascading Equation:** $$\text{Corporate Goal Achievement} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Departmental Goal Achievement}_i)$$ Where $n$ is the number of departments. This multiplicative relationship shows that **failure at any level** can compromise the entire corporate goal. **Tools for Monitoring:** - **Dashboards** (real-time KPI tracking) - **Management Review Meetings** (monthly/quarterly) - **Internal Audits** (ISO 9001 compliance) - **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts - **PDCA cycles** for continuous refinement --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing Policy with Objective** — Marks are deducted when students write "achieve 100% quality" as a policy (it is an objective). Policies are **commitments**, objectives are **measurable targets**. > 2. **Missing SMART verification** — When asked about quality objectives, students often forget to verify each SMART criterion and lose marks. > 3. **Ignoring ISO 9001:2015 clauses** — Always cite the relevant ISO clause (5.2 for policy, 6.2 for objectives) to demonstrate technical accuracy. > 4. **Generic examples** — Using real or realistic examples (e.g., Infosys, Tata Motors) earns higher marks than vague statements. > 5. **Not showing cascading** — For "developing objectives" questions, always show the hierarchical cascade — this is a KTU favorite. > 6. **Skipping communication aspect** — Quality statements must be *communicated, understood, and applied* — students often forget this deployment aspect. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Quality Statements** are the **strategic foundation** of any TQM implementation, comprising **Vision, Mission, Quality Policy, and Quality Objectives**. - The **Vision** is aspirational and long-term; the **Mission** defines the current purpose; the **Quality Policy** is the formal top-management commitment; the **Quality Objectives** are SMART, measurable targets. - **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2** mandates the Quality Policy; **Clause 6.2** mandates Quality Objectives. - A Quality Policy must be **appropriate, provide a framework, commit to satisfying requirements, commit to continual improvement, be communicated, be available, and be reviewed**. - Quality Objectives must follow the **SMART** framework: **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound**. - Quality objectives must be **cascaded** from corporate level to individual level using **Hoshin Kanri** or **Balanced Scorecard** approaches. - The **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is used to review and continually improve quality statements. - **Deming's PDSA** and **Juran's Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) all rely on well-defined quality statements. - **Communication and deployment** of quality statements is as important as their creation — they must be displayed, trained, and reinforced. - **Customer focus** is the central theme — every quality statement must ultimately serve customer satisfaction. - The **multiplicative cascade equation** (corporate goal = product of departmental goals) highlights that success requires alignment at every level. - **Continual improvement** is a mandatory commitment in modern quality policies (per ISO 9001:2015). - For KTU exams, always **cite ISO clauses**, use **realistic examples**, and **show the hierarchical relationship** in your answers.

topics.title: Strategic Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strategic Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Planning** is the systematic, long-range planning process by which an organization defines its **Vision**, **Mission**, and **Values**, analyzes its internal and external environments (using tools such as **SWOT** and **PEST**), formulates strategic objectives, deploys them across all functional levels, and continuously monitors performance to achieve sustainable competitive advantage and total customer satisfaction.

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, strategic planning is **not** a one-time corporate exercise — it is a **continuous, customer-driven, data-based leadership process** that aligns every department, every process, and every employee to the organization's quality policy and long-term survival goals.

### Key KTU 2024 Glossary Terms (High-Yield)

| Term | KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Vision** | A vivid, futuristic statement of *what the organization wants to become* |
| **Mission** | The fundamental *purpose and reason for existence* of the organization |
| **Values** | Core beliefs and ethical principles guiding daily behavior |
| **Strategic Objectives** | SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) long-term goals |
| **Strategic Deployment (Hoshin Kanri)** | Top-down and bottom-up alignment of strategy to operations |
| **SWOT** | Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats — situational analysis framework |
| **QSPM** | Quantitative Strategic Planning Matrix — prioritization tool |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hiking Analogy** 🥾🏔️
> 
> Imagine you and your friends decide to climb **Mount Everest**.
> 
> - The **Vision** = *"We want to stand on the summit."*
> - The **Mission** = *"We are an experienced mountaineering team committed to safe high-altitude expeditions."*
> - The **SWOT Analysis** = Before climbing, you ask: *"What are our strengths? (fitness) Weaknesses? (no winter gear) Opportunities? (clear weather window) Threats? (avalanche risk)?"*
> - The **Strategic Plan** = Choosing the South Col route, buying oxygen cylinders, fixing dates, and assigning Sherpas.
> - **Strategic Deployment (Hoshin)** = Every team member knows *their* role — cook, medic, lead climber — and their personal goals match the team's summit goal.
> - **Performance Monitoring** = Checking altitude, oxygen levels, and weather *continuously* (Plan–Do–Check–Act cycle).
> 
> **Climbing without a plan = death by avalanche. Business without strategic planning = death by competition.**

In business, a company like **Toyota** uses strategic planning to declare its vision of *"becoming the most respected automobile manufacturer."* Every Toyota plant, every supplier, and every employee in every department knows *exactly* how their daily work contributes to that vision. **That is strategic planning in action.**

---

## 1.3 Why Strategic Planning is the **Foundation** of TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Concept Anchor:**
> *"Quality is **planned in**, not inspected in."* — Joseph Juran
> 
> Strategic planning is **the highest level of "planning quality in"**. If strategy is wrong, every downstream quality tool (SPC, FMEA, Six Sigma) is wasted on the wrong targets.

The three foundational TQM gurus each emphasize strategic planning:

1. **Joseph Juran** — *"Strategic quality planning is the most under-deployed element of TQM."*
2. **W. Edwards Deming** — *"A system must have an aim. Without an aim, there is no system."*
3. **Philip Crosby** — *"Quality is free, but only if you plan for it."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Planning Process — Structured Logic Breakdown

The **KTU 2024 prescribed strategic planning process** follows a clear, sequential flow:

### Step 1: Establish the **Organizational Vision**
- *Why?* Defines long-term aspiration. Without it, the organization drifts.
- *How?* Senior leadership crafts a concise, memorable, inspiring statement (5–10 years horizon).

### Step 2: Define the **Mission Statement**
- *Why?* States *what we do, for whom, and how* — every day.
- *How?* A 1–3 sentence declaration answering: *Who are our customers? What value do we deliver? What makes us unique?*

### Step 3: Articulate **Core Values**
- *Why?* Values = the *ethical guardrails* of strategy.
- *How?* List 4–7 guiding principles (e.g., Customer First, Continuous Improvement, Integrity, Innovation).

### Step 4: Conduct **Situational (External & Internal) Analysis**
- *Why?* You cannot plan what you do not understand.
- *How?* Use **SWOT**, **PEST**, **Porter's Five Forces**, and **value-chain analysis**.

### Step 5: Formulate **Strategic Objectives** (SMART)
- *Why?* Translates vision into measurable targets.
- *How?* Apply SMART criteria: **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-bound.

### Step 6: **Strategic Deployment** (Hoshin Kanri / Policy Deployment)
- *Why?* Cascades strategy to every level of the organization.
- *How?* Top-down goal setting + bottom-up feedback loop using **X-Matrix**.

### Step 7: **Monitor, Review & Improve** (PDCA)
- *Why?* Strategic plans fail without ongoing measurement.
- *How?* Use **Balanced Scorecard (BSC)** KPIs across 4 perspectives.

---

## 2.2 The Three Strategic Planning Models (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Model | Proponent | Key Idea | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Juran Trilogy** | Joseph Juran | Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 2 | **Hoshin Kanri** | Japanese origin (Toyota) | Strategy Deployment using Catchball & X-Matrix | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High |
| 3 | **Balanced Scorecard** | Kaplan & Norton | 4-perspective KPI system (Financial, Customer, Process, Learning) | ⭐⭐ High |

---

## 2.3 SWOT Analysis — The Core Strategic Tool

| Quadrant | Type | Focus | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S**trengths | Internal / Positive | What we do better than competitors | Skilled workforce, strong brand |
| **W**eaknesses | Internal / Negative | What we lack | Old machinery, high defects |
| **O**pportunities | External / Positive | Favorable market conditions | New technology, growing market |
| **T**hreats | External / Negative | Risks from environment | New competitors, regulations |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"SWOT — Sail With Our Talent."*
> S = Sail (push forward), W = Withstand (fix), O = Opportunity, T = Threats to defend against.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (QSPM)

The **Quantitative Strategic Planning Matrix (QSPM)** is the most formula-heavy topic in strategic planning and is a frequent **14-mark KTU question**.

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Logic | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AS** | Attractiveness Score | Rated $1$ (not attractive) to $4$ (very attractive) | Dimensionless integer |
| **TAS** | Total Attractiveness Score | $\text{TAS} = \text{AS} \times W$ | Dimensionless |
| **STAS** | Sum Total Attractiveness Score | $\text{STAS} = \sum \text{TAS}$ across all factors | Dimensionless |
| **W** | Weight of a strategic factor | $0.0 \leq W \leq 1.0$, $\sum W = 1.0$ | Dimensionless |
| **$W_{\text{internal}}$** | Sum of internal factor weights | $\sum W_{\text{internal}} = 1.0$ | Dimensionless |
| **$W_{\text{external}}$** | Sum of external factor weights | $\sum W_{\text{external}} = 1.0$ | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Rule:** Weights of internal factors sum to 1.0; weights of external factors sum to 1.0 — **separately**, not combined. Students often lose marks by mixing them up.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Strategic planning is the **upstream input** to all engineering quality systems:
- **ISO 9001 QMS** requires a documented quality policy linked to strategic direction (Clause 4.1, 5.2).
- **Six Sigma** projects are *prioritized* based on strategic goals (DMAIC project selection).
- **Lean Manufacturing** value-stream maps are redesigned only after strategic direction is set.
- **Industry 4.0** adoption is a *strategic decision*, not a tactical one.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing the QSPM Total Attractiveness Score

The QSPM evaluates alternative strategies against key internal and external factors.

### Given (Hypothetical KTU Exam Scenario):
- A firm has **3 alternative strategies**: S1, S2, S3
- **Internal factor F1** has weight $W_1 = 0.15$
- **External factor F2** has weight $W_2 = 0.20$
- Attractiveness Scores are given in the table.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1: Define the Attractiveness Score (AS)**

$$
\text{AS}_{ij} \in \left\{ 1, \, 2, \, 3, \, 4 \right\}
$$

where $i$ indexes the strategy and $j$ indexes the factor. The integer values mean:
- $1$ = strategy does not address the factor
- $2$ = strategy partially addresses the factor
- $3$ = strategy addresses the factor well
- $4$ = strategy addresses the factor very well

**Step 2: Compute Total Attractiveness Score (TAS)**

$$
\text{TAS}_{ij} = \text{AS}_{ij} \times W_j
$$

**Step 3: Worked Numerical Example**

Suppose:
- For strategy S1 against factor F1: $\text{AS}_{11} = 3$, $W_1 = 0.15$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TAS}_{11} &= \text{AS}_{11} \times W_1 \\
&= 3 \times 0.15 \\
&= 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The score 3 means "addresses well," and 0.15 is the weight. Multiply them to get a weighted contribution.

**Step 4: Sum across all factors for each strategy**

$$
\text{STAS}_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{TAS}_{ij}
$$

For example, if S1 has 4 factors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STAS}_1 &= \text{TAS}_{11} + \text{TAS}_{12} + \text{TAS}_{13} + \text{TAS}_{14} \\
&= 0.45 + 0.60 + 0.30 + 0.20 \\
&= 1.55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Decision Rule**

$$
\text{Strategy selected} = \arg\max_{i} \left( \text{STAS}_{i} \right)
$$

The strategy with the **highest** STAS is recommended for implementation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Weight Normalization (Sum-to-One Constraint)

Weights in SWOT/QSPM must sum to 1.0 for internal and external factors **separately**. Here is the normalization derivation.

### Given:
- Raw importance ratings for 3 internal factors: $r_1 = 8$, $r_2 = 5$, $r_3 = 7$ (on a 1–10 scale).

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Compute sum of raw ratings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= r_1 + r_2 + r_3 \\
&= 8 + 5 + 7 \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Normalize each weight**

$$
W_i = \frac{r_i}{R}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate each weight**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= \frac{8}{20} = 0.40 \\
W_2 &= \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 \\
W_3 &= \frac{7}{20} = 0.35
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify the sum-to-one constraint**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{3} W_i &= 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.35 \\
&= 1.00 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

If the sum is not 1.00, weights are invalid for the QSPM.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Three Strategic Planning Models

| Criterion | Juran Trilogy | Hoshin Kanri | Balanced Scorecard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Joseph Juran (1954) | Japan / Deming (1950s) | Kaplan & Norton (1992) |
| **Core Focus** | Quality process | Strategy deployment | Performance measurement |
| **Phases** | Plan → Control → Improve | Catchball → X-Matrix → PDCA | 4 perspectives |
| **Top-Down** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Bottom-Up** | No | Yes (Catchball) | No |
| **Quantitative?** | Moderate | High (X-Matrix) | High (KPIs) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ |
| **Strengths** | Simple, classical | Holistic, employee-involved | Balanced, dashboard view |
| **Weaknesses** | Less detailed on deployment | Time-consuming to implement | Can become metric-only |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Vision-Mission-Objectives Hierarchy

The cascading logic from vision to daily tasks is **mathematically hierarchical**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vision} &\longrightarrow \text{Mission} \\
\text{Mission} &\longrightarrow \text{Strategic Objectives (3–5 yrs)} \\
\text{Strategic Objectives} &\longrightarrow \text{Tactical Goals (1 yr)} \\
\text{Tactical Goals} &\longrightarrow \text{Operational Targets (daily)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, for an engineering college:
1. **Vision:** *"To be a globally recognized center of engineering excellence."*
2. **Mission:** *"To produce industry-ready engineers with strong ethical values."*
3. **Strategic Objective:** *"Achieve 90% placement by 2028."*
4. **Tactical Goal (Year 1):** *"Train 500 students in Industry 4.0 tools."*
5. **Operational Target (Daily):** *"Conduct 2-hour hands-on lab sessions weekly in each department."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Each lower level must **directly support** the level above. If a daily operational target does not link to a strategic objective, it is **waste (Muda)** in lean terms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Strategic Planning Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Define Vision] --> A2[Step 2: Define Mission]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Articulate Values]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: SWOT and PEST Analysis]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Formulate SMART Objectives]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Strategic Deployment Hoshin Kanri]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Monitor via Balanced Scorecard]
    A7 --> A8{Performance Gap Detected?}
    A8 -- Yes --> A4
    A8 -- No --> A9[Continuous Improvement Kaizen]
    A9 --> A7
```

## 4.2 SWOT Analysis Quadrant Diagram

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title SWOT Analysis Framework for Strategic Planning
    x-axis Internal Origin --> External Origin
    y-axis Negative Impact --> Positive Impact
    quadrant-1 Opportunities
    quadrant-2 Strengths
    quadrant-3 Weaknesses
    quadrant-4 Threats
    Skilled Workforce: [0.85, 0.90]
    Strong Brand: [0.75, 0.80]
    Old Machinery: [0.30, 0.20]
    High Defect Rate: [0.20, 0.15]
    New Market Demand: [0.90, 0.85]
    Emerging AI Tech: [0.80, 0.95]
    New Competitors: [0.85, 0.15]
    Regulatory Changes: [0.90, 0.10]
```

## 4.3 Hoshin Kanri X-Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HP[Long Term Objectives Horizon 3 to 5 Yrs] --> AM[Annual Objectives]
    AM --> SP[Strategic Priorities]
    SP --> KPI[KPIs and Targets]
    KPI --> AOP[Action Plans and Owners]
    AOP --> HP
    HP -.Catchball.-> AOP
    AOP -.Feedback.-> HP
    HP -.Review.-> KPI
    AM -.PDCA.-> SP
```

## 4.4 Juran Trilogy for Strategic Quality Planning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QP[Quality Planning Strategic Level] --> QC[Quality Control Operational Level]
    QC --> QI[Quality Improvement Project Level]
    QI --> QP
    QP --> I1[Identify Customers]
    QP --> I2[Determine Customer Needs]
    QP --> I3[Develop Product Features]
    QP --> I4[Establish Quality Goals]
    QP --> I5[Plan Process Capability]
```

## 4.5 Balanced Scorecard 4-Perspective Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BSC[Balanced Scorecard Framework] --> P1[Financial Perspective]
    BSC --> P2[Customer Perspective]
    BSC --> P3[Internal Process Perspective]
    BSC --> P4[Learning and Growth Perspective]
    P1 --> K1[Revenue Growth ROI Cost Reduction]
    P2 --> K2[Customer Satisfaction NPS Retention Rate]
    P3 --> K3[Defect Rate Cycle Time Process Yield]
    P4 --> K4[Employee Training Skill Index Innovation Rate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Interpretation:** The Balanced Scorecard's 4 perspectives ensure that **financial metrics alone never dominate** the strategy — a key TQM principle. Organizations that only chase profit lose sight of customers, processes, and people.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define strategic planning in the context of Total Quality Management. List any four strategic planning models.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Strategic planning is the long-range leadership process of defining the organization's vision, mission, values, and strategic objectives, analyzing the internal and external environment, deploying strategy across all levels, and continuously monitoring performance to achieve total customer satisfaction and sustainable competitive advantage.
>
> **Four Strategic Planning Models (1.5 marks, 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Juran Trilogy** — Quality Planning, Control, Improvement
> 2. **Hoshin Kanri** — Japanese strategy deployment using X-Matrix
> 3. **Balanced Scorecard** — 4-perspective KPI measurement
> 4. **SWOT Analysis** — Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between Vision and Mission statements with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Vision | Mission |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Horizon** | 5–10 years (futuristic) | Present (today) |
| **Purpose** | *What we aspire to become* | *What we do and for whom* |
| **Nature** | Aspirational, emotional | Operational, factual |
| **Example: Infosys** | *"To be a globally respected corporation that provides best-of-breed business solutions."* | *"To achieve our objectives in an environment of fairness, honesty, and courtesy towards our clients, employees, vendors, and society at large."* |

> **Award:** 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the example distinction.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **SWOT Analysis** framework in detail. How is it used in strategic planning? Discuss with an example of an engineering college. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Hoshin Kanri** model of strategic deployment. Explain the **X-Matrix** and the concept of **Catchball**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — SWOT Analysis (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** SWOT stands for **S**trengths, **W**eaknesses (internal factors), **O**pportunities, and **T**hreats (external factors). It is a strategic planning tool developed by Albert Humphrey in the 1960s.

**Detailed Explanation (4 marks):**

| Quadrant | Definition | Example for an Engineering College |
|---|---|---|
| **Strengths** | Internal positive attributes | Experienced faculty, NAAC A++ accreditation, strong alumni network |
| **Weaknesses** | Internal negative attributes | Outdated labs, low research output, limited industry tie-ups |
| **Opportunities** | External favorable conditions | Growth of AI/ML industry, government Skill India mission, foreign collaborations |
| **Threats** | External unfavorable conditions | New private universities, online education platforms, AI-driven unemployment fears |

**Role in Strategic Planning (1 mark):** SWOT provides the **diagnostic base** from which strategic objectives are formulated. A strong opportunity with internal capability (SO strategy) suggests an **aggressive growth** path. A weakness with a threat (WT strategy) suggests a **defensive/defensive retreat** path.

**Matching Strategies (1 mark):** Four generic strategies emerge from SWOT combinations:
- **SO (Strength-Opportunity):** Aggressive / Maxi-Maxi
- **WO (Weakness-Opportunity):** Reorientation / Mini-Maxi
- **ST (Strength-Threat):** Diversification / Maxi-Mini
- **WT (Weakness-Threat):** Retrenchment / Mini-Mini

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Hoshin Kanri (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Hoshin Kanri (Japanese: 方針管理, "policy deployment") is a systematic strategic planning and deployment methodology that aligns an organization's long-term vision with day-to-day operations through **catchball** and the **X-Matrix**.

**Origin & Use (1 mark):** Originated in post-WWII Japan; widely used by **Toyota**, **Hewlett-Packard**, **Xerox**, and **Bridgestone** as a TQM strategic deployment tool.

**X-Matrix (3 marks):** The X-Matrix is a 4×4 grid that links:
1. **Long-term objectives (Horizon)** — top of X
2. **Annual objectives** — left arm
3. **Strategic priorities / Critical Success Factors** — right arm
4. **KPIs and targets** — bottom of X

Each cell shows the **strength of relationship** (strong, medium, weak) between the corresponding row and column elements. A **diagonal chain** of strong relationships reveals the *critical path* of strategy execution.

**Catchball Concept (2 marks):** Catchball is the **two-way communication process** of strategy deployment.
- **Top-down (throw):** Senior leaders "throw" the high-level goals to middle management.
- **Bottom-up (catch & return):** Middle management "catches," refines with operational realities, and "returns" a more detailed implementation plan.
- The ball is "tossed" 4–6 times until strategy and execution are perfectly aligned.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Juran Trilogy** for strategic quality planning with a neat diagram. How does it support TQM? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Balanced Scorecard (BSC)**? Explain its **four perspectives** and how it integrates with strategic planning. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Juran Trilogy (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The Juran Trilogy, proposed by **Joseph M. Juran**, is a three-part quality management philosophy that integrates strategic planning with operational quality activities. The three components are **Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement**.

**Three Components (4 marks):**

| Phase | Purpose | Key Steps |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Quality Planning** | Develop products/processes to meet customer needs | (i) Identify customers, (ii) Determine needs, (iii) Develop product features, (iv) Set quality goals, (v) Plan process capability, (vi) Prove process capability, (vii) Transfer to operations |
| **2. Quality Control** | Maintain stable operations; evaluate actual vs. planned | (i) Evaluate actual performance, (ii) Compare to goals, (iii) Act on differences |
| **3. Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure for continuous breakthrough | (i) Prove need, (ii) Identify projects, (iii) Establish teams, (iv) Provide resources, (v) Establish controls |

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QP[Quality Planning] --> QC[Quality Control]
    QC --> QI[Quality Improvement]
    QI --> QP
    QP --> R1[Roadmap to New Products]
    QC --> R2[Day to Day Operations]
    QI --> R3[Breakthrough Improvements]
```

**Support to TQM (1 mark):** Juran's trilogy places **strategic planning at the top** (Quality Planning), making TQM a leadership-driven philosophy, not just a toolset. It directly answers *"What does the customer need?"* before any quality tool is applied — the essence of customer-focused TQM.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Balanced Scorecard (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The **Balanced Scorecard (BSC)**, developed by **Robert Kaplan and David Norton** in 1992, is a strategic performance management framework that translates an organization's vision and strategy into a coherent set of **performance measures** across four perspectives.

**Four Perspectives (4 marks, 1 each):**

| # | Perspective | Core Question | Example KPIs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Financial** | *"How do we look to shareholders?"* | Revenue growth, ROI, cost reduction |
| 2 | **Customer** | *"How do customers see us?"* | Customer satisfaction, NPS, retention rate |
| 3 | **Internal Business Process** | *"What processes must we excel at?"* | Defect rate, cycle time, process yield |
| 4 | **Learning & Growth** | *"How do we sustain our ability to change and improve?"* | Employee training hours, innovation rate, skill index |

**Integration with Strategic Planning (2 marks):**
- The BSC is the **execution arm** of strategic planning. It turns vague vision/mission statements into **measurable, balanced KPIs**.
- It prevents the **"financial-only trap"** — many firms collapse despite strong financials because they ignored customers, processes, or innovation.
- The BSC is updated **annually** to align with evolving strategic objectives, creating a tight feedback loop with SWOT and Hoshin Kanri.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Strategic Planning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Vision with Mission** — Vision is *future/aspirational*; Mission is *present/purposeful*. Mixing them up costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **SWOT Quadrant Mix-up** — Strengths/Weaknesses are **internal**; Opportunities/Threats are **external**. Many students write "skilled workforce" under threats. ❌
> 3. **Hoshin Kanri vs. PDCA Confusion** — Hoshin is *strategic deployment*; PDCA is *operational improvement*. They are NOT the same.
> 4. **Balanced Scorecard = 4 perspectives, not 3** — A frequent error is missing the "Learning & Growth" perspective. KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **QSPM Weight Sum = 1.0 separately for internal AND external** — Do not sum them together. Internal weights sum to 1.0; external weights sum to 1.0 — *in two separate QSPM sections*.
> 6. **Not drawing a diagram** — For 7-mark questions, KTU awards **0.5–1 mark** specifically for a labeled diagram. Always include one.
> 7. **Ignoring "engineering context"** — KTU values examples from engineering/industrial settings. Use manufacturing, IT, automotive, or construction examples whenever possible.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Strategic Planning (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Strategic planning is a *systematic, long-range leadership process* integrating vision, mission, values, SWOT, objectives, deployment, and monitoring.
- ✅ **Vision** = Future aspiration. **Mission** = Present purpose. **Values** = Ethical guardrails.
- ✅ **SMART Objectives** = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- ✅ **SWOT** = Strengths, Weaknesses (internal); Opportunities, Threats (external).
- ✅ **Four SWOT strategies** = SO (Aggressive), WO (Reorientation), ST (Diversification), WT (Retrenchment).
- ✅ **Juran Trilogy** = Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement (cycle).
- ✅ **Hoshin Kanri** = Japanese strategy deployment using **X-Matrix** and **Catchball**.
- ✅ **Catchball** = Two-way (top-down and bottom-up) strategy refinement process.
- ✅ **Balanced Scorecard** = 4 perspectives: Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth.
- ✅ **QSPM Formula:** $\text{TAS} = \text{AS} \times W$, and $\text{STAS} = \sum \text{TAS}$.
- ✅ **Weight Constraint:** Internal weights sum to 1.0, external weights sum to 1.0 — *separately*.
- ✅ **PDCA** = Plan, Do, Check, Act — applied at both operational and strategic levels.
- ✅ **TQM Linkage:** Strategic planning is the *highest level* of "planning quality in" — without it, all quality tools (SPC, FMEA, Six Sigma) target the wrong goals.
- ✅ **Juran's Quote:** *"Strategic quality planning is the most under-deployed element of TQM."* — A favorite KTU quote.
- ✅ **Engineering Examples:** Toyota (Hoshin), Infosys (Vision/Mission), ISO 9001 Clause 5.2 (Quality Policy).
- ✅ **Examiner Tip:** Always include a *labeled diagram* in 7-mark answers and use *engineering/industrial examples* for full marks.

---

**End of Notes — Strategic Planning (Module 1, PESFT746)**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech examination standards.*
