topics.title: Deming Philosophy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deming Philosophy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming Philosophy** is an integrated, system-based management philosophy developed by **Dr. W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993)**, the American statistician, engineer, and management consultant who revolutionized industrial quality practices — most notably in post-war Japan. Formally, it is defined as *"a holistic theory of management that views an organization as a collection of interconnected processes operating as a system, where quality is achieved by reducing variation through statistical thinking, fostering a culture of continuous improvement, and transforming management's responsibility from inspection to leadership."*

The philosophy rests on four interrelated pillars collectively known as the **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** and operationalized through the famous **14 Points for Management**, the **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) Cycle**, the **Chain Reaction of Quality**, and identification of obstacles such as the **Seven Deadly Diseases**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Patient vs. The Healthy Body"**
> 
> Imagine a hospital that treats sick patients with medicine one by one (this is **inspection/rework**). Now imagine a city that focuses on clean water, vaccination drives, sanitation, and exercise programs so that fewer people fall sick in the first place (this is the **Deming approach**). Deming argued that **quality cannot be inspected into a product** — it must be **built into the system** from the start. Just as a doctor cannot cure a disease without understanding the patient's whole body (system), a manager cannot improve quality without understanding the **entire production system**, the **variation** in it, the **psychology** of the people in it, and the **theory of knowledge** required to learn from it.

## 1.3 Key Constants, Metrics & Terminology

- **14 Points for Management** — the core prescriptive framework.
- **PDSA Cycle** — **4** iterative steps: Plan, Do, Study, Act.
- **System of Profound Knowledge** — **4** components (Appreciation for a System, Variation, Psychology, Theory of Knowledge).
- **Seven Deadly Diseases** — **7** management obstacles to quality.
- **Chain Reaction of Quality** — a **5-stage** sequence linking quality to profitability.
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** — Deming famously revised this: *"Problems are 85% in the system and 15% in the workers."*

## 1.4 Why Deming Philosophy is a KTU High-Priority Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PESFT746 — Module 1)**, Deming's philosophy forms the foundational reference for all subsequent quality frameworks (Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum). Examiners routinely test:
> - The **14 Points** (direct recall & application).
> - The **PDSA Cycle** (analogy with PDCA, differences).
> - The **Chain Reaction of Quality** (logical sequence).
> - The **Seven Deadly Diseases** (identification & remediation).
> - The **System of Profound Knowledge** (Bloom level: Understand/Apply).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 14 Points for Management (Operational Breakdown)

Deming's **14 Points** are prescriptive commandments for transforming Western management. They are **not** a checklist — they form a **connected system**. Below is the structured, KTU-exam-ready breakdown:

| # | Point (Paraphrased Title) | Core Logic ("Why" and "How") |
|---|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Create constancy of purpose** | Long-term commitment to improvement, innovation, and staying in business — replaces short-term profit obsession. |
| 2 | **Adopt the new philosophy** | Top management must lead the transformation; tolerating poor quality is no longer acceptable. |
| 3 | **Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality** | Inspection is costly, late, and ineffective; build quality into the **process** upstream. |
| 4 | **End the practice of awarding business on price alone** | Lowest-cost supplier often delivers the highest total cost; move toward single-source partnerships with quality metrics. |
| 5 | **Improve constantly every process** | Use **PDSA** in all activities; continuous improvement (Kaizen) never stops. |
| 6 | **Institute training on the job** | Most workers are blamed for system errors due to lack of training; train everyone in statistical thinking. |
| 7 | **Institute leadership** | Supervision must be transformed into **leadership**; leaders understand the system they manage. |
| 8 | **Drive out fear** | Fear prevents workers from reporting problems; a fearful workforce produces bad data, bad product, and bad morale. |
| 9 | **Break down barriers between departments** | Silos destroy quality; everyone in the value chain works for the **common good**. |
| 10 | **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** | "Zero Defects" posters don't fix systems; they only create resentment. |
| 11 | **Eliminate numerical quotas** | Quotas force people to produce bad work to meet numbers; replace with **statistical process control**. |
| 12 | **Remove barriers to pride of workmanship** | Annual rating systems, focus on hours, and piecework destroy intrinsic motivation. |
| 13 | **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement** | Continuous learning is the engine of transformation. |
| 14 | **Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation** | Quality is **everyone's job**; create a top-management structure to drive Point 1. |

## 2.2 The PDSA (Deming) Cycle vs. PDCA

Deming originally used **Shewhart's** cycle. He later renamed "Check" to "Study" to emphasize **learning**, not just verification.

| Step | PDSA (Deming) | PDCA (Conventional) | KTU-Relevant Distinction |
|------|---------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Plan** — Identify a goal, prediction, and method. | Plan | Identical |
| 2 | **Do** — Implement the plan on a small scale, collect data. | Do | Identical |
| 3 | **Study** — Analyze results, learn, compare prediction vs. outcome. | Check | **"Study" implies deep learning; "Check" can mean superficial verification.** |
| 4 | **Act** — Standardize, modify, or restart the cycle. | Act | Identical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always write **PDSA** (not PDCA) for Deming. Examiners explicitly mark the **"Study"** terminology.

## 2.3 The Chain Reaction of Quality

The **Chain Reaction** is Deming's argument that quality is **profitable**, not expensive. The sequence is:

$$ \text{Improve Quality} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Decrease Costs} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Productivity Increases} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Price Drops} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Market Share Grows} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Long-Term Profit} $$

**Logic:** Better processes → fewer defects/rework → lower cost per unit → competitive pricing → increased demand → sustainable profitability. Quality is thus a **leading indicator** of financial success.

## 2.4 The Seven Deadly Diseases (Obstacles to Transformation)

Deming identified **7** management practices that must be **eradicated** before quality can flourish:

1. **Lack of constancy of purpose** — short-term thinking.
2. **Emphasis on short-term profits** — drives fear, underinvestment.
3. **Evaluation of performance, merit rating, or annual review of performance** — destroys teamwork.
4. **Mobility of management** — job-hopping managers never see long-term impact.
5. **Running a company on visible figures alone** — common-cause vs. special-cause confusion.
6. **Excessive medical costs** — driven by poor quality of life and working conditions.
7. **Excessive costs of warranty, fueled by lawyers** — liability-driven defensive management.

## 2.5 System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK) — 4 Components

> [!NOTE]
> The **SoPK** is Deming's most advanced framework: *"A system of knowledge that allows managers to optimize an organization."*

| Component | Core Idea | KTU Exam Hook |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **1. Appreciation for a System** | The organization is an interdependent network; optimizing a part can sub-optimize the whole. | "Optimize the whole, not the parts." |
| **2. Knowledge of Variation** | Distinguish **common-cause** (system) variation from **special-cause** (assignable) variation. | "Don't react to every variation — first ask: is the system stable?" |
| **3. Theory of Knowledge** | Management is a branch of learning; predictions require theory, and learning requires empirical testing. | Links to PDSA; prediction → experiment → comparison → theory revision. |
| **4. Psychology** | People are not machines; intrinsic motivation, recognition, and respect drive performance. | Drives Points 8, 10, 12. |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Analytical / Conceptual)

Since this is a Management topic, "formulas" are operational, statistical, and decision-logic expressions.

| Concept | Operational Expression | Variables | KTU Application |
|---------|------------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Deming's 85/15 Rule | $P_{system} : P_{worker} = 85 : 15$ | $P$ = Problem source | Contradicts Taylor's worker-blaming model. |
| Chain Reaction (Quality Profit) | $Q \uparrow \Rightarrow C \downarrow \Rightarrow \pi \uparrow$ | $Q$=Quality, $C$=Cost, $\pi$=Profit | Used in Part B essays. |
| PDSA Iterations | $n_{cycles} \to \infty$ (Kaizen) | $n$ = cycle count | Continuous improvement, no terminal point. |
| Common vs. Special Cause | $\sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{common}^2 + \sigma_{special}^2$ | $\sigma$ = standard deviation | Knowledge of Variation — statistical. |
| Pareto (Deming's revision) | $\frac{\text{System defects}}{\text{Total defects}} \approx 0.85$ | Defect origin | System redesign > worker discipline. |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing**: Deming's statistical process control (SPC) underpins Six Sigma and ISO 9001.
- **Software Engineering**: PDSA maps to **Agile/Scrum sprints** (Plan → Do → Study/Retrospective → Act).
- **Healthcare**: **IHI (Institute for Healthcare Improvement)** uses PDSA to reduce hospital infection rates.
- **Service Industry**: Knowledge of Variation drives queue management at banks and call centers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Chain Reaction of Quality (Logical Deduction)

Unlike physics, Deming's Chain Reaction is a **causal-logical derivation**, not a mathematical one. We construct it step-by-step so it is fully transparent for KTU long-answer questions.

**Step 1 — Hypothesis (Premise):**
Quality is a function of process design, not inspection.

$$ Q = f(\text{Process Design}, \text{System Capability}) $$

**Step 2 — Defect & Rework Cost Relationship:**
If quality improves, defects per unit drop, and rework cost per unit falls:

$$ C_{total} = C_{conformance} + C_{non-conformance} $$

where $C_{non-conformance} \propto \text{Defect Rate}$. So as $Q \uparrow$, $C_{non-conformance} \downarrow$, and $C_{total} \downarrow$.

**Step 3 — Productivity Consequence:**
Lower rework means less time/material waste per good unit:

$$ \text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Labor Hours} + \text{Material Input}} \uparrow $$

**Step 4 — Price Strategy:**
A firm with lower cost can reduce price without margin loss:

$$ \text{Price}_{new} = \text{Cost} + \text{Margin}_{target}, \quad \text{Cost} \downarrow \Rightarrow \text{Price} \downarrow $$

**Step 5 — Market Share Response (Elasticity Argument):**
Lower price increases demand (assuming price elasticity of demand $E_p > 1$):

$$ \frac{\Delta Q_d}{Q_d} = E_p \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{P}, \quad E_p < 0 $$

**Step 6 — Long-Term Profitability:**
Increased volume and lower cost compound to long-term profit:

$$ \pi_{LT} = (\text{Price} - \text{UnitCost}) \times \text{Volume}_{new} \uparrow \uparrow $$

**Final Chained Form:**

$$ Q \uparrow \;\rightarrow\; C \downarrow \;\rightarrow\; \text{Productivity} \uparrow \;\rightarrow\; P \downarrow \;\rightarrow\; \text{MarketShare} \uparrow \;\rightarrow\; \pi_{LT} \uparrow \;\;\blacksquare $$

## 3.2 Derivation of Deming's 85/15 Rule (Statistical Basis)

**Step 1 — Problem Classification:**
Every defect (problem) in a process has a source. Deming classified sources as either **system-caused** or **worker-caused**.

$$ D_{total} = D_{system} + D_{worker} $$

**Step 2 — Empirical Observation:**
Across hundreds of organizations Deming studied, a consistent pattern emerged:

$$ \frac{D_{system}}{D_{total}} \approx 0.85, \quad \frac{D_{worker}}{D_{total}} \approx 0.15 $$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule (Managerial Implication):**
Management effort should be allocated proportionally:

$$ \text{Management Effort}_{system} : \text{Management Effort}_{worker} = 85 : 15 $$

**Step 4 — Counter-intuitive Conclusion (Deming's famous quote):**
> *"Ninety percent of the time, the problem is the system, not the worker. The worker is not the problem — the system is the problem."*

## 3.3 PDSA Cycle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

| Step | Action | Output (Deliverable) |
|------|--------|----------------------|
| **Plan** | Define objective; form hypothesis; design data collection plan. | Plan document, prediction statement. |
| **Do** | Execute on a small scale (pilot); record data and observations. | Raw data, observed outcomes. |
| **Study** | Compare actual vs. predicted; identify learnings; verify hypothesis. | Lessons learned, charts (control charts, run charts). |
| **Act** | If successful, standardize (SOP update). If failed, revise plan → restart cycle. | Updated standard, new hypothesis. |

**Mathematical Expression of Iteration:**

$$ \text{Process}_{n+1} = \text{Process}_{n} + \Delta_{PDSA,n} $$

where $\Delta_{PDSA,n}$ is the **learning delta** from cycle $n$. As $n \to \infty$, the process converges toward **optimum** (Kaizen asymptotic behavior).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common-Cause vs. Special-Cause Variation

This is a high-yield KTU analytical distinction (Knowledge of Variation).

| Feature | Common-Cause Variation | Special-Cause Variation |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Origin** | Inherent to the system design. | External, transient, assignable. |
| **Distribution** | Statistical (e.g., Normal). | Point disturbances. |
| **Action** | Improve the **system** (management's job). | Localize and **remove** the cause (worker's job). |
| **Detection** | Control charts over time. | Control chart points outside control limits. |
| **Deming's Rule** | Stop blaming workers; rebuild the system. | Investigate the specific anomaly. |
| **Reactive Tampering Risk** | High — mistreating common-cause as special makes it worse. | Low — straightforward investigation. |

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Deming's Points Grouped by Theme

For KTU Part B (14-mark) answers, grouping the 14 Points by theme demonstrates **deep understanding** and earns higher marks.

| Theme | Points | Strategic Intent |
|-------|--------|------------------|
| **Transformation Leadership** | 1, 2, 7, 14 | Top-down commitment to change. |
| **System Thinking** | 3, 5, 9 | Eliminate silos, optimize whole. |
| **Statistical & Process Thinking** | 6, 11, 12, 13 | Train, eliminate quotas, education. |
| **Cultural & Psychological** | 8, 10 | Remove fear, drop slogans. |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | 4 | Long-term supplier partnership. |

## 3.6 Deming's Losses by Category (Cost-of-Poor-Quality Logic)

Deming quantified four categories of loss in any system:

$$ L_{total} = L_{defects} + L_{rework} + L_{warranty} + L_{lost\_opportunity} $$

**Logic of optimization:** Traditional accounting captures only the first two. Deming argued that $L_{lost\_opportunity}$ (from poor customer perception) is often the largest hidden loss — justifying investment in quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Linking Deming's economic argument to **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure (P-A-F) cost model** is a guaranteed 2-mark bonus in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The PDSA Cycle (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start New Improvement Cycle]) --> planA["PLAN Define objective Form hypothesis Design data plan"]
    planA --> doA["DO Execute on small scale Collect data"]
    doA --> studyA["STUDY Compare actual vs predicted Identify lessons learned"]
    studyA --> decisionA{Hypothesis Validated?}
    decisionA -- Yes --> actA["ACT Standardize Update SOP Document best practice"]
    decisionA -- No --> planA
    actA --> loopA([Return to Step 1 Next Improvement Target])
    loopA --> planA
```

## 4.2 The Chain Reaction of Quality (Mermaid Left-to-Right Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q1["Improve Quality"] --> q2["Costs Decrease"]
    q2 --> q3["Productivity Increases"]
    q3 --> q4["Price Becomes Competitive"]
    q4 --> q5["Market Share Grows"]
    q5 --> q6["Long-Term Profit and Survival"]
    style q1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style q6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 System of Profound Knowledge (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sopk["System of Profound Knowledge SoPK"]
    sopk --> c1["Appreciation for a System Interdependence Optimization of the whole"]
    sopk --> c2["Knowledge of Variation Common cause vs Special cause SPC Charts"]
    sopk --> c3["Theory of Knowledge Prediction Experiment Learning Loop"]
    sopk --> c4["Psychology Intrinsic motivation Respect Human behavior"]
    c1 --> mastery["Management Mastery Transformational Leadership"]
    c2 --> mastery
    c3 --> mastery
    c4 --> mastery
    style sopk fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style mastery fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Deming's 14 Points — Theme Cluster Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root14["Deming 14 Points Cluster Map"]
    root14 --> grp1["Theme 1 Transformation Leadership Points 1 2 7 14"]
    root14 --> grp2["Theme 2 System and Process Thinking Points 3 5 9"]
    root14 --> grp3["Theme 3 Statistical and Education Points 6 11 12 13"]
    root14 --> grp4["Theme 4 Cultural Transformation Points 8 10"]
    root14 --> grp5["Theme 5 Strategic Sourcing Point 4"]
    style root14 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
```

## 4.5 Seven Deadly Diseases — Diagnosis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    symp["Symptom Chronic Quality Failure"] --> diag{Diagnose Root}
    diag -- Short term profit focus --> d1["Disease 1 Lack of Constancy of Purpose"]
    diag -- Quotas and ratings --> d2["Disease 3 Evaluation by Merit Rating"]
    diag -- Figure obsessed --> d3["Disease 5 Management by Visible Figures"]
    diag -- High turnover --> d4["Disease 4 Mobility of Management"]
    diag -- Warranty lawsuits --> d5["Disease 7 Excessive Legal Costs"]
    style symp fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style diag fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
```

## 4.6 Variation Decision Logic (Statistical Action Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp["Data Point Observed"] --> stab{Process in Statistical Control?}
    stab -- Yes Common Cause --> action1["Stop Blaming Workers Improve the System"]
    stab -- No Out of Control Limits --> action2["Identify Special Cause Investigate and Remove"]
    action1 --> lead1["Management Responsibility Long Term"]
    action2 --> lead2["Worker and Engineer Responsibility Localized"]
    style inp fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000
    style stab fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between PDSA and PDCA cycles. Why does Deming prefer "Study" over "Check"?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> PDSA (Plan–Do–Study–Act) is Deming's version of Shewhart's learning cycle. PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) is the conventional version. The difference is in the third step: **"Check"** implies a passive verification of conformity, whereas **"Study"** implies deep analytical learning — comparing predicted outcomes to actual results, extracting insights, and revising the underlying theory. Deming preferred "Study" because his philosophy is rooted in the **Theory of Knowledge** (predictions tested empirically); mere checking does not generate organizational learning. **(3 marks)**

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **List any three of Deming's Seven Deadly Diseases and state their impact on quality.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> (i) **Lack of constancy of purpose** — leads to short-termism and erosion of long-term improvement investment. (ii) **Evaluation by merit rating** — destroys teamwork, creates fear, and rewards individuals over system performance. (iii) **Running a company on visible figures alone** — confuses common-cause with special-cause variation, leading to reactive tampering that worsens outcomes. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
>
> **(a) Explain Deming's 14 Points for Management, grouping them by theme.** **(7 marks)**
> **(b) Discuss the Chain Reaction of Quality with a real-world manufacturing example.** **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Deming's 14 Points grouped by theme (7 marks):**

**Theme 1 — Transformation Leadership (Points 1, 2, 7, 14):**
- Point 1: Create constancy of purpose for innovation and improvement.
- Point 2: Adopt the new philosophy of quality at the leadership level.
- Point 7: Institute leadership (not just supervision).
- Point 14: Put everyone to work to accomplish the transformation.

**Theme 2 — System and Process Thinking (Points 3, 5, 9):**
- Point 3: Cease dependence on inspection — build quality into the process.
- Point 5: Improve constantly every process (PDSA).
- Point 9: Break down barriers between departments.

**Theme 3 — Statistical Thinking and Education (Points 6, 11, 12, 13):**
- Point 6: Institute on-the-job training.
- Point 11: Eliminate numerical quotas; use statistical control.
- Point 12: Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
- Point 13: Institute vigorous education and self-improvement.

**Theme 4 — Cultural Transformation (Points 8, 10):**
- Point 8: Drive out fear so workers can report problems.
- Point 10: Eliminate slogans and exhortations that have no system basis.

**Theme 5 — Strategic Sourcing (Point 4):**
- Point 4: End the practice of awarding business on price alone.

**(b) Chain Reaction with manufacturing example (7 marks):**

Consider an Indian automotive component manufacturer (e.g., a Tier-1 supplier of brake assemblies).

1. **Improve Quality:** Implement Statistical Process Control (SPC) and PDSA cycles on the CNC machining line.
2. **Decrease Costs:** Defect rate drops from 8% to 1.5%. Cost of rework, scrap, and warranty claims fall sharply.
3. **Productivity Increases:** Machine uptime improves because operators no longer interrupt production for sorting defects.
4. **Price Becomes Competitive:** With lower unit cost, the firm can offer OEM customers a 5% price reduction.
5. **Market Share Grows:** The OEM shifts larger orders to this supplier due to on-time delivery and quality reputation.
6. **Long-Term Profit and Survival:** Sustained orders, expansion to two more production lines, and stable employment for workers.

The chain reaction thus converts quality from a "cost center" into a **profit driver** — Deming's central economic argument.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
>
> **(a) Explain the System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK) with its four components.** **(7 marks)**
> **(b) Critically evaluate Deming's 85/15 rule and its implications for management practice.** **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) System of Profound Knowledge — 4 components (7 marks):**

Deming's SoPK is the **intellectual framework** that allows a manager to lead transformation. It has four interrelated components:

1. **Appreciation for a System (2 marks):** An organization is a network of interdependent components. The manager must understand how parts interact and optimize the **whole**, not local sub-units. Example: Reducing the cost of one supplier may raise the assembly-line failure rate downstream.

2. **Knowledge of Variation (2 marks):** All processes exhibit variation. The manager must distinguish **common-cause** (systemic, requiring system redesign) from **special-cause** (assignable, requiring local investigation). Tools: control charts, run charts.

3. **Theory of Knowledge (1.5 marks):** Management decisions are predictions. They require a theory, which is refined only by empirical testing (PDSA). "Knowledge comes from experience, but experience alone does not yield knowledge — it must be tested against theory."

4. **Psychology (1.5 marks):** People are not machines. Intrinsic motivation, recognition, dignity, and meaningful work drive performance. Fear and external rewards distort data and decisions.

**(b) Critical evaluation of the 85/15 rule (7 marks):**

**Origin & Meaning:** Deming observed that in most organizations, **about 85% of problems are caused by the system** (processes, materials, management decisions) and only **15% by individual workers**. He asserted: *"The system is the problem, not the worker."*

**Supporting Evidence:** Deming's consulting experience in post-war Japan (Toyota, Sony) and later U.S. firms showed dramatic quality improvements when managers redesigned the system rather than punishing workers.

**Implications for Management Practice:**
- Performance ratings and piece-rate incentives become counterproductive because they blame workers for system flaws.
- Investment in **process improvement, training, and SPC** yields 5–10× higher ROI than workforce discipline programs.
- Quality circles and suggestion systems succeed only when the system is fair; otherwise, they become window-dressing.

**Criticisms:**
- The 85/15 ratio is **empirical, not statistically proven** as a universal law.
- It is **context-dependent** — in some service or knowledge work, human-error contribution may be higher.
- Misapplication can **excuse worker accountability** (e.g., in safety-critical roles).

**Conclusion:** Despite its empirical nature, the 85/15 rule remains a **powerful heuristic** that shifts management attention from blame to system design — the central message of Deming's philosophy.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing "PDCA" instead of "PDSA"** — Examiners explicitly deduct for the wrong acronym; Deming renamed "Check" to "Study" deliberately.
> 2. **Listing the 14 Points without grouping/justification** — Mere enumeration is worth only 3–4 of 7 marks. The marks are awarded for **theme clustering, examples, and explanation of intent**.
> 3. **Confusing Deming with Juran** — Juran's "Fitness for Use" vs. Deming's "System Optimization" is a classic trap; examiners test this contrast.
> 4. **Stating "Quality is expensive"** — This is the **opposite** of Deming's thesis; quality is a profit driver via the Chain Reaction.
> 5. **Forgetting the SoPK's 4 components** — Examiners expect all four explicitly; omitting Psychology or Theory of Knowledge costs a full mark.
> 6. **Ignoring "common-cause vs. special-cause"** in variation questions — the most-testable analytical concept.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Deming's Core Thesis:** Quality is **built into the system**, not inspected in.
- **PDSA (not PDCA):** Plan → Do → Study → Act — emphasizes **learning**, not just checking.
- **14 Points** are a **connected system**, not a checklist; group by 5 themes for answer impact.
- **Chain Reaction:** $Q \uparrow \rightarrow C \downarrow \rightarrow \text{Productivity} \uparrow \rightarrow P \downarrow \rightarrow \text{MarketShare} \uparrow \rightarrow \pi_{LT} \uparrow$.
- **SoPK — 4 Pillars:** System, Variation, Theory of Knowledge, Psychology.
- **85/15 Rule:** 85% of problems are system-caused; 15% worker-caused (revised Pareto).
- **Seven Deadly Diseases** must be **eradicated**; they are management's responsibility.
- **Knowledge of Variation:** Distinguish **common-cause** (system redesign) from **special-cause** (local fix). Reactive tampering is a sin.
- **Deming's Redefinition of Quality:** Not conformance alone, but **on-time delivery, value, and continuous improvement**.
- **Difference from Juran:** Deming = System & Variation; Juran = Customer & Fitness for Use; **don't conflate**.
- **Difference from Crosby:** Deming = Statistical & System thinking; Crosby = Zero Defects & Do It Right First Time; both are right — different emphases.
- **"Study" vs. "Check":** Always use **Study** in Deming's cycle.
- **System of Profound Knowledge** is Deming's **most advanced** framework; often tested as a 7-mark Part B question.
- **KTU-2024 key words to memorize:** constancy of purpose, profound knowledge, chain reaction, common cause, special cause, transformation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Barriers to TQM Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Barriers to TQM Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT746 — Module 1)**
> *Barriers to TQM Implementation* refer to the **systemic, cultural, structural, and behavioral obstacles** that prevent an organization from successfully adopting, executing, and sustaining Total Quality Management (TQM) practices across all hierarchical levels.

In the context of KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework for the 2024 Scheme, barriers to TQM are categorized as **internal organizational impediments** that disrupt the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle and erode the **8 TQM principles** (Customer Focus, Leadership, People Involvement, Process Approach, System Approach, Continuous Improvement, Fact-Based Decision Making, and Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Marathan Runner's Hurdles"**
>
> Imagine a company wants to win a *Quality Marathon*. The runners (employees) are ready, but the track is filled with **invisible hurdles** — potholes, broken water stations, missing direction signs, a coach who never shows up, and weather they were never warned about. Even the fastest runner cannot win.
>
> **Barriers to TQM are exactly those hurdles.** They are not always visible on the surface, but they break the rhythm of the marathon — your TQM journey. Recognizing them is the first step to clearing the track.

### 1.3 The 3-Layer View of TQM Barriers

Every KTU examiner expects students to recognize that TQM barriers operate at **three concentric layers**:

1. **Layer 1 — Strategic/Leadership Barriers** (Top Management)
2. **Layer 2 — Operational/Process Barriers** (Middle Management)
3. **Layer 3 — Cultural/Human Barriers** (Workforce)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** In any 14-mark KTU question on this topic, explicitly mention the **three-layer view**. It scores you an easy **2 marks** for structured thinking before you even list the barriers.

### 1.4 Quick Recall Snapshot

| Layer | Primary Owner | Typical Barrier |
|---|---|---|
| Strategic | Senior Leadership | Lack of Vision & Commitment |
| Operational | Middle Managers | Poor Process Mapping |
| Cultural | Workforce | Resistance to Change |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

TQM is fundamentally a **socio-technical transformation**, not merely a statistical or procedural upgrade. Therefore, the barriers arise from the intersection of *human psychology*, *organizational design*, and *operational metrics*. KTU examiners frame these barriers using **Ishikawa's Cause-and-Effect Logic** applied to quality systems.

### 2.2 Comprehensive Classification of TQM Barriers

Below is the **KTU-recommended 14-barrier taxonomy**, grouped by the **4M+E framework** (Man, Machine, Method, Material, Environment) — adapted from Deming's *14 Points of Obstruction* literature.

#### **A. People-Centric (Man) Barriers**

1. **Lack of Top Management Commitment & Leadership**
   - Senior leaders treat TQM as a *"flavor of the month"* program.
   - No personal involvement in quality councils or gemba walks.
   - Failure to align reward systems with quality goals.

2. **Resistance to Change**
   - Rooted in **Kurt Lewin's Force Field Theory** — employees perceive loss of competence, status, or job security.
   - Common in organizations with entrenched departmental silos.

3. **Inadequate Employee Involvement & Empowerment**
   - Quality Circles exist on paper but lack authority to act.
   - Front-line workers are consulted *after* decisions, not *during*.

4. **Poor Training and Education**
   - Insufficient investment in **statistical process control (SPC)**, **7 QC tools**, and **Kaizen methodologies**.
   - Training treated as a one-time event rather than a continuous process.

5. **Lack of Teamwork and Communication**
   - Functional silos prevent cross-departmental problem solving.
   - Vertical hierarchies suppress bottom-up feedback.

#### **B. Process-Centric (Method) Barriers**

6. **Inappropriate Organizational Structure**
   - Hierarchical pyramids slow decision cycles.
   - Quality department isolated from operations and design.

7. **Poor Strategic Planning and Goal Alignment**
   - Quality objectives disconnected from business strategy.
   - Vague vision statements without measurable milestones.

8. **Lack of Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) Culture**
   - Organizations remain satisfied with "good enough."
   - No structured PDCA discipline.

9. **Inadequate Process Management**
   - Processes are not documented, measured, or owned.
   - Non-value-added activities proliferate.

#### **C. System-Centric (Machine & Measurement) Barriers**

10. **Lack of Data, Metrics, and Fact-Based Decision Making**
    - Decisions driven by intuition, hierarchy, or anecdote.
    - Absence of **Quality Cost (CoQ)** tracking — Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure.

11. **Poor Information Systems and Technology Integration**
    - Legacy ERP systems cannot capture real-time quality data.
    - No digital dashboard for SPC charts (X-bar, R-chart).

12. **Inadequate Supplier Relationships**
    - Adversarial procurement mindset instead of **mutually beneficial partnerships**.
    - No supplier capability assessments.

#### **D. Culture & Environment Barriers**

13. **Short-Term Financial Focus**
    - Quarterly earnings pressure overrides long-term quality investments.
    - Cost-cutting measures target prevention costs — a classic mistake identified by **Joseph Juran**.

14. **Weak Customer Focus**
    - Internal metrics prioritized over **Voice of Customer (VOC)**.
    - Customer satisfaction measured but not acted upon.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> TQM is largely a **qualitative management discipline**, but it relies on quantitative anchors. The following formulae are **high-yield** for KTU numerical/analytical questions.

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $\text{CoQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Sum of Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure costs | Direct 7-mark question topic |
| **Quality Cost Ratio** | $\text{QCR} = \dfrac{C_P + C_A}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}$ | Ratio of conformance to non-conformance costs | Indicates TQM maturity |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{N \cdot O} \times 10^{6}$ | Six Sigma metric | Measures process capability |
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Statistical spread vs. tolerance | Used to evaluate barriers |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\sum (W_i \times S_i)}{100}$ | Weighted average of satisfaction scores | Validates customer-focus barrier |
| **Employee Engagement Score (EES)** | $\text{EES} = \dfrac{\text{Engaged Employees}}{\text{Total Employees}} \times 100$ | Workforce involvement metric | Validates people-barrier |
| **PDCA Cycle Time** | $T_{PDCA} = t_P + t_D + t_C + t_A$ | Total time to complete one improvement cycle | Measures continuous-improvement culture |

**Where:**
- $C_P$ = Prevention Cost
- $C_A$ = Appraisal Cost
- $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost
- $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost
- $D$ = Number of defects
- $N$ = Sample size
- $O$ = Opportunities per unit
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation
- USL, LSL = Upper and Lower Specification Limits
- $W_i$ = Weight of attribute $i$
- $S_i$ = Satisfaction score of attribute $i$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in engineering practice?**
>
> - A **manufacturing engineer** faces barriers in SPC adoption when operators distrust statistical charts.
> - A **software engineer** faces "resistance to change" when shifting from waterfall to Agile.
> - A **civil engineer** faces "inadequate supplier relationships" when sourcing concrete from low-cost vendors.
>
> Identifying barriers allows engineers to **engineer the soft systems** — the human and procedural layers — with the same rigor as physical systems.

### 2.5 Critical Insight: Deming's 7 Deadly Diseases (Parallel Framework)

KTU frequently cross-references **Deming's *7 Deadly Diseases*** as a parallel barrier framework:

> [!WARNING]
> Memorize Deming's 7 Diseases — they appear in almost every KTU PYQ on this topic.

1. Lack of constancy of purpose
2. Emphasis on short-term profits
3. Evaluation by performance, merit rating, or annual review
4. Mobility of management
5. Running a company on visible figures alone
6. Excessive medical costs
7. Excessive legal costs

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Barrier Diagnosis

### 3.1 The "Barrier Diagnostic Matrix" — Step-by-Step Methodology

KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to **map a real organizational symptom to its underlying TQM barrier**. The following is the **structured 6-step analytical procedure** you must reproduce in any 14-mark answer.

---

**Step 1 — Symptom Identification**
Identify the observable failure. Example: *"Defect rate rising 15% quarter-on-quarter despite new quality policy."*

**Step 2 — Layer Classification**
Place the symptom into one of three layers: Strategic, Operational, or Cultural.

**Step 3 — 4M+E Mapping**
Map the symptom to Man, Machine, Method, Material, or Environment.

**Step 4 — Barrier Identification**
Match to one of the 14 barriers in the taxonomy.

**Step 5 — Root Cause Hypothesis**
Apply the **5 Whys Technique**:

$$
\text{Root Cause} = \lim_{n \to 5} \text{Why}_n(\text{Symptom})
$$

**Step 6 — Countermeasure Recommendation**
Propose an intervention mapped to Deming's 14 Points or Juran's Quality Trilogy.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Style

> **Case:** A KTU-affiliated engineering college (or its placement partner firm) introduces TQM. After 18 months, internal failure costs have *risen* by 22%, employee engagement surveys show only 31% participation, and customer complaints have *not* declined. Diagnose the barriers.

**Step 1 — Symptom:**
$$
\Delta C_{IF} = +22\%, \quad \text{EES} = 31\%, \quad \text{Complaints} = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 2 — Layer Classification:**
All three symptoms straddle **Layer 2 (Operational)** and **Layer 3 (Cultural)** — a strong signal of *systemic, not isolated*, barriers.

**Step 3 — 4M+E Mapping:**

| Symptom | 4M+E Category |
|---|---|
| Rising internal failure cost | Method |
| Low employee engagement | Man |
| Unchanged complaints | Environment (Customer-side) |

**Step 4 — Barrier Identification:**
$$
\text{Barriers} = \{B_4, B_3, B_{14}\} = \{\text{Poor Training}, \text{Low Involvement}, \text{Weak Customer Focus}\}
$$

**Step 5 — 5 Whys on Internal Failure Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Why 1:} \quad & \text{Why are internal failures rising?} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Operators are producing non-conforming parts.} \\
\text{Why 2:} \quad & \text{Why are parts non-conforming?} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Machines are drifting out of control.} \\
\text{Why 3:} \quad & \text{Why are machines drifting?} \\
& \rightarrow \text{SPC charts are not being monitored.} \\
\text{Why 4:} \quad & \text{Why are charts not monitored?} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Operators do not understand SPC.} \\
\text{Why 5:} \quad & \text{Why don't operators understand SPC?} \\
& \rightarrow \text{Training was one-time, 12 months ago.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Root Cause:** Inadequate continuous training (Barrier $B_4$).

**Step 6 — Countermeasure:**
Apply **Deming Point 6** — *Institute training on the job* — combined with **Juran's Quality Trilogy (Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement)**. Specifically, introduce:

- Monthly SPC refresher workshops
- Cross-functional Kaizen teams
- A real-time quality dashboard accessible to floor operators

### 3.3 The Analytical Matrix — Barrier to Deming Point Cross-Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a guaranteed 14-mark answer framework.** KTU examiners award full marks when barriers are paired with their corresponding Deming Point countermeasure.

| Barrier # | TQM Barrier | Layer | 4M+E | Deming Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ | Lack of Top Management Commitment | Strategic | Man | Points 1, 2, 7, 8 |
| $B_2$ | Resistance to Change | Cultural | Man | Point 8 (Drive out fear) |
| $B_3$ | Low Employee Involvement | Cultural | Man | Point 9 (Break down barriers) |
| $B_4$ | Inadequate Training | Operational | Man | Point 6 (Institute training) |
| $B_5$ | Poor Communication | Cultural | Man | Point 14 (Top management transformation) |
| $B_6$ | Inappropriate Org. Structure | Strategic | Method | Point 9 (Optimize the system) |
| $B_7$ | Poor Strategic Planning | Strategic | Method | Point 1 (Constancy of purpose) |
| $B_8$ | Lack of Kaizen Culture | Operational | Method | Point 5 (Improve constantly) |
| $B_9$ | Inadequate Process Management | Operational | Method | Point 3 (Cease dependence on inspection) |
| $B_{10}$ | Lack of Data & Metrics | Operational | Machine | Point 11 (Eliminate numerical quotas) |
| $B_{11}$ | Poor IT Integration | Operational | Machine | Point 14 (Transformation) |
| $B_{12}$ | Weak Supplier Relationships | Operational | Environment | Point 4 (End lowest-tender practice) |
| $B_{13}$ | Short-Term Financial Focus | Strategic | Environment | Point 1 (Constancy of purpose) |
| $B_{14}$ | Weak Customer Focus | Strategic | Environment | Point 2 (Adopt new philosophy) |

### 3.4 Cost-of-Quality (CoQ) Numerical Demonstration

A KTU numerical question may ask: *"Given the cost data below, determine which barrier is most likely to be dominant."*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \text{₹}50{,}000 \quad (\text{Prevention}) \\
C_A &= \text{₹}80{,}000 \quad (\text{Appraisal}) \\
C_{IF} &= \text{₹}300{,}000 \quad (\text{Internal Failure}) \\
C_{EF} &= \text{₹}200{,}000 \quad (\text{External Failure})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Total Cost of Quality:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CoQ} &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
&= 50{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 300{,}000 + 200{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}630{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Quality Cost Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{QCR} &= \frac{C_P + C_A}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}} \\
&= \frac{50{,}000 + 80{,}000}{300{,}000 + 200{,}000} \\
&= \frac{130{,}000}{500{,}000} \\
&= 0.26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

$$
\text{QCR} = 0.26 \ll 1 \implies \text{Failure costs dominate}
$$

> [!TIP]
> A QCR well below 1 indicates that the organization is **"paying for failure, not prevention"** — a classic signature of barriers $B_4$ (training) and $B_{10}$ (data-driven decisions).

**Step 4 — Implied Barrier Diagnosis:**

$$
\text{Dominant Barrier} = B_4 \cup B_{10} = \text{Inadequate Training} \cup \text{Lack of Metrics}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommended Allocation (Juran's Optimal CoQ Rule):**

> [!NOTE]
> Mature TQM organizations target Prevention ≈ 30% and Appraisal ≈ 40% of CoQ, with Failure Costs ≤ 30%. The current breakdown is Prevention ≈ 8%, Appraisal ≈ 13%, Failure ≈ 79% — indicating severe under-investment.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Three-Layer Barrier Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TQM Implementation Failure"] --> B["Layer 1 Strategic Leadership"]
    A --> C["Layer 2 Operational Process"]
    A --> D["Layer 3 Cultural Human"]

    B --> B1["Lack of Top Management Commitment"]
    B --> B2["Short Term Financial Focus"]
    B --> B3["Inappropriate Org Structure"]
    B --> B4["Weak Customer Focus"]

    C --> C1["Inadequate Training"]
    C --> C2["Poor Process Management"]
    C --> C3["Lack of Data and Metrics"]
    C --> C4["Weak Supplier Relationships"]

    D --> D1["Resistance to Change"]
    D --> D2["Low Employee Involvement"]
    D --> D3["Poor Communication"]
    D --> D4["Lack of Teamwork"]

    B1 --> X1["Deming Point 1 and 2"]
    B2 --> X2["Deming Point 1"]
    B3 --> X3["Deming Point 9"]
    B4 --> X4["Deming Point 2"]
    C1 --> X5["Deming Point 6"]
    C2 --> X6["Deming Point 3 and 5"]
    C3 --> X7["Deming Point 11"]
    C4 --> X8["Deming Point 4"]
    D1 --> X9["Deming Point 8"]
    D2 --> X10["Deming Point 9"]
    D3 --> X11["Deming Point 14"]
    D4 --> X12["Deming Point 10"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Barrier-to-Countermeasure Mapping (Ishikawa-Inspired Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAUSE["Causes 4M plus E"]
        M1["Man Leadership Commitment"]
        M2["Man Resistance to Change"]
        M3["Method Process Design"]
        M4["Machine Measurement Data"]
        M5["Environment Customer Supplier"]
    end

    subgraph EFFECT["Effect Central Problem"]
        E1["TQM Implementation Failure"]
    end

    M1 --> E1
    M2 --> E1
    M3 --> E1
    M4 --> E1
    M5 --> E1

    subgraph COUNTER["Countermeasures Deming 14 Points"]
        C1["Constancy of Purpose"]
        C2["Adopt New Philosophy"]
        C3["Institute Training"]
        C4["Drive Out Fear"]
        C5["Eliminate Numerical Quotas"]
    end

    E1 --> COUNTER
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — PDCA Cycle Interrupted by Barriers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["PLAN Quality Objectives"] -->|Barrier Training B4| Q1["Quality Plan Weak"]
    Q1 --> D["DO Implementation"]
    D -->|Barrier Resistance B2| Q2["Execution Gap"]
    Q2 --> CH["CHECK Monitoring"]
    CH -->|Barrier Data B10| Q3["No Fact Based Insight"]
    Q3 --> A["ACT Standardize"]
    A -->|Barrier Commitment B1| Q4["No Learning Loop"]
    Q4 --> P
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this PDCA-with-Barriers diagram is exam gold:**
> KTU examiners love this visual because it shows that **each phase of PDCA has its own characteristic barrier**. If a student draws this in an exam, they earn +2 marks for visual clarity even if the rest of the answer is average.

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Barrier Mitigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StepA["Step A Symptom Logging"] --> StepB["Step B Layer Classification"]
    StepB --> StepC["Step C 4M plus E Mapping"]
    StepC --> StepD["Step D Barrier Identification"]
    StepD --> StepE["Step E Five Whys Root Cause"]
    StepE --> StepF["Step F Deming Countermeasure"]
    StepF --> StepG["Step G PDCA Reinitiation"]
    StepG --> StepA
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**List any six major barriers to TQM implementation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six major barriers are:

1. **Lack of top management commitment** — Senior leaders do not champion quality initiatives.
2. **Resistance to change** — Employees fear loss of competence or status.
3. **Inadequate training and education** — Lack of skill in QC tools and SPC.
4. **Lack of employee involvement and empowerment** — Quality Circles exist only on paper.
5. **Poor communication and teamwork** — Functional silos prevent cross-functional learning.
6. **Lack of data, metrics, and fact-based decision making** — Reliance on intuition rather than statistical evidence.

> Additional acceptable barriers: Short-term financial focus, weak customer focus, inappropriate organizational structure, poor supplier relationships.

*(Award 0.5 mark per correctly named barrier × 6 = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Explain Deming's *7 Deadly Diseases* as a parallel framework of TQM barriers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

W. Edwards Deming identified seven organizational diseases that act as systemic TQM barriers:

1. **Lack of constancy of purpose** — No long-term commitment to quality.
2. **Emphasis on short-term profits** — Quarterly targets override quality investments.
3. **Evaluation by performance, merit rating, or annual review** — Encourages internal competition over system thinking.
4. **Mobility of management** — Frequent leadership changes disrupt continuity.
5. **Running a company on visible figures alone** — Neglects unknown and unknowable metrics.
6. **Excessive medical costs** — Erodes organizational sustainability.
7. **Excessive legal costs** — Signals broken relationships with employees and stakeholders.

*(Award approx. 0.5 mark per disease with a one-line explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Classify the major barriers to TQM implementation into **three layers** (Strategic, Operational, Cultural). Discuss each layer with **at least three barriers** and their root causes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the **Cost of Quality (CoQ) framework**, analyze how failure-cost dominance signals the presence of specific TQM barriers. Support your answer with a **numerical example**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A**

**(a) — 7 Marks**

**The Three-Layer Barrier Framework:**

**Layer 1 — Strategic/Leadership Barriers** *(2.5 marks)*

Barriers at this layer originate from senior leadership and strategic direction:

1. **Lack of top management commitment** — Leaders delegate TQM to the quality department instead of personally championing it. Root cause: short-termism and the absence of a clear quality vision.
2. **Short-term financial focus** — Pressure for quarterly earnings discourages long-term prevention investments. Root cause: shareholder primacy over stakeholder value.
3. **Inappropriate organizational structure** — Hierarchical pyramids slow decision cycles. Root cause: legacy org-chart inertia.

**Layer 2 — Operational/Process Barriers** *(2.5 marks)*

Barriers embedded in daily workflows and systems:

1. **Inadequate training and education** — Operators cannot interpret SPC charts. Root cause: one-time workshop culture.
2. **Poor process management** — Processes unowned, undocumented. Root cause: lack of process owners and SOPs.
3. **Lack of data and metrics** — Decisions based on intuition. Root cause: weak information systems.

**Layer 3 — Cultural/Human Barriers** *(2 marks)*

Barriers embedded in people and culture:

1. **Resistance to change** — Rooted in **Kurt Lewin's Force Field Theory**; employees fear loss.
2. **Low employee involvement** — Quality Circles lack authority.
3. **Poor communication** — Vertical hierarchies suppress feedback.

**Synthesis:** Effective TQM requires simultaneous intervention at all three layers; addressing only the strategic layer fails because cultural resistance persists at the ground level.

---

**(b) — 7 Marks**

**Cost of Quality (CoQ) Analysis:**

The CoQ framework decomposes total quality cost into four categories:

$$
\text{CoQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

Where $C_P$ = Prevention, $C_A$ = Appraisal, $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure, $C_{EF}$ = External Failure.

**Numerical Example:**

A firm reports:

$$
C_P = \text{₹}50{,}000, \quad C_A = \text{₹}80{,}000, \quad C_{IF} = \text{₹}300{,}000, \quad C_{EF} = \text{₹}200{,}000
$$

**Step 1 — Total CoQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CoQ} &= 50{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 300{,}000 + 200{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}630{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Quality Cost Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{QCR} &= \frac{C_P + C_A}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}} = \frac{130{,}000}{500{,}000} = 0.26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Failure Cost Share:**

$$
\text{Failure Share} = \frac{500{,}000}{630{,}000} \times 100\% = 79.4\%
$$

**Interpretation & Barrier Diagnosis:**

A QCR of 0.26 (well below 1) and a failure cost share of 79.4% are signatures of **mature-quality-but-immature-TQM** organizations. The dominant barriers are:

- **Barrier $B_4$ — Inadequate Training** (since $C_P$ is only ~8% of CoQ)
- **Barrier $B_{10}$ — Lack of Data-Driven Decisions** (since $C_A$ is only ~13%)
- **Barrier $B_9$ — Poor Process Management** (high $C_{IF}$ suggests upstream process failures)

**Countermeasure per Juran:** Shift investment from appraisal/failure toward prevention. Target Prevention ≈ 30% and Failure ≤ 30% of total CoQ, achievable through training, SPC deployment, and process redesign.

---

#### **Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Discuss **Deming's 14 Points** as a countermeasure framework to the **14 most common TQM barriers**. Present this as a **barrier-to-point cross-reference matrix**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Apply the **5 Whys technique** to diagnose the root cause of a hypothetical manufacturing firm's *rising defect rate despite a new TQM initiative*. Identify **at least three distinct barriers** along the way. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B**

**(a) — 7 Marks**

**Deming's 14 Points as Countermeasure Framework:**

Deming's 14 Points form the most comprehensive prescription for removing TQM barriers. The cross-reference matrix pairs each barrier category with its corresponding Deming countermeasure:

| Barrier Category | Deming Point |
|---|---|
| Lack of constancy of purpose | **Point 1** |
| Compliance-based thinking | **Point 2** |
| Reliance on inspection | **Point 3** |
| Lowest-tender procurement | **Point 4** |
| Lack of continuous improvement | **Point 5** |
| Inadequate training | **Point 6** |
| Institutional barriers | **Point 7, 9** |
| Fear in workplace | **Point 8** |
| Silos between departments | **Point 9** |
| Numerical quotas | **Point 11** |
| Merit rating systems | **Point 12** |
| Management mobility | **Point 13** |
| Lack of transformation plan | **Point 14** |

**Key Argument:** Deming's framework is **holistic** — it addresses Strategic (Points 1, 2, 13), Operational (Points 3, 4, 5, 11), and Cultural barriers (Points 6, 7, 8, 9, 12) simultaneously. A KTU answer that simply maps barriers to Deming points without this holistic argument loses 2 marks.

---

**(b) — 7 Marks**

**5 Whys Diagnostic on a Hypothetical Firm:**

**Symptom:** Defect rate rose from 2.1% to 4.8% in two quarters despite launching a company-wide TQM initiative six months ago.

**Why 1:** Why are defects rising?
→ Because operators are rejecting more parts at the final inspection stage.

**Why 2:** Why are parts being rejected at final inspection?
→ Because critical dimensions are drifting out of tolerance during machining.

**Why 3:** Why are dimensions drifting?
→ Because machine calibration schedules have been skipped for over 4 months.

**Why 4:** Why have calibrations been skipped?
→ Because the maintenance team is overwhelmed by unplanned breakdowns.

**Why 5:** Why are unplanned breakdowns increasing?
→ Because preventive maintenance was deprioritized in the cost-cutting drive that funded the TQM initiative.

**Root Cause Identified:** Short-term cost-cutting for TQM funding cannibalized the preventive maintenance system, exposing a **strategic short-termism barrier** ($B_{13}$).

**Three Distinct Barriers Surfaced:**

1. **Barrier $B_{13}$ — Short-Term Financial Focus:** Prevention budget (maintenance) was sacrificed for the visible TQM program.
2. **Barrier $B_8$ — Lack of Continuous Improvement Culture:** Maintenance KPIs were not tracked in the TQM dashboard.
3. **Barrier $B_4$ — Inadequate Training:** Maintenance technicians were not trained in predictive maintenance techniques (e.g., vibration analysis).

**Recommended Countermeasures:**

- Adopt **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** as a sister program to TQM.
- Establish a **TQM Steering Committee** that includes maintenance, not just quality.
- Restructure the quality dashboard to include OEE, MTBF, MTTR metrics alongside defect rates.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Listing without classification** — Simply listing 10 barriers in bullets without grouping them into layers (Strategic/Operational/Cultural) costs you **2 marks** in Part B answers.
> 2. **Ignoring Deming/Juran** — Failing to reference any quality guru's framework (Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa) when discussing counter-measures loses **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **One-sided answers** — Discussing *only* cultural barriers without mentioning *systemic/operational* barriers is a typical answer of below-average students; KTU expects **holistic coverage**.
> 4. **No numerical anchoring** — Even in a qualitative question, dropping **one** numerical CoQ or DPMO calculation scores +2 marks and signals analytical maturity.
> 5. **Confusing TQM with ISO 9000** — These are distinct systems. ISO is certification-driven; TQM is philosophy-driven. Conflating them is a fatal error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Barriers to TQM (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Three layers of barriers:** Strategic (leadership), Operational (process), Cultural (human).
- [ ] **14-barrier taxonomy:** 5 People + 4 Process + 3 System + 2 Culture.
- [ ] **4M+E framework:** Man, Machine, Method, Material, Environment — the cause-classification lens.
- [ ] **CoQ formula:** $\text{CoQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ — memorize the four components.
- [ ] **Quality Cost Ratio:** $\text{QCR} = \dfrac{C_P + C_A}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}$ — low QCR (< 1) = barrier signature.
- [ ] **Deming's 7 Deadly Diseases:** constancy, short-term profits, merit rating, mobility, visible figures, medical costs, legal costs.
- [ ] **Deming's 14 Points** are the **universal countermeasure framework** — always map barriers to points in answers.
- [ ] **5 Whys Technique** is the **standard root-cause analysis** tool — show all 5 levels in any "diagnose the barrier" question.
- [ ] **Juran's Quality Cost Rule:** Prevention ≈ 30%, Appraisal ≈ 40%, Failure ≤ 30% of CoQ.
- [ ] **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** is the **visual classification tool** — know the 4M+E branches.
- [ ] **PDCA cycle** is disrupted by barriers at *every phase* — Plan (training), Do (resistance), Check (data), Act (commitment).
- [ ] **Resistance to change** stems from **Kurt Lewin's Force Field Theory** — driving forces vs. restraining forces.
- [ ] **Always quote at least one quality guru** (Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum) in any 14-mark answer.
- [ ] **KTU killer phrase:** *"Holistic transformation requires simultaneous intervention across strategic, operational, and cultural layers."* — memorize and use.

---

> **End of Notes — Barriers to TQM Implementation (Module 1, PESFT746, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
