topics.title: TQM Principles - Customer Satisfaction - Customer Perception of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TQM Principles - Customer Satisfaction - Customer Perception of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 TQM Principles: The Foundational Pillars

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that centres on continuously meeting customer expectations by integrating quality into every functional level of the enterprise — from product design and production to delivery and after-sales service.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> TQM is built upon a set of universally accepted **principles** (often derived from the work of quality gurus like Deming, Juran, Crosby, and Ishikawa) that guide organizations in implementing quality as a strategic imperative rather than a departmental function.

The **core TQM principles** discussed in this module typically include:

1. **Customer Focus** — Quality is defined by the customer.
2. **Leadership and Vision** — Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Involvement of People** — Employees at all levels are the essence of an organization.
4. **Process Approach** — Desired results are achieved more efficiently when activities are managed as interconnected processes.
5. **System Approach to Management** — Identifying, understanding, and managing interrelated processes as a system.
6. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — A permanent objective of the organization.
7. **Factual Approach to Decision Making** — Decisions based on data and information analysis.
8. **Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships** — Organizations and suppliers are interdependent.

### 1.2 Customer Satisfaction: The Ultimate Quality Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Satisfaction** is the customer's perceived evaluation of a product or service relative to their expectations. It is the *emotional and cognitive response* that results from the gap between what the customer expected and what they actually experienced.

The formal definition adopted by KTU (and ISO 9000:2015):
> *"Customer satisfaction is the customer's opinion about a product, service, or experience — measured at a point in time, reflecting how well the offering met or exceeded expectations."*

### 1.3 Customer Perception of Quality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Perception of Quality** is the customer's *subjective judgment* about the overall excellence or superiority of a product or service. It is shaped not by technical specifications alone, but by how the customer *interprets, experiences, and values* the offering.

The two foundational scholars — **Zeithaml (1988)** and **A. Parasuraman** — defined perceived quality as:
> *"The consumer's judgment about an entity's overall excellence or superiority."*

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Restaurant Story")

Imagine you walk into a restaurant expecting a great dinner (your **expectation**). The waiter is friendly, the food arrives hot and tasty, the ambience is pleasant, and the bill is reasonable. You leave saying, *"I am very satisfied."* Your **perception of quality** is positive.

Now imagine the same restaurant, but the waiter is rude, the food is cold, and the bill is too high. Even if the chef used premium ingredients (high **technical quality**), your perception of quality is *negative*, and you are *dissatisfied*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Lesson:** Quality does **not** live inside the factory or the kitchen. Quality lives inside the **customer's mind**. Two customers receiving the *exact same product* can have *completely different* perceptions of quality based on their expectations, past experiences, and personal values.

### 1.5 Key Distinctions Students Must Remember

| Term | Focus | Measured By |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality (Technical)** | Conformance to specifications | Internal metrics (defects, tolerances) |
| **Customer Perception of Quality** | Subjective judgment of excellence | Customer surveys, reviews, NPS |
| **Customer Satisfaction** | Emotional response after use | CSAT scores, satisfaction indices |
| **Customer Value** | Benefits received vs. price paid | Willingness-to-pay, retention rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In TQM, **perception is reality**. Even a technically perfect product fails in TQM if the *customer perceives* it as low quality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 TQM Principles in Detail — The "Why" and the "How"

#### Principle 1: Customer Focus
- **Why?** Without customers, no business survives. Every process must add value for the customer.
- **How?** Capture the *Voice of Customer (VOC)*, segment markets, monitor satisfaction, and design offerings around customer needs.

#### Principle 2: Leadership
- **Why?** Quality culture cannot be mandated by memos; it must be modelled by top management.
- **How?** Leaders set the vision, allocate resources, and personally champion quality initiatives.

#### Principle 3: Involvement of People
- **Why?** Front-line employees understand processes intimately; their engagement is critical.
- **How?** Empowerment, training, recognition, and removing fear of speaking up.

#### Principle 4: Process Approach
- **Why?** Outputs are only as good as the processes that create them.
- **How?** Map processes using SIPOC (Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers), identify CTQs (Critical-to-Quality) parameters.

#### Principle 5: System Approach
- **Why?** Departments do not operate in isolation; sub-optimization in one area harms the whole.
- **How?** Manage interdependencies, align goals across functions.

#### Principle 6: Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
- **Why?** Stagnation invites competition to overtake.
- **How?** PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle, Kaizen events, Six Sigma DMAIC.

#### Principle 7: Factual Approach to Decision Making
- **Why?** Opinions lead to variation; data leads to insight.
- **How?** Statistical Process Control (SPC), root-cause analysis, hypothesis testing.

#### Principle 8: Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships
- **Why?** Quality cannot be inspected into incoming materials; it must be built with suppliers.
- **How?** Long-term partnerships, shared quality metrics, supplier development programs.

### 2.2 Determinants of Customer Perception of Quality

Customer perception is shaped by **multiple dimensions** — particularly for services. **Zeithaml (1988)** identified **ten** determinants, later refined by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1985, 1988)** into the famous **SERVQUAL model** with **five dimensions**:

1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance.
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately.
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service.
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of employees; ability to inspire trust and confidence.
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention given to customers.

### 2.3 The SERVQUAL Gap Model (High-Yield for KTU)

The SERVQUAL framework identifies **five gaps** that cause customer dissatisfaction:

| Gap | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Gap 1** | Knowledge Gap | Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations. |
| **Gap 2** | Standards Gap | Difference between management's perception and the service quality specifications set. |
| **Gap 3** | Delivery Gap | Difference between service specifications and actual service delivered. |
| **Gap 4** | Communication Gap | Difference between service delivered and what is communicated to customers (over-promising). |
| **Gap 5** | Customer Gap | The *overall* gap between customer expectations and perceived service — the ultimate measure of satisfaction. |

### 2.4 The Satisfaction Equation (Core KTU Formula)

The foundational relationship used throughout TQM is:

$$
\text{Satisfaction} = f(\text{Perceived Performance} - \text{Expectation})
$$

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
S = P - E
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Customer Satisfaction score
- $P$ = Perceived performance (what the customer actually experienced)
- $E$ = Expectation (what the customer anticipated)

> [!NOTE]
> If $P > E$, the customer is **delighted** (positive disconfirmation).
> If $P = E$, the customer is **satisfied** (confirmation).
> If $P < E$, the customer is **dissatisfied** (negative disconfirmation).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Satisfaction Index (SI)** | $SI = P - E$ | Basic gap between perception and expectation |
| **CSAT Score (%)** | $CSAT = \frac{\text{Number satisfied}}{\text{Total responses}} \times 100$ | Percentage of satisfied customers |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Loyalty and advocacy metric |
| **Customer Effort Score (CES)** | $CES = \text{Mean rating of effort required}$ | Measures ease of interaction |
| **SERVQUAL Score** | $Q = P - E$ (per dimension) | Gap on each of 5 service dimensions |
| **Weighted Satisfaction** | $WS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | Composite score with attribute weights $w_i$ |
| **Loyalty Rate** | $LR = \frac{\text{Repeat customers}}{\text{Total customers}} \times 100$ | Behavioural measure of satisfaction |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t)}{(1 + r)^t}$ | Long-term value of customer relationship |
| **AHP Weighting (Saaty)** | $CR = \frac{CI}{RI}$ | Consistency of pairwise comparisons |
| **Kano Satisfaction Coeff.** | $S_i = \frac{A_i + O_i}{(A_i + O_i + M_i)}$ | Normalized satisfaction coefficient for attribute $i$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** While these metrics originate in management science, they are used universally in **manufacturing quality engineering**, **service engineering**, **software quality assurance**, and **operations management** — making them directly relevant to PESFT746.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **automotive industries** (e.g., Toyota), CSAT and NPS drive product design improvements.
- In **software engineering**, the SERVQUAL dimensions map to usability, reliability, and support quality (akin to ISO/IEC 25010).
- In **process industries**, customer perception metrics guide SPC efforts and Six Sigma project selection.
- In **B2B supply chains**, CLV calculations justify long-term supplier partnerships — a direct link to TQM Principle 8.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Composite Weighted Satisfaction Score

In real KTU exam problems, satisfaction across multiple attributes is often **weighted** to reflect customer priorities. Let us derive the formula rigorously.

**Given:**
- $n$ quality attributes (e.g., reliability, price, durability)
- For each attribute $i$:
  - $s_i$ = satisfaction score on that attribute
  - $w_i$ = weight (importance) given by the customer

**Step 1: Total weight must equal unity (normalized weights).**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1
$$

**Step 2: The weighted satisfaction $WS$ is the linear combination of scores and weights.**

$$
WS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

**Step 3: Substituting the individual gap $s_i = P_i - E_i$:**

$$
WS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot (P_i - E_i)
$$

**Step 4: Distributing the summation:**

$$
WS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot P_i \; - \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot E_i
$$

**Step 5: Recognizing each term as a weighted mean:**

$$
WS = \overline{P} - \overline{E}
$$

Where:
- $\overline{P} = \sum w_i P_i$ = weighted perceived performance
- $\overline{E} = \sum w_i E_i$ = weighted expectation

**Conclusion:** The composite weighted satisfaction is simply the *weighted gap* between perception and expectation. This is the multi-attribute generalization of the basic satisfaction equation $S = P - E$.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SERVQUAL Gap Closure Workflow

The following matrix walks through **how a company should close each of the 5 SERVQUAL gaps**, with corresponding KTU marks allocation logic.

| Step | Gap | Diagnostic Question | Corrective Action | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gap 1** (Knowledge) | Does management know what customers want? | Conduct market research, focus groups, VOC analysis | Remember / Understand |
| 2 | **Gap 2** (Standards) | Are service standards aligned with perception? | Set SMART service standards, train employees | Apply |
| 3 | **Gap 3** (Delivery) | Is service delivered to standard? | Use technology, empower front-line staff, monitor | Apply / Analyze |
| 4 | **Gap 4** (Communication) | Are we over-promising? | Align marketing messages with actual capability | Analyze / Evaluate |
| 5 | **Gap 5** (Customer) | Is the customer satisfied? | Measure CSAT, NPS, CES, and feed back to Gap 1 | Evaluate / Create |

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A company receives the following customer feedback for **three quality attributes** on a 10-point scale:

| Attribute | Importance Weight ($w_i$) | Performance ($P_i$) | Expectation ($E_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability | 0.5 | 8 | 9 |
| Responsiveness | 0.3 | 7 | 7 |
| Tangibles | 0.2 | 6 | 5 |

**Calculate the composite weighted satisfaction score and interpret the result.**

**Step 1: Compute individual gaps $s_i = P_i - E_i$.**

$$
s_1 = 8 - 9 = -1
$$

$$
s_2 = 7 - 7 = 0
$$

$$
s_3 = 6 - 5 = +1
$$

**Step 2: Verify weights sum to 1.**

$$
0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Apply the weighted satisfaction formula.**

$$
WS = \sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

$$
WS = (0.5 \times -1) + (0.3 \times 0) + (0.2 \times 1)
$$

$$
WS = -0.5 + 0 + 0.2
$$

$$
WS = -0.3
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

Since $WS = -0.3 < 0$, the **overall customer satisfaction is negative**. The most damaging factor is **Reliability** (weight 0.5, gap −1) — a 0.5-point weighted dissatisfaction. Management must prioritize improving reliability to close the customer gap.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Net Promoter Score (NPS) Logic

**Step 1:** Customers rate willingness to recommend on a 0–10 scale.

**Step 2:** Classify respondents:
- **Promoters** = ratings 9 or 10
- **Passives** = ratings 7 or 8
- **Detractors** = ratings 0 to 6

**Step 3:** Calculate the percentage of each group.

$$
\%P = \frac{N_{\text{promoters}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%D = \frac{N_{\text{detractors}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4:** Compute NPS.

$$
NPS = \%P - \%D
$$

**Range of NPS:** $-100$ (all detractors) to $+100$ (all promoters). A positive NPS is generally considered good; values above **50** are excellent and benchmark industry leaders.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: TQM Principles vs. Quality Gurus (Comparative Table)

| Principle | Deming | Juran | Crosby | Ishikawa |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Customer Focus | Yes | Yes (Fitness for Use) | Yes (Conformance to Requirements) | Yes (Quality = Customer Satisfaction) |
| Leadership | System of Profound Knowledge | Quality Planning at top | Leadership commitment | Quality Circles |
| Process Approach | PDCA | Trilogy (Q-P-C) | Prevention over Inspection | Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) |
| Continuous Improvement | 14 Points | Breakthrough Projects | Zero Defects | Company-wide Quality Control (CWQC) |
| Factual Decisions | Statistical Thinking | Statistical Methods | Cost of Quality | Quality Data Presentation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: TQM Principles Framework (Eight Pillars)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TQM Core Philosophy] --> B[Customer Focus]
    A --> C[Leadership]
    A --> D[Involvement of People]
    A --> E[Process Approach]
    A --> F[System Approach]
    A --> G[Continuous Improvement]
    A --> H[Factual Decision Making]
    A --> I[Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relations]
    B --> J[Customer Satisfaction]
    C --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Organizational Excellence]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: The SERVQUAL Gap Model (Five Gaps)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT[Customer Side]
        EC[Customer Expectations]
    end
    subgraph MID[Management Side]
        MG[Mgmt Perception of Expectations]
        SQ[Service Quality Standards]
        SD[Service Delivery]
        EC2[External Communications]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT[Outcome]
        PS[Customer Perception of Service]
    end
    EC -->|Gap 1: Knowledge| MG
    MG -->|Gap 2: Standards| SQ
    SQ -->|Gap 3: Delivery| SD
    EC2 -->|Gap 4: Communication| SD
    SD -->|Gap 5: Customer Gap| PS
    PS --> JSAT[Customer Satisfaction]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: The Satisfaction Equation (P - E Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Expectation E] --> C{Comparison}
    B[Perceived Performance P] --> C
    C -->|P greater than E| D[Positive Disconfirmation - Delighted]
    C -->|P equals E| E[Confirmation - Satisfied]
    C -->|P less than F| F[Negative Disconfirmation - Dissatisfied]
    D --> G[Customer Loyalty and Advocacy]
    E --> H[Continued Purchase]
    F --> I[Complaint or Churn]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Kano Model (Customer Perception Categories)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quality Attribute] --> B{How does customer perceive it?}
    B -->|Must Be Present| C[Basic Needs - Dissatisfier if missing]
    B -->|More is Better| D[One-Dimensional Needs - Linear Satisfaction]
    B -->|Unexpected Bonus| E[Exciter Needs - High Delight]
    B -->|Indifferent| F[Indifferent Needs - No Effect]
    B -->|Reverse| G[Reverse Needs - Some dislike]
    C --> H[Minimum Quality Threshold]
    D --> I[Competitive Quality]
    E --> J[Differentiator Quality]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: TQM Implementation Cycle for Customer Satisfaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Capture Voice of Customer VOC] --> B[Set Quality Standards]
    B --> C[Design Processes to Meet Standards]
    C --> D[Deliver Service or Product]
    D --> E[Measure Customer Perception CSAT NPS]
    E --> F[Analyze Gaps and Root Causes]
    F --> G[Implement Improvements PDCA]
    G --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> These five diagrams collectively represent the **mental model** a KTU student should be able to draw from memory in the examination hall. Practice reproducing them by hand for full marks in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Customer Perception of Quality. List any two determinants that influence it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Customer Perception of Quality is the customer's subjective judgment about the overall excellence or superiority of a product or service (as defined by Zeithaml, 1988). It is the customer's *interpretation* of how well the offering meets their needs and expectations, distinct from the technical or objective quality of the product.

**Two Determinants (1.5 marks – 0.75 each):**
1. **Service Quality Dimensions** (tangibles, reliability, responsiveness, assurance, empathy — SERVQUAL model).
2. **Price and Value Perception** — customers equate higher price with higher quality in many categories (the price–quality heuristic).
3. *Any other valid:* brand reputation, past experience, word-of-mouth, personal needs, situational factors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Customer Satisfaction and Customer Perception of Quality.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Customer Satisfaction | Customer Perception of Quality |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The emotional response after comparing perceived performance with expectations. | The cognitive judgment about overall excellence of a product/service. |
| **Timing** | Measured *after* consumption/use. | Can be formed *before, during, or after* purchase. |
| **Nature** | Transaction-specific and emotional. | Broader, image-based, and attitudinal. |
| **Measurement** | CSAT, satisfaction surveys, complaint rates. | Perceived quality scales, brand image studies. |
| **Result** | Satisfaction or dissatisfaction with a *specific transaction*. | Overall quality rating of a *brand or product*. |

(1.5 marks for definition difference + 1.5 marks for the comparative table)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **SERVQUAL model** in detail. List and describe the **five service quality dimensions** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **five gaps of the SERVQUAL model**. For each gap, explain its cause and suggest one practical method to close it in a service organization. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks:**

The **SERVQUAL model** (Service Quality Gap Model) was developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry** in **1985–1988** to measure service quality. It is based on the premise that **service quality = perceived service − expected service** (i.e., $Q = P - E$).

The model identifies **five dimensions** of service quality:

1. **Tangibles (1.4 marks):** Physical evidence of the service — facilities, equipment, personnel appearance, communication materials.
   - *Example:* Clean hospital wards, well-dressed cabin crew, modern ATM machines.

2. **Reliability (1.4 marks):** Ability to perform the promised service **dependably and accurately**.
   - *Example:* A courier delivering a parcel on the promised date, a bank posting the correct monthly statement.

3. **Responsiveness (1.4 marks):** Willingness to help customers and provide **prompt service**.
   - *Example:* A restaurant serving food within 15 minutes of ordering; a call centre answering within 3 rings.

4. **Assurance (1.4 marks):** Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire **trust and confidence**.
   - *Example:* A certified financial advisor explaining complex investment products clearly.

5. **Empathy (1.4 marks):** Providing **caring, individualized attention** to customers.
   - *Example:* A doctor who listens patiently to an elderly patient's concerns.

(Each dimension: 1.4 marks, totalling 7 marks)

---

**Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks:**

| Gap | Description (1 mark) | Cause | Closing Method (0.4 marks) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gap 1 — Knowledge Gap** | Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations. | Inadequate market research; lack of upward communication. | Conduct regular VOC studies, focus groups, customer interviews, feedback analysis. |
| **Gap 2 — Standards Gap** | Difference between management's perception and the service quality specifications set. | Inadequate commitment to service quality; lack of process design. | Translate customer expectations into SMART service standards; use service blueprinting. |
| **Gap 3 — Delivery Gap** | Difference between service standards and actual service delivered. | Poor training, role conflict, low empowerment, unsuitable technology. | Employee training, empowerment, technology upgrades, internal service quality audits. |
| **Gap 4 — Communication Gap** | Difference between service delivered and what is communicated externally. | Over-promising in advertising; lack of horizontal coordination. | Coordinate marketing and operations; realistic advertising; integrated marketing communication. |
| **Gap 5 — Customer Gap** | The overall gap between customer expectations and perceived service — leads directly to satisfaction/dissatisfaction. | Cumulative effect of Gaps 1–4. | Continuous monitoring of CSAT and NPS; closing all upstream gaps; total TQM culture. |

(Each gap: ~1.4 marks, totalling 7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **TQM principles** with reference to the work of any **two quality gurus** (Deming, Juran, Crosby, or Ishikawa). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company surveys 200 customers about three product attributes. The results are as follows:

| Attribute | Weight | Performance ($P_i$) | Expectation ($E_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Durability | 0.4 | 7 | 9 |
| Price | 0.4 | 6 | 5 |
| Aesthetics | 0.2 | 8 | 7 |

Calculate the **composite weighted satisfaction score** and **interpret** the result with managerial recommendations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks:**

**W. Edwards Deming (3.5 marks):**
Deming contributed 14 Points for Management and the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle. Key TQM principles he championed:
- **Create constancy of purpose** (Principle 2: Leadership).
- **Improve constantly every process** (Principle 6: Continuous Improvement).
- **Institute training on the job** (Principle 3: Involvement of People).
- **Drive out fear** so that everyone can work effectively.
- **Use statistical methods** for continuous improvement (Principle 7: Factual Decisions).

**Joseph M. Juran (3.5 marks):**
Juran defined quality as **"fitness for use"** and emphasized three processes (the Juran Trilogy):
1. **Quality Planning** — Identify customers and their needs (Principle 1: Customer Focus).
2. **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs. goals (Principle 7: Factual Decisions).
3. **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement (Principle 6).

Juran's "fitness for use" directly anchors the **customer perception** concept — quality is defined by the user, not the producer.

---

**Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Verify weights (0.5 mark):**

$$
0.4 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2: Compute individual gaps $s_i = P_i - E_i$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
s_1 = 7 - 9 = -2 \quad \text{(Durability)}
$$

$$
s_2 = 6 - 5 = +1 \quad \text{(Price)}
$$

$$
s_3 = 8 - 7 = +1 \quad \text{(Aesthetics)}
$$

**Step 3: Apply weighted satisfaction formula (3 marks):**

$$
WS = \sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

$$
WS = (0.4 \times -2) + (0.4 \times 1) + (0.2 \times 1)
$$

$$
WS = -0.8 + 0.4 + 0.2
$$

$$
WS = -0.2
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation and Recommendations (2 marks):**

The composite weighted satisfaction is **−0.2**, indicating **overall customer dissatisfaction**, even though the product is exceeding expectations on Price and Aesthetics. The dominant cause is the **Durability gap of −2 with a heavy weight of 0.4**, contributing −0.8 to the score.

**Managerial Recommendations:**
1. **Prioritize durability improvement** — it is the heaviest-weighted attribute and the largest dissatisfier.
2. **Maintain price competitiveness** — customers are pleased.
3. **Enhance aesthetic appeal** — but only as a secondary improvement lever.
4. Implement a **PDCA cycle** to monitor durability improvements, since this is a process-related issue.
5. Re-survey customers in 6 months to track movement in the composite score.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Customer Satisfaction with Customer Perception of Quality.** They are related but distinct. Examiners frequently award 0 marks for definitions that conflate the two.
> 2. **In numerical problems, always verify that weights sum to 1.** If not, normalize first: $w_i^{\text{norm}} = w_i / \sum w_i$. Skipping this step costs full marks.
> 3. **The five SERVQUAL gaps must be remembered in order** (Knowledge → Standards → Delivery → Communication → Customer). Writing them in random sequence loses method marks.
> 4. **Always interpret the result** in numerical problems. Computing $WS = -0.2$ without explaining what it means forfeits the 2-mark interpretation component.
> 5. **Avoid using the phrase "quality control" instead of "quality management."** TQM is a *philosophy*, not an inspection function. Examiners penalize terminological inaccuracies.
> 6. **In diagrams, do not skip the arrow labels or the final outcome node.** An incomplete SERVQUAL diagram loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Customer Perception of Quality** = the *subjective judgment* of a customer about overall excellence (Zeithaml, 1988).
- **Customer Satisfaction** = the *emotional response* after comparing performance with expectations.
- **TQM has 8 universal principles** — Customer Focus, Leadership, Involvement, Process Approach, System Approach, Continuous Improvement, Factual Decisions, Supplier Partnerships.
- **SERVQUAL has 5 dimensions** — Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy.
- **SERVQUAL has 5 gaps** — Knowledge, Standards, Delivery, Communication, Customer (the final gap that determines satisfaction).
- **Core equation:** $S = P - E$ (Satisfaction = Perception − Expectation).
- **CSAT** is a percentage, **NPS** is a difference of percentages, **CES** is a mean rating, **CLV** is a discounted cash-flow sum.
- **Composite weighted satisfaction:** $WS = \sum w_i (P_i - E_i) = \overline{P} - \overline{E}$.
- **Deming** emphasized statistical thinking and the **PDCA cycle**.
- **Juran** emphasized **fitness for use** and the **Quality Trilogy** (Planning, Control, Improvement).
- **Crosby** emphasized **conformance to requirements**, **zero defects**, and the **cost of quality**.
- **Ishikawa** emphasized **company-wide quality control (CWQC)** and the **fishbone diagram**.
- **TQM Principle–Guru Mapping** is a high-yield 14-mark answer type — practice mapping principles to guru philosophies.
- **For 7-mark numerical questions**, always show: (1) weight verification, (2) gap calculation, (3) weighted sum, (4) managerial interpretation.
- **Draw at least one diagram** (gap model, PDCA, or Kano) in long-answer questions — it earns 1–2 easy marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** This topic is the *theoretical heart* of Module 2. Expect either a **14-mark long answer** (SERVQUAL or TQM principles with gurus) or a **7-mark numerical** on weighted satisfaction. Master the definitions, the five gaps, the four gurus, and the basic $S = P - E$ equation — these four pillars cover approximately 80% of marks from this topic in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Complaints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Customer Complaints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Complaint (KTU Definition):** A **customer complaint** is a formal expression of **dissatisfaction** made by a customer (or prospective customer) to an organization, its agents, or a third party, regarding a product, service, complaint handling process, or any aspect of the organization's operations where a **response or resolution** is explicitly or implicitly expected.

In the KTU 2024 TQM framework (aligned with **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.2.2** and **ISO 10002:2018 — Quality Management — Customer Satisfaction — Guidelines for Complaints Handling in Organizations**), a complaint is treated as a **valuable gift** — a free-of-cost diagnostic indicator of process failure. Every complaint is an actionable data point that, when correctly analyzed, reveals a **breakdown in Quality Assurance (QA)** somewhere in the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Story"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition for First-Time Readers**

Imagine you walk into a famous restaurant in Kochi and order a *Kerala fish curry*. When the dish arrives, you find:

1. The fish is **overcooked and rubbery** (product/service failure).
2. The waiter takes **15 minutes** to even acknowledge you (service delay).
3. When you finally call the manager, he **argues** with you instead of listening (poor complaint handling).

This single experience triggers **three distinct categories of complaint**: *Product Quality*, *Service Speed*, and *Behaviour of Staff*. 

Now, in TQM philosophy, the restaurant manager who is wise will:
- **Thank** you for bringing this to his attention,
- **Document** the complaint in a register,
- **Investigate** root cause (was it the chef, the supplier of fish, or the recipe?),
- **Compensate** you (free meal / discount),
- **Prevent recurrence** by training the chef or changing suppliers.

This is precisely the **TQM complaint management lifecycle**.

---

## 1.3 Why Customer Complaints Are Strategically Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Pareto Insight (1-26-100 Rule):** For every **1 customer who actually complains**, **26 customers remain silent** but dissatisfied, and these silent defectors tell approximately **9–10 others** about their bad experience (Source: TARP / Customer Service Institute research, widely cited in KTU-recommended TQM textbooks).

This makes complaint management a **high-leverage activity**. The cost of *acquiring* a new customer is roughly **5 to 7 times** the cost of *retaining* an existing one (the **5:1 Retention Ratio** in CRM literature).

---

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme – Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, this topic maps to:
> - **CO2:** *Understand the various tools and techniques of TQM including customer relationship management.*
> - **Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Customer Complaints

Customer complaints are systematically classified along multiple dimensions. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following taxonomy:

### A) Classification by **Nature of Defect**

| Type | Definition | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Complaint** | Failure in physical good specifications | Defective mobile phone battery |
| **Service Complaint** | Failure in intangible service delivery | Delayed bank loan processing |
| **Process Complaint** | Failure in administrative/business process | Wrong invoice issued twice |
| **Behavioural Complaint** | Failure in employee conduct | Rude billing counter staff |

### B) Classification by **Severity**

| Severity Level | Symbol | Response Time (KTU-recommended) |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical / Safety** | S1 | ≤ 24 hours |
| **Major** | S2 | ≤ 72 hours |
| **Minor** | S3 | ≤ 7 working days |
| **Informational** | S4 | Acknowledgement only |

---

## 2.2 The Gaps Model of Service Quality (Parasuraman-Zeithaml-Berry, 1985)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most exam-relevant model** for KTU on Customer Complaints. It explains *why* complaints arise by identifying **5 gaps** in service delivery.

```
GAP 1:  Customer Expectations  →  Management Perceptions
GAP 2:  Management Perceptions →  Service Quality Specs
GAP 3:  Service Quality Specs →  Service Delivery
GAP 4:  Service Delivery      →  External Communications
GAP 5:  Expected Service      →  Perceived Service   (= Gap 5 = f(Gap1, Gap2, Gap3, Gap4))
```

Mathematically:

$$GAP\_5 = f(GAP\_1, GAP\_2, GAP\_3, GAP\_4)$$

Where $f$ denotes a **functional dependence**, indicating that the perceived service gap is a **composition** of the four upstream gaps.

---

## 2.3 The Customer Complaint Behaviour Model

Research by **Singh (1988)** and **TARP (Technical Assistance Research Programs)** categorizes complainants into four behavioural types:

| Complainant Type | % of Complainers (Industry Average) | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Voicers (Actives)** | ~ **25%** | Complain directly to the firm |
| **Activists** | ~ **5%** | Complain to third parties (consumer court, social media) |
| **Passives** | ~ **55%** | Complain silently and exit |
| **Ironsides** | ~ **15%** | Complain but continue buying |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The **Passives (55%)** are the most dangerous group — they leave without giving the firm a chance to recover, making complaint-invitation systems essential.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulae and ratios are repeatedly tested in KTU ESE — memorize them.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Engineering / Business Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Complaint Rate** | $CR = \dfrac{N_{complaints}}{N_{transactions}} \times 100$ | % of transactions generating complaints |
| 2 | **Resolution Rate** | $RR = \dfrac{N_{resolved}}{N_{complaints}} \times 100$ | % of complaints closed satisfactorily |
| 3 | **First Contact Resolution (FCR)** | $FCR = \dfrac{N_{resolved\_first\_call}}{N_{total\_calls}} \times 100$ | Quality of complaint handling |
| 4 | **Average Resolution Time (ART)** | $ART = \dfrac{\sum (T_{resolved} - T_{filed})}{N_{resolved}}$ | Speed of resolution (hours / days) |
| 5 | **Recovery Paradox Ratio** | $R_{paradox} = \dfrac{Loyalty_{after\_recovery}}{Loyalty_{before\_failure}}$ | Can exceed **1.0** if recovery is excellent |
| 6 | **Complaint Cost Ratio** | $CCR = \dfrac{C_{handling}}{R_{revenue}} \times 100$ | Cost of complaint handling as % of revenue |
| 7 | **Defection Rate** | $DR = \dfrac{N_{customers\_lost}}{N_{customers\_start}} \times 100$ | Churn due to poor complaint handling |
| 8 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | Promoters (%) − Detractors (%) |
| 9 | **Customer Effort Score (CES)** | $CES = \dfrac{\sum Effort_{1}^{10}}{N}$ | Lower effort → better complaint handling |
| 10 | **VOC Coverage Index** | $VOC = \dfrac{N_{feedback\_collected}}{N_{customers\_contacted}} \times 100$ | How broadly Voice of Customer is captured |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Business Utility

In engineering firms (e.g., **automotive OEMs**, **electronics manufacturing**), customer complaints drive:

1. **Corrective Action Requests (CAR)** under ISO 9001:2015 Clause 10.2.
2. **8D Problem Solving** (especially in automotive — Ford Q-101).
3. **Supplier Corrective Action (SCAR)** propagation upstream.
4. **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** updates.
5. **Design of Experiments (DOE)** for root-cause verification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** When a **Toyota Camry owner** complains of brake failure, the complaint cascades through: Dealer → Regional Office → Toyota HQ Quality Division → Supplier (e.g., Bosch for brake actuator) → Engineering change in next model year. **This is complaint-driven quality improvement.**

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 The 8-Step Complaint Handling Process (ISO 10002:2018)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Framework:** This 8-step process is the canonical answer for 14-mark questions on complaint management.

**Step 1 — Receive the Complaint**

The organization must:
- Acknowledge receipt within **24 hours** (critical severity) or **72 hours** (standard).
- Record in a centralized **Complaint Management System (CMS)**.
- Assign a unique **Complaint ID** (e.g., `CMP-2024-00137`).

**Step 2 — Acknowledge and Provide Visibility**

Send an acknowledgement containing:
- Complaint ID,
- Expected resolution timeline,
- Designated handler's name & contact.

**Step 3 — Investigate Root Cause**

Apply the **5-Why Analysis** or **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram**:

$$
\text{Why}_1 \rightarrow \text{Why}_2 \rightarrow \text{Why}_3 \rightarrow \text{Why}_4 \rightarrow \text{Why}_5 = \text{Root Cause}
$$

> **Example Derivation:**
> - *Why did the customer receive a defective phone?* → Quality check missed it.
> - *Why was QC missed?* → Inspector on leave, no backup.
> - *Why no backup?* → Single-point failure in manpower planning.
> - *Why single-point failure?* → No cross-training program.
> - *Why no cross-training?* → HR budget didn't allocate for it.
> - **Root Cause:** HR planning process missing cross-training clause.

**Step 4 — Decide on Corrective Action**

Choose from the **Action Hierarchy (most → least preferred)**:

$$
\text{Eliminate Source} \succ \text{Substitute} \succ \text{Engineer Out} \succ \text{Admin Control} \succ \text{PPE}
$$

**Step 5 — Communicate the Resolution**

- Provide clear, jargon-free explanation.
- Include **apology**, **compensation**, and **preventive commitment**.

**Step 6 — Track and Monitor**

Set up **KPIs**: FCR, ART, CSAT post-resolution. Standard formula:

$$
CSAT = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5}(Rating_i \times N_i)}{5 \times N_{total}} \times 100
$$

Where $N_i$ = number of customers rating $i$ on a 1–5 scale.

**Step 7 — Verify Effectiveness**

Close the loop by:
- Calling the customer **30 days after** resolution.
- Confirming the issue did not recur.

**Step 8 — Institutional Learning**

Feed aggregated complaint data into:
- **FMEA** RPN (Risk Priority Number) updates,
- **Kaizen / Continuous Improvement** backlog,
- **Management Review (MR)** meetings.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Service Recovery & The Recovery Paradox

> [!NOTE]
> **The Recovery Paradox (Hart, Heskett & Sasser, 1990):** If a service failure is recovered *exceptionally well*, customer loyalty can **exceed** the pre-failure level.

Mathematical representation:

$$
\text{Loyalty}_{after} = \text{Loyalty}_{before} \times \left[ 1 + \alpha \cdot Q_{recovery} - \beta \cdot D_{failure} \right]
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = sensitivity coefficient for recovery quality (positive),
- $\beta$ = damage coefficient for failure severity (positive),
- $Q_{recovery}$ = quality of recovery (0 to 1),
- $D_{failure}$ = severity of failure (0 to 1),
- $\alpha > \beta$ leads to **Recovery Paradox** (loyalty gain).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A telecom company receives **2,400 complaints** in Q1 2024. Of these, **1,920** are resolved within SLA, and **1,440** are resolved on the very first contact. Total revenue for the quarter is **₹12 crore**, and complaint-handling cost is **₹18 lakh**. Calculate:
> (a) Complaint Rate, given total customer interactions = 80,000.
> (b) Resolution Rate.
> (c) First Contact Resolution (FCR).
> (d) Complaint Cost Ratio (CCR).

**Solution:**

**(a) Complaint Rate:**

$$CR = \frac{N_{complaints}}{N_{transactions}} \times 100 = \frac{2400}{80000} \times 100 = 3.0\%$$

**(b) Resolution Rate:**

$$RR = \frac{N_{resolved}}{N_{complaints}} \times 100 = \frac{1920}{2400} \times 100 = 80.0\%$$

**(c) First Contact Resolution:**

$$FCR = \frac{N_{resolved\_first\_call}}{N_{total\_calls}} \times 100 = \frac{1440}{2400} \times 100 = 60.0\%$$

**(d) Complaint Cost Ratio:**

$$CCR = \frac{C_{handling}}{R_{revenue}} \times 100 = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 0.15\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmark (Industry):** FCR > 70% is considered *Best-in-Class*. Here **60%** indicates room for improvement — likely actionable in KTU 14-mark answer.

---

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 End-to-End Customer Complaint Lifecycle (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Customer Experiences Issue"] --> B["Customer Decides to Complain"]
    B --> C{"Channel of Complaint"}

    D1 --> E["Receipt and Acknowledgement"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> Z["Lost Without Recovery"]

    G3 --> H

    L1 --> M["Feed into Kaizen and FMEA Updates"]
    M --> N["Management Review Meeting"]
    N --> O["Continuous Improvement - PDCA Loop"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define customer complaint. Mention any four types of customer complaints.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** A customer complaint is a formal expression of dissatisfaction made by a customer regarding a product, service, or process, where a response or resolution is expected. As per ISO 10002:2018, it is a *"feedback"* opportunity.

**Four Types (2 Marks – 0.5 each):**

1. **Product Complaint** — Defect in tangible goods (e.g., cracked phone screen).
2. **Service Complaint** — Failure in intangible service delivery (e.g., late delivery).
3. **Process Complaint** — Failure in administrative procedure (e.g., wrong billing).
4. **Behavioural Complaint** — Inappropriate conduct of staff (e.g., rude response).

---

### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Explain the "Recovery Paradox" in service quality with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Recovery Paradox**, proposed by Hart, Heskett & Sasser (1990), states that if a service failure is recovered exceptionally well, customer loyalty can **exceed** its pre-failure level. The customer thinks, *"If they handle this so well, they must be excellent overall."*

**Example (1.5 Marks):** A passenger's lost baggage by an airline is not only found within 4 hours but also delivered personally to the hotel with a written apology and ₹5,000 voucher. The passenger's loyalty to the airline becomes higher than before the incident.

**Mathematical Note (0.5 Mark):** $\alpha \cdot Q_{recovery} > \beta \cdot D_{failure}$ leads to net positive loyalty.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) Explain the Gaps Model of Service Quality in detail with a suitable diagram. How does it explain the origin of customer complaints? (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the 8-step ISO 10002 complaint handling process with reference to a real engineering organization (e.g., an automobile manufacturer). (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) The Gaps Model — Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry (1985):** (7 Marks)

The model identifies **5 gaps** that collectively explain customer dissatisfaction and complaint generation:

- **Gap 1 — Knowledge Gap:** Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations. *Cause of complaint:* Misreading the market (e.g., expecting basic phones, getting only premium models).

- **Gap 2 — Standards Gap:** Difference between management perception and the formal service quality specifications set. *Cause of complaint:* Vague or absent SOPs.

- **Gap 3 — Delivery Gap:** Difference between specifications and actual service delivered. *Cause of complaint:* Employee incompetence, equipment failure.

- **Gap 4 — Communication Gap:** Difference between delivered service and what was promised in external marketing. *Cause of complaint:* Over-promising in advertisements.

- **Gap 5 — Service Gap (Customer View):** The cumulative difference between expected and perceived service, mathematically:

$$GAP\_5 = f(GAP\_1, GAP\_2, GAP\_3, GAP\_4)$$

This gap **directly triggers complaints** — the larger the gap, the more dissatisfied the customer, the higher the complaint rate.

**Diagram (3 Marks):** Refer to the Gaps Model Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2.

**Real-world application (1 Mark):** A 5-star hotel promises "24x7 room service" (Gap 4: communication) but delivers only till midnight (Gap 3: delivery) → guest complains on checkout.

---

**(b) 8-Step ISO 10002 Complaint Handling Process — Automobile Example:** (7 Marks)

Apply to a hypothetical **Maruti Suzuki service center** receiving a complaint from Mr. Ravi about an AC malfunction in his newly purchased Swift:

1. **Receive Complaint (1 Mark):** Customer logs complaint via Maruti "SERVICE PLUS" app; auto-acknowledgement SMS within 30 minutes. Unique ID: `MS-2024-KL-04582`.

2. **Acknowledge (0.5 Mark):** Email sent confirming receipt, expected timeline (72 hours for major), assigned Service Manager Mr. Anoop.

3. **Triage / Classify (0.5 Mark):** Severity S2 (Major — comfort issue, not safety).

4. **Investigate Root Cause (1 Mark):** 5-Why reveals: AC not cooling → refrigerant low → leak in compressor O-ring → supplier defect (sub-standard rubber).

5. **Corrective Action (1 Mark):** Replace compressor under warranty; escalate to **SCAR (Supplier Corrective Action Request)** to the compressor vendor.

6. **Communicate Resolution (0.5 Mark):** Mr. Ravi informed via phone + email; complimentary interior cleaning offered.

7. **Verify Effectiveness (1 Mark):** Follow-up call at 15 days and 30 days; customer confirms satisfaction.

8. **Institutional Learning (1.5 Marks):** SCAR data fed into Maruti's **central FMEA database**, RPN for AC subsystem recalculated, supplier audit scheduled, engineering team notified for next model year improvement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) "Every complaint is a gift." Discuss this statement in the light of Total Quality Management. What are the costs and consequences of ignoring customer complaints? (7 Marks)**

**(b) Calculate Complaint Rate, Resolution Rate, FCR, and CCR for the following data: Total transactions = 50,000; Total complaints received = 1,500; Resolved complaints = 1,200; First-contact resolutions = 900; Quarterly revenue = ₹8 crore; Complaint-handling cost = ₹12 lakh. Interpret each metric. (7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) "Every Complaint is a Gift" — TQM Perspective:** (7 Marks)

**The Philosophy (2 Marks):** Coined by Jan Carlzon (former SAS CEO) and popularized in TQM, this statement conveys that complaints are *free, unsolicited, actionable market research*. Each complaint saves the firm the cost of a customer survey, focus group, or consultant.

**Why Complaints Are Gifts (3 Marks):**

- Reveal **hidden defects** before they become widespread (think pre-recall detection).
- Identify **process gaps** unobservable to internal staff (outside-in view).
- Offer **recovery opportunities** (Recovery Paradox — can increase loyalty).
- Drive **continuous improvement** (Kaizen backlog).
- Reduce **cost of poor quality (COPQ)** by early detection.

**Costs of Ignoring Complaints (2 Marks):**

1. **Customer defection** — 1 complainer represents ~26 silent defectors.
2. **Reputation damage** — viral social media backlash.
3. **Legal exposure** — consumer protection cases, warranty lawsuits.
4. **Warranty cost escalation** — same root cause causing multiple failures.
5. **Employee demotivation** — frontline staff feel unheard when complaints are dismissed.

**Example:** Samsung Galaxy Note 7 battery fiasco (2016) — initial complaints dismissed led to **$5.3 billion loss** and global recall.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:** (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $N_{transactions} = 50{,}000$
- $N_{complaints} = 1{,}500$
- $N_{resolved} = 1{,}200$
- $N_{first\_contact} = 900$
- $R_{revenue} = \text{₹}8{,}00{,}00{,}000$
- $C_{handling} = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000$

**Solution:**

**(i) Complaint Rate (1.5 Marks):**

$$CR = \frac{1500}{50000} \times 100 = 3.0\%$$

*Interpretation:* 3 out of every 100 transactions result in a complaint — slightly above the industry benchmark of 2.5% for the service sector.

**(ii) Resolution Rate (1.5 Marks):**

$$RR = \frac{1200}{1500} \times 100 = 80.0\%$$

*Interpretation:* 80% of complaints are closed — acceptable but 20% remain open, indicating backlog or complexity issues.

**(iii) First Contact Resolution (1.5 Marks):**

$$FCR = \frac{900}{1500} \times 100 = 60.0\%$$

*Interpretation:* Only 60% resolved on first contact — below best-in-class benchmark of 70%+. Needs training in frontline agents and better knowledge-base access.

**(iv) Complaint Cost Ratio (1.5 Marks):**

$$CCR = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 0.15\%$$

*Interpretation:* Spending 0.15% of revenue on complaint handling — within healthy range (0.1%–0.5%). However, every 1% reduction in CR saves ₹80,000 in direct handling cost.

**(v) Strategic Conclusion (1 Mark):** Firm should invest in **employee training** and **self-service portals** to improve FCR and reduce CR.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Complaint" with "Feedback":** A complaint demands *resolution*; feedback is a general suggestion. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is muddled.
> 2. **Missing the "Service Gap" formula** $GAP\_5 = f(GAP_1, GAP_2, GAP_3, GAP_4)$ — this is a *sure-shot 2-mark question* in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Numerical errors in percentage calculations:** Always divide numerator by denominator, multiply by 100 — *not* the other way. KTU examiners are strict on units.
> 4. **Not mentioning ISO 10002:2018 by name** in complaint-handling answers — losing 1 easy mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the Recovery Paradox** in any service-quality answer — examiners specifically scan for this concept.
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark answers:** Even a hand-drawn block diagram of the Gaps Model earns 2–3 marks. Skipping visuals = guaranteed 2-mark penalty.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points):**

- ✅ **Customer Complaint** = Expression of dissatisfaction expecting resolution (ISO 10002:2018).
- ✅ **1-26-100 Rule:** 1 complainant → 26 silent defectors → ~10 negative word-of-mouth.
- ✅ **Gaps Model:** 4 internal gaps → Gap 5 (customer perception). $GAP\_5 = f(GAP_1, GAP_2, GAP_3, GAP_4)$.
- ✅ **Recovery Paradox:** Excellent recovery can increase loyalty beyond pre-failure level.
- ✅ **Complainant Types:** Voicers (25%), Activists (5%), Passives (55%), Ironsides (15%).
- ✅ **8-Step ISO Process:** Receive → Acknowledge → Triage → Root Cause → CAPA → Communicate → Verify → Learn.
- ✅ **Key KPIs:** CR, RR, FCR, ART, CSAT, NPS, CCR.
- ✅ **5-Why Analysis** is the standard root-cause technique.
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle** drives continuous improvement from complaint data.
- ✅ **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** includes warranty, rework, returns — all complaint-driven.
- ✅ **"Every complaint is a gift"** — Jan Carlzon philosophy; treat complaints as free market research.
- ✅ **Severity Classification:** S1 (Critical, ≤24h), S2 (Major, ≤72h), S3 (Minor, ≤7d), S4 (Info only).
- ✅ **CAPA Hierarchy:** Eliminate → Substitute → Engineer Out → Admin Control → PPE.
- ✅ **FMEA RPN** gets updated post-complaint resolution for engineering systems.
- ✅ **Customer Retention Cost < Customer Acquisition Cost** (5:1 ratio).

---

topics.title: Service Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Service Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Service Quality — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Service Quality is a high-yield topic appearing frequently in **2-mark, 3-mark, 12-mark, and 14-mark** questions. Mastery of the **SERVQUAL Model**, **Gap Model**, and **Service Quality Dimensions** is non-negotiable for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Service Quality (SQ)** is formally defined as the degree of discrepancy between customers' **expectations** of a service and their **perceptions** of the actual service performance delivered. In the canonical KTU reference framework (rooted in the seminal work of **Valarie A. Zeithaml, A. Parasuraman, and Leonard L. Berry**, 1988–1990), service quality is conceptualized as an **attitudinal construct** — a long-run, cumulative evaluation by the customer rather than a momentary satisfaction score.

$$SQ = P - E$$

Where:
- **P** = Customer's **Perception** of the actual service received
- **E** = Customer's **Expectation** of the service that should have been delivered

> [!NOTE]
> **Service Quality ≠ Customer Satisfaction.** Satisfaction is transaction-specific (single encounter). Service Quality is a global, long-term attitude spanning multiple encounters.

## 1.2 The 4 Unique Characteristics of Services (IHIP)

Services differ fundamentally from physical goods. The **IHIP** framework is mandatory in KTU answers:

| Letter | Characteristic | Plain-English Meaning |
|--------|---------------|----------------------|
| **I** | **Intangibility** | Services cannot be seen, touched, or stored before purchase |
| **H** | **Heterogeneity** | Service quality varies from customer to customer, day to day |
| **I** | **Inseparability** | Production and consumption happen simultaneously |
| **P** | **Perishability** | Unused service capacity is wasted forever (empty airline seat = lost forever) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Visit 🍽️**
>
> Imagine you book a table at a premium restaurant (your **Expectation** is set by the menu, reviews, and price). When you arrive:
> - The food is cold (**Tangibles fail**)
> - The waiter forgets your order (**Reliability fails**)
> - No one responds to your calls (**Responsiveness fails**)
> - The manager seems untrained (**Assurance fails**)
> - Nobody remembers your dietary preference (**Empathy fails**)
>
> Your **Perception** of the experience is now far below your **Expectation**. The gap (P − E) is highly negative. That negative gap **is** your perceived **Service Quality**. The restaurant must close all 5 gaps to win you back.

This is why service quality is fundamentally a **gap-closing exercise**, not a feature-adding exercise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Service Quality — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SERVQUAL Model (Zeithaml, Parasuraman & Berry — 1990)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SERVQUAL** stands for **SERV**ice **QUAL**ity. It is the most-tested service quality framework in KTU examinations. It measures service quality across **5 dimensions** using a **22-item** paired questionnaire (expectations + perceptions on a 7-point Likert scale).

### The 5 SERVQUAL Dimensions

| # | Dimension | Definition | Illustrative Item |
|---|-----------|------------|--------------------|
| **1** | **Tangibles** | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance | "Modern-looking equipment" |
| **2** | **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately | "Insistence on error-free records" |
| **3** | **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service | "Employees tell exactly when services will be performed" |
| **4** | **Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy of employees; ability to inspire trust and confidence | "Employees are consistently courteous" |
| **5** | **Empathy** | Caring, individualized attention provided to customers | "Employees give you personal attention" |

### 2.1.1 The SERVQUAL Formula

For each of the **22 items**, customers provide two scores on a **7-point Likert scale (1 = Strongly Disagree, 7 = Strongly Agree)**:
- **Eᵢ** = Expectation score for item *i*
- **Pᵢ** = Perception score for item *i*

The gap for each item is computed as:

$$SQ_i = P_i - E_i$$

The aggregate Service Quality score is the average across all items:

$$\overline{SQ} = \frac{1}{22}\sum_{i=1}^{22}(P_i - E_i)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A **positive** score means perception EXCEEDS expectation (delight). A **negative** score means perception FALLS SHORT of expectation (dissatisfaction). Many students misread the sign — examiners award partial credit accordingly.

## 2.2 SERVPERF — The Performance-Only Alternative

**Cronin & Taylor (1992)** argued that measuring only **performance (P)** is superior to measuring (P − E). The SERVPERF score is:

$$\overline{SERVPERF} = \frac{1}{22}\sum_{i=1}^{22} P_i$$

## 2.3 The Gap Model of Service Quality (5 Gaps)

> [!CRITICAL]
> **The Gap Model is the SINGLE most important KTU 14-mark question format on this topic.** Always close your answer with a diagram of the 5 gaps.

The Gap Model identifies **5 distinct gaps** that cumulatively determine service quality. **Gap 5 (the customer gap) is a function of Gaps 1, 2, 3, and 4**.

| Gap # | Name | Equation | Root Cause |
|-------|------|----------|------------|
| **Gap 1** | Knowledge Gap | Management Perception − Customer Expectation | Insufficient marketing research |
| **Gap 2** | Standards Gap | Service Quality Specs − Management Perception | Lack of service standards |
| **Gap 3** | Delivery Gap | Service Delivery − Service Quality Specs | Performance ambiguity |
| **Gap 4** | Communication Gap | External Communications − Service Delivery | Inadequate horizontal communication |
| **Gap 5** | Customer Gap | Customer Expectation − Customer Perception | **Σ of Gaps 1+2+3+4** |

### 2.3.1 The Master Equation of Service Quality Gap

$$\text{Gap}_5 = \text{Gap}_1 + \text{Gap}_2 + \text{Gap}_3 + \text{Gap}_4$$

Where each individual gap can be expressed directionally:

$$\text{Gap}_5 = (E - P) = (E - M) + (M - S) + (S - D) + (D - C)$$

> - **E** = Customer Expectation
> - **M** = Management Perception
> - **S** = Service Quality Specification
> - **D** = Service Delivery
> - **C** = External Communications

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In service-driven engineering firms (telecom, airlines, IT services, e-commerce logistics), closing Gap 3 is most challenging because services depend on **human variabilty** — exactly the **Heterogeneity** challenge.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| $SQ = P - E$ | Service Quality Score per item | SERVQUAL item-level analysis |
| $\overline{SQ} = \frac{1}{22}\sum_{i=1}^{22}(P_i - E_i)$ | Aggregate Service Quality | Final SERVQUAL score |
| $\overline{SERVPERF} = \frac{1}{22}\sum_{i=1}^{22} P_i$ | Performance-only score | SERVPERF (Cronin & Taylor) |
| $\text{Gap}_5 = \sum_{k=1}^{4} \text{Gap}_k$ | Total customer gap | Gap Model analysis |
| $\text{Satisfaction Index} = \frac{\sum P_i}{\sum E_i} \times 100$ | % Performance vs Expectation | Service benchmarking |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Telecom (Jio, Airtel):** Network uptime = Reliability dimension; call center resolution time = Responsiveness dimension.
2. **Airlines (IndiGo, Emirates):** On-time performance = Reliability; cabin crew courtesy = Assurance.
3. **IT Services (TCS, Infosys):** SLA adherence = Reliability; 24×7 helpdesk = Responsiveness.
4. **Healthcare (Apollo, KIMS):** Doctor empathy + diagnostic accuracy = Empathy + Assurance.
5. **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Delivery speed + return policy = Reliability + Tangibles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Service Quality — Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Numerical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern — 7 Mark Sub-Part):** A hospital surveyed 100 in-patients using the SERVQUAL 7-point Likert scale across 22 items. The aggregate scores obtained are:
> - $\sum E_i = 14{,}200$
> - $\sum P_i = 11{,}900$
>
> **Calculate:** (i) Average Expectation per item, (ii) Average Perception per item, (iii) Aggregate Service Quality Score, and (iv) Interpret the result.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Average Expectation per item:**

$$\overline{E} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{22} E_i}{22} = \frac{14{,}200}{22} = 645.45$$

Wait — this is the **total** across 100 patients. The standard approach is to first average across patients, then across items. Let me redo this properly.

Given 100 patients × 22 items = **2,200 total response pairs**.

$$\overline{E}_{\text{patient avg}} = \frac{14{,}200}{100} = 142$$

$$\overline{E}_{\text{per item}} = \frac{142}{22} = 6.45$$

**Step 2 — Average Perception per item:**

$$\overline{P}_{\text{patient avg}} = \frac{11{,}900}{100} = 119$$

$$\overline{P}_{\text{per item}} = \frac{119}{22} = 5.41$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate Service Quality Score:**

$$\overline{SQ} = \overline{P} - \overline{E} = 5.41 - 6.45 = -1.04$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

Since $\overline{SQ} < 0$, patients perceive the service quality to be **below** their expectations. The hospital must close the negative gap of **−1.04 points per item** to achieve service quality parity.

## 3.2 Gap Model Numerical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A retail bank measured the following gap scores from internal audits:
> - Gap 1 (Knowledge) = 0.8
> - Gap 2 (Standards) = 0.5
> - Gap 3 (Delivery) = 1.2
> - Gap 4 (Communication) = 0.3
>
> **Find Gap 5 and identify the priority gap.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the Master Equation:**

$$\text{Gap}_5 = \text{Gap}_1 + \text{Gap}_2 + \text{Gap}_3 + \text{Gap}_4$$

$$\text{Gap}_5 = 0.8 + 0.5 + 1.2 + 0.3$$

$$\boxed{\text{Gap}_5 = 2.8}$$

**Step 2 — Priority Identification:**

The largest contributor is **Gap 3 = 1.2 (Delivery Gap)**. This indicates the bank's frontline staff are **unable/unwilling to deliver services to specification**. Recommended interventions:
- Employee training
- Empowerment programs
- Internal service quality audits
- Technology enablement (CRM, queue management)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing SERVQUAL vs. SERVPERF

| Parameter | SERVQUAL (P − E) | SERVPERF (P only) |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Items** | 44 (22 × 2 scales) | 22 (single scale) |
| **Cognitive Load** | High | Low |
| **Captures Expectation** | Yes | No |
| **Captures Performance** | Yes | Yes |
| **Predictive Power** | Moderate | High (Cronin & Taylor) |
| **Industry Preference** | Academic / Consulting | Operations Management |
| **KTU Favourite?** | ✅ **Most asked** | ⚠️ Occasionally asked |

## 3.4 Decision Logic — Choosing the Right Framework

```
IF question mentions "expectations AND perceptions"  →  SERVQUAL
IF question mentions "performance only"               →  SERVPERF
IF question mentions "5 gaps" or "customer gap"       →  GAP MODEL
IF question mentions "5 dimensions" + "22 items"      →  SERVQUAL DIMENSIONS
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Service Quality — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Gap Model of Service Quality (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CUST["CUSTOMER SIDE"]:::cust
    PROV["PROVIDER SIDE"]:::prov

    E["EXPECTATION"]:::node
    P["PERCEPTION"]:::node
    M["MANAGEMENT PERCEPTION"]:::node
    S["SERVICE QUALITY SPECIFICATION"]:::node
    D["SERVICE DELIVERY"]:::node
    EC["EXTERNAL COMMUNICATIONS"]:::node

    E -->|Gap 1| M
    M -->|Gap 2| S
    S -->|Gap 3| D
    D -->|Gap 4| EC
    EC -->|Delta| E
    P -->|Result| E

    G5["GAP 5 = CUSTOMER GAP"]:::critical
    G5 -.summation.-> G1["GAP 1 + GAP 2 + GAP 3 + GAP 4"]:::critical

    classDef cust fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef prov fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef critical fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
```

## 4.2 SERVQUAL 5-Dimension Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SQ["SERVICE QUALITY"]:::root
```
