# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Kaizen. Explain its origin and significance in Total Quality Management. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Kaizen is a Japanese management philosophy meaning "Change for the Better" (改善). It emphasizes *small, continuous, incremental improvements* involving every employee — from top management to shop-floor workers — as a daily way of working.

**Origin (1 mark):** The term was popularized globally by **Masaaki Imai** in his 1986 book *"Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success."* Rooted in post-World War II Japanese industrial practice (Toyota Production System), it became the philosophical foundation of Total Quality Management in Japanese firms.

**Significance in TQM (1 mark):** Kaizen provides the *human-centric, process-oriented, low-cost, sustainable* improvement mechanism that makes TQM a living system rather than a one-time project. It ensures that quality improvement is a **daily habit, not a periodic campaign**.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Kaizen and Innovation. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | **Kaizen** | **Innovation** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Small, incremental, continuous improvements | Large, radical, disruptive changes |
| 2 | Bottom-up — driven by every employee | Top-down — driven by R&D specialists |
| 3 | Low investment, low risk | High investment, high risk |
| 4 | Ongoing, no end date | Episodic, project-based |
| 5 | Focuses on **process** improvement | Focuses on **product/technology** leap |

(Any **3 clear contrasting points** with one mark each = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**  
> • Students often write generic definitions without naming **Masaaki Imai, Toyota, or the post-war Japanese context** — losing 1–2 marks.  
> • For 14-mark questions, you **must include diagrams** (House of Kaizen / PDCA cycle). Diagrams carry 2–3 marks minimum.  
> • Avoid mixing up "Kaizen Event" with "Kaizen Teian" — examiners deduct marks.  
> • Always conclude long answers with the **standardize-improve loop** to show depth.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks):  
**"Explain the Kaizen philosophy in detail. Discuss its five fundamental principles and the PDCA cycle. How does Kaizen differ from Innovation?"** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]`

#### Part (a) — Kaizen Philosophy and Five Principles (7 marks)

**1. Introduction (2 marks):**
Kaizen (改善) is a Japanese term combining "Kai" (change) and "Zen" (good), literally meaning "Change for the Better." It was popularized by **Masaaki Imai** (1986) as a management philosophy centered on **continuous, small-step improvement** powered by *every employee*. Originating from post-WWII Japanese reconstruction and the Toyota Production System, Kaizen transformed companies like Toyota, Sony, and Canon into global quality leaders by accumulating millions of small improvements rather than relying solely on disruptive innovation.

**2. Five Fundamental Principles of Kaizen (5 marks):**

- **Principle 1 — Process Thinking:** Quality outcomes are products of quality processes. When defects occur, the *process* is at fault, not the worker. Managers must standardize and improve processes first.
- **Principle 2 — PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act):** All improvements follow a disciplined cycle. Kaizen is PDCA applied repeatedly at every organizational level.
- **Principle 3 — Gemba Orientation:** "Gemba" (現場) means the *real place* where work happens — typically the shop floor. Managers must spend at least 30% of their time at Gemba observing, asking, and listening.
- **Principle 4 — Empower Everyone (Bottom-Up Suggestions):** Every employee submits improvement ideas through a structured suggestion system. Toyota, for example, averages **30+ suggestions per employee per year**.
- **Principle 5 — Standardization:** Every successful Kaizen must result in an updated Standard Operating Procedure (SOP). Without standardization, improvement regresses.

#### Part (b) — PDCA Cycle and Kaizen vs Innovation (7 marks)

**1. PDCA Cycle Explained (4 marks):**

| Stage | Purpose | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Identify problem; analyze root cause using tools like 5 Whys; draft a countermeasure | Improvement plan |
| **Do** | Pilot the countermeasure on small scale | Pilot data |
| **Check** | Validate whether pilot achieved target | Evidence (charts, statistical comparison) |
| **Act** | If success → standardize; if failure → revise and loop back to Plan | New standard OR revised plan |

The cycle is **continuous** — it never terminates. Each Act becomes the new Plan baseline.

**2. Kaizen vs Innovation (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **Kaizen** | **Innovation** |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Incremental, small, continuous | Radical, disruptive, episodic |
| Driver | All employees | R&D specialists |
| Investment | Low (often zero) | High |
| Risk | Low | High |
| Example | Reduce setup time 12→11 min | Develop electric vehicle |

Kaizen and innovation are **complementary, not contradictory**. Total improvement = Kaizen + Innovation.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks):  
**"Discuss the concept of 5S. How does 5S act as the foundation of Kaizen implementation? Describe any two tools used in Kaizen with examples."** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 2]`

#### Part (a) — 5S and its Role as Kaizen Foundation (7 marks)

**1. Introduction to 5S (2 marks):**
5S is a five-step workplace organization methodology originating from Japan. It is the **physical and cultural foundation** without which any Kaizen program will collapse. 5S creates a *visual, clean, standardized, self-disciplined* environment where improvements can be observed, measured, and sustained.

**2. The Five Pillars of 5S (5 marks):**

| # | Japanese | English | Meaning | Practical Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Seiri (整理)** | Sort | Keep only essentials; remove all else | Tag-red unwanted items; discard |
| 2 | **Seiton (整頓)** | Set in Order | A place for everything | Shadow boards; floor markings |
| 3 | **Seiso (清掃)** | Shine | Daily cleaning | 5-min morning cleanup |
| 4 | **Seiketsu (清潔)** | Standardize | Visual SOPs, color codes | Checklists; photo standards |
| 5 | **Shitsuke (躾)** | Sustain | Self-discipline, audit | Monthly 5S audits; training |

**Why 5S is the Foundation (extra 1 mark from synthesis):**
Without 5S, workers cannot *identify* waste, cannot *locate* tools, cannot *maintain* equipment, and cannot *sustain* improvements. Gemba walks become meaningless in a chaotic workplace. Hence, Imai's House of Kaizen places 5S as the **ground floor**.

#### Part (b) — Two Kaizen Tools with Examples (7 marks)

**1. The 5 Whys — Root Cause Analysis (3.5 marks):**

The 5 Whys is a questioning technique developed within Toyota by **Sakichi Toyoda**. It iteratively asks "Why?" to penetrate beyond the symptom to the *systemic root cause*.

**Worked Example:**  
*"Machine stopped producing parts."*
- Why? → *Fuse blew.*
- Why? → *Circuit overloaded.*
- Why? → *Bearing was not lubricated.*
- Why? → *Lubrication pump was not running.*
- Why? → *Pump maintenance schedule was skipped.*
- **Root Cause: Absence of preventive maintenance culture.**

**2. Gemba Walk — Managerial Observation Tool (3.5 marks):**

Gemba (現場) means *"the real place."* A Gemba Walk is the practice of managers physically going to the shop floor to observe processes, ask questions, and engage workers — instead of relying on reports and dashboards.

**Procedure:** Observe → Ask → Listen → Document (A3) → Reflect.

**Example:** A plant manager at a fastener manufacturing unit walks the shop floor daily. During a Gemba walk, he notices workers reaching awkwardly across a conveyor to pick up bolts. A simple Kaizen of repositioning the bin reduces motion waste by 15 seconds per cycle, saving 2.5 hours/day across the line.

**Synthesis:** Both 5 Whys and Gemba Walk embody the Kaizen principles of *process thinking* and *gemba orientation*. They are complementary: Gemba Walk *identifies* the problem; 5 Whys *analyzes* its root cause.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Module 2 — Kaizen Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Kaizen (改善)** = "Change for the Better" = Kai (change) + Zen (good).  
- ✅ Founder of modern Kaizen thought: **Masaaki Imai** (1986 book).  
- ✅ Origin: Post-WWII Japan; Toyota Production System.  
- ✅ **Two Pillars of Improvement:** Kaizen (incremental) + Innovation (radical).  
- ✅ **Five Principles:** Process focus, PDCA, Gemba, Bottom-up suggestions, Standardization.  
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (continuous).  
- ✅ **5S:** Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke — the foundation of all Kaizen.  
- ✅ **Gemba:** The real workplace; managers must visit it regularly.  
- ✅ **5 Whys:** Iterative root-cause analysis tool.  
- ✅ **Kaizen Teian:** Continuous suggestion-based.  
- ✅ **Kaizen Event / Blitz:** Time-boxed 3–5 day intensive workshop.  
- ✅ **Compound 1% Rule:** $(1.01)^{365} \approx 37.78$ — small daily improvements compound dramatically.  
- ✅ **Standardize → Improve → Standardize** is the meta-loop.  
- ✅ Always include a **diagram** in 14-mark answers (House of Kaizen or PDCA).  
- ✅ Always mention **Toyota benchmark**: 30+ suggestions/employee/year.  
- ✅ Always distinguish **Kaizen (process)** from **Innovation (product/technology)**.  
- ✅ Kaizen is **low-cost, low-risk, sustainable** — its biggest advantage over innovation.  
- ✅ The **"Hidden Factory"** concept by Taiichi Ohno justifies why Kaizen is essential.  
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, structure answer as: *Definition → Origin → Principles → PDCA/5S → Tools → Conclusion.*

> **End of Module 2 — Kaizen Study Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PESFT746)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier Partnership - Partnering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2: Supplier Partnership — Partnering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supplier Partnership (Partnering)** is a structured, long-term, win-win collaborative relationship between a buying organization and its supplier(s), built upon mutual trust, open communication, shared vision, and joint investment of resources, aimed at achieving superior quality, lower total cost, faster cycle time, and continuous innovation beyond what either party could achieve independently.

In KTU 2024 Scheme TQM terminology, partnering moves the buyer–supplier interface away from an **adversarial (arm's-length)** posture to a **cooperative, process-oriented, quality-driven** alliance. The seminal work by **Lamming (1993)** and the **Toyota Production System** literature describe this as the transition from "price-per-piece" thinking to **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** thinking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):**
> The student is expected to differentiate between *traditional purchasing* and *partnering*, list the *benefits* and *barriers*, describe the *partnering process*, identify the *seven tools of supplier partnership*, and apply the *supplier evaluation/selection models* mathematically.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Marriage vs. The Blind Date":**
>
> Imagine going to a vegetable market daily and haggling with a stranger for tomatoes (**arm's-length / transactional**). Tomorrow you may buy from someone else; there is **no trust, no loyalty, no shared future**.
>
> Now imagine a farmer who supplies your family's kitchen every week. You know his fields, his irrigation practices, and his family. You agree on a **fair annual price**, share the **risk of crop failure**, and jointly invest in a **cold-storage unit** so both of you benefit.
>
> **That second relationship is SUPPLIER PARTNERING.**
>
> - **Buyer = Restaurant**
> - **Supplier = Vegetable Farmer**
> - **Partnership = Shared cold-storage, agreed quality specs, joint cost reduction**

The farmer stops cutting corners (because he is paid fairly), and the restaurant stops switching vendors weekly (because quality is consistent). The **Total Value** rises for both — that is the **essence of TQM partnering**.

### 1.3 Core Vocabulary (Bold the Standard KTU Metrics)

- **TCO** — **Total Cost of Ownership** (the lifetime cost of buying from a supplier, not just unit price).
- **SQA** — **Supplier Quality Assurance**.
- **JIT** — **Just-In-Time** delivery (which is impossible without partnering).
- **KPI** — **Key Performance Indicator** (quality, delivery, cost, responsiveness, technology).
- **SPC** — **Statistical Process Control** data shared between buyer and supplier.
- **Keiretsu** — **Japanese supplier grouping** (Toyota-style).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Archetypes of Buyer–Supplier Relationships

| Archetype | Trust Level | Contract Length | Information Sharing | Typical Use |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Arm's Length (Adversarial)** | Very Low | Short-term PO | Minimal | Commodity items |
| **Partnership (Cooperative)** | High | 1–5 years | Extensive | Engineered components |
| **Strategic Alliance / Single Sourcing** | Very High | 5+ years | Fully transparent | Critical sub-assemblies |

## 2.2 Why Partner? The Six Strategic Drivers ("The Why")

1. **Quality at Source** — Defects are prevented, not inspected out.
2. **Cost Reduction** — Joint Value Engineering lowers TCO, not unit price.
3. **Cycle-Time Compression** — Integrated planning enables JIT.
4. **Innovation Leverage** — Suppliers contribute early in design (DFM/DFA).
5. **Risk Sharing** — Capacity, currency, technology, and demand risk distributed.
6. **Competitive Advantage** — A robust supply base is itself a *strategic moat*.

## 2.3 The Three Pillars of Successful Partnering ("The How")

- **Pillar A — Trust & Transparency:** Open-book cost sharing, shared SPC charts.
- **Pillar B — Mutual Goal Alignment:** Common scorecards, balanced scorecards aligned to one strategy.
- **Pillar C — Joint Investment:** Training, capital equipment, ERP integration.

## 2.4 Seven Tools of Supplier Partnership (TQM Framework)

1. **Understand Customer Needs** — Voice of Customer (VOC) flowed downstream.
2. **Establish Supplier Selection Criteria** — TCO, capability, capacity, culture.
3. **Select Supplier** — Weighted scoring model.
4. **Develop Partnership** — Joint training, common quality system.
5. **Implement Partnership** — EDI/ERP linkage, co-location, Kaizen.
6. **Evaluate Performance** — Continuous scorecards, Pareto analysis of defects.
7. **Recognize Achievement** — Supplier awards, shared savings.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Supplier Evaluation & Cost Models

> [!NOTE]
> **Important LaTeX Note for Tables:** Throughout the formula sheet below, the vertical bar used inside mathematical expressions (e.g., for absolute value) is written as `\vert` or `\mid` so that markdown table syntax remains intact.

| # | Model / Concept | Governing Equation / Logic | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Weighted Point Score** for supplier $i$ | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ with $\sum w_j = 1$ | $S_i$ = dimensionless score (0–10); $w_j$ = weight; $r_{ij}$ = rating of supplier $i$ on criterion $j$ |
| 2 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = P_p + C_q + C_d + C_i + C_m + C_s$ | All costs in monetary units per unit / per year |
| 3 | **Cost-Per-Point Method** | $CPP_i = \dfrac{C_i}{S_i}$ | Lower is better; used when both price and score matter |
| 4 | **Total Cost Ratio Method** | $TCR_i = \dfrac{C_i \cdot \sum_{j} w_j \cdot \overline{r_j}}{C_i \cdot \sum_{j} w_j \cdot r_{ij}} = \dfrac{\overline{S}}{S_i}$ | Ratio form; > 1 means supplier is worse than average |
| 5 | **Categorical Method** | Pass/Fail on pre-set qualitative criteria | Boolean logic, no units |
| 6 | **Defect Rate Target** (ppm framework) | $D_{ppm} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{Units inspected}} \times 10^6$ | Six-Sigma target: $D_{ppm} \le 3.4$ |
| 7 | **Supplier Capability Index** | $Cpk = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $Cpk \ge 1.33$ is the typical partnering threshold |

### Real-World Utility

These equations are the *mathematical spine* of an **incoming-quality audit** in any ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 / AS9100 certified industry — automotive, aerospace, electronics, pharma. KTU examiners frequently test the **Weighted Point Model** and the **Cost-Per-Point Method** as direct 7-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Weighted Point Supplier Selection Model

The standard KTU problem gives multiple suppliers, multiple criteria, and asks which supplier is best.

### Step 1 — Define the Criteria Matrix
Let there be $m$ suppliers indexed $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ and $n$ criteria indexed $j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$.

Build the raw rating matrix $R = [r_{ij}]$ where each $r_{ij}$ is the rating of supplier $i$ on criterion $j$ on a fixed scale (commonly **0–10** or **1–5**).

### Step 2 — Normalize the Weights
Weights $w_j$ are assigned by the buying team reflecting strategic priority. They must satisfy:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1, \quad w_j > 0
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Composite Score

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S_i = w_1 r_{i1} + w_2 r_{i2} + w_3 r_{i3} + \cdots + w_n r_{in}\,}
$$

### Step 4 — Rank
The supplier with the highest $S_i$ is selected, **provided $S_i \ge S_{\min}$**, where $S_{\min}$ is the minimum acceptable partnering score (often 7/10).

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose 3 suppliers (A, B, C) are rated on 4 criteria with weights $w_1 = 0.4$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$, $w_4 = 0.1$.

| Supplier | Quality ($r_1$) | Delivery ($r_2$) | Cost ($r_3$) | Tech ($r_4$) |
|----------|-----------------|------------------|--------------|--------------|
| A | 8 | 7 | 6 | 5 |
| B | 7 | 9 | 7 | 6 |
| C | 9 | 6 | 5 | 8 |

**Verify weight normalization:**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{4} w_j = 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 1.000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Compute $S_A$:**

$$
S_A = (0.4)(8) + (0.3)(7) + (0.2)(6) + (0.1)(5)
$$

$$
S_A = 3.2 + 2.1 + 1.2 + 0.5 = 7.00
$$

**Compute $S_B$:**

$$
S_B = (0.4)(7) + (0.3)(9) + (0.2)(7) + (0.1)(6)
$$

$$
S_B = 2.8 + 2.7 + 1.4 + 0.6 = 7.50
$$

**Compute $S_C$:**

$$
S_C = (0.4)(9) + (0.3)(6) + (0.2)(5) + (0.1)(8)
$$

$$
S_C = 3.6 + 1.8 + 1.0 + 0.8 = 7.20
$$

**Ranking:** $S_B = 7.50 > S_C = 7.20 > S_A = 7.00$.

**Decision:** Choose **Supplier B** (highest composite score).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Cost-Per-Point (CPP) Method

When price cannot be ignored, the **Cost-Per-Point** metric corrects the bias of pure weighted scoring.

### Step 1 — Obtain Quoted Unit Price $C_i$ for Each Supplier
The price must be the **Total Cost** (including logistics, duties, inspection), not just the invoice price.

### Step 2 — Compute the Composite Score $S_i$ as Above

### Step 3 — Compute the CPP Ratio

$$
\boxed{\,CPP_i = \dfrac{C_i}{S_i}\,}
$$

The **lower** $CPP_i$ is, the better the supplier — it means "less money paid per unit of quality/service delivered."

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Suppose the unit prices quoted are: $C_A = \text{₹}120$, $C_B = \text{₹}135$, $C_C = \text{₹}110$.

Using scores from the previous example ($S_A = 7.00$, $S_B = 7.50$, $S_C = 7.20$):

$$
CPP_A = \frac{120}{7.00} = 17.14 \ \text{₹/point}
$$

$$
CPP_B = \frac{135}{7.50} = 18.00 \ \text{₹/point}
$$

$$
CPP_C = \frac{110}{7.20} = 15.28 \ \text{₹/point}
$$

**Ranking by CPP:** $CPP_C = 15.28 < CPP_A = 17.14 < CPP_B = 18.00$.

**Decision:** **Supplier C** now wins because its superior cost-per-point overcomes its slightly lower weighted score. This illustrates the **TCO philosophy** of partnering.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Breakdown

KTU often asks students to "compute TCO and justify the supplier choice."

$$
\boxed{\,TCO = P_p + C_q + C_d + C_i + C_m + C_s\,}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| $P_p$ | Purchase price | ₹100 / unit |
| $C_q$ | Cost of poor quality (rework, scrap, warranty) | ₹8 / unit |
| $C_d$ | Downstream cost of defects (line stoppage, recall) | ₹15 / unit |
| $C_i$ | Inbound logistics + inspection | ₹5 / unit |
| $C_m$ | Internal handling, storage, carrying cost | ₹3 / unit |
| $C_s$ | Service / support / downtime cost | ₹4 / unit |
| **TCO** | **Total** | **₹135 / unit** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A cheap supplier with $P_p = ₹90$ but $C_q + C_d = ₹60$ gives $TCO = ₹165$, which is **worse** than the partner with $P_p = ₹100$ but $C_q + C_d = ₹23$. This is the **central insight of TQM partnering**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Partnering Continuum (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["ARM LENGTH ADVERSARIAL"] --> nodeB["TRANSITIONAL PARTNERSHIP"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["OPERATIONAL PARTNERSHIP"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STRATEGIC ALLIANCE SINGLE SOURCING"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["KEIRETSU STYLE EMBEDDED PARTNERSHIP"]
    nodeA --- note1["LOW TRUST SHORT CONTRACTS MANY SUPPLIERS"]
    nodeB --- note2["PILOT JOINT COST INITIATIVES"]
    nodeC --- note3["SHARED SPC DATA ANNUAL REVIEWS"]
    nodeD --- note4["JOINT R AND D CO DESIGN"]
    nodeE --- note5["EQUITY LINKS GENERATIONAL TIES"]
```

## 4.2 The Seven-Tool Partnering Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["STEP 1 UNDERSTAND CUSTOMER NEEDS"] --> step2["STEP 2 ESTABLISH SELECTION CRITERIA"]
    step2 --> step3["STEP 3 SELECT SUPPLIER USING WEIGHTED SCORING"]
    step3 --> step4["STEP 4 DEVELOP PARTNERSHIP JOINT TRAINING"]
    step4 --> step5["STEP 5 IMPLEMENT EDI ERP LINKAGE JIT"]
    step5 --> step6["STEP 6 EVALUATE PERFORMANCE SCORECARDS"]
    step6 --> step7["STEP 7 RECOGNIZE AND AWARD SUPPLIER"]
    step7 --> step1
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Supplier Partnership

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BUYER_ORG["BUYER ORGANIZATION"]
        b1["PROCUREMENT"]
        b2["DESIGN AND ENGINEERING"]
        b3["QUALITY ASSURANCE"]
        b4["LOGISTICS"]
    end
    subgraph SHARED["SHARED PARTNERSHIP LAYER"]
        s1["JOINT QUALITY PLAN"]
        s2["MUTUAL COST REDUCTION TARGETS"]
        s3["INTEGRATED IT PLATFORM"]
        s4["TRAINING ACADEMY"]
    end
    subgraph SUPPLIER_ORG["SUPPLIER ORGANIZATION"]
        su1["SUPPLIER PRODUCTION"]
        su2["SUPPLIER QA LAB"]
        su3["SUPPLIER DESIGN"]
        su4["SUPPLIER LOGISTICS"]
    end
    b1 <--> s1
    b2 <--> s3
    b3 <--> s1
    b4 <--> s3
    su1 <--> s2
    su2 <--> s1
    su3 <--> s3
    su4 <--> s3
    s4 --- b1
    s4 --- su1
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: Traditional Purchasing vs. Partnering

| Dimension | Traditional Purchasing | Supplier Partnering |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Supplier count | Many (price-driven) | Few (capability-driven) |
| Contract length | Short, transactional | Long-term, relational |
| Information flow | One-way (PO) | Two-way (open book) |
| Quality philosophy | Inspect-in | Build-in at source |
| Price basis | Lowest unit price | Total Cost of Ownership |
| Innovation | Driven by buyer | Co-driven by both |
| Conflict resolution | Litigation / switching | Joint problem solving |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define Supplier Partnership. List any four benefits of partnering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Supplier Partnership is a long-term cooperative relationship between a buyer and a supplier based on mutual trust, open communication, and shared risks and rewards, with the objective of achieving superior quality and lower total cost.
>
> **Four benefits:**
> 1. Reduced Total Cost of Ownership through joint Value Engineering.
> 2. Improved incoming quality via SPC at source.
> 3. Shorter product-development cycle through early supplier involvement.
> 4. Risk and capacity sharing, especially during demand fluctuations.

**(Distribution: 1 mark definition + 0.5 × 4 = 2 marks benefits.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Differentiate between Traditional Purchasing and Supplier Partnering (any four points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| # | Traditional Purchasing | Supplier Partnering |
|---|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Many suppliers, low switching cost | Few trusted suppliers, high switching cost |
| 2 | Lowest-unit-price focus | Total-Cost-of-Ownership focus |
| 3 | Adversarial, contract-heavy | Cooperative, trust-driven |
| 4 | Inspection at receiving | Quality built-in at supplier source |
| 5 | One-way PO communication | Open-book, two-way information sharing |

**(4 points × 0.75 = 3 marks.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the weight-normalization step** ($\sum w_j = 1$) in weighted scoring problems and lose **1 mark**. Always show it explicitly.
> 2. In CPP problems, students often forget to **convert to the same units** (₹ vs. $) before dividing. **Dimensional consistency is a 1-mark item.**
> 3. When discussing "benefits of partnering," **bullet points alone fetch only 1 mark**; the examiner expects a **1-line explanation per point** (≈3–4 marks total for benefits).
> 4. Never confuse *partnering* with *outsourcing*. Outsourcing is the act of transferring activity; partnering is the **style of relationship** during that transfer.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme, Module 2, Q.2(a)]**

#### **(a) Explain the concept of Supplier Partnership. Discuss its benefits and the barriers to its implementation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
Supplier Partnership (or *Partnering*) is a long-term, process-oriented, cooperative buyer–supplier relationship founded on mutual trust, transparency, and shared destiny, with the joint objective of delivering superior value to the end customer. It replaces the adversarial, price-driven arm's-length model with a collaborative system where both parties co-invest in capability, technology, and continuous improvement.

**2. Benefits (3 marks):**
- **Quality improvement:** Joint SPC, FMEA, and poka-yoke at source drastically reduce incoming-defect ppm.
- **Cost reduction:** Value engineering, common platforms, and reduced inspection lower TCO.
- **Cycle-time compression:** Vendor-managed inventory (VMI) and EDI enable true JIT.
- **Innovation:** Early Supplier Involvement (ESI) brings design-for-manufacturability ideas upstream.
- **Risk sharing:** Capacity, currency, and technology risk are distributed.
- **Competitive advantage:** A stable, capable supply base is a strategic moat in volatile markets.

**3. Barriers (2 marks):**
- Cultural mismatch and lack of top-management commitment.
- Short-term CFO pressure (quarterly earnings vs. long-term investment).
- Lack of trust, fear of sharing proprietary data.
- Geographic distance and language/communication gaps.
- Incompatible IT systems.

---

#### **(b) A manufacturing company wants to select a supplier among three options (P, Q, R). The criteria, weights and ratings (on a 1–10 scale) are given below. Use the Weighted Point Model to identify the best supplier. (7 marks)**

| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ | Rating P | Rating Q | Rating R |
|-----------|--------------|----------|----------|----------|
| Quality | 0.35 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Delivery | 0.25 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| Technology | 0.15 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
| Responsiveness | 0.05 | 5 | 6 | 7 |

**Step 1 — Weight normalization check:**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} w_j = 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.05 = 1.00 \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $S_P$:**

$$
S_P = (0.35)(9) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(6) + (0.05)(5)
$$

$$
S_P = 3.15 + 2.00 + 1.40 + 0.90 + 0.25 = 7.70
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_Q$:**

$$
S_Q = (0.35)(7) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(7) + (0.05)(6)
$$

$$
S_Q = 2.45 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.30 = 7.65
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S_R$:**

$$
S_R = (0.35)(8) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(8) + (0.05)(7)
$$

$$
S_R = 2.80 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.20 + 0.35 = 7.90
$$

**Step 5 — Rank:** $S_R = 7.90 > S_P = 7.70 > S_Q = 7.65$.

**Conclusion:** **Supplier R** should be selected as the partner. **(1 mark for final decision + 0.5 each for intermediate scores = 7 marks total.)**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU Model Paper – 2024 Scheme, Module 2, Q.2(b)]**

#### **(a) Describe the Seven Tools of Supplier Partnership as per the TQM framework. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (1 mark per tool, with 1-line description):**

1. **Understand Customer Needs:** Capture Voice of Customer (VOC) and translate into supplier-relevant Critical-To-Quality (CTQ) characteristics.
2. **Establish Supplier Selection Criteria:** Define objective, weighted criteria covering quality, cost, delivery, technology, and culture.
3. **Select Supplier:** Apply Weighted Point Model and/or Cost-Per-Point Method to choose objectively.
4. **Develop Partnership:** Conduct joint training, share quality systems, agree on common goals and protocols.
5. **Implement Partnership:** Roll out EDI/ERP integration, vendor-managed inventory, co-location, and joint Kaizen projects.
6. **Evaluate Performance:** Use balanced scorecards, ppm tracking, on-time-delivery metrics, and Pareto analysis of supplier defects.
7. **Recognize Achievement:** Public awards, preferred-supplier status, share-of-business growth, and joint PR.

**(7 marks: 1 per tool × 7 = 7.)**

---

#### **(b) Two suppliers A and B quote unit prices of ₹150 and ₹160 respectively. Their Weighted Point Scores are $S_A = 8.0$ and $S_B = 7.5$. Using the Cost-Per-Point method, determine which supplier offers better value and justify. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — State the formula:**

$$
CPP_i = \frac{C_i}{S_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $CPP_A$:**

$$
CPP_A = \frac{150}{8.0} = 18.75 \ \text{₹ per point}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $CPP_B$:**

$$
CPP_B = \frac{160}{7.5} = 21.33 \ \text{₹ per point}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare and conclude:**

$$
CPP_A = 18.75 \ < \ CPP_B = 21.33
$$

**Decision:** **Supplier A** offers better value because every quality-point delivered costs ₹18.75 vs. ₹21.33 from Supplier B — a saving of approximately **₹2.58 per point (≈12.1 %)**.

**TQM Insight (1 extra mark for high-quality answer):**
Although Supplier B is cheaper on raw unit price perception difference is only ₹10 (~6 %), the quality gap is 0.5 points (~6.7 %). The CPP metric captures *both* dimensions simultaneously, embodying the **Total Cost of Ownership** philosophy central to TQM partnering.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Supplier Partnership — Partnering"**

- ✅ **Definition:** Long-term, trust-based, win-win buyer–supplier relationship aimed at superior quality, lower TCO, and innovation.
- ✅ **Three archetypes:** Arm's length → Partnership → Strategic Alliance.
- ✅ **Six drivers:** Quality, Cost, Cycle-time, Innovation, Risk-sharing, Competitive Advantage.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Trust & Transparency; Mutual Goals; Joint Investment.
- ✅ **Seven Tools:** VOC → Criteria → Select → Develop → Implement → Evaluate → Recognize (cyclic).
- ✅ **Weighted Point Model:** $S_i = \sum w_j r_{ij}$, with $\sum w_j = 1$. Always verify weight normalization.
- ✅ **Cost-Per-Point:** $CPP_i = C_i / S_i$. Lower = better value.
- ✅ **TCO Equation:** $TCO = P_p + C_q + C_d + C_i + C_m + C_s$. Never judge by unit price alone.
- ✅ **Supplier Capability:** $Cpk \ge 1.33$ is the typical partnering threshold; Six-Sigma target is $D_{ppm} \le 3.4$.
- ✅ **Japanese model:** Keiretsu (Toyota) is the gold standard of *embedded* partnership.
- ✅ **KTU pitfall:** Always show $\sum w_j = 1$ explicitly; maintain dimensional consistency in CPP; never confuse partnering with outsourcing.

> **Module 2 Take-away:** Partnering is *not* a contract — it is a **strategic posture** that turns the supply base from a cost-center into a **value-creating extension of the firm**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sourcing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sourcing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sourcing** in the context of Total Quality Management (TQM) is defined as the **strategic, systematic, and continuous process** of identifying, evaluating, selecting, managing, and developing suppliers of goods, materials, and services to ensure that the organization's quality, cost, delivery, and innovation objectives are consistently met. It is not merely the act of purchasing; it is an integrated supply-base management discipline that aligns the external supplier network with internal quality and operational goals.

As per the KTU 2024 scheme treatment of TQM, sourcing is positioned as a **strategic lever** for embedding quality upstream in the value chain — long before a product reaches the customer. The formal scope includes supplier qualification, supplier rating, supplier development, partnership formation, and strategic decision-making such as **Make-or-Buy** analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** Sourcing encompasses the *strategic decisions* a firm makes regarding its supplier base, the *evaluation methodologies* used to rank suppliers, the *criteria* governing selection, and the *long-term partnership models* that ensure continuous quality improvement across the supply chain.

### 1.2 Sub-Topics Covered Under "Sourcing"

The topic is decomposed into the following KTU-aligned sub-areas:

1. **Supplier Selection** — Criteria and methodology.
2. **Supplier Rating / Evaluation** — Continuous performance measurement.
3. **Supplier Development** — Capability enhancement of weak suppliers.
4. **Partnership Sourcing** — Long-term collaborative alliances.
5. **Strategic Sourcing vs. Tactical Purchasing**.
6. **Single, Dual, and Multiple Sourcing** strategies.
7. **Make-or-Buy Decision** analysis.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Marriage Analogy":**
> Imagine you are planning a grand wedding feast. You wouldn't simply walk into the nearest shop and pick random vegetables the morning of the event. Instead, you would:
> - **Identify** the best vegetable vendors in your region (sourcing).
> - **Evaluate** them on freshness, price, hygiene, and reliability (supplier rating).
> - **Negotiate** a long-term relationship so they prioritize you (partnership sourcing).
> - **Continuously monitor** their quality and even teach them better farming methods if needed (supplier development).
> - **Decide** whether to grow vegetables at home for purity-critical items like spices (Make-or-Buy).
>
> Sourcing in TQM is exactly this — except the "feast" is a manufactured product, and the "guests" are end customers who demand nothing less than perfection.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Sourcing (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

The following bolded metrics are **standardized quality-cost-delivery indicators** that appear repeatedly in KTU examination answers:

- **PQR (Price, Quality, Response)** — Three-dimensional supplier evaluation framework.
- **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** — All-in cost beyond purchase price.
- **OTD (On-Time Delivery) %** — Reliability metric.
- **PPM (Parts Per Million) Defects** — Quality benchmark from incoming inspection.
- **LSL / USL (Lower / Upper Specification Limits)** — Acceptance boundaries.
- **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** — Failure cost attributable to suppliers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 scheme valuation, examiners award full credit only when a student **explicitly names the metric** (e.g., "PPM defects") along with its numerical context. Vague phrases like "supplier is good" fetch zero marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Sourcing in TQM

Sourcing operates on the foundational TQM principle: **"Quality must be built into the supply chain, not inspected into the final product."** This implies that supplier quality is the *first domino* in the chain of total quality.

#### 2.1.1 The Strategic Sourcing Process (8-Step Logic)

1. **Profile the Spend Category** — Understand what is being bought, in what volume, and why.
2. **Analyze the Supply Market** — Identify potential suppliers, market structure, and risks.
3. **Develop a Sourcing Strategy** — Choose single, dual, or multiple sourcing.
4. **Identify Potential Suppliers** — Through tenders, audits, referrals.
5. **Evaluate & Select Suppliers** — Using weighted scoring, TCO, and quality audits.
6. **Negotiate Contracts** — Including SLAs (Service Level Agreements).
7. **Implement & Integrate** — Onboard suppliers into systems (ERP, EDI, QMS).
8. **Continuous Monitoring & Development** — Periodic rating, audits, and improvement programs.

#### 2.1.2 Types of Sourcing Strategies

| **Strategy** | **Definition** | **TQM Implication** |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Sourcing** | One supplier for a given item | Deep partnership, high risk if supplier fails, strong quality control |
| **Dual Sourcing** | Two suppliers for redundancy | Balanced risk-coverage, enables supplier benchmarking |
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Many suppliers, often competitive | High competition on price, lower partnership depth, harder quality consistency |
| **Partnership Sourcing** | Long-term collaborative alliance | Joint problem-solving, shared quality goals, *ideal TQM alignment* |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | Holistic, data-driven, long-term | Aligns procurement with business strategy |
| **Tactical Purchasing** | Transactional, short-term, price-driven | Anti-TQM; focuses only on cost, not quality |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Which sourcing strategy best supports TQM?"* The textbook-correct answer is **Partnership Sourcing**, because it institutionalizes mutual quality improvement.

#### 2.1.3 Supplier Selection Criteria — The "7 Cs" Framework

1. **Capability** — Technical competence.
2. **Capacity** — Ability to scale.
3. **Commitment** — Top management dedication to quality.
4. **Consistency** — Repeatable performance (low process variation).
5. **Cost** — Competitive and transparent pricing.
6. **Culture** — Alignment with buyer's quality values.
7. **Communication** — Open, timely, and accurate information flow.

#### 2.1.4 Make-or-Buy Decision — Strategic Logic

A firm chooses to **make** a component in-house when:
- The component is **strategically critical** (e.g., proprietary IP).
- **Quality control** is difficult to enforce externally.
- **Volume is high enough** to justify fixed costs.
- **Confidentiality / security** is paramount.

A firm chooses to **buy** when:
- The supplier has **core competence** in producing that item.
- **Capital investment** for in-house production is prohibitive.
- The item is **non-critical** or standardized.
- The firm wants to **focus on its own core competence**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is used inside tables to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. All absolute values and determinants are written using `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX form.

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Application Context** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weighted Supplier Score** | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_i$ | $w_i$ = weight (%), $r_i$ = rating (1–10), $S$ = total score | Rank suppliers on multi-criteria basis |
| **Normalized Weight** | $w_i^{norm} = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | Sum of all weights must equal 1 (or 100%) | Ensures fairness in scoring |
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_p + C_t + C_q + C_i + C_d + C_s$ | $C_p$=purchase, $C_t$=transport, $C_q$=quality cost, $C_i$=inventory, $C_d$=downtime, $C_s$=service | True cost of sourcing decision |
| **Make-or-Buy Crossover** | $MC = FC + VC \cdot Q$ | $MC$=make cost, $FC$=fixed cost, $VC$=variable cost/unit, $Q$=quantity (units) | Break-even comparison with buy price |
| **Buy Cost (Alternative)** | $BC = P \cdot Q + C_t$ | $P$=unit purchase price, $C_t$=transport/logistics | Compared with $MC$ |
| **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %** | $OTD = \dfrac{N_{on\_time}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{on\_time}$ = deliveries on time, $N_{total}$ = total deliveries | Supplier reliability metric |
| **Defect Rate (PPM)** | $PPM = \dfrac{D}{U} \times 10^6$ | $D$ = defects, $U$ = units inspected | Six-Sigma-level quality indicator |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\_failure} + C_{external\_failure}$ | Categorical cost buckets | Drives supplier quality improvement |
| **Supplier Performance Index** | $SPI = \dfrac{\sum_{i} w_i \cdot \dfrac{A_i}{T_i}}{100}$ | $A_i$ = actual performance, $T_i$ = target, $w_i$ = weight | Composite KPI for supplier rating |
| **Break-Even Make-or-Buy Quantity** | $Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | $Q^*$ = break-even units | Threshold beyond which "make" becomes cheaper |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The sourcing framework is operationally applied in:

- **Automotive Industry (e.g., Toyota, Tata Motors):** Keiretsu-style partnership sourcing ensures Tier-1 and Tier-2 suppliers meet IATF 16949 standards and Six-Sigma defect levels (≤ 3.4 PPM).
- **Aerospace (e.g., Boeing, Airbus):** AS9100-certified suppliers are selected through multi-criteria weighted scoring; TCO analysis dominates because a single defective bolt can ground an aircraft.
- **Electronics Manufacturing Services (Foxconn, Wistron):** Dual sourcing for ICs mitigates geopolitical and supply-shock risks.
- **Pharmaceuticals (USFDA-regulated plants):** Single sourcing with full traceability and partnership auditing is mandatory to avoid COPQ catastrophes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Weighted Supplier Selection Model

**Problem Setup:** A manufacturing firm must select a vendor for a critical machined component. Four suppliers (A, B, C, D) are evaluated on **five criteria**. Each criterion is assigned a **weight (w_i)** based on strategic priority, and each supplier is rated on a **scale of 1 to 10** for each criterion.

#### Step 3.1.1 — Define the Criteria and Weights

| **Criterion** | **Strategic Priority** | **Weight (w_i)** |
|---|---|---|
| Quality (PPM defects) | Highest | 30 |
| Cost (TCO) | High | 25 |
| On-Time Delivery | Medium | 20 |
| Technical Capability | Medium | 15 |
| Communication | Low | 10 |
| **Total** | — | **100** |

#### Step 3.1.2 — Verify Normalization of Weights

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 30 + 25 + 20 + 15 + 10 = 100$$

This satisfies $\sum w_i = 100$, so no further normalization is needed. (If weights summed to, say, 92, we would divide each by 92.)

#### Step 3.1.3 — Collect Ratings from the Evaluation Team

| **Criterion** | **Supplier A** | **Supplier B** | **Supplier C** | **Supplier D** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 8 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Cost | 7 | 6 | 9 | 8 |
| Delivery | 9 | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| Technical | 6 | 8 | 8 | 7 |
| Communication | 7 | 7 | 8 | 9 |

#### Step 3.1.4 — Compute Weighted Scores Using $S = \sum w_i \cdot r_i$

**Supplier A:**

$$S_A = (30 \times 8) + (25 \times 7) + (20 \times 9) + (15 \times 6) + (10 \times 7)$$

$$S_A = 240 + 175 + 180 + 90 + 70$$

$$\boxed{S_A = 755}$$

**Supplier B:**

$$S_B = (30 \times 9) + (25 \times 6) + (20 \times 8) + (15 \times 8) + (10 \times 7)$$

$$S_B = 270 + 150 + 160 + 120 + 70$$

$$\boxed{S_B = 770}$$

**Supplier C:**

$$S_C = (30 \times 7) + (25 \times 9) + (20 \times 7) + (15 \times 8) + (10 \times 8)$$

$$S_C = 210 + 225 + 140 + 120 + 80$$

$$\boxed{S_C = 775}$$

**Supplier D:**

$$S_D = (30 \times 8) + (25 \times 8) + (20 \times 6) + (15 \times 7) + (10 \times 9)$$

$$S_D = 240 + 200 + 120 + 105 + 90$$

$$\boxed{S_D = 755}$$

#### Step 3.1.5 — Rank and Decide

| **Rank** | **Supplier** | **Score** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | C | 775 |
| 2 | B | 770 |
| 3 (tie) | A | 755 |
| 3 (tie) | D | 755 |

**Decision:** Award the contract to **Supplier C**, with Supplier B as a backup (dual sourcing).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** If two suppliers tie, the *secondary tiebreaker* in KTU answers is usually the **Quality sub-score alone** — pick the supplier with higher Quality rating. Here, Supplier A (Quality=8) beats Supplier D (Quality=8) on tiebreak by inspection of **Delivery** (A=9 vs D=6), so **A ranks 3rd, D ranks 4th**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Make-or-Buy Decision via Break-Even Analysis

**Problem Setup:** A firm needs 20,000 units/year of a sub-assembly. Buying cost is ₹180/unit. In-house production requires a one-time fixed cost of ₹6,00,000 and a variable cost of ₹150/unit.

#### Step 3.2.1 — Formulate the Total Cost Equations

**Make Cost:**

$$MC(Q) = FC + VC \cdot Q$$

**Buy Cost:**

$$BC(Q) = P \cdot Q$$

(In this simplified problem we ignore transport/inspection for clarity; the KTU answer should *mention* these as additional factors.)

#### Step 3.2.2 — Compute Break-Even Quantity

At break-even, $MC = BC$:

$$FC + VC \cdot Q^* = P \cdot Q^*$$

$$FC = (P - VC) \cdot Q^*$$

$$Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$$

Substituting values:

$$Q^* = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{180 - 150} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{30}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = 20{,}000 \text{ units}}$$

#### Step 3.2.3 — Compare with Required Volume

| **Volume (Q)** | **Make Cost ₹** | **Buy Cost ₹** | **Cheaper Option** | **Savings ₹** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10,000 | $6{,}00{,}000 + 150 \times 10{,}000 = 21{,}00{,}000$ | $180 \times 10{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000$ | **Buy** | 3,00,000 |
| 20,000 | $6{,}00{,}000 + 150 \times 20{,}000 = 36{,}00{,}000$ | $180 \times 20{,}000 = 36{,}00{,}000$ | **Indifferent** | 0 |
| 30,000 | $6{,}00{,}000 + 150 \times 30{,}000 = 51{,}00{,}000$ | $180 \times 30{,}000 = 54{,}00{,}000$ | **Make** | 3,00,000 |

#### Step 3.2.4 — Decision Rule

If expected annual demand $Q > Q^*$ → **MAKE**.
If expected annual demand $Q < Q^*$ → **BUY**.

At exactly $Q = Q^*$ → either is fine; the firm should then consider **qualitative factors** (quality control, supplier reliability, strategic importance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often compute only the *numerical* breakeven and forget to add **qualitative discussion** (e.g., "Even at Q > Q*, we may still buy if the supplier holds a proprietary technology"). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this omission.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Supplier Rating Using PPM and OTD Combined

**Problem Setup:** A buyer tracks two metrics — **Defect Rate (PPM)** and **On-Time Delivery %** — for three suppliers over a quarter. Targets: $PPM_{target} = 500$, $OTD_{target} = 95\%$. Weights: PPM = 60%, OTD = 40%.

Given data:

| **Supplier** | **Actual PPM** | **Actual OTD %** |
|---|---|---|
| X | 400 | 92 |
| Y | 300 | 96 |
| Z | 600 | 98 |

Using the **Supplier Performance Index** formula:

$$SPI = \frac{w_1 \cdot \dfrac{PPM_{target}}{PPM_{actual}} + w_2 \cdot \dfrac{OTD_{actual}}{OTD_{target}}}{100}$$

*Note: For PPM, lower is better, so we invert the ratio.*

**Supplier X:**

$$SPI_X = \frac{60 \cdot \dfrac{500}{400} + 40 \cdot \dfrac{92}{95}}{100} = \frac{60 \cdot 1.25 + 40 \cdot 0.9684}{100}$$

$$SPI_X = \frac{75 + 38.736}{100} = 1.1374$$

**Supplier Y:**

$$SPI_Y = \frac{60 \cdot \dfrac{500}{300} + 40 \cdot \dfrac{96}{95}}{100} = \frac{60 \cdot 1.6667 + 40 \cdot 1.0105}{100}$$

$$SPI_Y = \frac{100 + 40.421}{100} = 1.4042$$

**Supplier Z:**

$$SPI_Z = \frac{60 \cdot \dfrac{500}{600} + 40 \cdot \dfrac{98}{95}}{100} = \frac{60 \cdot 0.8333 + 40 \cdot 1.0316}{100}$$

$$SPI_Z = \frac{50 + 41.263}{100} = 0.9126$$

**Ranking:** Y (1.40) > X (1.14) > Z (0.91). **SPI > 1** indicates above-target performance; **SPI < 1** signals underperformance.

> Supplier Z fails on PPM and requires **Supplier Development** (training, process audits, CAPA — Corrective and Preventive Action).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Strategic Sourcing Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **8-step Strategic Sourcing Process** with explicit decision gates and feedback loops, conforming to KTU Module 2 expectations for process visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Procurement Need] --> step1[Step 1: Profile Spend Category]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Analyze Supply Market]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Develop Sourcing Strategy]
    step3 --> stratChoice{Choose Sourcing Type}
    stratChoice -->|Single| stratSingle[Single Sourcing]
    stratChoice -->|Dual| stratDual[Dual Sourcing]
    stratChoice -->|Multiple| stratMulti[Multiple Sourcing]
    stratChoice -->|Partnership| stratPart[Partnership Sourcing]
    stratSingle --> step4[Step 4: Identify Suppliers]
    stratDual --> step4
    stratMulti --> step4
    stratPart --> step4
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Evaluate and Select Suppliers]
    step5 --> evalMethod{Selection Method}
    evalMethod -->|Qualitative| method1[Site Audits and Certifications]
    evalMethod -->|Quantitative| method2[Weighted Point Model]
    evalMethod -->|Economic| method3[Total Cost of Ownership TCO]
    method1 --> step6[Step 6: Negotiate Contract with SLAs]
    method2 --> step6
    method3 --> step6
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Onboard and Integrate Supplier]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Continuous Monitoring and Rating]
    step8 --> monitorKPIs[Track OTD, PPM, COPQ]
    monitorKPIs --> reviewGate{Quality Acceptable}
    reviewGate -->|Yes| renew[Renew Contract and Deepen Partnership]
    reviewGate -->|No| develop[Supplier Development Program]
    develop --> step8
    renew --> step8
```

### 4.2 Make-or-Buy Decision Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mobStart[Component Requirement Identified] --> q1{Is component strategically critical?}
    q1 -->|Yes| makeBranch[Go to Make Branch]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is volume high enough for fixed cost recovery?}
    q2 -->|No| buyBranch[Go to Buy Branch]
    q2 -->|Yes| q3{Does supplier have proven core competence?}
    q3 -->|Yes| buyBranch
    q3 -->|No| makeBranch
    makeBranch --> calcMake[Compute MC = FC plus VC times Q]
    buyBranch --> calcBuy[Compute BC = P times Q plus TCO overhead]
    calcMake --> crossover[Find Break Even Q star = FC divided by P minus VC]
    calcBuy --> crossover
    crossover --> compare{Q greater than Q star?}
    compare -->|Yes| recMake[Recommendation: MAKE]
    compare -->|No| recBuy[Recommendation: BUY]
    compare -->|Equal| qualCheck[Apply Qualitative Factors]
    qualCheck --> recMake
    qualCheck --> recBuy
```

### 4.3 Supplier Rating and Development Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sup[Supplier] --> delivers[Delivers Material]
    delivers --> inspect[Incoming Inspection]
    inspect --> measure[Measure PPM and OTD]
    measure --> rate[Compute Supplier Performance Index SPI]
    rate --> classify{Classify Supplier Tier}
    classify -->|SPI above 1.2| tierA[Tier A: Preferred Partner]
    classify -->|SPI 0.9 to 1.2| tierB[Tier B: Approved with Watchlist]
    classify -->|SPI below 0.9| tierC[Tier C: Needs Development]
    tierA --> grow[Increase Order Share and Joint Innovation]
    tierB --> monitor[Quarterly Audits and Improvement Targets]
    tierC --> develop[CAPA, Training, Process Reengineering]
    develop --> reaudit[Re-audit after 90 days]
    reaudit --> measure
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sourcing Functions in TQM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ExternalLayer["External Layer: Supply Market"]
        sup1[Supplier Pool A]
        sup2[Supplier Pool B]
        sup3[Supplier Pool C]
    end

    subgraph InternalLayer["Internal Layer: Procurement and Quality"]
        req[User Department Requirement]
        proc[Procurement Function]
        qual[Quality Assurance Function]
    end

    subgraph DecisionLayer["Decision Layer: Strategic Sourcing"]
        strat[Strategy Formulation]
        eval[Supplier Evaluation Engine]
        contract[Contract and SLA Management]
    end

    subgraph OutcomeLayer["Outcome Layer: TQM Goals"]
        qgoal[Quality Improvement]
        cgoal[Cost Optimization]
        dgoal[Delivery Reliability]
        igoal[Innovation Pipeline]
    end

    req --> proc
    proc --> strat
    strat --> eval
    sup1 --> eval
    sup2 --> eval
    sup3 --> eval
    eval --> qual
    qual --> contract
    contract --> sup1
    contract --> sup2
    contract --> sup3
    sup1 --> qgoal
    sup2 --> cgoal
    sup3 --> dgoal
    sup1 --> igoal
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define sourcing in the context of Total Quality Management. List any four supplier selection criteria.*

**Model Answer:**

Sourcing in TQM is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, selecting, and continuously developing suppliers to ensure that purchased goods and services conform to the organization's quality, cost, and delivery objectives. Unlike conventional purchasing, it embeds quality upstream and treats the supplier as an extension of the firm's own quality system.

Four supplier selection criteria are:

1. **Quality capability** — demonstrated by process control, certifications (ISO 9001, IATF 16949), and PPM defect history.
2. **Cost competitiveness** — evaluated through Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), not just unit price.
3. **Delivery reliability** — measured by On-Time Delivery (OTD) percentage.
4. **Technical capability and responsiveness** — capacity to support engineering changes, customization, and innovation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Strategic Sourcing and Tactical Purchasing. Which one aligns better with TQM philosophy?*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Strategic Sourcing** | **Tactical Purchasing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Horizon** | Long-term (3–5 years) | Short-term (transactional) |
| **Focus** | Total value, quality, partnership | Lowest unit price |
| **Supplier Relationship** | Collaborative partnership | Adversarial, arm's-length |
| **Decision Basis** | Data analytics, TCO, risk | Spot price, availability |
| **Supplier Base** | Narrow, qualified | Wide, rotated frequently |
| **TQM Alignment** | **Excellent** | Poor |

**Strategic Sourcing** aligns better with TQM because it embeds continuous improvement, mutual quality goals, and long-term reliability into the supplier-customer interface, whereas tactical purchasing rewards opportunistic price minimization that often compromises quality consistency.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **strategic sourcing process** with its major steps. Why is partnership sourcing considered the most TQM-aligned sourcing strategy? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A firm wants to select a supplier among three vendors (P, Q, R) based on the weighted criteria given below. Compute the weighted score for each supplier and recommend the best one. Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)**

| **Criterion** | **Weight** | **P** | **Q** | **R** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 35 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Cost | 25 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| Delivery | 20 | 8 | 8 | 9 |
| Technical Support | 15 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Communication | 5 | 7 | 9 | 8 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Strategic Sourcing Process (7 Marks):**

The strategic sourcing process is an 8-step methodology used by world-class firms to align their supply base with quality and business strategy:

1. **Profile the Spend Category** — Classify purchases (direct vs. indirect, critical vs. non-critical) using models such as the Kraljic Matrix.
2. **Analyze the Supply Market** — Map potential suppliers, identify risks, assess market competitiveness.
3. **Develop Sourcing Strategy** — Decide between single, dual, multiple, or partnership sourcing based on risk-cost-quality trade-offs.
4. **Identify Potential Suppliers** — Through RFI/RFQ, market surveys, certifications, and supplier audits.
5. **Evaluate and Select Suppliers** — Using weighted scoring models, TCO analysis, and on-site capability audits.
6. **Negotiate Contracts** — Incorporate SLAs (Service Level Agreements), quality clauses, and continuous improvement clauses.
7. **Implement and Integrate** — Onboard suppliers into QMS, ERP, and EDI systems.
8. **Continuous Monitoring and Supplier Development** — Periodic rating, audits, CAPA, and joint improvement projects.

**Why Partnership Sourcing is Most TQM-Aligned (2 of the 7 marks):**

Partnership sourcing establishes a long-term, trust-based, mutually beneficial relationship where both parties share:
- **Quality goals and metrics** (e.g., Six-Sigma targets, PPM reduction).
- **Process data** (joint process capability studies).
- **Improvement investments** (Kaizen, FMEA, Poka-Yoke).
- **Risks and rewards** (gain-sharing, cost transparency).

This institutionalizes Deming's principle of *building long-term relationships based on mutual trust* and is the cornerstone of Toyota's supplier management philosophy.

**(b) Weighted Scoring Calculation (7 Marks):**

Verify weights: $35 + 25 + 20 + 15 + 5 = 100$. Sum is normalized.

**Supplier P:**

$$S_P = (35 \times 9) + (25 \times 7) + (20 \times 8) + (15 \times 6) + (5 \times 7)$$

$$S_P = 315 + 175 + 160 + 90 + 35$$

$$\boxed{S_P = 775}$$

**Supplier Q:**

$$S_Q = (35 \times 8) + (25 \times 9) + (20 \times 8) + (15 \times 8) + (5 \times 9)$$

$$S_Q = 280 + 225 + 160 + 120 + 45$$

$$\boxed{S_Q = 830}$$

**Supplier R:**

$$S_R = (35 \times 7) + (25 \times 8) + (20 \times 9) + (15 \times 7) + (5 \times 8)$$

$$S_R = 245 + 200 + 180 + 105 + 40$$

$$\boxed{S_R = 770}$$

| **Rank** | **Supplier** | **Score** |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Q** | **830** |
| 2 | P | 775 |
| 3 | R | 770 |

**Recommendation:** Award the contract to **Supplier Q** with a backup arrangement involving **Supplier P** (dual sourcing) to mitigate supply risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often *forget to verify that weights sum to 100*. If your weights sum to 92 or 105, your comparison is mathematically invalid, and **2 marks are deducted** even if the final ranking is correct. Always normalize: $w_i^{norm} = w_i / \sum w_i$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** What is the **Make-or-Buy decision**? Explain the qualitative factors that influence this decision with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company requires 50,000 units/year of a component. Buying price is ₹120/unit. In-house manufacture involves a fixed cost of ₹15,00,000 and a variable cost of ₹95/unit. Using break-even analysis, recommend Make or Buy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Make-or-Buy Decision — Qualitative Factors (7 Marks):**

The Make-or-Buy decision is the strategic choice between producing a component or service in-house (Make) versus procuring it from an external supplier (Buy).

**Qualitative Factors Favouring MAKE:**

1. **Strategic secrecy / IP protection** — e.g., Apple insources its A-series chip design to retain proprietary architecture.
2. **Quality control difficulty** — When suppliers cannot consistently meet specifications (e.g., aerospace forgings).
3. **Capacity availability** — Excess in-house capacity reduces marginal cost.
4. **Supply chain risk** — Geopolitical or pandemic disruptions (e.g., semiconductor shortages during 2020–22 pushed firms to in-source).
5. **Lead time reduction** — In-house production offers faster response.
6. **Workforce retention** — Critical skills retained within the firm.

**Qualitative Factors Favouring BUY:**

1. **Supplier has core competence** — e.g., an EMS firm specializes in PCB assembly better than the OEM can.
2. **Capital conservation** — Avoids heavy capital expenditure.
3. **Flexibility** — Easy to switch suppliers as demand fluctuates.
4. **Focus on firm's own core** — Allows management to concentrate on R&D and marketing.
5. **Lower risk of obsolescence** — Supplier absorbs technology refresh risk.

**Examples:**

- *Tata Motors makes* its own engines (Make) but *buys* electronic ECUs from Bosch (Buy).
- *Nike outsources* all manufacturing (Buy) but *keeps* design and marketing in-house (Make).

**(b) Break-Even Make-or-Buy Analysis (7 Marks):**

Given:
- $Q = 50{,}000$ units/year
- $P = ₹120$/unit (Buy price)
- $FC = ₹15{,}00{,}000$ (Make fixed cost)
- $VC = ₹95$/unit (Make variable cost)

**Step 1 — Break-even quantity:**

$$Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{120 - 95} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{25}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = 60{,}000 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with required demand:**

Required $Q = 50{,}000 < Q^* = 60{,}000$

Since demand is **below** break-even, **BUY is cheaper**.

**Step 3 — Verify by total cost comparison:**

Make Cost: $MC = 15{,}00{,}000 + 95 \times 50{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 + 47{,}50{,}000 = ₹62{,}50{,}000$

Buy Cost: $BC = 120 \times 50{,}000 = ₹60{,}00{,}000$

Savings by Buying: $62{,}50{,}000 - 60{,}00{,}000 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$ per year.

**Step 4 — Sensitivity check:**

If demand rises to 70,000 units (> 60,000), Make becomes cheaper:

$MC(70{,}000) = 15{,}00{,}000 + 95 \times 70{,}000 = 81{,}50{,}000$

$BC(70{,}000) = 120 \times 70{,}000 = 84{,}00{,}000$

Savings by Making: $84{,}00{,}000 - 81{,}50{,}000 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$.

**Recommendation:** At current demand of 50,000 units/year, the firm should **BUY**. The decision should be revisited if annual demand grows beyond 60,000 units or if a long-term supply contract lowers $P$ further.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Make-or-Buy):** Two common mistakes cost students 2–3 marks each:
> 1. **Forgetting the "Plus" sign in MC** — writing $MC = VC \cdot Q$ alone, omitting the fixed cost $FC$.
> 2. **Not interpreting the result** — merely computing $Q^*$ without comparing to actual demand and stating a clear recommendation in a sentence. Always **end with a one-line decision**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sourcing (Module 2)**

- **Sourcing ≠ Purchasing** — Sourcing is strategic; purchasing is transactional.
- **TQM-aligned sourcing = Partnership Sourcing** — long-term, trust-based, joint improvement.
- **Single sourcing** offers deep quality control but high risk; **Multiple sourcing** offers price competition but inconsistent quality.
- **Strategic sourcing follows an 8-step process** ending in continuous supplier rating and development.
- **Supplier Selection Criteria (7 Cs):** Capability, Capacity, Commitment, Consistency, Cost, Culture, Communication.
- **Weighted Point Model** is the most frequently tested quantitative tool: $S = \sum w_i \cdot r_i$. Always normalize weights to sum to 100.
- **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** = Purchase + Transport + Quality + Inventory + Downtime + Service.
- **OTD %** and **PPM defects** are the two pillars of supplier rating.
- **SPI (Supplier Performance Index)** > 1 indicates acceptable supplier; < 1 requires development.
- **Make-or-Buy Break-even formula:** $Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$. Compare with actual demand to decide.
- **Supplier Development** involves CAPA, training, audits — applied when SPI < 1 or quality drops below threshold.
- **Always end numerical answers** with a one-line decision statement, e.g., *"Supplier Q is recommended with a weighted score of 830."*
- **Always cite the formula used** — KTU examiners reward showing the formula even before substitution.
- **Qualitative factors matter as much as quantitative ones** in sourcing decisions; ignoring them costs marks.
- **Deming's chain reaction** — better supplier quality → fewer rework/scrap → lower cost → improved productivity → market share growth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
