topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Supplier Selection** is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, and contracting with external sources of supply capable of delivering the right quality of materials, components, or services at the right price, at the right time, and in the right quantity, in alignment with the buying organization's Total Quality Management (TQM) philosophy.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, supplier selection is positioned as a **Strategic Sourcing Decision** under the broader umbrella of **Supply Chain Quality Management**. It involves three interrelated activities:

1. **Identification** of potential vendors capable of meeting the buyer's quality specifications.
2. **Evaluation** of these vendors against a structured set of criteria (quantitative + qualitative).
3. **Selection** of the optimal vendor (or set of vendors) using a transparent, defensible decision-making model.

The Chartered Institute of Procurement & Supply (CIPS) and ISO 9001:2015 both treat supplier selection as a *risk-based, value-driven* decision rather than purely a *lowest-bidder* exercise.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Choosing a Life Partner / Hiring a Chef**
>
> Imagine you are hosting a **massive wedding banquet for 500 guests**. You need a caterer. Would you simply pick the one who quoted the lowest price? **Absolutely not.** You would investigate:
>
> - **Taste & Hygiene (Quality)** — Will the food be safe and delicious?
> - **On-time Delivery** — Will they serve hot food at 8 PM sharp?
> - **Past Reputation** — Have they handled big events before?
> - **Financial Stability** — Will they still be in business next week if I need a refund?
> - **Cost** — Is the quote fair for the value?
> - **Flexibility & Service** — Can they accommodate last-minute vegetarian guests?
>
> Supplier Selection in a manufacturing company works **exactly the same way**. The factory is the wedding venue, the finished product is the feast, and the supplier is the caterer. A poor supplier can **spoil the entire product**, no matter how brilliant your assembly line is. This is the essence of the **GIGO principle** — *Garbage In, Garbage Out* — in supply chains.

---

## 1.3 Why Supplier Selection is a Pillar of TQM

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In TQM, the *customer* is not only the end-user but also the *internal next-process*. Suppliers are the **first link** in this internal customer chain. Deming's 14 Points explicitly emphasize **"Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality"** and instead **"build quality into the product in the first place"** — which can only happen if the *incoming raw material* itself is defect-free.

A well-designed supplier selection framework contributes to TQM in the following ways:

| TQM Principle | Contribution of Supplier Selection |
|---|---|
| Customer Focus | Suppliers become partners in delighting the end customer |
| Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) | Long-term suppliers engage in joint improvement projects |
| Process Approach | Upstream process (supplier) is controlled, reducing downstream defects |
| Evidence-based Decision Making | Vendor rating is data-driven, not intuitive |
| Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships | Strategic partnerships replace adversarial negotiations |

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> - **Vendor / Supplier:** Any external entity providing goods, materials, or services.
> - **Single Sourcing:** Procuring a specific item from only one approved supplier.
> - **Multiple Sourcing:** Procuring the same item from two or more approved suppliers.
> - **Strategic Supplier / Partner Supplier:** A long-term, collaborative vendor involved in co-design and co-engineering.
> - **Preferred Supplier:** A vendor granted repeat business based on a proven track record.
> - **Approved Supplier List (ASL):** A qualified register of suppliers vetted against documented criteria.
> - **Vendor Rating:** A quantitative score expressing a supplier's performance over a period.
> - **Supplier Certification:** A formal third-party or buyer-driven acknowledgment that a supplier's QMS is competent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strategic Framework of Supplier Selection

The supplier selection process, in its professional maturity, follows a **seven-stage sequential framework**. Each stage feeds the next with refined information, culminating in a contract.

### Stage 1 — Identify the Requirement
- Define the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** item, technical specifications, grade, tolerances, and expected annual volume.
- Determine the **criticality class** (e.g., safety-critical, performance-critical, commodity).

### Stage 2 — Market Research & Vendor Identification
- Search industry directories, trade exhibitions, internet portals, and existing referrals.
- Prepare a **long-list** (typically 8–15 vendors).

### Stage 3 — Preliminary Screening
- Eliminate vendors lacking basic legal, financial, or technical credentials.
- Reduce to a **short-list** (typically 3–6 vendors).

### Stage 4 — Request for Quotation (RFQ) / Request for Proposal (RFP)
- Issue formal documents containing drawings, specs, quality plans, delivery schedules, and commercial terms.

### Stage 5 — Supplier Evaluation & Rating
- Apply quantitative models (covered below) to score each vendor.

### Stage 6 — Negotiation & Selection
- Discuss commercial terms, payment cycles, Incoterms, and quality agreements.

### Stage 7 — Award & Onboarding
- Issue Purchase Order, sign Quality Assurance Agreement, and onboard the supplier to the ASL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks students to "Describe the supplier selection procedure in a manufacturing organization." Always present it as a **flowchart with at least seven stages**, then explain each stage in one or two crisp lines.

---

## 2.2 Criteria for Supplier Selection

Modern supplier selection uses a **multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM)** approach. The classical "Seven R's" of purchasing translate into the following measurable criteria:

1. **Quality** (Incoming Quality Level, rejection %, PPM defects)
2. **Cost** (Unit price, total cost of ownership, freight, duties)
3. **Delivery Performance** (On-Time-In-Full — OTIF %)
4. **Capability / Technical Competence** (Machinery, R&D, testing facilities)
5. **Financial Stability** (Credit rating, working capital, profitability)
6. **Reputation & Past Performance** (Reference customers, litigation history)
7. **Location & Logistics** (Distance, lead time, geopolitical risk)
8. **Flexibility / Responsiveness** (Ability to handle design changes, surges)
9. **Communication & IT Integration** (EDI capability, ERP compatibility)
10. **Environmental, Social & Governance (ESG)** (ISO 14001, SA 8000 compliance)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Modern syllabi (post-NEP 2020) emphasize **ESG and Sustainability criteria**. If your syllabus module references "green supply chain," you must explicitly mention ISO 14001, carbon footprint, and supplier CSR audits.

---

## 2.3 Methods of Supplier Selection — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Cheat Sheet (Print & Memorize)**

| # | Method | Formula / Logic | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Categorical Method** | Decision based on a single dominant criterion (e.g., lowest price) | Commodity purchases |
| 2 | **Weighted Point Model** | $\displaystyle S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \times R_i$ | Most common in KTU exams |
| 3 | **Cost-Ratio Method** | $\displaystyle CR = \dfrac{L_C}{L_E}$ where $L_C$ = lowest cost, $L_E$ = expected cost | Cost-dominant decisions |
| 4 | **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)** | Pairwise comparison matrix with Eigenvector weights | Complex multi-criteria decisions |
| 5 | **Linear Programming (LP)** | Minimize $\sum C_j X_j$ subject to constraints | Volume allocation among vendors |
| 6 | **Vendor Rating Index** | $\displaystyle VR = \dfrac{\sum (P_i \times W_i)}{\sum W_i} \times 100$ | Periodic performance reviews |
| 7 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = Price + Acquisition + Operating + Disposal$ | Strategic sourcing |
| 8 | **Conjoint Analysis** | Statistical decomposition of vendor attributes | Market research |
| 9 | **TOPSIS** | Closeness coefficient to ideal solution | MCDM ranking |
| 10 | **Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA)** | Efficiency frontier benchmarking | Comparing supplier productivity |

**Where:**
- $S$ = Weighted score of the supplier
- $W_i$ = Weight of criterion $i$ (such that $\sum W_i = 1$ or $100$)
- $R_i$ = Rating of supplier on criterion $i$ (usually on a $1$–$10$ scale)
- $P_i$ = Performance on criterion $i$
- $VR$ = Vendor Rating (percentage)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** Inside any markdown table, never use the vertical pipe `|` character. If a formula requires absolute value, write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$, not $|x|$.

---

## 2.4 The Weighted Point Method — Worked Logic

The **Weighted Point Method (WPM)** is the *most frequently tested* supplier selection model in KTU examinations. The logic is:

1. Identify the **n** evaluation criteria from the buyer's needs.
2. Assign a **relative weight** $W_i$ to each criterion such that $\sum W_i = 1$ (or $100\%$).
3. Rate each supplier on each criterion on a **common scale** (typically $1$ to $10$, where $10$ = best).
4. Compute the **weighted score** for each supplier as $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \times R_{ij}$.
5. The supplier with the **highest weighted score** is selected (subject to negotiation & commercial approval).

### Real-World Utility
- Used by **automotive OEMs** (Toyota, Tata Motors) for vendor onboarding.
- Embedded in ERP modules (SAP MM, Oracle Procurement Cloud).
- Forms the basis of **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.4** — *Control of externally provided processes, products, and services*.

---

## 2.5 Single Sourcing vs. Multiple Sourcing

| Dimension | Single Sourcing | Multiple Sourcing |
|---|---|---|
| **Supplier Relationship** | Long-term partnership | Transactional / competitive |
| **Volume per Supplier** | High | Low–Medium |
| **Cost** | Lower (economies of scale) | Higher |
| **Risk** | High (supplier failure = line stoppage) | Low (buffer available) |
| **Quality Focus** | Joint quality improvement | Inspection-driven |
| **Innovation** | Co-development possible | Limited incentive |
| **Best Suited For** | Strategic, critical, or sole-source items | Commodities, low-value, high-availability items |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** The 2024 scheme increasingly favours **Single Sourcing with Strategic Partnership** for critical components and **Multiple Sourcing** for commodity items. Justify your choice in exam answers based on the *criticality* and *value* of the item.

---

## 2.6 Supplier Rating — Performance Monitoring

After selection, suppliers are continuously rated. Two common indicators:

**Quality Rejection Ratio (QRR):**

$$QRR = \dfrac{\text{Number of rejected lots}}{\text{Total lots received}} \times 100$$

**On-Time-In-Full (OTIF):**

$$OTIF = \dfrac{\text{Orders delivered on time and in full}}{\text{Total orders placed}} \times 100$$

A composite **Vendor Performance Index (VPI)** may be computed as:

$$VPI = w_1 \cdot QRR + w_2 \cdot OTIF + w_3 \cdot \text{Price Compliance}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Weighted Point Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A KTU-affiliated electronics manufacturing firm, *KeralaCircuits Pvt. Ltd.*, must select a vendor for printed circuit boards (PCBs). After preliminary screening, three vendors — A, B, and C — remain on the short-list. The procurement manager has identified **four key criteria** and assigned weights based on strategic priorities.

### Step 1 — Identify Criteria and Weights

| Criterion | Symbol | Weight $W_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Quality (Incoming defect rate) | $C_1$ | $0.40$ |
| Cost (Competitive pricing) | $C_2$ | $0.25$ |
| Delivery (Lead-time reliability) | $C_3$ | $0.20$ |
| Service (Technical support & responsiveness) | $C_4$ | $0.15$ |
| **Total** | — | **$1.00$** |

### Step 2 — Rate Each Supplier on a 1–10 Scale

| Criterion | Vendor A | Vendor B | Vendor C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality $C_1$ | $8$ | $9$ | $7$ |
| Cost $C_2$ | $7$ | $6$ | $9$ |
| Delivery $C_3$ | $9$ | $8$ | $7$ |
| Service $C_4$ | $6$ | $8$ | $7$ |

### Step 3 — Compute the Weighted Score for Vendor A

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= (W_1 \times R_{1A}) + (W_2 \times R_{2A}) + (W_3 \times R_{3A}) + (W_4 \times R_{4A}) \\
&= (0.40 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6) \\
&= 3.20 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 0.90 \\
&= 7.65
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Weighted Score for Vendor B

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (W_1 \times R_{1B}) + (W_2 \times R_{2B}) + (W_3 \times R_{3B}) + (W_4 \times R_{4B}) \\
&= (0.40 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8) \\
&= 3.60 + 1.50 + 1.60 + 1.20 \\
&= 7.90
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Weighted Score for Vendor C

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (W_1 \times R_{1C}) + (W_2 \times R_{2C}) + (W_3 \times R_{3C}) + (W_4 \times R_{4C}) \\
&= (0.40 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 7) \\
&= 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.05 \\
&= 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Rank and Select

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= 7.90 \;\;>\;\; S_A = 7.65 \;\;>\;\; S_C = 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** **Vendor B** is selected with a weighted score of **$7.90 / 10$**, despite not being the cheapest. The high quality rating ($9$) and reliable delivery ($8$) outweigh the slightly higher cost ($6$), aligning with the firm's quality-first TQM policy.

### Step 7 — Sensitivity Check

A good KTU answer always performs a **sensitivity check**: *What if the weights change?*

If Quality weight drops from $0.40$ to $0.25$ and Cost rises to $0.40$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A^{\text{new}} &= (0.25 \times 8) + (0.40 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6) \\
&= 2.00 + 2.80 + 1.80 + 0.90 = 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B^{\text{new}} &= (0.25 \times 9) + (0.40 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 8) \\
&= 2.25 + 2.40 + 1.60 + 1.20 = 7.45
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C^{\text{new}} &= (0.25 \times 7) + (0.40 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 7) \\
&= 1.75 + 3.60 + 1.40 + 1.05 = 7.80
\end{aligned}
$$

The selection flips to **Vendor C** under a cost-driven policy. This demonstrates that **the choice of weights is itself a strategic decision**.

---

## 3.2 Cost-Ratio Method — Detailed Derivation

The **Cost-Ratio Method** is the simplest cost-dominant model. Two costs are computed:

- $L_C$ = Lowest total cost across all vendors
- $L_E$ = Expected (vendor's quoted) total cost

The **Cost Ratio** is:

$$
CR = \dfrac{L_C}{L_E}
$$

The vendor with $CR$ closest to $1.00$ (i.e., the lowest cost overall) is preferred. A common extension is the **Total Cost Ownership Model**:

$$
TCO = P + A_c + O_c + D_c + Q_c
$$

Where $P$ = purchase price, $A_c$ = acquisition cost (inspection, freight), $O_c$ = operating cost (downtime from poor quality), $D_c$ = disposal cost, $Q_c$ = quality failure cost.

---

## 3.3 Vendor Rating Formula — Complete Derivation

A periodic vendor rating combines three sub-ratings. Suppose we assign:

- **Quality Rating** $Q_r$: percentage of lots accepted
- **Delivery Rating** $D_r$: percentage of orders OTIF
- **Price Rating** $P_r$: percentage conformity to budget

Weights are $w_q + w_d + w_p = 1$.

$$
VR = w_q \cdot Q_r + w_d \cdot D_r + w_p \cdot P_r
$$

If $w_q = 0.5$, $w_d = 0.3$, $w_p = 0.2$, $Q_r = 96$, $D_r = 88$, $P_r = 92$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VR &= (0.5 \times 96) + (0.3 \times 88) + (0.2 \times 92) \\
&= 48.0 + 26.4 + 18.4 \\
&= 92.8
\end{aligned}
$$

A score above $90$ qualifies the vendor as a **Preferred Supplier**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick-Reference Decision Matrix**

| Method | Quantitative | Qualitative | Complexity | Best For | Marks Weightage in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Categorical | ✗ | ✓ | Very Low | Commodities | Low |
| Weighted Point | ✓ | ✓ | Low | General procurement | **Very High** |
| Cost-Ratio | ✓ | ✗ | Low | Cost-dominant buys | Medium |
| AHP | ✓ | ✓ | High | Strategic / multi-criteria | High |
| LP | ✓ | ✗ | Medium | Volume allocation | Medium |
| TCO | ✓ | ✓ | Medium | Capital equipment | Medium |
| TOPSIS | ✓ | ✓ | High | Complex trade-offs | Low–Medium |
| DEA | ✓ | ✗ | Very High | Benchmarking many suppliers | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Supplier Selection Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1: Identify Requirement and BOM Specification] --> A2[Stage 2: Market Research and Long List of Vendors]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3: Preliminary Screening via ASL Pre Qualification]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4: Issue RFQ and RFP with Quality Plans]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5: Apply Weighted Point or AHP Evaluation]
    A5 --> A6{Stage 6: Score above Threshold?}
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Stage 7: Negotiation and Final Commercial Approval]
    A6 -- No --> A8[Reject or Re evaluate Vendor]
    A7 --> A9[Stage 8: Award PO and Sign Quality Assurance Agreement]
    A9 --> A10[Stage 9: Onboard Vendor into ASL]
    A10 --> A11[Stage 10: Continuous Vendor Rating and Development]
    A11 --> A12[Annual Performance Review and Decision]
    A12 -- Pass --> A10
    A12 -- Fail --> A8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The flow is **cyclical**, not linear. Even after onboarding, vendors return to **continuous rating (Stage 10)**. A vendor falling below threshold is sent back to Stage 8 for re-evaluation or rejection. This embodies the **PDCA cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) at the supply-chain level.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Weighted Point Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Input: Criteria List from Buyer Strategy] --> B2[Weight Assignment Module]
    B2 --> B3[Weight Vector W1 to Wn]
    B1 --> B4[Vendor Rating Module]
    B4 --> B5[Rating Matrix R: m vendors x n criteria]
    B3 --> B6[Matrix Multiplication Engine]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7[Weighted Score Vector S1 to Sm]
    B7 --> B8[Sensitivity Analysis Module]
    B8 --> B9[Ranked Vendor List]
    B9 --> B10[Final Selection Output]
    B8 -- What if Weights Change --> B3
```

---

## 4.3 Supplier Segmentation Matrix (Kraljic's Portfolio Model)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Kraljic Supplier Portfolio Matrix
    x-axis Low Supply Risk --> High Supply Risk
    y-axis Low Profit Impact --> High Profit Impact
    quadrant-1 Strategic Items
    quadrant-2 Bottleneck Items
    quadrant-3 Non Critical Items
    quadrant-4 Leverage Items
    Quadrant1: [0.75, 0.85]
    Quadrant2: [0.85, 0.25]
    Quadrant3: [0.15, 0.15]
    Quadrant4: [0.20, 0.75]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Procurement Strategy Mapping:**
> - **Strategic Items** (high risk + high impact) → Long-term partnerships, single sourcing, joint development.
> - **Bottleneck Items** (high risk + low impact) → Volume security, multiple sourcing, safety stock.
> - **Leverage Items** (low risk + high impact) → Competitive bidding, multiple sourcing.
> - **Non-Critical Items** (low risk + low impact) → Standardize, automate, e-procurement.

---

## 4.4 Vendor Rating Computation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Quality Data: Rejection Lots and PPM] --> C2[Quality Sub Rating Qr out of 100]
    C3[Delivery Data: OTIF Percent] --> C4[Delivery Sub Rating Dr out of 100]
    C5[Cost Data: Price Deviation Percent] --> C6[Cost Sub Rating Pr out of 100]
    C2 --> C7[Weighted Aggregation Engine]
    C4 --> C7
    C6 --> C7
    C8[Strategic Weights wq wd wp] --> C7
    C7 --> C9[Composite Vendor Rating VR]
    C9 --> C10{VR above 90?}
    C10 -- Yes --> C11[Promote to Preferred Supplier]
    C10 -- No --> C12[Issue Corrective Action Request]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Model answers are calibrated for **3-mark KTU valuation** — concise, definitional, and crisp.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define supplier selection. List any four criteria used for supplier evaluation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Supplier selection is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, and choosing the most suitable external vendor to provide materials, components, or services, based on a defined set of quality, cost, delivery, and capability criteria, in alignment with the buying organization's TQM objectives.

**Four Criteria (1.5 marks):**
1. **Quality** — Incoming material quality, rejection percentage, capability indices (Cpk).
2. **Cost** — Unit price, total cost of ownership, payment terms.
3. **Delivery** — On-time-in-full (OTIF), lead time, reliability.
4. **Technical Capability / Service** — R&D strength, technical support, after-sales service.

*(Acceptable alternates: Financial stability, Reputation, Location, ESG compliance.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between single sourcing and multiple sourcing. When is single sourcing preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Single Sourcing | Multiple Sourcing |
|---|---|---|
| Number of vendors per item | One | Two or more |
| Relationship style | Long-term, strategic | Transactional, competitive |
| Risk | Higher | Lower |
| Cost | Lower (volume discount) | Higher (split volumes) |

**When Single Sourcing is Preferred (1 mark):**
- When the item is **technically critical** or has **sole-source proprietary design**.
- When volumes are **high** and **economies of scale** matter.
- When the firm wishes to build a **long-term strategic partnership** (e.g., JIT supply).
- When **quality consistency** is paramount and joint process control is established.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven-stage supplier selection procedure** used in a manufacturing organization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Three vendors — P, Q, and R — are being evaluated for the supply of precision-machined shafts. The procurement manager uses the following weights: Quality $0.50$, Cost $0.20$, Delivery $0.20$, Service $0.10$. On a 1–10 scale, the ratings are:

| Criterion | P | Q | R |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Cost | 6 | 7 | 9 |
| Delivery | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| Service | 7 | 6 | 8 |

Apply the **Weighted Point Method** to identify the best vendor. Also conduct a **sensitivity check** if Quality weight is reduced to $0.30$ and Cost weight is increased to $0.40$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks

The seven stages are explained below with **one-line crisp points** for each stage (each line ≈ 1 mark):

1. **Identify the Requirement:** Define the item, specifications, grade, tolerances, annual volume, and criticality. *(1 mark)*
2. **Market Research and Vendor Identification:** Search industry directories, trade shows, referrals, and online portals to prepare a long-list of 8–15 potential vendors. *(1 mark)*
3. **Preliminary Screening:** Verify legal, financial, and technical credentials; eliminate non-compliant vendors to create a short-list of 3–6 vendors. *(1 mark)*
4. **Request for Quotation / Proposal (RFQ/RFP):** Issue detailed documents including drawings, specifications, quality plans, delivery schedules, and commercial terms. *(1 mark)*
5. **Supplier Evaluation and Rating:** Apply structured models such as the Weighted Point Method, AHP, or Cost-Ratio Method to score each vendor. *(1 mark)*
6. **Negotiation and Final Selection:** Discuss commercial terms, Incoterms, payment cycles, and quality assurance agreements. *(1 mark)*
7. **Award and Onboarding:** Issue the Purchase Order, sign the Quality Assurance Agreement, and add the vendor to the Approved Supplier List (ASL) for continuous monitoring. *(1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute Weighted Scores (Original Weights)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_P &= (0.50 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 4.50 + 1.20 + 1.60 + 0.70 = 8.00
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Q &= (0.50 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 4.00 + 1.40 + 1.80 + 0.60 = 7.80
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R &= (0.50 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 3.50 + 1.80 + 1.40 + 0.80 = 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking (Original):** $S_P = 8.00 > S_Q = 7.80 > S_R = 7.50$.  
**Selected: Vendor P.**

**Step 2 — Sensitivity Check (New Weights: Quality $0.30$, Cost $0.40$, Delivery $0.20$, Service $0.10$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_P^{\text{new}} &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.40 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 2.70 + 2.40 + 1.60 + 0.70 = 7.40
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_Q^{\text{new}} &= (0.30 \times 8) + (0.40 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 2.40 + 2.80 + 1.80 + 0.60 = 7.60
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R^{\text{new}} &= (0.30 \times 7) + (0.40 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 8) \\
&= 2.10 + 3.60 + 1.40 + 0.80 = 7.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking (Sensitivity):** $S_R^{\text{new}} = 7.90 > S_Q^{\text{new}} = 7.60 > S_P^{\text{new}} = 7.40$.  
**Selected: Vendor R** under a cost-dominant policy.

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The selection is sensitive to weight assignment. If the firm prioritizes cost over quality, Vendor R wins; if quality dominates, Vendor P wins. This shows that weight selection itself is a **strategic policy decision**, not a mathematical artifact.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **criteria** used in supplier evaluation. Explain how the **Weighted Point Method** is applied in supplier selection with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Vendor Rating** system. A vendor has the following monthly performance data:

- Lots received: $1000$, Lots rejected: $40$
- Orders placed: $200$, Orders delivered on-time-in-full: $170$
- Budgeted price per unit: ₹$100$, Actual price paid per unit: ₹$110$

Using weights Quality = $0.5$, Delivery = $0.3$, Price = $0.2$, compute the **composite vendor rating** and comment on the action to be taken. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks

**Criteria for Supplier Evaluation (4 marks):**

1. **Quality:** Incoming rejection rate, Parts Per Million (PPM) defects, capability indices (Cpk), process audit scores, certifications (ISO 9001, AS 9100).
2. **Cost:** Unit price, freight, duties, payment terms, total cost of ownership, price stability over time.
3. **Delivery Performance:** On-Time-In-Full (OTIF) percentage, lead-time reliability, flexibility in handling schedule changes.
4. **Technical Capability:** R&D strength, manufacturing technology, testing infrastructure, design support.
5. **Financial Stability:** Annual turnover, profitability, credit rating, working capital.
6. **Reputation:** Reference customers, market standing, litigation history.
7. **Service & Responsiveness:** Communication quality, complaint resolution time, after-sales support.
8. **ESG Compliance:** ISO 14001 certification, carbon footprint, social audits, governance practices.

**Weighted Point Method — Application (3 marks):**

*(Use the same procedural explanation as in Section 3.1 above.)*

1. List $n$ criteria and assign weights $W_i$ such that $\sum W_i = 1$.
2. Rate each vendor on each criterion on a uniform scale (e.g., 1–10).
3. Compute $S_j = \sum W_i \times R_{ij}$ for each vendor $j$.
4. Select the vendor with the highest $S_j$.
5. Validate via sensitivity analysis by perturbing weights.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute Quality Rejection Ratio (1 mark)**

$$
QRR = \dfrac{40}{1000} \times 100 = 4\%
$$

Therefore, **Quality Acceptance Ratio** $= 96\%$.

$$
Q_r = 96
$$

**Step 2 — Compute OTIF (1 mark)**

$$
OTIF = \dfrac{170}{200} \times 100 = 85\%
$$

$$
D_r = 85
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Price Compliance Score (1 mark)**

Price deviation $= (110 - 100) = 10\%$. Price compliance $= 90\%$.

$$
P_r = 90
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Composite Vendor Rating (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VR &= (w_q \times Q_r) + (w_d \times D_r) + (w_p \times P_r) \\
&= (0.5 \times 96) + (0.3 \times 85) + (0.2 \times 90) \\
&= 48.0 + 25.5 + 18.0 \\
&= 91.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision and Justification (2 marks)**

Since $VR = 91.5 > 90$, the vendor qualifies as a **Preferred Supplier**. However, specific sub-ratings raise flags:
- **Delivery ($85\%$)** is below the $90\%$ threshold — issue a **Corrective Action Request (CAR)**.
- **Price ($90\%$)** is borderline — negotiate for price stabilization in the next contract.
- **Quality ($96\%$)** is acceptable but should improve to $98\%$+ for strategic sourcing.

**Action:** Retain as approved vendor, issue a **formal improvement plan** for delivery, and re-rate after 3 months.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Supplier Selection Questions:**
>
> 1. **Weight sum error:** Students often forget to ensure $\sum W_i = 1$. If weights are percentages summing to $100$, the weighted score is correct; otherwise, marks are deducted. **Always check the sum first.**
> 2. **Rating scale ambiguity:** If the rating scale is not specified (e.g., 1–5 vs 1–10), state your assumption clearly: *"Assuming a 1–10 rating scale, where 10 = best."*
> 3. **No sensitivity check:** A 7-mark numerical answer that does **not** include a sensitivity or "what-if" analysis loses 1–2 marks in the KTU 2024 scheme.
> 4. **Mixing definitions:** Confusing **supplier selection** (one-time decision) with **supplier rating** (periodic performance tracking). Keep them distinct in your answer.
> 5. **Ignoring ESG:** If the module syllabus mentions "sustainable supply chain," failing to mention ISO 14001 / CSR leads to a deduction under the **CO4** Course Outcome.
> 6. **Forgetting diagram:** A 14-mark question on procedure without a flowchart typically loses 1–2 marks. Always include a labelled diagram or table.
> 7. **Overlooking TQM linkage:** TQM questions always expect a connection to **customer focus, PDCA, and continuous improvement**. End your answer with one line on how supplier selection *feeds back into* the Deming Cycle.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Supplier Selection** = Identifying + Evaluating + Choosing external vendors for quality, cost, delivery fit.
- [x] **Seven Stages:** Requirement → Market Research → Screening → RFQ/RFP → Evaluation → Negotiation → Award & Onboarding.
- [x] **Top Criteria:** Quality, Cost, Delivery, Capability, Financial stability, Reputation, ESG.
- [x] **Weighted Point Formula:** $\displaystyle S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \times R_i$ with $\sum W_i = 1$.
- [x] **Cost-Ratio Formula:** $CR = L_C / L_E$.
- [x] **Vendor Rating:** $VR = w_q Q_r + w_d D_r + w_p P_r$.
- [x] **OTIF Formula:** $\text{OTIF} = \dfrac{\text{Orders on-time-in-full}}{\text{Total orders}} \times 100$.
- [x] **Single vs Multiple Sourcing:** Single = strategic, partnership, low cost, high risk. Multiple = commodity, competitive, high cost, low risk.
- [x] **Kraljic Matrix:** Strategic / Bottleneck / Leverage / Non-Critical — pick sourcing strategy accordingly.
- [x] **Always include:** Sensitivity analysis + Flowchart + TQM linkage in your answer.
- [x] **Common Marks Traps:** Weight-sum errors, missing sensitivity check, no diagram, ignoring ESG.
- [x] **KTU Buzzwords to Use:** TQM, PDCA, ASL (Approved Supplier List), OTIF, PPM, Cpk, ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.4, Deming's Chain Reaction, Jidoka, Keiretsu (Toyota-style supplier cluster).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supplier Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier Rating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supplier Rating** is a systematic, quantitative, and qualitative evaluation methodology employed within the framework of Total Quality Management (TQM) and Supply Chain Management (SCM) to assess, score, and rank the performance capability of vendors/suppliers against a predefined set of criteria. It is a structured decision-making tool that converts subjective supplier judgments into objective, comparable metrics.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme TQM terminology, supplier rating is defined as:

> A continuous performance measurement and comparative assessment process where purchasing organizations periodically evaluate their registered vendors on parameters such as **Quality**, **Delivery**, **Price**, **Service**, and **Responsiveness** to determine their suitability for current and future business.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Supplier Rating is a core sub-topic under *Module 2 – Supplier Management* and directly links to the Course Outcome **CO2: Apply quality management principles in supplier selection and partnership development.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing a restaurant for a special dinner. You do not blindly pick one — you check:
- **Food quality** (taste, hygiene)
- **Delivery speed** (service time)
- **Price** (value for money)
- **Customer service** (staff behavior)
- **Consistency** (same experience every time)

You then *rate* these on a scale (say 1–10) and combine them with weights based on what matters most to you. The restaurant with the highest weighted score wins your business.

**Supplier Rating works exactly the same way** — except the "restaurant" is a vendor supplying raw materials/components, and the "dinner" is the finished product reaching the end customer. The company becomes the "food critic" who keeps scorecards on every supplier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as a critic's rating drives customer footfall, a buyer's supplier rating drives purchasing decisions, contract renewals, and strategic partnerships.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Supplier Rating

The following metrics are universally recognized in KTU-aligned TQM textbooks:

- **Quality Acceptance Rate (QAR)** — measured in **percentage (%)**
- **On-Time Delivery Rate (OTD)** — measured in **percentage (%)**
- **Price Competitiveness Index** — dimensionless ratio
- **Defect Rate** — measured in **parts per million (PPM)**
- **Weighted Overall Score** — dimensionless index (0–100)
- **Performance Index (PI)** — dimensionless ratio

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of Supplier Rating

The core objectives (mapped to KTU CO2) are:

1. **Identify and Reward Superior Suppliers** — incentivize high performance through increased business share.
2. **Detect Weak Suppliers Early** — prevent quality escapes into the customer value stream.
3. **Establish a Data-Driven Vendor Base** — eliminate emotional/subjective purchasing.
4. **Drive Continuous Improvement** — push suppliers toward higher TQM maturity levels.
5. **Reduce Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — balance price with quality and reliability.

### 2.2 Key Performance Criteria (The "Five Pillars")

| # | Criterion | What is Measured | Typical Data Source |
|---|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Quality** | Conformance to specs, defect level | Incoming inspection logs |
| 2 | **Delivery** | On-time in-full (OTIF) performance | Receiving records |
| 3 | **Price** | Cost competitiveness vs. market | Purchase orders, invoices |
| 4 | **Service** | Responsiveness, complaint handling | Communication logs |
| 5 | **Capability** | Technical, financial, capacity strength | Audits, financial reports |

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Rating Process

The supplier rating workflow follows a **closed-loop PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle embedded in TQM:

- **Plan:** Define evaluation criteria, assign weights, set rating period.
- **Do:** Collect performance data continuously (incoming inspection, delivery logs, complaints).
- **Check:** Compute weighted scores, rank suppliers, identify gaps.
- **Act:** Communicate results, reward, develop, or de-list suppliers. Feed learning back into Plan.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles every formula a student must memorize for KTU 2024 examinations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Quality Acceptance Rate | $QAR = \dfrac{\text{Accepted Lots}}{\text{Total Lots Received}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| 2 | Rejection Rate | $RR = 100 - QAR$ | **%** |
| 3 | On-Time Delivery Rate | $OTD = \dfrac{\text{On-Time Deliveries}}{\text{Total Deliveries}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| 4 | Price Rating | $PR = \dfrac{\text{Lowest Quoted Price}}{\text{Supplier's Quoted Price}} \times 100$ | **%** (max = 100) |
| 5 | Defect Rate (PPM) | $PPM = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced}} \times 10^{6}$ | **parts/million** |
| 6 | Weighted Overall Score | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ where $\sum w_i = 1$ | Dimensionless (0–100) |
| 7 | Weighted Average Quality Score (using point system) | $WAQS = \dfrac{\sum w_i s_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Performance Index (combined Q+D) | $PI = \sqrt{QAR \times OTD}$ (Geometric mean) | **%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Always remember that weights $w_i$ are expressed as **decimals summing to 1** (e.g., 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 1.0). If weights are given in percentages, divide by 100 before applying.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Supplier rating is applied in:

- **Automotive Industry** (e.g., ISO/TS 16949 / IATF 16949 mandates supplier performance monitoring).
- **Aerospace** (AS9100 requires supplier scorecards).
- **Electronics Manufacturing** (Six-Sigma driven vendor PPM tracking).
- **Pharmaceuticals** (FDA cGMP demands qualified and monitored suppliers).
- **Construction & Infrastructure** (vendor rating for cement, steel, aggregates).

In essence, **no modern quality-managed industry operates without a formal supplier rating system**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Problem — Weighted Point Method

> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A manufacturing firm evaluates its four suppliers on three criteria: Quality (weight = 0.5), Delivery (weight = 0.3), Price (weight = 0.2). The scorecards (out of 100) are given below. Compute the Weighted Overall Score for each supplier and rank them.

| Supplier | Quality Score ($s_1$) | Delivery Score ($s_2$) | Price Score ($s_3$) |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| A        | 90                     | 85                       | 70                     |
| B        | 80                     | 95                       | 85                     |
| C        | 95                     | 75                       | 60                     |
| D        | 70                     | 80                       | 95                     |

**Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The governing equation for the Weighted Overall Score is:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

With $n = 3$ and weights $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$, we verify the weight constraint:

$$
\sum w_i = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 1 — Compute $S_A$ for Supplier A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_A &= w_1 \cdot s_{1A} + w_2 \cdot s_{2A} + w_3 \cdot s_{3A} \\
S_A &= (0.5)(90) + (0.3)(85) + (0.2)(70) \\
S_A &= 45.0 + 25.5 + 14.0 \\
S_A &= 84.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $S_B$ for Supplier B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_B &= (0.5)(80) + (0.3)(95) + (0.2)(85) \\
S_B &= 40.0 + 28.5 + 17.0 \\
S_B &= 85.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_C$ for Supplier C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= (0.5)(95) + (0.3)(75) + (0.2)(60) \\
S_C &= 47.5 + 22.5 + 12.0 \\
S_C &= 82.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $S_D$ for Supplier D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_D &= (0.5)(70) + (0.3)(80) + (0.2)(95) \\
S_D &= 35.0 + 24.0 + 19.0 \\
S_D &= 78.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rank the Suppliers (Descending Order of $S$):**

$$
S_B = 85.5 \; > \; S_A = 84.5 \; > \; S_C = 82.0 \; > \; S_D = 78.0
$$

**Final Ranking:** $\boxed{B > A > C > D}$ — Supplier **B** is preferred.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Problem — Categorical Rating Method

> **Problem:** Classify suppliers into A, B, C categories using the rule: **A** if score $\geq 90$, **B** if $75 \leq$ score $< 90$, **C** if score $< 75$. From the above results:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Supplier A:} \quad S_A &= 84.5 \;\; \Rightarrow \; \text{Category B} \\
\text{Supplier B:} \quad S_B &= 85.5 \;\; \Rightarrow \; \text{Category B} \\
\text{Supplier C:} \quad S_C &= 82.0 \;\; \Rightarrow \; \text{Category B} \\
\text{Supplier D:} \quad S_D &= 78.0 \;\; \Rightarrow \; \text{Category B}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If no supplier crosses 90, the organization must trigger a **Supplier Development Program** — a hallmark TQM intervention.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Rating Methods

| Method | Description | Best Used When | Limitation |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|------------|
| **Categorical Method** | A, B, C tiers based on cutoffs | Quick screening of large vendor base | Loss of granularity |
| **Weighted Point Method** | Multi-criteria weighted scoring | Strategic sourcing decisions | Subjective weight assignment |
| **Cost-Based Method** | Compare total landed cost | Price-sensitive commodity items | Ignores quality |
| **Performance Index (PI)** | Geometric mean of Q & D | Balanced Q+D evaluation | Equal weight to Q and D |
| **Six-Sigma PPM Method** | Defects per million tracking | High-precision components | Requires robust data systems |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Supplier Rating Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the closed-loop, PDCA-aligned supplier rating process as taught in the KTU 2024 TQM syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Step1: Define Criteria and Weights] --> node2[Step2: Collect Performance Data]
    node2 --> node3[Step3: Compute Weighted Scores]
    node3 --> node4{Step4: Compare Against Threshold}
    node4 -->|Score Above Threshold| node5[Step5A: Reward and Grow Business]
    node4 -->|Score Below Threshold| node6[Step5B: Initiate Supplier Development]
    node6 --> node7[Step5C: Re-audit After 90 Days]
    node7 --> node2
    node5 --> node2
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Criteria Rating Engine

This block diagram maps the interactions between the rating engine, data sources, and decision outputs in a real engineering organization.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Data Layer]
        in1[Incoming Inspection Records]
        in2[Delivery Logs]
        in3[Purchase Orders]
        in4[Audit Reports]
        in5[Customer Complaints]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[Rating Engine]
        e1[Criteria Definition Module]
        e2[Weight Assignment Module]
        e3[Score Calculator]
        e4[Comparator and Ranker]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision Output Layer]
        out1[Vendor Scorecard]
        out2[Approved Vendor List]
        out3[Supplier Development Plan]
        out4[De-list Notification]
    end

    in1 --> e3
    in2 --> e3
    in3 --> e3
    in4 --> e1
    in5 --> e3
    e1 --> e2
    e2 --> e3
    e3 --> e4
    e4 --> out1
    e4 --> out2
    e4 --> out3
    e4 --> out4
```

### 4.3 PDCA Cycle for Continuous Supplier Improvement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Plan: Define rating criteria, weights, frequency] --> p2[Do: Collect vendor data continuously]
    p2 --> p3[Check: Compute scores, identify gaps]
    p3 --> p4[Act: Communicate, reward, develop, or de-list]
    p4 --> p1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Every block is a *process step*. Every arrow is a *data or decision flow*. Notice how the engine has a clean separation between *input*, *logic*, and *output* — this is industry best practice and frequently asked in KTU descriptive answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **Supplier Rating** and list any four criteria used for evaluating suppliers.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Supplier Rating is a systematic evaluation technique used in TQM to assess and rank vendors on their ability to meet the buyer's quality, delivery, cost, and service expectations.

Four key criteria:

1. **Quality** — Conformance to specifications (QAR, defect rate).
2. **Delivery** — On-time in-full performance.
3. **Price** — Cost competitiveness.
4. **Service** — Responsiveness and complaint handling.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for 4 criteria = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State the **Weighted Point Method** formula and explain why weights must sum to unity.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Weighted Overall Score is given by:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight and $s_i$ is the score of the $i$-th criterion.

**Why $\sum w_i = 1$:** Normalization ensures that the final score $S$ lies on the same scale (e.g., 0–100) as the individual scores $s_i$, enabling fair comparison across suppliers. If weights do not sum to 1, the score gets artificially inflated or deflated.

*(1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark for sum-to-unity rationale)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Instruction:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each carries 14 marks divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five major criteria** used for supplier rating under TQM. Discuss how each criterion is measured and sourced. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The five major criteria (mapped to KTU CO2) are:

1. **Quality:** Measured by incoming inspection QAR, defect rate in PPM, and customer-rejection feedback. Data source: Quality inspection logs.

2. **Delivery:** Measured by OTD = (On-time deliveries / Total deliveries) × 100. Data source: Stores and receiving records.

3. **Price:** Measured via Price Rating Index = (Lowest quoted price / Supplier's price) × 100. Data source: Purchase orders and quotations.

4. **Service / Responsiveness:** Measured via lead time to acknowledge complaints, RMA turnaround, and technical support quality. Data source: Service tickets and CRM logs.

5. **Capability / Financial Health:** Measured via audit scores (process capability $Cpk$, certifications like ISO 9001) and annual financial ratios. Data source: Vendor self-assessment, third-party audits.

*(Award 1.2 marks per criterion + 1 mark for conclusion linking to TQM philosophy)*

---

**(b)** A firm has four suppliers with the following performance scores. Quality carries a weight of **0.5**, Delivery **0.3**, and Price **0.2**. Compute the weighted score, rank them, and identify which supplier should be awarded the largest contract. **(7 Marks)**

| Supplier | Quality | Delivery | Price |
|----------|---------|----------|-------|
| P        | 88      | 90       | 75    |
| Q        | 92      | 80       | 85    |
| R        | 85      | 95       | 80    |
| S        | 78      | 88       | 92    |

**Model Answer:**

Apply $S = \sum w_i s_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_P &= (0.5)(88) + (0.3)(90) + (0.2)(75) = 44 + 27 + 15 = 86 \\
S_Q &= (0.5)(92) + (0.3)(80) + (0.2)(85) = 46 + 24 + 17 = 87 \\
S_R &= (0.5)(85) + (0.3)(95) + (0.2)(80) = 42.5 + 28.5 + 16 = 87 \\
S_S &= (0.5)(78) + (0.3)(88) + (0.2)(92) = 39 + 26.4 + 18.4 = 83.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking:** $Q = 87 = R > P = 86 > S = 83.8$.

Since **Q** and **R** are tied, the firm should award the largest contract to **Supplier Q** (slightly higher on quality, which has the highest weight) or split volume 50:50 between Q and R.

*(1 mark for formula, 3 marks for calculation, 1 mark ranking, 2 marks decision justification)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Supplier Rating** and **Supplier Selection**. How are they interlinked in TQM practice? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supplier Selection | Supplier Rating |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Timing** | Pre-contract (one-time) | Post-contract (periodic) |
| **Purpose** | Identify capable vendors | Measure ongoing performance |
| **Inputs** | Capability, capacity, financials | Quality, delivery, price data |
| **Output** | Approved Vendor List (AVL) | Scorecard + rankings |
| **Frequency** | Once per vendor onboarding | Quarterly / Annual |
| **Decision** | Include or exclude | Reward, develop, or de-list |

**Interlinkage in TQM:**

- Selection acts as the **gatekeeper** (Plan phase).
- Rating acts as the **performance monitor** (Do–Check–Act phase).
- Feedback from rating informs future **re-selection** decisions, forming a closed loop.

*(3 marks for table, 4 marks for interlinkage explanation)*

---

**(b)** The Quality Acceptance Rate (QAR) of a supplier over 5 months is: 92%, 95%, 89%, 96%, 94%. Compute the **average QAR** and **Performance Index** if On-Time Delivery (OTD) for the same period averages 88%. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Average QAR:**

$$
\overline{QAR} = \frac{92 + 95 + 89 + 96 + 94}{5} = \frac{466}{5} = 93.2\%
$$

**Step 2 — Performance Index (Geometric Mean):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \sqrt{\overline{QAR} \times OTD} \\
PI &= \sqrt{93.2 \times 88} \\
PI &= \sqrt{8201.6} \\
PI &\approx 90.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A PI of ~90.56% indicates a **good but not excellent** supplier. A TQM-mature organization would set a threshold of ≥95% and trigger supplier development if breached.

*(2 marks average, 3 marks PI computation, 2 marks interpretation)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Supplier Rating Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize weights** — if weights are in %, divide by 100. Marks deducted for dimensional inconsistency.
> 2. **Confusing QAR with Rejection Rate** — these are complements ($QAR + RR = 100\%$).
> 3. **Ignoring the closed-loop nature** — TQM expects you to mention *feedback* and *continuous improvement*, not just one-time scoring.
> 4. **Treating Price Rating inversely** — lower supplier price gives **higher** rating; many students invert this.
> 5. **Not stating units** — QAR, OTD, PPM must always carry **%** or **parts/million** units.
> 6. **Skipping ranking justification** — always conclude *who wins and why*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Supplier Rating** = systematic, criteria-based vendor performance evaluation under TQM.
- **Five Core Criteria:** Quality, Delivery, Price, Service, Capability.
- **Governing Formula:** $S = \sum w_i s_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **QAR** = (Accepted Lots / Total Lots) × 100.
- **OTD** = (On-Time Deliveries / Total Deliveries) × 100.
- **PPM** = (Defects / Units Produced) × $10^6$.
- **Performance Index** = Geometric mean of QAR and OTD.
- **Methods:** Categorical, Weighted Point, Cost-Based, PI, Six-Sigma PPM.
- **TQM Link:** Operates within the **PDCA cycle** for continuous improvement.
- **Strategic Output:** Drives reward, development, or de-listing of vendors.
- **Always** include units, normalize weights, and rank suppliers in the final answer.
- **Closed-loop principle:** Rating feeds back into future selection and development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **Definition → Criteria → Formula → Worked Numerical → Ranking → TQM Interpretation**. This mirrors the valuation key and secures full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relationship Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, **Relationship Development** refers to the strategic, long-term, and mutually beneficial process of building, nurturing, and sustaining collaborative partnerships between an organization and its key stakeholders — primarily **customers**, **suppliers**, **employees**, and **internal departments** — to achieve superior quality outcomes, customer delight, and organizational excellence.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *"Relationship Development in TQM is the systematic creation of trust-based, win-win partnerships across the value chain, where every entity (customer or supplier) is treated as an integral partner rather than a transactional counterparty."*

In simpler terms, it is the **human and process glue** that transforms isolated business transactions into enduring quality partnerships.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **traditional marriage** versus an **arranged transactional marriage**:

- In a **transactional relationship** (old-style buyer-seller), the customer and supplier meet only when there's a need, haggle over price, and disappear once the deal is done. There is **no trust, no shared future, and zero commitment** to each other's growth.
- In a **TQM Relationship Development model** (modern marriage), the customer and supplier **share information openly**, **plan joint futures**, **solve problems together**, and **grow together**. If one struggles, the other helps. If one wins, both celebrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Example:** Apple and Foxconn are not just buyer-supplier — they co-engineer products. Toyota and its tier-1 suppliers share production schedules, quality metrics, and cost data in real time. **That is Relationship Development.**

## 1.3 Core Pillars of Relationship Development in TQM

| Pillar | Meaning | KTU-Critical Tag |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** | Managing all customer interactions to drive loyalty | High-Yield |
| **Supplier Relationship Management (SRM)** | Strategic, long-term alliance with suppliers | High-Yield |
| **Internal Customer-Supplier Chain** | Treating every internal department as a customer | Frequently Asked |
| **Customer-Supplier Partnership** | Co-development, joint planning, shared vision | Board Favorite |
| **Key Customer / Key Supplier Management** | Differentiated focus on vital partners | Theory Question |

## 1.4 Physical & Strategic Constants / Metrics

The following metrics are **bold-marked as KTU board essentials**:

- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** = (Customers at End − New Customers) / Customers at Start × **100%**
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** = Average Purchase × Frequency × Customer Lifespan
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** ranges from **−100 to +100**
- **Supplier Defect Rate (PPM)** target in TQM = **< 10 PPM** (Six Sigma equivalent)
- **Relationship Duration** (average years of partnership) is the qualitative benchmark for loyalty

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 2 explicitly tests the transition from *transactional* to *relational* mindset, the **internal customer concept**, and **customer-supplier partnerships** as the cornerstone of TQM philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transactional vs. Relational Paradigm

TQM rejects the **adversarial, win-lose** model and replaces it with a **win-win partnership** model.

### 2.1.1 Key Differences (Board Table Favorite)

| Parameter | Transactional Approach | Relationship Development (TQM) |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Short-term, single deal | Long-term, lifetime value |
| Communication | Limited, formal | Open, frequent, multi-level |
| Trust Level | Low (contract-driven) | High (commitment-driven) |
| Information Sharing | Minimal | Full transparency (forecasts, costs) |
| Conflict Resolution | Litigation / legal | Joint problem-solving |
| Quality Focus | Incoming inspection | Prevention at source |
| Cost Focus | Purchase price only | Total cost of ownership (TCO) |
| Number of Suppliers | Many (parallel sourcing) | Few, strategic partners |
| Mindset | Adversarial ("us vs them") | Collaborative ("we're one team") |

## 2.2 The Internal Customer–Supplier Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Every person or department that receives the output of another person or department, **within the same organization**, is an *internal customer*. The provider is the *internal supplier*.

### 2.2.1 Logic Flow of Internal Customer Concept

1. **Identify** every handoff point in the process.
2. **Treat the next process** as a valued customer with documented requirements.
3. **Negotiate internal service-level agreements (SLAs)** between departments.
4. **Measure** internal satisfaction periodically.
5. **Continuously improve** the internal handoff quality.

**Why does this matter?** Because **80% of quality problems originate internally** (according to Ishikawa). If internal customers are not satisfied, the external customer will never be satisfied.

## 2.3 Customer-Supplier Partnership Model

The partnership model has **four cardinal levels** — frequently asked in KTU exams:

### Level 1: Adversarial Relationship
- Many suppliers, lowest-bidder wins, no loyalty.

### Level 2: Transactional Cooperation
- Repeat orders, basic quality compliance, contract-bound.

### Level 3: Coordinated Partnership
- Shared forecasting, joint quality programs, **Supplier Quality Assurance (SQA)**.

### Level 4: Strategic Alliance (KTU Gold Standard)
- **Single-source partnership**, joint product development, integrated information systems, shared destiny.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A 14-mark question typically asks you to **describe the customer-supplier partnership model and explain how TQM transforms buyer-supplier relations.** Use the 4-level ladder as your answer backbone.

## 2.4 Customer Relationship Management (CRM) — Pillars

1. **Identification** — Who is the customer?
2. **Differentiation** — Which customers are most valuable? (Pareto: 80/20 rule)
3. **Interaction** — How do we engage them?
4. **Customization** — Tailor offerings per customer segment.
5. **Retention & Loyalty** — Keep them for life.

### 2.4.1 Customer Loyalty Hierarchy (Kotler / TQM)

The progression ladder:
1. **Suspect** → 2. **Prospect** → 3. **Customer** → 4. **Client** → 5. **Supporter** → 6. **Advocate** → 7. **Partner**

At every rung, the relationship deepens. The TQM goal is to push customers from "Customer" to "Partner."

## 2.5 Supplier Relationship Management (SRM) — Pillars

- **Supplier Selection** based on TCO, not just price.
- **Supplier Certification** (e.g., ISO 9001, QS-9000).
- **Supplier Development** through training, audits, kaizen support.
- **Supplier Recognition** (e.g., Toyota's "Superior Supplier Award").
- **Long-term Contracts** with shared risk/reward.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside markdown tables to avoid syntax breakage.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer Retention Rate (CRR) | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100$ | % | Loyalty measurement |
| Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) | $\text{CLV} = A \times F \times L$ | Currency (₹ / $) | Strategic CRM |
| Net Promoter Score (NPS) | $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | Score $\vert -100 \text{ to } +100$ | Loyalty benchmarking |
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{U}{T} \times 100$ | % | Supplier quality |
| Defect Rate (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N}$ | PPM | Six Sigma / TQM |
| Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Marketing Spend}}{\text{New Customers}}$ | Currency | CRM efficiency |
| CLV : CAC Ratio | $\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{CLV}}{\text{CAC}}$ | Pure number ($\geq 3$ ideal) | Sustainability check |

**Where:**
- $E$ = Customers at end of period, $N$ = New customers acquired, $S$ = Customers at start
- $A$ = Avg purchase value, $F$ = Purchase frequency, $L$ = Customer lifespan (years)
- $P$ = Promoters (9–10 NPS), $D$ = Detractors (0–6 NPS)
- $U$ = Units passed first time, $T$ = Total units produced
- $D$ = Defects observed, $N$ = Total opportunities

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Relationship Development is the **backbone of modern supply chain management**. In industries like **automotive (Toyota Production System)**, **aerospace (Boeing–Airbus tier-1 network)**, and **electronics (Apple–Foxconn)**, the depth of customer-supplier relationships directly determines **defect rates, lead times, innovation speed, and ultimately profitability**. TQM-certified firms treat suppliers as **extended family**, not interchangeable vendors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Step-by-Step: Customer Retention Rate (CRR)

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU Module 2. Below is the complete, exam-ready derivation.

### Problem Setup

A company started the year with **1,000 customers**. During the year, it acquired **300 new customers**, and ended the year with **1,150 customers**. Calculate the **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)**.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 1000 \quad \text{(Starting customers)} \\
N &= 300 \quad \text{(New customers acquired)} \\
E &= 1150 \quad \text{(Ending customers)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Recall the Standard Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRR} = \frac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this formula?** Because the *ending* customer count includes both *retained old* customers and *new* customers. To isolate the *retained* customers, we must subtract the new acquisitions from the total ending pool. Then we compare this retained pool against the starting pool.

### Step 3: Substitute the Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRR} &= \frac{(1150 - 300)}{1000} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Numerator

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRR} &= \frac{850}{1000} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, **850 represents** the number of original customers who remained loyal throughout the year. The other 150 from the original 1000 either switched to competitors or churned out.

### Step 5: Compute the Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRR} &= 0.85 \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Final Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CRR} &= 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The company retained **85% of its original customer base**, which is a strong indicator of relationship quality. Industry benchmarks in TQM-oriented firms target **CRR ≥ 90%**.

---

## 3.2 Derivative Step-by-Step: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)

### Problem Setup

An online SaaS company has the following data:
- Average purchase per transaction = **₹2,000**
- Average purchases per customer per year = **4**
- Average customer lifespan = **5 years**

Calculate the **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**.

### Step 1: Identify Variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= ₹2000 \quad \text{(Avg transaction value)} \\
F &= 4 \quad \text{(Frequency per year)} \\
L &= 5 \quad \text{(Lifespan in years)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Standard CLV Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} = A \times F \times L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical reasoning:** A customer is worth the *sum of all* future revenues they will generate. If they buy ₹2,000 worth, four times a year, for five years, the cumulative revenue is the product of these three factors.

### Step 3: Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} = 2000 \times 4 \times 5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: First Multiplication

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} = 8000 \times 5
\end{aligned}
$$

This ₹8,000 represents the **annual revenue per loyal customer**.

### Step 5: Final Multiplication

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CLV} &= ₹40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every customer acquired is worth **₹40,000 over their lifetime**. This justifies spending up to ₹13,333 in Customer Acquisition Cost (since the ideal CLV : CAC ratio is ≥ 3).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Relationship Strategy

| Customer Segment | Strategic Priority | Investment Level | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key / Strategic Customers** (High CLV, High Volume) | Co-develop products, joint R&D | Maximum (Top 20%) | Apple ↔ Foxconn |
| **Loyal Regular Customers** | Retention via loyalty programs | High | Amazon Prime members |
| **New Prospects** | Conversion via trust-building | Moderate | Free trials, demos |
| **Low-Value Customers** | Automation, self-service | Minimal | ATM users, chatbots |
| **Defectors / Churned** | Win-back campaigns | Selective | Re-engagement emails |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pearl:** KTU often asks: *"Differentiate between transactional and relationship marketing."* Always anchor your answer around the **5 parameters of difference** listed in Section 2.1.1 and use the **CLV / CRR metrics** as evidence of why relationships matter.

## 3.4 Analytical Check: Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule) in CRM

In most businesses, **80% of revenue comes from 20% of customers**. TQM emphasizes **differentiated relationship depth** — the top 20% deserve *strategic alliance*, the next 30% get *coordinated partnership*, the bottom 50% get *transactional* treatment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Strategic Depth} = f(\text{CLV}, \text{Volume}, \text{Strategic Fit})
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $f$ is a weighted scoring function. This reinforces why relationship development is **not uniform** — it is *segmented and prioritized*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Customer Loyalty Progression Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["SUSPECT - Unknown lead"]
    node2["PROSPECT - Interested lead"]
    node3["CUSTOMER - First purchase"]
    node4["CLIENT - Repeat buyer"]
    node5["SUPPORTER - Recommends brand"]
    node6["ADVOCATE - Public champion"]
    node7["PARTNER - Strategic ally"]
    node8["TQM GOAL Push toward deeper loyalty"]
    node1 -->|"Awareness campaign"| node2
    node2 -->|"First transaction"| node3
    node3 -->|"Satisfaction builds"| node4
    node4 -->|"Trust deepens"| node5
    node5 -->|"Loyalty program"| node6
    node6 -->|"Co-creation invite"| node7
    node7 -.->|"Continuous engagement"| node8
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram visually maps the customer journey from a cold suspect to a strategic partner. The dashed arrow at the end emphasizes that the journey is **cyclic and continuous** — TQM never declares the relationship "finished."

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Customer-Supplier Partnership Maturity Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lvl1["LEVEL 1 ADVERSARIAL Lowest bidder wins"]
    lvl2["LEVEL 2 TRANSACTIONAL Repeat orders only"]
    lvl3["LEVEL 3 COORDINATED Joint quality programs"]
    lvl4["LEVEL 4 STRATEGIC ALLIANCE Co-engineering and shared destiny"]
    lvl1 -->|"Trust builds"| lvl2
    lvl2 -->|"Information shared"| lvl3
    lvl3 -->|"Joint R and D"| lvl4
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **4-level maturity model** for buyer-supplier relationships. KTU examiners love this diagram because it captures the entire philosophy of TQM's relationship transformation in one frame.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Internal Customer-Supplier Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    dept1["SALES DEPARTMENT External customer interface"]
    dept2["ORDER PROCESSING Receives orders"]
    dept3["PRODUCTION Manufactures goods"]
    dept4["QUALITY ASSURANCE Inspects output"]
    dept5["WAREHOUSE Stores finished goods"]
    dept6["DISPATCH Delivers to external customer"]
    dept1 -->|"Internal order handoff"| dept2
    dept2 -->|"Production plan"| dept3
    dept3 -->|"Finished units"| dept4
    dept4 -->|"QC passed goods"| dept5
    dept5 -->|"Pick and pack"| dept6
    dept6 -.->|"External customer receives"| Customer["EXTERNAL CUSTOMER End user"]
```

**Interpretation:** Each department is both a **supplier** (to the next) and a **customer** (from the previous). The external customer at the end receives only the **cumulative quality** of every internal handoff. One weak link breaks the chain.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Relationship Development

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXTERNAL["EXTERNAL STAKEHOLDERS"]
        C["CUSTOMERS"]
        S["SUPPLIERS"]
    end
    subgraph INTERNAL["INTERNAL DEPARTMENTS"]
        MKT["MARKETING"]
        PROD["PRODUCTION"]
        QA["QUALITY ASSURANCE"]
        HR["HUMAN RESOURCES"]
        FIN["FINANCE"]
    end
    subgraph FRAMEWORK["TQM RELATIONSHIP FRAMEWORK"]
        CRM["CUSTOMER RELATIONSHIP MANAGEMENT"]
        SRM["SUPPLIER RELATIONSHIP MANAGEMENT"]
        ICR["INTERNAL CUSTOMER RELATIONS"]
        KP["KEY PARTNERSHIP DEVELOPMENT"]
    end
    subgraph OUTCOMES["STRATEGIC OUTCOMES"]
        LOY["CUSTOMER LOYALTY"]
        QTY["SUPPLIER QUALITY"]
        EXC["OPERATIONAL EXCELLENCE"]
        WIN["MUTUAL WIN WIN"]
    end
    C --> CRM
    S --> SRM
    MKT --> CRM
    PROD --> ICR
    QA --> ICR
    HR --> ICR
    FIN --> ICR
    CRM --> LOY
    SRM --> QTY
    ICR --> EXC
    KP --> WIN
    LOY --> EXC
    QTY --> EXC
    EXC --> WIN
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **master architecture diagram** of Relationship Development. The left column shows the **stakeholders** (customers, suppliers, internal departments), the middle column is the **framework** (CRM, SRM, ICR, KP), and the right column is the **outcomes** (loyalty, quality, excellence, win-win). Every arrow is a documented interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `lvl1`, `dept1`, `C`, `MKT`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone node names. All labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the "Internal Customer" concept in TQM.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 3 Marks | CO2 / Bloom Level: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**
Within an organization, every person or department that receives the output of another person or department is called an **internal customer**. The provider of that output is the **internal supplier**. For example, the Production department is an internal customer of the Purchase department. Recognizing internal customers promotes quality at every handoff, eliminates the "not my problem" attitude, and ensures that the **external customer receives only fully built quality**. This concept is foundational to TQM because it transforms every internal handoff into a service transaction that must be measured and improved.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between transactional and relationship marketing.
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 | 3 Marks | CO2 / Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Transactional | Relationship (TQM) |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Single transaction | Long-term partnership |
| Time Horizon | Short-term | Long-term / lifetime |
| Communication | Minimal | Continuous, open |
| Trust | Contract-driven | Commitment-driven |
| Goal | Maximize single profit | Maximize customer lifetime value |

TQM strongly favors the **relationship approach** because sustainable quality requires sustained cooperation between buyer and supplier.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks**, split into **Part (a) – 7 marks** and **Part (b) – 7 marks**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B in full.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Customer-Supplier Partnership in TQM

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the evolution of customer-supplier relationships from adversarial to strategic alliance. Use the four-level maturity model.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 7 Marks | CO2 / Bloom Level: Analyze]**

**Model Solution:**

The customer-supplier relationship in modern TQM is **not static**; it evolves through four distinct levels based on the depth of trust, integration, and shared vision.

**Level 1 — Adversarial Relationship:**
In this primitive stage, the buyer treats suppliers as interchangeable commodities. Multiple suppliers are pitted against each other to extract the lowest possible price. There is **no information sharing, no loyalty, and constant suspicion** of supplier opportunism. Quality is enforced only through incoming inspection, and defects are returned at the supplier's cost. This model produces short-term savings but creates long-term quality collapse.

**Level 2 — Transactional Cooperation:**
Here, the buyer maintains a short list of approved suppliers and places repeat orders. Quality compliance is enforced through specifications and audits. Communication remains formal and contractual. While more stable than Level 1, this is still a **price-driven, arms-length relationship** with limited trust.

**Level 3 — Coordinated Partnership:**
The buyer and supplier begin **sharing forecasts, joint quality data, and process improvement initiatives**. The supplier is brought into early product design discussions (Supplier-Involved Design). Certifications like **ISO 9001 and Six Sigma at supplier end** become mandatory. The relationship is **stable, mutually beneficial, and quality-focused**.

**Level 4 — Strategic Alliance:**
This is the TQM pinnacle. A single strategic partner is selected for life. **Joint product development, shared cost-reduction targets, integrated IT systems, and even equity partnerships** characterize this level. Both firms share destiny — when one grows, the other grows. Examples include **Toyota and Denso**, **Boeing and Pratt & Whitney**.

**Conclusion:** TQM drives relationships from **Level 1 to Level 4** because deeper integration yields higher quality, faster innovation, lower total cost, and superior customer satisfaction.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of Customer Relationship Management (CRM) in achieving TQM objectives.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 7 Marks | CO2 / Bloom Level: Apply]**

**Model Solution:**

CRM is the **operational arm of customer-focused TQM**. It plays the following critical roles:

1. **Customer Identification and Segmentation:** CRM enables the firm to differentiate customers by CLV, allowing differentiated service quality — a core TQM principle.

2. **Voice of Customer (VoC) Capture:** Through CRM tools (surveys, NPS, complaint logs), customer feedback flows back into product design and process improvement (PDCA cycle).

3. **Customer Retention:** By tracking CRR and applying loyalty programs, CRM reduces churn — directly contributing to the TQM goal of long-term customer satisfaction over short-term sales.

4. **Service Recovery:** CRM systems enable rapid complaint resolution. TQM research shows that a **well-handled complaint actually increases loyalty** beyond a non-complaining customer.

5. **Cross-functional Coordination:** CRM data breaks silos between Marketing, Sales, Service, and Production — making the *internal customer chain* visible and manageable.

6. **Continuous Improvement Feedback Loop:** CRM analytics drive the **Kaizen cycle** by identifying recurring pain points and quantifying improvement opportunities.

In essence, **CRM converts customer data into customer delight**, which is the ultimate TQM outcome.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Internal Customers & Supplier Development

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the concept of internal customer-supplier chain. Why is it critical to TQM success?**
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 | 7 Marks | CO2 / Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The internal customer-supplier chain refers to the **sequence of internal handoffs** within an organization where each department or individual receives inputs from a predecessor (internal supplier) and provides outputs to a successor (internal customer). For example: *Sales → Order Processing → Production → Quality → Warehouse → Dispatch → External Customer*.

**Why it is critical to TQM:**

1. **External quality is the sum of internal quality.** Ishikawa's research confirmed that the majority of quality defects originate *inside* the organization, often at the handoff points. If each handoff is defective, the final external delivery is defective.

2. **Eliminates the "silo mentality."** When departments recognize each other as customers, they stop blaming each other and start serving each other — a fundamental TQM cultural shift.

3. **Enables internal SLAs.** Departments can negotiate service-level agreements (e.g., "Production will deliver batches within 24 hours of QA approval"), making quality measurable.

4. **Drives continuous improvement at every node.** Each handoff becomes a candidate for Kaizen, mistake-proofing (Poka-Yoke), and process re-engineering.

5. **Improves employee engagement.** Employees who feel their internal customers value their work feel pride and ownership — a Deming-era insight now confirmed by modern engagement research.

**Conclusion:** The internal chain is the **hidden factory within the visible factory**. TQM succeeds only when this chain is healthy, transparent, and continuously improving.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the key components of Supplier Relationship Management (SRM) in a TQM organization.**
**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 | 7 Marks | CO2 / Bloom Level: Apply]**

**Model Solution:**

SRM is the **supplier-side counterpart of CRM** and is essential because purchased materials and services often constitute **60–80% of a manufacturer's total cost and quality exposure**.

**Key Components of SRM:**

1. **Supplier Selection and Certification:** Suppliers are evaluated on TCO (Total Cost of Ownership), not just unit price. Certifications like **ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and Six Sigma** are mandatory screening criteria.

2. **Supplier Development:** The buyer actively invests in upgrading supplier capabilities through training, audits, and kaizen workshops. (Toyota famously sends its engineers *into* supplier factories.)

3. **Long-Term Contracts:** SRM replaces short-term bidding with multi-year agreements that provide the supplier with **volume stability** and the buyer with **supply security**.
