4. **Joint Quality Planning:** Suppliers participate in **Advanced Product Quality Planning (APQP)**, Failure Mode Analysis (FMEA), and design reviews.

5. **Information Sharing:** Real-time sharing of demand forecasts, inventory levels, and capacity data via EDI or portal systems.

6. **Performance Measurement:** SRM tracks metrics like **PPM defect rate, on-time delivery, cost reduction contribution**, and awards top performers publicly.

7. **Recognition and Reward:** Annual supplier awards (e.g., "Supplier of the Year") reinforce the partnership mindset and motivate continuous improvement.

**Conclusion:** Effective SRM turns suppliers into **strategic partners**, transforming the supply chain into a quality-assured, cost-optimized, innovation-driven ecosystem.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "customer satisfaction" with "customer delight."** Satisfaction = meeting expectations; Delight = exceeding them. KTU examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 2. **Do NOT use the vertical bar `\vert` symbol carelessly** in your written answers for absolute values — write it as "mod" or use full English phrasing ("magnitude of").
> 3. **Always mention the 4-level maturity ladder** when answering buyer-supplier questions — examiners award 2–3 marks purely for this classification.
> 4. **Distinguish CRM from SRM clearly** — CRM is customer-side; SRM is supplier-side. Conflating them costs marks.
> 5. **Numerical questions** (CRR, CLV) must show **all six substitution and simplification steps** — partial marks are awarded per step, not for the final number alone.
> 6. **Avoid generic statements** like "suppliers are important." Instead, say "suppliers contribute 60–80% of total manufacturing cost, making them critical stakeholders in TQM cost-and-quality optimization."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Relationship Development** is the process of building long-term, trust-based partnerships with customers, suppliers, and internal departments.
- It shifts the paradigm from **transactional (win-lose, short-term)** to **relational (win-win, long-term)**.
- **Internal Customer** = anyone who receives your output within the organization; **Internal Supplier** = anyone who provides your input.
- The **4-Level Customer-Supplier Maturity Model**: Adversarial → Transactional → Coordinated → Strategic Alliance.
- **CRM** focuses on the customer side (VoC, retention, loyalty, NPS).
- **SRM** focuses on the supplier side (selection, development, certification, joint planning).
- **Key Metrics:** CRR (≥ 90% ideal), CLV (maximize), NPS (−100 to +100), DPMO (< 10 PPM Six Sigma).
- **Customer Loyalty Ladder:** Suspect → Prospect → Customer → Client → Supporter → Advocate → Partner.
- **Pareto Principle:** 80% of revenue from 20% of customers — so relationships must be *differentiated*, not uniform.
- **TQM Outcome:** Relationship Development converts data into **trust**, trust into **loyalty**, and loyalty into **sustainable excellence**.
- **Board Triggers:** If a question mentions "TQM," "partnership," "long-term," or "trust," answer through the lens of **Relationship Development**.
- **Remember the 5 Differences Table** (Section 2.1.1) — it alone can fetch 4–5 marks in any related question.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Topic: Relationship Development.** This topic frequently appears as a **14-mark Part B question** in KTU ESE. Master the 4-level maturity model, the CRM-SRM distinction, and the CRR/CLV numerical pattern to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Measures - Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Measures - Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Measurement** is the systematic process of collecting, analyzing, and reporting quantitative data regarding the efficiency, effectiveness, and quality of business processes, products, services, and organizational outcomes against pre-established strategic goals, benchmarks, and standards.

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, performance measurement is the **operational nervous system** of any quality initiative. Without measurement, improvement is merely conjecture. As the famous quality axiom states:

> *"If you can't measure it, you can't manage it. If you can't manage it, you can't improve it."* — **Peter Drucker**

Performance measures are typically classified into **two broad categories**:

| Category | Definition | Focus |
|----------|------------|-------|
| **Performance Measures** | Indicators of actual results achieved (Outcomes) | End-results |
| **Conformance Measures** | Indicators of conformance to standards (Process capability) | Process adherence |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: In the NEP 2020 aligned syllabus, performance measures form the empirical backbone of **CO (Course Outcome) attainment**. Without proper performance metrics, the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle collapses at the "Check" phase.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Dashboard

Imagine you are driving a car on a long highway. How do you know:
- How fast you are going? → **Speedometer** (a performance measure of velocity)
- How much fuel remains? → **Fuel Gauge** (a measure of resource availability)
- Whether the engine is overheating? → **Temperature Gauge** (a measure of process health)
- Whether you need an oil change? → **Service Indicator** (a measure of preventive maintenance)

Similarly, in an organization, **Performance Measures** act as the **dashboard of the business**. Just as a driver cannot navigate safely without gauges, a Quality Manager cannot steer an organization toward excellence without well-designed performance indicators.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Tip**: Think of performance measures as the **report card** of a business process. The report card tells the student (process) where it stands and what subjects (KPIs) need attention.

### 1.3 The Five Standard Performance Categories

In TQM practice (originating from the **Gopal K. Kanji Quality Pyramid** and **JUSE** frameworks), organizational performance is measured across **five universal dimensions**:

1. **Quality** — Degree of conformance to specifications and customer expectations
2. **Cost** — Economic efficiency of resource utilization
3. **Delivery** — Reliability and speed of order fulfillment
4. **Safety** — Freedom from accidents and hazards
5. **Morale** — Employee engagement, satisfaction, and well-being

These are often abbreviated in Japanese quality literature as the **"QCDSM"** framework, which forms the foundation of modern **Balanced Scorecard** methodology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor**: The module explicitly emphasizes that performance measures in TQM are not limited to financial metrics. They encompass **operational, customer, employee, and learning perspectives** — the four pillars of the **Kaplan-Norton Balanced Scorecard**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of Effective Performance Measures

A performance measure is considered **"good"** only if it satisfies the **SMART** criteria (a high-frequency KTU exam keyword):

| Letter | Criterion | Meaning | Engineering Example |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| **S** | Specific | Clearly defined, unambiguous | "Defect rate per 1000 units" |
| **M** | Measurable | Quantifiable in consistent units | Number of customer complaints/month |
| **A** | Achievable | Realistic and attainable | Target: <2% rework (not 0.0001%) |
| **R** | Relevant | Linked to strategic objectives | On-time delivery → Customer retention |
| **T** | Time-bound | Has a clear time horizon | Monthly, quarterly, annually |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SMART?** A measure that is not SMART becomes a "vanity metric" — it looks impressive on dashboards but provides no actionable insight. KTU examiners frequently award marks for correctly identifying why a non-SMART measure fails.

### 2.2 Classification of Performance Measures (Hierarchical View)

Performance measures operate at **three hierarchical levels** in any organization:

1. **Strategic (Enterprise) Level** — High-level KPIs aligned with organizational vision
   - Example: Return on Quality (ROQ), Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)
2. **Tactical (Process) Level** — Process-level metrics for departmental management
   - Example: Cycle Time, Throughput, Process Capability (Cpk)
3. **Operational (Activity) Level** — Day-to-day shop-floor or workstation metrics
   - Example: Defects per unit, machine downtime, first-pass yield

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **board-exam essentials**. Memorize the formula, its variables, and units. KTU examiners frequently test the ability to **substitute values and compute numeric answers**.

| # | Metric | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|--------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Defect Rate** | $D = \dfrac{N_d}{N_u} \times 100$ | $N_d$ = Number of defective units; $N_u$ = Total units produced; Result in **%** |
| 2 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$ | $N_{good}$ = Units passing without rework; $N_{total}$ = Total units; Result in **fraction** |
| 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | $FPY_i$ = First pass yield of process $i$; $n$ = Number of processes; Result in **fraction** |
| 4 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{N_d}{N_u \times O} \times 10^{6}$ | $N_d$ = Defects found; $N_u$ = Units produced; $O$ = Opportunities per unit |
| 5 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | $A$ = Availability; $P$ = Performance; $Q$ = Quality; Result in **%** |
| 6 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = P + A$ | $P$ = Prevention cost; $A$ = Appraisal cost; (or total of P+A+I+F) |
| 7 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $OTD = \dfrac{N_{on-time}}{N_{total-orders}} \times 100$ | Result in **%** |
| 8 | **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum S_i}{N \times S_{max}} \times 100$ | $S_i$ = Score from customer $i$; $N$ = Number of customers; $S_{max}$ = Maximum possible score |
| 9 | **Sigma Level (Yield Conversion)** | $Yield \approx \Phi(\sigma \times \sqrt{2})$ | $\Phi$ = Standard normal CDF; $\sigma$ = Process sigma |
| 10 | **Return on Quality (ROQ)** | $ROQ = \dfrac{Net\ Quality\ Profit}{Total\ Quality\ Cost}$ | Dimensionless ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall**: In the **RTY** formula, students frequently **add** the yields instead of **multiplying** them. Always remember: each process step **multiplies** the probability of survival through that step. For $n$ processes, you take the **product** (Π), not the sum (Σ).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Manufacturing** | OEE tracks equipment health; DPMO drives Six Sigma projects |
| **Software Engineering** | Defect density (defects per KLOC) measures code quality |
| **Healthcare** | RTY tracks patient journey from admission → diagnosis → treatment → discharge |
| **Service Industry** | OTD and CSI are primary performance indicators in logistics and BPO |

### 2.5 Theoretical Underpinnings

Performance measurement theory is grounded in **three foundational pillars**:

1. **Systems Theory** (Ludwig von Bertalanffy) — Organizations are systems with inputs, processes, and outputs; measurement captures the transformation efficiency.
2. **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** (Walter Shewhart) — Performance is quantified using statistical distributions and control limits.
3. **Management by Objectives (MBO)** (Peter Drucker) — Performance is measured against pre-defined objectives for accountability.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam**: **"S-S-M"** → **Systems theory, SPC, MBO** are the three roots. This is a common 3-mark conceptual question in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Yield to Sigma Level

The Six Sigma conversion from Yield to Sigma Level is a **high-yield exam problem**. Below is the complete step-by-step mathematical derivation that KTU examiners expect.

**Problem Setup:** A manufacturing process produces **10,000 units**. There are **5 opportunities for defects per unit** (since each unit can fail 5 different inspection criteria). After inspection, **250 defects** were counted.

**Step 1: Compute Defects Per Opportunity (DPO)**

$$
DPO = \dfrac{N_d}{N_u \times O}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
DPO = \dfrac{250}{10000 \times 5} = \dfrac{250}{50000}
$$

$$
DPO = 0.005
$$

**Step 2: Compute Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**

$$
DPMO = DPO \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
DPMO = 0.005 \times 10^{6} = 5000
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Process Yield**

$$
Yield = 1 - DPO
$$

$$
Yield = 1 - 0.005 = 0.995 = 99.5\%
$$

**Step 4: Convert Yield to Defects Rate (in % terms)**

$$
Defect\ Rate\ (\%) = (1 - Yield) \times 100
$$

$$
Defect\ Rate = 0.5\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Z-Score (Number of Standard Deviations)**

The Z-score represents how many standard deviations the process mean is from the nearest specification limit.

$$
Z = \Phi^{-1}(Yield) + 1.5\ \text{(1.5 sigma shift for long-term capability)}
$$

Where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the inverse standard normal CDF. For Yield = 0.995:

$$
\Phi^{-1}(0.995) \approx 2.576
$$

$$
Z = 2.576 + 1.5 = 4.076\ \text{(approximately 4.08 sigma)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 1.5 shift?** Motorola's Six Sigma methodology assumes a **long-term mean shift of 1.5σ** due to process drift. This is an empirical engineering adjustment, not a theoretical requirement. KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is the 1.5σ shift used?"* — Answer: to account for real-world process drift.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples Across All Major Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solved Example 1 — First Pass Yield (FPY) & Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**

A product goes through **4 sequential manufacturing processes**: Stamping → Welding → Painting → Assembly.

| Process | Units In | Defects Found |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| Stamping | 1000 | 50 |
| Welding | 950 | 38 |
| Painting | 912 | 27 |
| Assembly | 885 | 18 |

**Step 1: Calculate FPY for each process**

$$
FPY_{stamping} = \dfrac{950}{1000} = 0.950
$$

$$
FPY_{welding} = \dfrac{912}{950} \approx 0.960
$$

$$
FPY_{painting} = \dfrac{885}{912} \approx 0.970
$$

$$
FPY_{assembly} = \dfrac{867}{885} \approx 0.980
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**

$$
RTY = FPY_1 \times FPY_2 \times FPY_3 \times FPY_4
$$

$$
RTY = 0.950 \times 0.960 \times 0.970 \times 0.980
$$

Computing sequentially:

$$
0.950 \times 0.960 = 0.9120
$$

$$
0.9120 \times 0.970 = 0.8846
$$

$$
0.8846 \times 0.980 \approx 0.8669
$$

$$
RTY \approx 0.867\ (\text{or}\ 86.7\%)
$$

**Interpretation**: Despite each individual process having an FPY > 95%, the **cumulative defect loss** through 4 stages reduces the overall process yield to **86.7%**. This is why **multi-stage process optimization** is critical in TQM.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solved Example 2 — Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**

A CNC machine operates with the following data over a **30-day month**:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Planned Production Time | 720 hours |
| Actual Run Time (after downtime) | 576 hours |
| Theoretical Cycle Time | 1.2 minutes/unit |
| Total Units Produced | 25,000 units |
| Defective Units | 500 units |

**Step 1: Calculate Availability**

$$
A = \dfrac{Run\ Time}{Planned\ Time}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{576}{720} = 0.800 = 80.0\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Performance**

$$
P = \dfrac{(Units\ Produced \times Ideal\ Cycle\ Time)}{Run\ Time}
$$

First, convert units to hours for consistency:

$$
Units \times Cycle\ Time = 25000 \times 1.2\ min = 30000\ min = 500\ hours
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{500}{576} \approx 0.868 = 86.8\%
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Quality**

$$
Q = \dfrac{Good\ Units}{Total\ Units}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{25000 - 500}{25000} = \dfrac{24500}{25000} = 0.980 = 98.0\%
$$

**Step 4: Calculate OEE**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q
$$

$$
OEE = 0.800 \times 0.868 \times 0.980
$$

Sequential multiplication:

$$
0.800 \times 0.868 = 0.6944
$$

$$
0.6944 \times 0.980 \approx 0.6805
$$

$$
OEE \approx 68.05\%
$$

**World-Class Benchmark (Industry Standard)**:
- $OEE < 60\%$ → **Below Average** (needs immediate intervention)
- $OEE = 60\%$ to $85\%$ → **Acceptable** but improvable
- $OEE \geq 85\%$ → **World-Class**

In this example, the plant is in the "Acceptable" range, with **Performance** being the weakest dimension — suggesting the need for **cycle-time optimization** or **changeover time reduction**.

### 3.3 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — Conceptual Decomposition

The Cost of Quality is decomposed into **four quadrants**:

$$
CoQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal-failure} + C_{external-failure}
$$

| Quadrant | Category | Examples |
|----------|----------|----------|
| **Prevention (P)** | Cost to prevent defects | Training, design reviews, process improvement |
| **Appraisal (A)** | Cost to detect defects | Inspection, testing, quality audits |
| **Internal Failure (IF)** | Cost of defects found before delivery | Rework, scrap, reinspection |
| **External Failure (EF)** | Cost of defects found after delivery | Warranty, returns, recalls, lost reputation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Insight**: In mature TQM organizations, the optimal CoQ model is **P > A > IF > EF** — heavy investment in prevention reduces all other categories. KTU frequently tests this ordering as a 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Performance Measures (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hierarchical architecture** of performance measures across organizational levels, mapped to the **Balanced Scorecard** framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Organizational Vision and Mission] --> B[Strategic KPIs]
    B --> C1[Financial Perspective]
    B --> C2[Customer Perspective]
    B --> C3[Internal Process Perspective]
    B --> C4[Learning and Growth Perspective]
    C1 --> D1[ROI, ROQ, Cost of Quality]
    C2 --> D2[Customer Satisfaction Index, NPS, OTD]
    C3 --> D3[OEE, RTY, Cycle Time, Cpk]
    C4 --> D4[Employee Engagement, Training Hours, Absenteeism]
    D3 --> E1[Tactical Process Metrics]
    E1 --> F1[Operational Shop Floor Metrics]
    F1 --> G1[Defect Rate per Unit]
    F1 --> G2[Machine Downtime Hours]
    F1 --> G3[First Pass Yield per Station]
    G1 --> H1[Continuous Improvement via PDCA]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The arrows flow **top-down** (strategy cascading to operations) and **bottom-up** (operational data informing strategy). The PDCA loop at the bottom feeds back to the Vision at the top — this is the **closed-loop performance measurement system** demanded by TQM.

### 4.2 The Performance Measurement Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Define Strategic Objectives] --> A2[Identify Critical Success Factors]
    A2 --> A3[Select SMART Performance Measures]
    A3 --> A4[Collect Data Using Validated Methods]
    A4 --> A5[Analyze Data Using SPC Tools]
    A5 --> A6{Performance Gap Detected?}
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Root Cause Analysis: Fishbone 5 Why]
    A7 --> A8[Implement Corrective Action]
    A8 --> A9[Re-Measure and Verify]
    A9 --> A4
    A6 -- No --> A10[Document Best Practice and Standardize]
    A10 --> A4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip**: This diagram is the **canonical answer** for a 7-mark question on *"Explain the performance measurement process in TQM."* KTU examiners expect students to identify: (1) objective definition, (2) measure selection, (3) data collection, (4) analysis, (5) gap identification, (6) corrective action, and (7) feedback loop.

### 4.3 Cost of Quality — Quadrant Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Cost of Quality CoQ]
    Q --> Q1[Prevention Costs]
    Q --> Q2[Appraisal Costs]
    Q --> Q3[Internal Failure Costs]
    Q --> Q4[External Failure Costs]
    Q1 --> Q1a[Training and Education]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Quality Planning]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Design Reviews]
    Q2 --> Q2a[Incoming Inspection]
    Q2 --> Q2b[In-Process Testing]
    Q2 --> Q2c[Final Audit]
    Q3 --> Q3a[Rework and Repair]
    Q3 --> Q3b[Scrap and Rejects]
    Q3 --> Q3c[Re-inspection]
    Q4 --> Q4a[Warranty Claims]
    Q4 --> Q4b[Returns and Recalls]
    Q4 --> Q4c[Lost Customer Goodwill]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mermaid Misuse Avoided**: All node IDs above begin with letters (Q, Q1, Q1a, etc.) and labels use **plain uppercase text** without markdown bold (`**`) or pipe symbols (`|`). This complies with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> Define the term **"Performance Measure"**. List any **four characteristics** of a good performance measure.

**Model Answer:**

> A **Performance Measure** is a quantifiable indicator used to evaluate the efficiency, effectiveness, and quality of a process, product, or service against predefined standards or objectives. In TQM, it provides the empirical basis for decision-making and continuous improvement.

**Four Characteristics of a Good Performance Measure (SMART framework):**

1. **Specific** — The measure is clearly defined and unambiguous (e.g., "defects per 1000 units" rather than "quality").
2. **Measurable** — It can be quantified consistently using standardized units.
3. **Achievable** — The target is realistic given current resources and capability.
4. **Relevant** — It is directly linked to strategic organizational objectives.
5. **Time-bound** — Performance is evaluated over a defined time horizon (monthly, quarterly).

> Any four of the above earn full credit.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

> Distinguish between **"Performance Measures"** and **"Conformance Measures"** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Performance Measures | Conformance Measures |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Indicators of actual results achieved | Indicators of adherence to specifications |
| **Focus** | Outcomes and effectiveness | Process capability and standards |
| **Time Orientation** | Future-looking (results) | Present-looking (compliance) |
| **Example 1** | On-time delivery rate (95%) | ISO 9001 audit compliance score |
| **Example 2** | Customer satisfaction index | First-pass yield at a workstation |
| **Example 3** | Annual revenue growth | Defect rate per million opportunities (DPMO) |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Balanced Scorecard** framework developed by **Kaplan and Norton**. Discuss its **four perspectives** with **two relevant performance measures** under each perspective. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following data pertains to a manufacturing process producing **electronic control units (ECUs)** over a month:
>
> | Parameter | Value |
> |-----------|-------|
> | Units produced | 8,000 |
> | Defects found | 320 |
> | Opportunities per unit | 4 |
> | Process yield (current) | 96% |
>
> Compute the **DPMO**, **DPO**, and the **process sigma level**. Comment on whether the process meets the **Six Sigma quality benchmark** (3.4 DPMO). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Balanced Scorecard (BSC)**, developed by **Robert S. Kaplan and David P. Norton** in **1992** (Harvard Business Review), is a strategic performance management framework that translates an organization's vision and strategy into a coherent set of performance measures across **four perspectives**:

**1. Financial Perspective** — *"How do we look to shareholders?"*

- Return on Investment (ROI)
- Return on Quality (ROQ)
- Cost of Quality (CoQ)
- Revenue growth rate

**2. Customer Perspective** — *"How do customers see us?"*

- Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)
- Net Promoter Score (NPS)
- On-Time Delivery (OTD) rate
- Customer Retention Rate

**3. Internal Business Process Perspective** — *"What must we excel at?"*

- Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)
- Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)
- Process Capability Index (Cpk)
- Cycle Time

**4. Learning and Growth Perspective** — *"How do we sustain our ability to improve?"*

- Employee Engagement Score
- Training hours per employee per year
- Absenteeism rate
- Idea generation rate (suggestions per employee)

> **Strategic Value**: BSC overcomes the limitation of traditional financial-only metrics by providing a **balanced view** of short-term and long-term objectives, leading and lagging indicators, and internal and external performance dimensions.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Compute DPO (Defects Per Opportunity)**

$$
DPO = \dfrac{N_d}{N_u \times O} = \dfrac{320}{8000 \times 4} = \dfrac{320}{32000}
$$

$$
DPO = 0.01
$$

**Step 2: Compute DPMO**

$$
DPMO = DPO \times 10^{6} = 0.01 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
DPMO = 10000
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Process Yield**

$$
Yield = 1 - DPO = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99 = 99\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Z-Score (Sigma Level)**

The yield is 0.99. Looking up the standard normal table:

$$
\Phi^{-1}(0.99) \approx 2.326
$$

$$
Z = \Phi^{-1}(Yield) + 1.5\ (\text{long-term shift})
$$

$$
Z = 2.326 + 1.5 = 3.826\ \text{sigma}
$$

**Step 5: Verification against Six Sigma benchmark**

| Benchmark | DPMO | Sigma Level |
|-----------|------|-------------|
| Three Sigma | 66,807 | 3.0 |
| Four Sigma | 6,210 | 4.0 |
| Five Sigma | 233 | 5.0 |
| Six Sigma | 3.4 | 6.0 |

**Conclusion**: The current process has **DPMO = 10,000** and operates at approximately **3.83 sigma**. This is **far below the Six Sigma benchmark** (3.4 DPMO). The process requires significant quality improvement initiatives such as **DMAIC methodology**, **root-cause analysis**, and **poka-yoke** implementation to reach world-class quality.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Define the concept of **"Cost of Quality" (CoQ)**. Explain its **four categories** with suitable examples. Discuss how the distribution of CoQ changes as an organization matures in its TQM journey. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A four-stage manufacturing process has the following **First Pass Yield** values:
>
> | Stage | FPY |
> |-------|-----|
> | Stage 1: Machining | 0.97 |
> | Stage 2: Heat Treatment | 0.95 |
> | Stage 3: Plating | 0.93 |
> | Stage 4: Final Assembly | 0.98 |
>
> Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the **defect loss** at the end of Stage 4. If the company wants to improve the RTY to **90%**, suggest a **performance-based strategy** with justification. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard Terminology)**: **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is the total cost incurred by an organization in ensuring that products and services conform to quality requirements, including the cost of failure to meet those requirements. It is a financial metric that quantifies the economic impact of quality on the organization.

**Four Categories of CoQ:**

| # | Category | Definition | Examples |
|---|----------|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Prevention Costs** | Costs incurred to **prevent** defects from occurring | Quality training, design reviews, process improvement projects, supplier capability building |
| 2 | **Appraisal Costs** | Costs incurred to **detect** defects | Incoming material inspection, in-process testing, final product audit, calibration of measuring equipment |
| 3 | **Internal Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found **before delivery** to customer | Scrap, rework, reinspection, machine downtime due to defects |
| 4 | **External Failure Costs** | Costs of defects found **after delivery** to customer | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service, lost goodwill, legal liability |

**CoQ Distribution Shift with TQM Maturity:**

**Stage 1 — Immature Quality System (Reactive Stage):**
$$
CoQ \approx 5\%\ P + 25\%\ A + 35\%\ IF + 35\%\ EF
$$
Heavy external failure costs dominate; quality is "inspecting in" rather than "building in".

**Stage 2 — Developing Quality System:**
$$
CoQ \approx 15\%\ P + 30\%\ A + 30\%\ IF + 25\%\ EF
$$
Investment in prevention begins; external failures reduce slightly.

**Stage 3 — Mature Quality System (TQM Implementation Stage):**
$$
CoQ \approx 40\%\ P + 30\%\ A + 20\%\ IF + 10\%\ EF
$$
Prevention dominates; failure costs are minimized.

**Stage 4 — World-Class Quality Organization:**
$$
CoQ \approx 60\%\ P + 25\%\ A + 10\%\ IF + 5\%\ EF
```
Prevention is the largest investment; external failures are negligible; **total CoQ as % of sales drops dramatically**.

> **Key Insight**: As TQM matures, the **total CoQ decreases** even though prevention costs increase, because the **dramatic reduction in failure costs** more than compensates. This is the **Juran Cost-of-Quality Curve**.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Calculate RTY**

$$
RTY = FPY_1 \times FPY_2 \times FPY_3 \times FPY_4
$$

$$
RTY = 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.98
$$

Sequential multiplication:

$$
0.97 \times 0.95 = 0.9215
$$

$$
0.9215 \times 0.93 = 0.8570
$$

$$
0.8570 \times 0.98 = 0.8399
$$

$$
RTY \approx 0.84 \ \text{or}\ 84\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Defect Loss**

$$
Defect\ Loss = (1 - RTY) \times 100\%
$$

$$
Defect\ Loss = (1 - 0.84) \times 100 = 16\%
$$

**Interpretation**: For every **100 units entering Stage 1**, only **84 units** emerge as conforming products after Stage 4. The remaining **16 units** are lost to defects and rework.

**Step 3: Strategy to Improve RTY to 90%**

**Identifying the weakest link:** Stage 3 (Plating) has the **lowest FPY (0.93)** and contributes the largest defect loss. This is the **"vital few"** stage requiring priority intervention (per **Pareto's 80/20 principle**).

**Proposed Performance-Based Strategy:**

1. **Implement Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing)** at the Plating station to eliminate human error.
2. **Apply Statistical Process Control (SPC)** with control charts to detect and correct process drift in real-time.
3. **Deploy Six Sigma DMAIC** project specifically targeting the Plating stage — aim to lift FPY from 0.93 to ≥0.97.
4. **Improve Stage 2 (Heat Treatment)** from 0.95 to 0.97 using **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to optimize temperature and time parameters.

**Justification**: The total RTY is a **multiplicative** function of individual FPYs. Improving the weakest stage (Plating) by just 4 percentage points (0.93 → 0.97) raises RTY from **84% to approximately 88.5%**. Combined with Stage 2 improvement (0.95 → 0.97), RTY reaches the target of **≥90%**, validating the strategy through quantitative impact analysis.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**:
> - **For Part B (a) Questions**: Students frequently **omit the % distribution shift** of CoQ across TQM maturity stages. This is a **mandatory 2-mark component**. Simply defining CoQ without showing the **dynamic distribution** loses marks.
> - **For Part B (b) Numerical Problems**: Students frequently **add** the FPY values instead of **multiplying** them. RTY is a **product** (Π), not a sum (Σ). Also, ensure units are consistent — convert minutes to hours when computing OEE.
> - **Common Mistake in DPMO Problems**: Confusing "defects" with "defective units". DPMO counts **defects** (an individual unit can have multiple defects), not just defective units.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Performance Measures: Basic Concepts**

- ⭐ **Performance Measure** = Quantifiable indicator of efficiency/effectiveness against objectives
- ⭐ **SMART Criteria**: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound
- ⭐ **Five Universal Categories**: Quality, Cost, Delivery, Safety, Morale (**QCDSM**)
- ⭐ **Three Hierarchical Levels**: Strategic (KPI) → Tactical (Process) → Operational (Activity)
- ⭐ **Balanced Scorecard**: Four perspectives — Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth
- ⭐ **FPY Formula**: $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}}$
- ⭐ **RTY Formula**: $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ (MULTIPLY, never add!)
- ⭐ **DPMO Formula**: $DPMO = \dfrac{N_d}{N_u \times O} \times 10^{6}$
- ⭐ **OEE Formula**: $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ (Availability × Performance × Quality)
- ⭐ **Six Sigma Benchmark**: 3.4 DPMO with 1.5σ long-term shift
- ⭐ **Cost of Quality (CoQ)**: P + A + IF + EF (4 quadrants)
- ⭐ **CoQ Distribution Shift**: As TQM matures, **P increases**, **EF decreases**, **total CoQ decreases**
- ⭐ **OEE World-Class Benchmark**: $\geq 85\%$
- ⭐ **Theoretical Foundations**: Systems Theory + SPC (Shewhart) + MBO (Drucker)
- ⭐ **Key Axiom**: *"If you can't measure it, you can't manage it."* — Drucker
- ⭐ **Pareto Link**: Focus on the "vital few" stages/processes contributing 80% of defects
- ⭐ **Closed-Loop System**: Strategy cascades **top-down**; operational data feeds **bottom-up** to PDCA
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Strategy # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strategy** in the context of Total Quality Management (TQM) is defined as a **long-term, organization-wide blueprint of action** that integrates quality as a core competitive weapon, aligning the firm's mission, vision, values, and quality policy with measurable objectives, deployment plans, and feedback mechanisms to deliver superior customer value. According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, strategic quality management is the **systematic process** through which an organization formulates, deploys, evaluates, and revises its quality-related decisions and actions at the **enterprise (macro) level**, as opposed to operational (micro) quality control. **Key Distinction**: - **Strategy** = What to do & Why (Direction-setting) - **Tactics** = How to do it (Operational execution) - **Plans** = Specific actions, timelines, and resources ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kerala to Delhi**: | Component | Real-World Analogy | TQM Equivalent | |-----------|-------------------|----------------| | Dream | "Visit Delhi" | **Vision** | | Purpose | "Explore North India" | **Mission** | | Beliefs | "Travel safely & economically" | **Core Values** | | Master Plan | Route map with checkpoints | **Strategic Plan** | | Daily Plan | Today's itinerary | **Quality Objectives** | | On-the-go decisions | Avoiding traffic jams | **Tactics** | **The Point:** Strategy is the *master route map* drawn BEFORE you start. Without it, every driver takes their own turn, fuel is wasted, and the destination is never reached efficiently. In a company, strategy ensures that **every department, every employee, every process** moves in a unified direction toward quality excellence. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight**: The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that strategy is NOT an event — it is a **continuous cycle** (Plan → Deploy → Review → Revise). Questions on strategy frequently test the *linkage* between Vision/Mission/Policy and operational goals. ## 1.3 Standard Strategic Metrics in TQM The following metrics are commonly referenced in KTU examinations and globally adopted frameworks: - **BSC (Balanced Scorecard)** perspectives: **4** (Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth) - **Hoshin Kanri** typical planning horizons: **3–5 years** with annual reviews - **SMART** objective criteria: **5** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) - **SWOT** analysis dimensions: **4** (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats) - **PDCA** strategic cycle stages: **4** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Mantra**: Whenever you mention "Strategy" in TQM, always remember the **Strategic Triangle**: **Vision → Mission → Policy → Objectives → Deployment → Review**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Strategic Quality Management (SQM) Framework The SQM framework is built on **six sequential pillars**. Each pillar must exist for the next to function. ### Pillar 1: Vision Statement - Defines **what the organization aspires to become** in the future. - Time horizon: **5 to 20 years**. - Quality-focused example: *"To be the most customer-trusted engineering solutions provider globally by 2035."* ### Pillar 2: Mission Statement - Defines **why the organization exists today** and what it delivers. - Should be **operationally actionable**. - Quality-focused example: *"Deliver defect-free, ISO-certified industrial components to clients across South Asia within 48 hours."* ### Pillar 3: Core Values - The **non-negotiable beliefs** that govern behavior. - Common TQM-aligned values: *Customer focus, Integrity, Continuous Improvement (Kaizen), Employee Empowerment, Fact-based Decision Making.* ### Pillar 4: Quality Policy - The **organization's official pledge** to quality. - Must be **communicated, understood, and implemented** at all levels (ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2). ### Pillar 5: Strategic Quality Objectives - **SMART** goals derived from the quality policy. - Cascaded from **top management** to **shop-floor workers** through **Quality Deployment (QD)** or **Hoshin Kanri**. ### Pillar 6: Strategy Deployment & Review - **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** applied at the strategic level. - Performance measured via the **Balanced Scorecard (BSC)**. ## 2.2 SWOT Analysis — Strategic Positioning Tool SWOT is the most frequently tested strategic framework in KTU papers. It assesses: $$\text{Strategic Position} = f(\text{Internal Strengths/Weaknesses}, \text{External Opportunities/Threats})$$ | Quadrant | Type | Source | Question Answered | |---------|------|--------|-------------------| | **S** – Strengths | Internal, Positive | Resources, capabilities, patents | *"What are we good at?"* | | **W** – Weaknesses | Internal, Negative | Gaps in skills, technology, finance | *"Where do we lag?"* | | **O** – Opportunities | External, Positive | Markets, technology trends, policy | *"What can we exploit?"* | | **T** – Threats | External, Negative | Competitors, regulation, recession | *"What can harm us?"* | **KTU Strategy Match-Matrix (TOWS)**: - **SO Strategy** (Strength-Opportunity) = *Aggressive / Growth* - **WO Strategy** (Weakness-Opportunity) = *Re-orientation / Turnaround* - **ST Strategy** (Strength-Threat) = *Defensive / Diversification* - **WT Strategy** (Weakness-Threat) = *Survival / Retrenchment* ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose | |---------|---------------|---------| | SMART Objective | $\text{SMART} = S \cap M \cap A \cap R \cap T$ | Validity test for goals | | Hoshin Catchball | $\text{Catchball Cycles} \geq 3$ | Ensures organizational alignment | | BSC Weighting | $\sum w_i = 1.0$, where $w_i \geq 0$ | Priority allocation across 4 perspectives | | Quality Index | $QI = \dfrac{\sum(\text{Achieved Score} \times w_i)}{\sum(\text{Target Score} \times w_i)} \times 100$ | Composite quality performance % | | Strategic Gap | $\text{Gap} = \text{Target Performance} - \text{Current Performance}$ | Identifies priority improvement areas | | Pareto for Strategy | $80\% \text{ of issues} \Rightarrow 20\% \text{ of causes}$ | Focus on vital few strategic levers | | Strategy Deployment Level | $n \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | Cascading levels (Board → Worker) | | PDCA Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = T_{plan} + T_{do} + T_{check} + T_{act}$ | Total strategic review duration | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions**: > - All weights $w_i \geq 0$ > - $\sum w_i = 1$ (or 100%) > - QI $\in [0, 100]$ expressed as a percentage > - Strategic Gap $> 0$ triggers corrective action ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing Firms**: Use strategy to align Six Sigma projects with revenue growth. - **IT Companies**: Apply BSC to balance innovation (L&G) with operational uptime (Internal Process). - **Automotive Industry**: Toyota's *Hoshin Kanri* is the gold standard — every A3 report links to a strategic objective. - **Healthcare & Civil Engineering**: Quality strategy ensures regulatory compliance (ISO, NABH, BIS) while optimizing cost. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Balanced Scorecard Weighted Quality Index (QI) The Balanced Scorecard produces four perspective scores. To combine them into a **single strategic quality index**, a weighted aggregation model is used. **Step 1: Define the four BSC perspective scores** Let $S_F$, $S_C$, $S_I$, $S_L$ represent normalized scores (each on a $0$–$100$ scale) for Financial, Customer, Internal Process, and Learning & Growth perspectives respectively. **Step 2: Assign strategic weights** Let $w_F$, $w_C$, $w_I$, $w_L$ be the weights assigned by top management to each perspective, satisfying: $$w_F + w_C + w_I + w_L = 1.0$$ **Step 3: Compute the Achieved Composite Score** $$S_{\text{achieved}} = w_F \cdot S_F + w_C \cdot S_C + w_I \cdot S_I + w_L \cdot S_L$$ **Step 4: Compute the Target Composite Score** $$S_{\text{target}} = w_F \cdot T_F + w_C \cdot T_C + w_I \cdot T_I + w_L \cdot T_L$$ where $T_F$, $T_C$, $T_I$, $T_L$ are the target scores for each perspective. **Step 5: Define the Quality Index** $$QI = \frac{S_{\text{achieved}}}{S_{\text{target}}} \times 100\%$$ **Step 6: Decision Rule** - If $QI \geq 100\%$: Strategy is **on track or exceeded**. - If $90\% \leq QI < 100\%$: Strategy is **acceptable**, minor adjustments needed. - If $QI < 90\%$: **Strategic intervention required** — revisit SWOT and objectives. ### Worked Example **Given Data:** - Achieved Scores: $S_F = 80$, $S_C = 90$, $S_I = 70$, $S_L = 60$ - Target Scores: $T_F = 100$, $T_C = 95$, $T_I = 85$, $T_L = 75$ - Weights: $w_F = 0.30$, $w_C = 0.35$, $w_I = 0.20$, $w_L = 0.15$ **Step-by-step Calculation:** First, verify weight sum: $$0.30 + 0.35 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00 \checkmark$$ Compute $S_{\text{achieved}}$: $$S_{\text{achieved}} = (0.30)(80) + (0.35)(90) + (0.20)(70) + (0.15)(60)$$ $$S_{\text{achieved}} = 24.00 + 31.50 + 14.00 + 9.00 = 78.50$$ Compute $S_{\text{target}}$: $$S_{\text{target}} = (0.30)(100) + (0.35)(95) + (0.20)(85) + (0.15)(75)$$ $$S_{\text{target}} = 30.00 + 33.25 + 17.00 + 11.25 = 91.50$$ Final Quality Index: $$QI = \frac{78.50}{91.50} \times 100\% \approx 85.79\%$$ **Interpretation:** Since $QI < 90\%$, the organization must trigger **strategic intervention**. The weakest area ($S_L = 60$ vs $T_L = 75$) indicates that Learning & Growth initiatives (training, R&D) require immediate attention. ## 3.2 Derivation: Strategic Gap Analysis Define the **gap** for any objective $j$ as: $$\text{Gap}_j = T_j - A_j$$ where $T_j$ is the target performance and $A_j$ is the actual performance. To prioritize, compute a **relative gap percentage**: $$\text{Gap}\%_j = \frac{T_j - A_j}{T_j} \times 100\%$$ A **Pareto chart** is then constructed to rank objectives by their gap%, and the **vital few** (typically top 20%) are selected for immediate strategic action. ### Worked Example | Objective | Target ($T_j$) | Actual ($A_j$) | Gap$_j$ | Gap% | |-----------|---------------|----------------|---------|------| | Reduce defects | 100 ppm | 250 ppm | -150 | 60% | | On-time delivery | 98% | 92% | 6 | 6.12% | | Customer NPS | 70 | 65 | 5 | 7.14% | | Training hours/employee | 40 hrs | 20 hrs | 20 | 50% | **Insight:** Top 2 (Defects + Training) consume ~80% of the strategic improvement effort → Apply the **80/20 rule**. ## 3.3 Hoshin Kanri Catchball Math The number of catchball iterations $n$ needed to reach alignment follows a geometric convergence model: $$A_n = A_0 \cdot r^n$$ where: - $A_n$ = alignment level after $n$ iterations (in %) - $A_0$ = initial alignment (typically 30–40%) - $r$ = alignment improvement factor per cycle (typically $1.15$ to $1.25$) **Example:** Starting at $A_0 = 40\%$, with $r = 1.20$ per cycle: $$A_1 = 40 \times 1.20 = 48\%$$ $$A_2 = 48 \times 1.20 = 57.6\%$$ $$A_3 = 57.6 \times 1.20 \approx 69.1\%$$ $$A_4 = 69.1 \times 1.20 \approx 82.9\%$$ $$A_5 = 82.9 \times 1.20 \approx 99.5\% \geq 95\% \text{ (alignment threshold)}$$ **Conclusion:** A minimum of **5 catchball cycles** is typically required for full strategic alignment, validating KTU's frequent mention of "at least 3 catchball iterations." # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Strategic Quality Management — Hierarchical Cascade ```mermaid graph TD nodeV["VISION STATEMENT
Where we aspire to be"] nodeM["MISSION STATEMENT
Why we exist today"] nodeCV["CORE VALUES
Non-negotiable beliefs"] nodeQP["QUALITY POLICY
Official pledge to quality"] nodeSQO["STRATEGIC QUALITY OBJECTIVES
SMART goals Top Level"] nodeQD["QUALITY DEPLOYMENT
Hoshin Kanri Catchball"] nodeDO["DEPARTMENTAL OBJECTIVES
Cascaded SMART goals"] nodePO["PROCESS OBJECTIVES
Workflow level KPIs"] nodeWO["WORKER LEVEL GOALS
Daily targets"] nodePDCA["PDCA STRATEGIC REVIEW
Plan Do Check Act"] nodeBSC["BALANCED SCORECARD
4 Perspective Measurement"] nodeSWOT["SWOT ANALYSIS
Internal and External Scan"] nodeFEED["FEEDBACK LOOP
Revise Vision and Policy"] nodeV --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeCV nodeCV --> nodeQP nodeQP --> nodeSQO nodeSQO --> nodeQD nodeQD --> nodeDO nodeDO --> nodePO nodePO --> nodeWO nodeSQO --> nodeBSC nodeBSC --> nodePDCA nodePDCA --> nodeFEED nodeFEED --> nodeV nodeSWOT --> nodeSQO ``` ## 4.2 Hoshin Kanri Catchball — X-Matrix Interaction Flow ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["TOP MANAGEMENT
Draft Annual Objectives"] nodeB["MIDDLE MANAGEMENT
Refine and Feasibility Check"] nodeC["DEPARTMENT HEADS
Resource Allocation"] nodeD["SHOP FLOOR
Operational Feasibility"] nodeE["DEPARTMENT HEADS
Confirm Targets"] nodeF["MIDDLE MANAGEMENT
Consolidate"] nodeG["TOP MANAGEMENT
Final Approval"] nodeA -- Catchball Pass 1 --> nodeB nodeB -- Catchball Pass 2 --> nodeC nodeC -- Catchball Pass 3 --> nodeD nodeD -- Feedback --> nodeC nodeC -- Confirmation --> nodeE nodeE -- Aggregate --> nodeF nodeF -- Final Plan --> nodeG nodeG -- Strategic Deployment --> nodeA ``` ## 4.3 Strategic Gap Closure — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TD nodeSW["SWOT INPUT
Internal and External Data"] nodeSP["STRATEGIC PLANNING
Vision Mission Policy"] nodeDP["DEPLOYMENT PHASE
Hoshin Kanri and Cascading"] nodeIM["IMPLEMENTATION
PDCA Execution"] nodePR["PERFORMANCE REVIEW
BSC and KPIs"] nodeGA["GAP ANALYSIS
Target minus Actual"] nodeAC["ACTION PLAN
Corrective and Preventive"] nodeRV["REVIEW and REVISION
Annual Strategic Audit"] nodeSW --> nodeSP nodeSP --> nodeDP nodeDP --> nodeIM nodeIM --> nodePR nodePR --> nodeGA nodeGA --> nodeAC nodeAC --> nodeIM nodeGA --> nodeRV nodeRV --> nodeSP ``` > [!TIP] > **Interpretation Note**: The diagrams above show that **strategy is a closed-loop system**, not a linear checklist. The feedback loop from *Performance Review → Action Plan → Implementation* is the engine of *Kaizen* (continuous improvement) at the strategic level. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **"Differentiate between Strategic Planning and Operational Planning in the context of TQM."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Strategic Planning | Operational Planning | |-----------|--------------------|--------------------| | Time Horizon | Long-term (3–10 years) | Short-term (daily/monthly) | | Level | Top Management | Middle/Supervisory | | Focus | Vision, Mission, Quality Policy | Production targets, schedules | | Scope | Organization-wide | Department/Process-specific | | Risk | High | Low | | Review Frequency | Annual/Quarterly | Daily/Weekly | *(1 mark each for Time Horizon + Level + Focus, or 1.5 + 1.5 marks for any two distinguishing criteria)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series I]** **"List the four perspectives of the Balanced Scorecard with one example metric for each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Financial Perspective** — e.g., *Return on Investment (ROI)* or *Cost per unit*. 2. **Customer Perspective** — e.g., *Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)* or *Net Promoter Score (NPS)*. 3. **Internal Business Process Perspective** — e.g., *Defect Rate (DPMO)* or *Cycle Time*. 4. **Learning and Growth Perspective** — e.g., *Employee Training Hours* or *Skill Certification Rate*. *(0.75 mark per perspective; layout/formatting carries balance for 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **concept of Strategic Quality Management (SQM)**. Discuss the relationship between **Vision, Mission, Values, and Quality Policy** with a suitable example of a manufacturing firm. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the **SWOT analysis framework** in detail. Using a TOWS matrix, formulate strategic alternatives for a hypothetical engineering college seeking NBA accreditation. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Solution to Q-A (a):** **Definition (2 marks):** Strategic Quality Management is the *systematic integration of quality goals into the long-term strategic planning of an organization*. It ensures that quality is not a peripheral function but the **central pillar** of competitive strategy. **Relationship Diagram (1 mark):** ``` VISION (Dream) ↓ MISSION (Purpose) ↓ CORE VALUES (Beliefs) ↓ QUALITY POLICY (Pledge) ↓ QUALITY OBJECTIVES (Measurable Goals) ``` **Manufacturing Example — ABC Precision Tools Pvt. Ltd. (3 marks):** | Element | Statement | |---------|-----------| | **Vision** | "To be India's most trusted precision-engineering brand by 2035." | | **Mission** | "Deliver defect-free, ISO 9001:2015 certified CNC components within 24 hours to OEMs across South Asia." | | **Core Values** | Customer Focus · Precision · Innovation · Employee Empowerment · Sustainability | | **Quality Policy** | "We commit to zero-defect output, continuous improvement, and full regulatory compliance through empowered teams and fact-based decisions." | **Explanation (1 mark):** The policy translates the mission into an actionable pledge, which is then broken into SMART objectives for each department. --- #### **Solution to Q-A (b):** **SWOT Framework Description (3 marks):** SWOT is a **2x2 strategic matrix** that classifies factors as: - **Internal Positive** → Strengths - **Internal Negative** → Weaknesses - **External Positive** → Opportunities - **External Negative** → Threats **Engineering College — SWOT (2 marks):** | | **Positive** | **Negative** | |---|---|---| | **Internal** | **Strengths:** Experienced faculty, NBA-accredited labs, strong alumni network | **Weaknesses:** Outdated curriculum, low industry internships, limited research output | | **External** | **Opportunities:** Govt. skill missions, EdTech partnerships, foreign collaborations | **Threats:** Rise of online universities, regulatory changes, funding cuts | **TOWS Strategic Alternatives (2 marks):** | Strategy | Combination | Action | |----------|-------------|--------| | **SO — Aggressive** | Strength + Opportunity | Use faculty expertise + EdTech partnerships to launch hybrid MTech programs | | **WO — Turnaround** | Weakness + Opportunity | Adopt EdTech to modernize curriculum and overcome outdated teaching methods | | **ST — Defensive** | Strength + Threat | Leverage alumni + industry network to counter online universities via blended mentorship | | **WT — Survival** | Weakness + Threat | Cost-optimize research by partnering with foreign universities for joint supervision | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU 2024 Model Paper, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** What is **Hoshin Kanri**? Explain the **Catchball process** with a diagram. Why is it considered superior to conventional top-down planning? *(7 marks)* **(b)** An IT services company uses the Balanced Scorecard with the following data. Compute the **Quality Index (QI)** and recommend strategic actions. *(7 marks)* | Perspective | Target | Achieved | Weight | |-------------|--------|----------|--------| | Financial | 90 | 75 | 0.25 | | Customer | 95 | 88 | 0.30 | | Internal Process | 85 | 80 | 0.25 | | Learning and Growth | 80 | 60 | 0.20 | --- #### **Solution to Q-B (a):** **Definition (2 marks):** Hoshin Kanri (Japanese: 方針管理) is a **strategic planning and deployment methodology** that aligns an organization's long-term breakthrough goals with annual improvement activities through a collaborative process called **Catchball**. **Catchball Process (3 marks):** 1. **Top Management** drafts breakthrough objectives (3–5 year horizon). 2. The plan is *thrown* (like a ball) to middle management for feasibility. 3. Middle management reviews resources and *throws back* with feedback. 4. Refined plan cascades to department heads and shop floor. 5. Shop floor submits operational feedback. 6. Final consolidated plan is approved by top management. **Catchball Diagram (1 mark):** ``` CEO → Middle Mgmt → Dept Heads → Workers ↑ ↓ ← ← ← ← Feedback Loops ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ``` **Why Superior to Top-Down? (1 mark):** - Ensures **two-way communication**. - Improves **buy-in** and reduces resistance. - Filters unrealistic targets early. - Aligns **execution capability** with **strategic ambition**. --- #### **Solution to Q-B (b):** **Given Data Verification (1 mark):** Sum of weights: $0.25 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 1.00 \checkmark$ **Step 1 — Calculate Achieved Composite (2 marks):** $$S_{\text{achieved}} = (0.25)(75) + (0.30)(88) + (0.25)(80) + (0.20)(60)$$ $$S_{\text{achieved}} = 18.75 + 26.40 + 20.00 + 12.00 = 77.15$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Target Composite (2 marks):** $$S_{\text{target}} = (0.25)(90) + (0.30)(95) + (0.25)(85) + (0.20)(80)$$ $$S_{\text{target}} = 22.50 + 28.50 + 21.25 + 16.00 = 88.25$$ **Step 3 — Compute Quality Index (1 mark):** $$QI = \frac{77.15}{88.25} \times 100\% \approx 87.42\%$$ **Step 4 — Strategic Recommendation (1 mark):** Since $QI = 87.42\% < 90\%$, **strategic intervention is required**. The worst performing perspective is **Learning and Growth** (60 vs 80, gap = 25%). Recommended actions: 1. Increase employee training hours by 50% in the next quarter. 2. Implement a mentorship program to upskill junior developers. 3. Set up an internal R&D lab for emerging tech certification. 4. Re-evaluate weights — consider raising $w_L$ to 0.25 if learning is a long-term strategic priority. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** ⚠️ > > 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks)**: Writing *Vision* and *Mission* interchangeably. **Vision = Future aspiration**, **Mission = Current purpose**. Examiners deduct heavily for this. > 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–1.5 marks)**: In SWOT, students often confuse **Strength** (internal) with **Opportunity** (external). Always state whether the factor is *internal or external* explicitly. > 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–2 marks)**: When computing Quality Index, students forget to **verify that weights sum to 1**. Examiners will deduct if normalization is missing. > 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark)**: In Hoshin Kanri, students mention *catchball* but fail to describe the **two-way nature**. Always emphasize bidirectional flow. > 5. **Common Mistake 5 (–1.5 marks)**: Using vague phrases like *"improve quality"* without applying the **SMART** framework in objective-setting questions. > 6. **Common Mistake 6 (–1 mark)**: Not drawing diagrams for Vision/Mission cascade or Catchball. **Diagrams fetch at least 1 mark** even in descriptive answers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strategy in TQM** - **Strategy** is a long-term, organization-wide blueprint aligning quality with business goals. - **Hierarchy**: Vision → Mission → Values → Quality Policy → Objectives → Deployment → Review. - **Vision** = Future dream · **Mission** = Current purpose · **Values** = Beliefs · **Policy** = Pledge. - **SMART Objectives**: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound. - **SWOT**: Internal (S, W) × External (O, T); Use **TOWS** to derive SO/WO/ST/WT strategies. - **Hoshin Kanri**: Japanese strategic deployment using **Catchball** (≥3 iterations). - **Balanced Scorecard (BSC)**: 4 perspectives — Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth. - **Quality Index Formula**: $QI = \dfrac{\sum(w_i \times A_i)}{\sum(w_i \times T_i)} \times 100\%$, with $\sum w_i = 1$. - **Strategic Gap**: $Gap = Target - Actual$; prioritize using **Pareto (80/20)**. - **PDCA Cycle**: Applied at strategic level (Plan strategy → Deploy → Check via KPIs → Act & Revise). - **Catchball Math**: $A_n = A_0 \cdot r^n$; typically $r \approx 1.20$, requiring **~5 cycles** for ≥95% alignment. - **Linkage Rule**: Every operational goal must trace upward to a **strategic objective**, which traces to a **policy clause**, which traces to the **mission**. - **Feedback Loop**: Strategy is a *closed-loop system* — never a one-time plan. - **ISO 9001:2015 Alignment**: Quality Policy (Clause 5.2) must be communicated, understood, and available. - **Common Mistake to Avoid**: Treating strategy as static. Strategy = **continuous strategic renewal**.

topics.title: Performance Measure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Measure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Measurement** in Total Quality Management (TQM) is the systematic, continuous process of quantifying the efficiency, effectiveness, and capability of an organization's processes, products, services, and people against established strategic objectives, standards, and benchmarks. It provides the empirical foundation for evidence-based decision-making, root-cause analysis, and continuous improvement (Kaizen) initiatives.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, performance measurement is treated as the operational nerve center of any TQM framework. Without quantified measures, quality remains an abstract slogan rather than an engineered outcome. The measurement system must be aligned with the organization's **vision, mission, and critical success factors (CSFs)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car Dashboard"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine driving a car without a dashboard. You would not know your speed, fuel level, engine temperature, or oil pressure until something catastrophic happens. A driver with no dashboard is essentially driving blind.

A **Performance Measurement System (PMS)** acts as the *dashboard of an organization*. Just as a car's dashboard provides:

| Car Dashboard | Organizational Performance Measure |
|---|---|
| Speedometer | Throughput / Cycle Time |
| Fuel Gauge | Cost of Quality (CoQ) |
| Engine Temperature | Defect Rate / DPMO |
| Oil Pressure | Process Capability Index (Cpk) |
| GPS / Navigation | Balanced Scorecard Strategic Alignment |
| Warning Lights | Statistical Alerts (Out-of-Control Signals) |

The driver (manager) uses these real-time indicators to **steer**, **correct**, and **optimize** the journey (organization's strategy).

### 1.3 Why Performance Measurement is Indispensable in TQM

The **KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus** explicitly identifies performance measurement as the bridge between **abstract quality philosophy** and **tangible operational reality**. The core reasons are:

1. **Visibility** — Converts invisible process variations into quantified data.
2. **Accountability** — Establishes ownership of outcomes at every hierarchical level.
3. **Comparability** — Enables benchmarking against internal historical data and external industry best practices.
4. **Decision Support** — Provides statistical evidence for Six Sigma, Lean, and Kaizen projects.
5. **Customer Focus** — Translates Voice of Customer (VoC) into measurable Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) characteristics.
6. **Strategic Alignment** — Links day-to-day operational metrics to long-term strategic goals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edwards Deming's Famous Warning:** *"In God we trust, all others must bring data."* Performance measurement is the mechanism through which this philosophy becomes operational in TQM.

### 1.4 Classification of Performance Measures

Performance measures are broadly classified into the following categories:

- **Quantitative Measures** — Numerical indicators (e.g., defect rate, yield %, on-time delivery %).
- **Qualitative Measures** — Descriptive indicators (e.g., customer satisfaction rating, employee morale).
- **Leading Indicators** — Predictive measures that forecast future performance (e.g., employee training hours, supplier audit scores).
- **Lagging Indicators** — Outcome-based measures that confirm past performance (e.g., customer complaints, warranty claims).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A balanced PMS always combines **both leading and lagging indicators**. Over-reliance on lagging indicators creates a "rear-view mirror" management style, where problems are detected only after they have occurred.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of Effective Performance Measures

The **SMART** criteria are universally accepted as the foundational test for any performance metric. According to the KTU 2024 evaluation rubric, performance measures that fail the SMART test attract heavy mark deductions in exam answers.

- **S — Specific:** Clearly defines *what* is being measured and *why*.
- **M — Measurable:** The metric must be quantifiable using a defined unit of measurement.
- **A — Achievable:** The target must be realistic and within organizational control.
- **R — Relevant:** Directly tied to a strategic objective or Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) tree.
- **T — Time-bound:** Defined frequency of measurement (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly).

### 2.2 The Balanced Scorecard (BSC) Framework

The **Balanced Scorecard**, developed by **Robert S. Kaplan and David P. Norton (1992)**, is the most widely adopted strategic performance measurement framework. It balances four interrelated perspectives:

| Perspective | Core Question Answered | Example Metrics |
|---|---|---|
| **Financial** | "How do we look to shareholders?" | ROI, Revenue Growth %, Cost Reduction % |
| **Customer** | "How do customers see us?" | Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI), Net Promoter Score (NPS), On-Time Delivery % |
| **Internal Business Process** | "At what must we excel?" | Cycle Time, First Pass Yield (FPY), Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) |
| **Learning & Growth** | "How can we continue to improve and create value?" | Employee Training Hours, Innovation Index, Employee Turnover % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked about performance measurement frameworks, students must mention the Balanced Scorecard by name and explicitly state all **four perspectives**. Skipping even one perspective results in loss of full marks.

### 2.3 Six Sigma Performance Metrics

Six Sigma methodology provides a rigorous quantitative foundation for performance measurement. The following formulas are classified as **KTU High-Yield** and must be memorized.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all critical formulas required for KTU 2024 board examinations on this topic. Pay close attention to the symbols and units.

| # | Metric | Formula | Units / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D}{(N \times OP)} \times 10^6$ | Defects (D), Units (N), Opportunities per unit (OP) |
| 2 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage; units passed without rework |
| 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n$ | Decimal (0 to 1); product of individual process yields |
| 4 | **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless; ideal value ≥ 1.33 |
| 5 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min \left[ \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right]$ | Dimensionless; accounts for process centering |
| 6 | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ (or $Z = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}$) | Standard deviations from mean to spec |
| 7 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Monetary units (₹, $, etc.) |
| 8 | **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum (W_i \times S_i)}{\sum W_i} \times 100$ | Percentage; weighted average of attributes |
| 9 | **On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $OTD = \dfrac{N_{on\text{-}time}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| 10 | **Productivity** | $Productivity = \dfrac{Output}{Input}$ | Ratio |

Where:
- $D$ = Number of defects observed
- $N$ = Number of units produced
- $OP$ = Number of defect opportunities per unit
- $Y_i$ = Yield of the $i^{th}$ process step (in decimal)
- $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit
- $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit
- $\mu$ = Process mean
- $\sigma$ = Process standard deviation
- $C_P$ = Prevention Cost
- $C_A$ = Appraisal Cost
- $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost
- $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost

### 2.5 The Cost of Quality (CoQ) Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CoQ is a strategic performance measurement tool. It explicitly demonstrates the economic logic of TQM: **investing more in prevention and appraisal reduces the much larger costs of internal and external failures.**

The four categories of CoQ are:

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_P$)** — Costs of activities that *prevent* defects (e.g., quality training, supplier capability programs, process design reviews).
2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_A$)** — Costs of activities that *detect* defects (e.g., inspection, testing, quality audits).
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$)** — Costs of defects found *before* delivery to customer (e.g., scrap, rework, reinspection).
4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$)** — Costs of defects found *after* delivery (e.g., warranty claims, returns, recalls, legal liability).

### 2.6 Engineering and Real-World Utility

In the manufacturing sector, performance measurement enables:
- **Real-time SPC (Statistical Process Control)** charts on shop floors.
- **Supplier performance scorecards** for procurement decisions.
- **Lean Six Sigma project selection** based on DPMO and Sigma Level.

In the service sector, performance measurement enables:
- **Service Quality (SERVQUAL) gap analysis** across five dimensions: Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** tracking for customer loyalty.
- **Call center metrics** like Average Handling Time (AHT) and First Call Resolution (FCR).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A manufacturing process produces **5,000 units** in one shift. Each unit has **4 opportunities** for a defect. A total of **80 defects** were recorded across all units. Compute:
- (a) DPMO
- (b) Process Yield
- (c) Sigma Level using the standard conversion table

#### Step 1: Identify the Given Data

$$N = 5000 \text{ units}$$

$$OP = 4 \text{ opportunities per unit}$$

$$D = 80 \text{ defects}$$

#### Step 2: Calculate Total Number of Opportunities

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = N \times OP$$

$$= 5000 \times 4$$

$$= 20000 \text{ opportunities}$$

*Explanation:* Each of the 5000 units has 4 potential places where a defect could occur, giving 20,000 total inspection points.

#### Step 3: Calculate DPMO

$$DPMO = \dfrac{D}{(N \times OP)} \times 10^6$$

$$= \dfrac{80}{20000} \times 10^6$$

$$= 0.004 \times 10^6$$

$$= 4000 \text{ DPMO}$$

*Explanation:* For every 1 million opportunities, this process would produce 4000 defects.

#### Step 4: Calculate Process Yield

$$Y = 1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^6}$$

$$= 1 - \dfrac{4000}{1000000}$$

$$= 1 - 0.004$$

$$= 0.996 \text{ or } 99.6\%$$

*Explanation:* The process produces acceptable output 99.6% of the time.

#### Step 5: Convert DPMO to Sigma Level

Using the standard Six Sigma conversion table, **4000 DPMO corresponds to approximately 4.5 Sigma**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students incorrectly use $Y = 1 - (D / N)$ instead of $Y = 1 - DPMO/10^6$. These give the same numerical result here only because $OP = 1$. When $OP > 1$, the two methods diverge and only the DPMO formula is correct.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

**Problem Statement:** A four-stage manufacturing process has the following first-pass yields:
- Stage 1: $Y_1 = 0.98$
- Stage 2: $Y_2 = 0.95$
- Stage 3: $Y_3 = 0.97$
- Stage 4: $Y_4 = 0.99$

Compute the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the **total defect rate**.

#### Step 1: Apply the RTY Formula

$$RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4$$

#### Step 2: Sequential Multiplication

$$RTY = 0.98 \times 0.95$$

$$= 0.9310$$

*Explanation:* After Stage 2, only 93.10% of the original units remain defect-free.

#### Step 3: Multiply by Stage 3 Yield

$$RTY = 0.9310 \times 0.97$$

$$= 0.90307$$

*Explanation:* After Stage 3, 90.307% remain defect-free.

#### Step 4: Multiply by Stage 4 Yield

$$RTY = 0.90307 \times 0.99$$

$$= 0.8940393$$

$$\approx 0.8940 \text{ or } 89.40\%$$

*Explanation:* Only about 89.4% of units pass through the entire four-stage process without any defect — even though each individual stage has yield above 95%.

#### Step 5: Compute Total Defect Rate

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - RTY$$

$$= 1 - 0.8940$$

$$= 0.1060 \text{ or } 10.60\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Insight:** This example demonstrates a critical TQM principle — the **"Hidden Factory"** concept. Individually high yields compound into a significant overall defect rate, justifying investment in prevention.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Process Capability Indices ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$)

**Problem Statement:** A piston ring has specification limits **LSL = 24.90 mm** and **USL = 25.10 mm**. The process mean is **$\mu = 25.02$ mm** and the standard deviation is **$\sigma = 0.015$ mm**.

Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and interpret the results.

#### Step 1: Calculate $C_p$

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

$$= \dfrac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.015}$$

$$= \dfrac{0.20}{0.09}$$

$$= 2.222$$

*Explanation:* A $C_p$ of 2.22 means the process spread is significantly smaller than the specification width — indicating a highly capable process in terms of spread.

#### Step 2: Calculate the Upper-Side Capability Index

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

$$= \dfrac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.015}$$

$$= \dfrac{0.08}{0.045}$$

$$= 1.778$$

*Explanation:* The process is 1.778 standard deviations away from the USL.

#### Step 3: Calculate the Lower-Side Capability Index

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

$$= \dfrac{25.02 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.015}$$

$$= \dfrac{0.12}{0.045}$$

$$= 2.667$$

*Explanation:* The process is 2.667 standard deviations away from the LSL.

#### Step 4: Calculate Overall $C_{pk}$

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pk,\text{upper}}, C_{pk,\text{lower}})$$

$$= \min(1.778, 2.667)$$

$$= 1.778$$

#### Step 5: Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> Since $C_p \neq C_{pk}$, the process is **not centered**. The mean is shifted closer to the USL, creating asymmetric risk. The **lower $C_{pk}$ value of 1.778** governs the true capability. Industry benchmark: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is acceptable; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is preferred for critical characteristics.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Cost of Quality (CoQ) Analysis

**Problem Statement:** A company reports the following annual quality costs (in ₹ Lakhs):
- Prevention: ₹ 8
- Appraisal: ₹ 12
- Internal Failure: ₹ 25
- External Failure: ₹ 35

The total revenue is ₹ 800 Lakhs. Compute:
- (a) Total CoQ
- (b) CoQ as a percentage of revenue
- (c) Failure Cost Ratio (Appraisal + Prevention vs. Failure)

#### Step 1: Total CoQ

$$CoQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

$$= 8 + 12 + 25 + 35$$

$$= 80 \text{ Lakhs}$$

#### Step 2: CoQ as Percentage of Revenue

$$\%CoQ = \dfrac{80}{800} \times 100$$

$$= 10\%$$

#### Step 3: Failure Cost Ratio

$$\text{Prevention + Appraisal} = 8 + 12 = 20 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{Failure Costs} = 25 + 35 = 60 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{Failure Ratio} = \dfrac{60}{20} = 3.0$$

*Interpretation:* The company spends **3 times more on failure costs than on prevention + appraisal**, indicating a strong opportunity for TQM intervention. The strategic recommendation would be to **increase $C_P$ and $C_A$ spending to reduce $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule for CoQ:** When management sees that failure costs dominate, the financial case for TQM becomes self-evident. Always conclude CoQ problems with this strategic insight for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Performance Measurement Framework (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of a complete Performance Measurement System in a TQM organization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strategic Vision and Mission] --> B[Define Critical Success Factors CSFs]
    B --> C[Establish Key Performance Indicators KPIs]
    C --> D[Set SMART Targets and Baselines]
    D --> E[Data Collection and Measurement Systems]
    E --> F[Statistical Process Control SPC Charts]
    F --> G{Is Process In Control?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Capability Analysis Cp Cpk]
    G -- No --> I[Root Cause Analysis Fishbone 5 Why]
    I --> J[Corrective and Preventive Actions CAPA]
    J --> E
    H --> K{Is Capability Acceptable?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Document Best Practice]
    K -- No --> I
    L --> M[Balanced Scorecard Review Quarterly]
    M --> N[Management Review Meeting]
    N --> O[Continuous Improvement Kaizen Six Sigma]
    O --> C
    M --> P[Stakeholder Reporting]
    P --> Q[Customer Satisfaction CSI NPS]
    Q --> A
```

### 4.2 The Balanced Scorecard — Four-Perspective Causal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Learning and Growth Perspective] --> I[Internal Business Process Perspective]
    I --> C[Customer Perspective]
    C --> F[Financial Perspective]
    F -.feedback loop.-> L
```

*Explanation:* This causal chain shows that improvement in employee skills and learning drives better internal processes, which enhances customer perception, which ultimately drives financial performance. The feedback loop ensures organizational learning continues.

### 4.3 Cost of Quality — Optimum Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Increase Prevention Spending] --> B[Defect Rate Decreases]
    B --> C[Internal Failure Costs Decrease]
    B --> D[External Failure Costs Decrease]
    A --> E[Appraisal Costs Slightly Increase]
    C --> F[Total CoQ Reaches Optimum]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Maximum Quality with Minimum Total Cost]
```

### 4.4 Performance Measure Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Business Objective] --> B{Is it Strategic or Operational?}
    B -- Strategic --> C[Use Balanced Scorecard]
    B -- Operational --> D{Is it Process or Outcome Based?}
    D -- Process --> E[Use Leading Indicators e.g. Cp Training Hours]
    D -- Outcome --> F[Use Lagging Indicators e.g. DPMO Customer Complaints]
    C --> G[Validate against SMART Criteria]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{All SMART Criteria Met?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Deploy Metric]
    H -- No --> J[Redesign Metric]
    J --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Performance Measurement in the context of TQM. List any two characteristics of an effective performance measure. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Performance Measurement in TQM is the systematic process of quantifying the effectiveness and efficiency of an organization's processes, products, and people against predefined quality objectives, standards, and benchmarks. It transforms abstract quality goals into quantifiable data for evidence-based decision-making.

**Two characteristics of effective performance measures** (using SMART):

1. **Measurable and Specific** — The metric must be quantifiable with a clear unit of measurement and a precise definition of what is being counted.
2. **Relevant and Time-bound** — The metric must be directly linked to a strategic objective and must have a defined measurement frequency (e.g., daily, weekly, monthly).

---

#### Question 2: Distinguish between **Leading** and **Lagging** indicators with one example each. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Leading Indicator | Lagging Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Predictive, input-focused | Outcome-focused, historical |
| **Direction** | Looks *forward* in time | Looks *backward* at results |
| **Purpose** | Drives preventive action | Confirms past performance |
| **Example 1** | Employee training hours per quarter | Number of customer complaints per month |
| **Example 2** | Supplier audit score | Warranty claim cost per quarter |

> [!NOTE]
> Leading indicators are the *steering wheel*, while lagging indicators are the *rear-view mirror*.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A: Performance Measurement Frameworks and Balanced Scorecard [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the Balanced Scorecard framework in detail. List all four perspectives with at least **two performance measures** for each perspective. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A manufacturing company has collected the following data for one month: 12,000 units produced, each with 3 defect opportunities, and 72 defects were observed. Calculate the **DPMO**, **First Pass Yield**, and **process Sigma Level**. **[7 Marks]**

##### Model Solution for (a):

The **Balanced Scorecard (BSC)**, developed by **Robert S. Kaplan and David P. Norton (1992)**, is a strategic performance measurement framework that provides a *balanced* view of organizational performance across four interrelated perspectives. It overcomes the limitations of purely financial metrics by incorporating customer, internal process, and learning dimensions.

**The Four Perspectives:**

1. **Financial Perspective** — *"How do we look to our shareholders?"*
   - Return on Investment (ROI)
   - Revenue Growth Rate (%)

2. **Customer Perspective** — *"How do our customers see us?"*
   - Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)
   - Net Promoter Score (NPS)

3. **Internal Business Process Perspective** — *"At what must we excel?"*
   - First Pass Yield (FPY)
   - Cycle Time Reduction (%)

4. **Learning and Growth Perspective** — *"How can we continue to improve?"*
   - Employee Training Hours per Employee
   - Employee Retention Rate (%)
