topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Population and Sample

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — POPULATION AND SAMPLE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Population and Sampling — The Statistical Foundation of Quality Decision Making**
> In Total Quality Management (TQM), **data drives every decision**. Before we can analyze data, control processes, or draw inferences about a product, service, or process, we must first clearly distinguish between what we *want* to study (the population) and what we *actually* observe (the sample).

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

| Term | Formal Definition | Mathematical Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Population** | The **entire set of measurements, items, or observations** about which we wish to draw conclusions. It is sometimes called the *universe* or *target population*. | Represented by size **N** |
| **Sample** | A **finite subset** drawn from the population, on which actual observations are recorded and measurements are taken. | Represented by size **n** |
| **Parameter** | A **numerical characteristic of the population** (a fixed but usually unknown value). | Greek letters: $\mu$, $\sigma$, $p$ |
| **Statistic** | A **numerical characteristic computed from the sample** (a known random variable). | Latin letters: $\bar{x}$, $s$, $\hat{p}$ |
| **Sampling** | The **process of selecting sample elements from the population** so that valid inferences can be made. | — |
| **Sampling Frame** | A **list, map, or physical representation** identifying every element of the population, from which the sample is drawn. | — |
| **Sampling Unit** | The **individual element** in the population or sampling frame. | — |

### 1.2 The Critical Distinction: Parameter vs Statistic

A **parameter** describes the *population* (what we wish we knew), while a **statistic** describes the *sample* (what we actually compute).

$$
\text{Population Parameter: } \mu, \sigma^2, p \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Sample Statistic: } \bar{x}, s^2, \hat{p}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this distinction matters in TQM:** When a quality engineer inspects a batch of 10,000 ball bearings and tests only 50, the engineer computes statistics (e.g., $\bar{x}$ = 12.04 mm) from those 50, but must use these statistics to *infer* the parameters ($\mu$) of the entire batch. **All quality tools — control charts, hypothesis tests, process capability indices — operate on this principle of inferential statistics.**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Soup Tasting Problem"

Imagine a chef has prepared a huge pot of soup containing **10,000 litres**. She wants to know the salt level.

- The **Population** = the entire 10,000-litre pot.
- The **Sample** = a spoonful of soup she actually tastes.
- The **Parameter** = the *true* average salt concentration $\mu$ of the whole pot.
- The **Statistic** = the salt level she *measures* from the spoonful, $\bar{x}$.
- **Sampling** = her method of stirring and spooning — if she stirs well, the spoonful represents the whole pot; if she scoops from the top, it doesn't.

If her tasting is done correctly, the tiny spoonful is *meaningful* about the whole pot. **That is exactly what proper sampling does for quality engineering.** A well-designed control plan can let a manufacturer judge the quality of millions of units by inspecting a few dozen.

### 1.4 Why Sample Instead of Studying the Whole Population?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In TQM practice, **100% inspection is rarely feasible** — and is frequently *less* effective than intelligent sampling. The following five reasons drive every sampling decision:

1. **Cost Reduction** — Inspecting 1,000 items is roughly 100× cheaper than inspecting 100,000.
2. **Time Efficiency** — Sampling delivers data *quickly*, enabling real-time process control.
3. **Destructive Testing** — Some tests (tensile strength, crash tests, fire resistance) destroy the item; 100% inspection is impossible.
4. **Practical Inaccessibility** — Some populations are infinite or unmeasurable (e.g., the true output of an ongoing production line over its lifetime).
5. **Reduced Inspection Error** — Trained inspectors doing fewer, careful checks on a sample often produce more reliable data than fatigued inspectors checking every unit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:** Students often state "sampling is cheaper, so it is less accurate." This is FALSE when *probability sampling* is used — accuracy is preserved *provided the sample is representative*. The error introduced is called **sampling error**, not "inaccuracy."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Population-Sample Relationship

Let us formalize the logic chain used in every quality investigation:

### Step 1 — Define the Population
The population must be defined **in operational terms** before sampling begins. A vague definition ("defective products") is useless; a sharp definition ("units produced by Machine M4 between 1 Jan 2025 and 31 Mar 2025, with part number AB-123, in Carton Size 24") is required.

### Step 2 — Construct the Sampling Frame
The frame must *match* the population definition. **Frame error** (sampling frame ≠ population) is a major source of bias in TQM audits.

### Step 3 — Choose the Sampling Method
The method determines how representative the sample will be. The choice is governed by:
- Variability within the population (heterogeneity)
- Cost and accessibility
- Desired precision (margin of error $e$)
- Whether the sampling is destructive or non-destructive

### Step 4 — Draw the Sample (size $n$)
The sample size $n$ is computed **before** data collection, using either the *precision-based formula* or *power-based formula*.

### Step 5 — Compute Statistics and Infer Parameters
The sample statistics $\bar{x}$, $s$, $\hat{p}$ are calculated and used as **point estimates** of the population parameters $\mu$, $\sigma$, $p$. **Confidence intervals** give a range of plausible values.

### Step 6 — Quantify the Uncertainty
The sampling error is captured by the **Standard Error (SE)**, which decreases as $\sqrt{n}$ increases — *not* linearly with $n$.

## 2.2 Classification of Sampling Techniques

```
                    SAMPLING METHODS
                          │
        ┌─────────────────┴─────────────────┐
        │                                   │
   PROBABILITY                          NON-PROBABILITY
   (Random / Known Chance)               (Judgment-based)
        │                                   │
   ┌────┴────┬─────────┬────────┐      ┌────┴────┬─────────┐
   │         │         │        │      │         │         │
 Simple  Systematic Stratified Cluster  Convenience Judgment Quota
Random
```

### 2.2.1 Probability Sampling Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probability sampling** is the *gold standard* in TQM and Six Sigma because **every element has a known, non-zero probability of selection**. This is what allows the use of confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.

**1. Simple Random Sampling (SRS)**
- Every possible sample of size $n$ has an **equal probability** of being selected.
- Selection is typically done using a **random number table** or a **random number generator**.
- *TQM Example:* From a lot of 5,000 capacitors, randomly select 75 for the reliability audit.

**2. Systematic Sampling**
- Every $k$-th element is selected after a random start.
- The sampling interval is $k = \dfrac{N}{n}$.
- *Caveat:* If the population has a periodic pattern coinciding with $k$, this introduces **selection bias**.
- *TQM Example:* Inspect every 20th item off a conveyor belt to monitor dimensional drift.

**3. Stratified Sampling**
- The population is divided into **homogeneous subgroups (strata)** based on a known characteristic (shift, supplier, machine, region), and a **random sample is drawn from each stratum**.
- Often **proportional allocation** is used: $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h}{N}$.
- *TQM Example:* Test 20 components from Machine A, 20 from Machine B, 20 from Machine C — even if Machine A produces 70% of output.

**4. Cluster Sampling**
- The population is divided into **heterogeneous clusters** (often geographically), and **entire clusters** are randomly selected.
- Used when populations are spread out and individual sampling is too expensive.
- *TQM Example:* Randomly select 5 depots out of 50 to audit for cold-chain compliance.

**5. Multi-Stage Sampling**
- Clusters are selected, then sub-clusters within them, then units within those — a hierarchical approach.
- Common in large-scale national surveys.

### 2.2.2 Non-Probability Sampling Methods

> [!WARNING]
> **Non-probability sampling CANNOT be used for inferential statistics** — no confidence intervals, no hypothesis tests. In TQM, it is restricted to **exploratory** or **pilot** work.

**1. Convenience Sampling** — Sample whoever is easiest to reach (visitors to a feedback kiosk). Low cost, high bias.

**2. Judgment (Purposive) Sampling** — The auditor uses expertise to pick "typical" cases. Used in cause-and-effect investigations.

**3. Quota Sampling** — The auditor fills pre-set quotas per category (e.g., 10 male + 10 female customers). Non-random within categories.

**4. Snowball Sampling** — Respondents refer further respondents. Used in supply-chain mapping for hard-to-trace sub-suppliers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sample Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | Used to estimate population mean $\mu$ |
| 2 | Sample Variance | $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ | **Bessel's correction** with $n-1$ |
| 3 | Sample Standard Deviation | $s = \sqrt{s^2}$ | — |
| 4 | Sample Proportion | $\hat{p} = \dfrac{x}{n}$ | Used to estimate population proportion $p$ |
| 5 | Sampling Interval (Systematic) | $k = \dfrac{N}{n}$ | Round down to nearest integer |
| 6 | Proportional Allocation (Stratified) | $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h}{N}$ | $h = 1, 2, \dots, L$ strata |
| 7 | Sample Size — Mean (Known $\sigma$) | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{e}\right)^2$ | $e$ = margin of error |
| 8 | Sample Size — Proportion | $n = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | Use $p = 0.5$ for max sample size |
| 9 | Standard Error of Mean | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Approx. $\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ from sample |
| 10 | Standard Error of Proportion | $\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{p(1-p)}{n}}$ | Approx. $\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ |
| 11 | Confidence Interval for $\mu$ | $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | 95% → $z = 1.96$; 99% → $z = 2.576$ |
| 12 | Confidence Interval for $p$ | $\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | Requires $n\hat{p} \ge 5$, $n(1-\hat{p}) \ge 5$ |
| 13 | Finite Population Correction | $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{N-n}{N}}$ | Apply when $n/N > 0.05$ |
| 14 | Sampling Error | $e = \vert \bar{x} - \mu \vert$ or $\vert \hat{p} - p \vert$ | Cannot be eliminated, only reduced |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incoming Inspection (Vendor Quality):** Acceptance Sampling Plans like **MIL-STD-105E / ISO 2859-1** (now superseded by **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4**) use stratified sampling principles — different sample sizes for different lot sizes and AQLs.
- **Process Control (SPC):** Control charts assume subgroups are drawn via *rational sampling* — short, frequent samples taken when the process is stable.
- **Six Sigma DMAIC:** The *Measure* phase explicitly requires an **Operational Definition** of the population and a documented Sampling Plan.
- **Reliability Testing:** Highly accelerated life tests sample a small number of components to estimate MTBF across thousands of deployed units.
- **Customer Satisfaction Surveys:** Cluster sampling by region, stratified sampling by customer segment.

---

## 2.5 The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) — The Engine That Makes Sampling Work

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Central Limit Theorem (CLT):** When independent random samples of size $n$ are drawn from *any* population with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$, the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{x}$ approaches a **normal distribution** as $n$ becomes large (typically $n \ge 30$).

Mathematically:

$$
\bar{X} \;\dot\sim\; N\!\left(\mu,\; \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

This is *why* we can use Z-tables and confidence intervals even when the underlying population is skewed (e.g., defect rates, waiting times) — as long as $n$ is sufficient.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Error of the Sample Mean

Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ be **independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.)** random variables drawn from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$.

### Step 3.1.1 — Define the Sample Mean

$$
\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i
$$

### Step 3.1.2 — Compute the Expected Value of $\bar{X}$

By the linearity of expectation:

$$
E(\bar{X}) = E\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right]
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(X_i)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot n\mu = \mu
$$

**Interpretation:** The sample mean is an **unbiased estimator** of the population mean.

### Step 3.1.3 — Compute the Variance of $\bar{X}$

Because the $X_i$ are **independent**, the variance of the sum is the sum of the variances:

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{X}) = \text{Var}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(X_i)
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma^2
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{n^2} \cdot n\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}
$$

### Step 3.1.4 — Take the Square Root to get the Standard Error

$$
\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sqrt{\text{Var}(\bar{X})} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

### Step 3.1.5 — Apply the Central Limit Theorem

For large $n$:

$$
\bar{X} \;\sim\; N\!\left(\mu,\; \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

This is the foundation of the **Z-test for means** and all confidence interval calculations.

## 3.2 Derivation of Sample Size Formula for Estimating a Mean

We wish to estimate $\mu$ such that the **margin of error** $e$ at confidence level $(1-\alpha)$ does not exceed a stipulated value.

### Step 3.2.1 — Margin of Error Condition

For a two-sided confidence interval:

$$
e = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}} = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

### Step 3.2.2 — Solve for $n$

$$
\sqrt{n} = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{e}
$$

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{e}\right)^2
$$

### Step 3.2.3 — Adjust for Finite Population

If $\dfrac{n}{N} > 0.05$, apply the finite population correction:

$$
n_{\text{adj}} = \dfrac{n}{1 + \dfrac{n-1}{N}}
$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — TQM Quality Audit Scenario

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A bearing manufacturer wants to estimate the mean outer diameter of a production lot of $N = 5{,}000$ bearings. Past experience indicates the standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.02$ mm. The quality engineer requires a **margin of error $e = 0.005$ mm** at **95% confidence**.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters**
- $N = 5000$, $\sigma = 0.02$ mm, $e = 0.005$ mm, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

**Step 2 — Apply the basic formula**

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{e}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{1.96 \times 0.02}{0.005}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{0.0392}{0.005}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = (7.84)^2 = 61.4656
$$

**Step 3 — Round up**

$$
n = 62 \text{ bearings}
$$

**Step 4 — Check finite population correction**

$$
\dfrac{n}{N} = \dfrac{62}{5000} = 0.0124 < 0.05
$$

No correction needed. **Sample 62 bearings.**

**Step 5 — Confidence Interval Construction (after sampling)**

Suppose the measured sample mean is $\bar{x} = 25.012$ mm:

$$
CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

$$
= 25.012 \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{0.02}{\sqrt{62}}
$$

$$
= 25.012 \pm 1.96 \cdot 0.00254
$$

$$
= 25.012 \pm 0.00498
$$

$$
= (25.007, \; 25.017) \text{ mm}
$$

**Interpretation:** The engineer is **95% confident** that the true mean outer diameter lies between **25.007 mm and 25.017 mm**. If the design specification is $25.000 \pm 0.020$ mm, the lot passes.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Estimating a Proportion (Defect Rate)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A cable manufacturer receives a shipment of 2,000 cables. A pilot sample of 50 cables reveals 4 defectives. Construct a 99% confidence interval for the true defect rate.

**Step 1 — Calculate sample proportion**

$$
\hat{p} = \dfrac{x}{n} = \dfrac{4}{50} = 0.08
$$

**Step 2 — Check normal approximation validity**

$$
n\hat{p} = 50 \times 0.08 = 4 < 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Approximation is borderline}
$$

$$
n(1-\hat{p}) = 50 \times 0.92 = 46 \ge 5 \quad \text{(OK)}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Error**

$$
\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.08 \times 0.92}{50}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0736}{50}} = \sqrt{0.001472} = 0.03837
$$

**Step 4 — Critical value**

$$
z_{0.005} = 2.576
$$

**Step 5 — Construct the 99% CI**

$$
CI_{99\%} = \hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma_{\hat{p}}
$$

$$
= 0.08 \pm 2.576 \times 0.03837
$$

$$
= 0.08 \pm 0.0988
$$

$$
= (-0.0188, \; 0.1788)
$$

**Step 6 — Truncate the lower bound to 0**

$$
CI_{99\%} = (0, \; 0.1788) \quad \text{or} \quad (0\%, \; 17.88\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** With 99% confidence, the true defect rate is **at most 17.88%**. Given the borderline approximation, the engineer should increase the sample to 100+ before taking binding action.

## 3.5 Sampling Error vs Non-Sampling Error — Analytical Comparison

| Feature | Sampling Error | Non-Sampling Error |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Origin** | Random fluctuation; sample ≠ population | Faulty procedures, instrument drift, bias |
| **Direction** | Random (no systematic bias) | Systematic (one direction) |
| **Reducible by** | Increasing $n$ | Improving process, training, calibration |
| **Eliminable?** | No — only reducible | Yes — through better design |
| **Magnitude** | Measured by SE | Measured by bias |
| **TQM Example** | $\bar{x}$ differs from $\mu$ by chance | Caliper mis-calibrated by $+0.01$ mm |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Population-Sample-Inference Architecture

The following block-level functional architecture maps the complete flow from population definition to parameter inference — the conceptual backbone of every TQM statistical investigation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["POPULATION DEFINITION"] --> B["SAMPLING FRAME CONSTRUCTION"]
    B --> C["CHOOSE SAMPLING METHOD"]
    C --> D{"PROBABILITY OR NON-PROBABILITY"}
    D -->|"PROBABILITY"| E["SIMPLE RANDOM"]
    D -->|"PROBABILITY"| F["SYSTEMATIC"]
    D -->|"PROBABILITY"| G["STRATIFIED"]
    D -->|"PROBABILITY"| H["CLUSTER"]
    D -->|"NON-PROB"| I["CONVENIENCE OR JUDGMENT"]
    E --> J["DRAW SAMPLE OF SIZE N"]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["MEASURE SAMPLE STATISTICS"]
    K --> K1["SAMPLE MEAN XBAR"]
    K --> K2["SAMPLE STD DEV S"]
    K --> K3["SAMPLE PROPORTION PHAT"]
    K1 --> L["COMPUTE STANDARD ERROR"]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M["CONSTRUCT CONFIDENCE INTERVAL"]
    M --> N["INFER POPULATION PARAMETER"]
    N --> O["TQM DECISION"]
    O --> P["ACCEPT LOT"]
    O --> Q["REJECT LOT"]
    O --> R["CONTINUE MONITORING"]
    P --> S["DOCUMENT IN QUALITY RECORD"]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T["FEEDBACK TO PROCESS CONTROL"]
    T --> A
```

## 4.2 Comparison of Probability Sampling Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    POP["POPULATION N"] --> DECIDE{"WHAT IS THE OBJECTIVE"}
    DECIDE -->|"REPRESENTATIVE SINGLE DRAW"| SRS["SIMPLE RANDOM SAMPLING"]
    DECIDE -->|"PERIODIC OUTPUT FLOW"| SYS["SYSTEMATIC SAMPLING"]
    DECIDE -->|"HETEROGENEOUS SUBGROUPS"| STR["STRATIFIED SAMPLING"]
    DECIDE -->|"GEOGRAPHIC DISPERSION"| CLU["CLUSTER SAMPLING"]
    SRS --> PRO1["PRO: UNBIASED, SIMPLE THEORY"]
    SRS --> CON1["CON: NEEDS COMPLETE FRAME"]
    SYS --> PRO2["PRO: EASY TO IMPLEMENT"]
    SYS --> CON2["CON: BIAS IF PERIODICITY EQUALS K"]
    STR --> PRO3["PRO: HIGHER PRECISION PER N"]
    STR --> CON3["CON: NEEDS ACCURATE STRATA INFO"]
    CLU --> PRO4["PRO: LOW COST FOR LARGE AREA"]
    CLU --> CON4["CON: LOWER PRECISION THAN SRS"]
```

## 4.3 The Standard Error Reduction Curve

The diagram below shows how the standard error $\sigma_{\bar{x}}$ shrinks as sample size $n$ grows. Note the **diminishing returns**: doubling $n$ only reduces SE by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, not 2.

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1["N = 1<br>SE = SIGMA"] --> N4["N = 4<br>SE = SIGMA / 2"]
    N4 --> N9["N = 9<br>SE = SIGMA / 3"]
    N9 --> N16["N = 16<br>SE = SIGMA / 4"]
    N16 --> N25["N = 25<br>SE = SIGMA / 5"]
    N25 --> N100["N = 100<br>SE = SIGMA / 10"]
    N100 --> N400["N = 400<br>SE = SIGMA / 20"]
    N400 --> NOTE["DIMINISHING RETURNS<br>4X EFFORT GIVES ONLY 2X PRECISION"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["NEW TQM AUDIT REQUIRED"] --> Q1{"IS 100% INSPECTION FEASIBLE?"}

    Q3 -->|"YES"| SYS["USE SYSTEMATIC SAMPLING"]

    SRS --> CALC
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a population and a sample. Why is sampling preferred over census (100% inspection) in Total Quality Management?**

**Model Answer:**

A **population** is the entire group of items, measurements, or observations about which information is desired, represented by size **N**. A **sample** is a subset of the population actually selected for measurement, represented by size **n**.

Sampling is preferred over census inspection in TQM for the following reasons:

| # | Reason | Explanation |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Lower Cost** | Inspecting fewer items requires fewer inspectors, less equipment time, and reduced material handling. |
| 2 | **Faster Results** | Sample data can be collected and analysed in a fraction of the time, enabling timely process control decisions. |
| 3 | **Destructive Testing** | Tests like tensile strength, hardness, or flammability destroy the item — making 100% inspection impossible. |
| 4 | **Reduced Inspection Error** | Trained inspectors performing fewer checks on a sample yield more reliable data than fatigued inspectors checking every unit. |
| 5 | **Infinite or Inaccessible Populations** | Some populations (e.g., ongoing production output) are effectively infinite or unmeasurable as a whole. |

**Conclusion:** When conducted via *probability sampling*, the sample yields statistically valid inferences about the population at substantially lower cost and time.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the difference between a parameter and a statistic with two examples each. Why are Greek letters used for parameters and Roman letters for statistics?**

**Model Answer:**

A **parameter** is a numerical descriptive measure of a *population*. It is fixed (though usually unknown) because the population itself is fixed. Parameters are conventionally denoted by **Greek letters**.

A **statistic** is a numerical descriptive measure computed from a *sample*. It is a random variable because its value depends on which sample is drawn. Statistics are conventionally denoted by **Roman (Latin) letters**.

| Concept | Population (Parameter) | Sample (Statistic) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mean | $\mu$ (mu) | $\bar{x}$ (x-bar) |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma$ (sigma) | $s$ |
| Variance | $\sigma^2$ | $s^2$ |
| Proportion | $p$ | $\hat{p}$ (p-hat) |

**Examples:**
1. The **true mean tensile strength** of all 1,00,000 bolts produced in a month ($\mu$) is a parameter; the **mean tensile strength** of the 60 bolts tested ($\bar{x}$) is a statistic.
2. The **true defect rate** of all incoming components ($p$) is a parameter; the **defect rate** observed in the inspected lot of 200 ($\hat{p}$) is a statistic.

The Greek-vs-Latin convention is a **universally accepted shorthand** in inferential statistics, instantly signalling whether a number describes the *universe under study* (Greek, often unknown) or the *observed data* (Latin, always known). This prevents ambiguity in formulae such as $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student attempts **ONE full question**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define **probability sampling**. Explain the following four probability sampling techniques with a TQM example for each:
   (i) Simple Random Sampling
   (ii) Systematic Sampling
   (iii) Stratified Sampling
   (iv) Cluster Sampling

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company produces tablets in batches of $N = 10{,}000$. A quality audit requires a 95% confidence interval estimate of the mean tablet weight. From past data, $\sigma = 0.08$ g. The acceptable margin of error is $e = 0.01$ g.
   (i) Calculate the required sample size.
   (ii) If the sample drawn yields $\bar{x} = 0.502$ g, construct the 95% confidence interval.
   (iii) Comment on the result if the design specification is $0.500 \pm 0.030$ g.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Probability Sampling — Definition [1 mark]:**
Probability sampling is a sampling technique in which **every element of the population has a known, non-zero probability of being selected**, and the selection is made by a randomized procedure. This permits valid statistical inference using confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.

**(a)(i) Simple Random Sampling (SRS) [1.5 marks]:**
Every possible sample of size $n$ drawn from a population of size $N$ has an equal chance of being selected. Selection is performed using a **random number table** or **computer-generated random numbers**.
**TQM Example:** From a lot of 5,000 capacitors, assign each a number from 0001 to 5000, then use a random number generator to pick 75 unique numbers. Inspect those 75 capacitors.

**(a)(ii) Systematic Sampling [1.5 marks]:**
After a random start, every $k$-th element of the population is selected, where the sampling interval is:

$$
k = \dfrac{N}{n}
$$

**TQM Example:** On a bottling line producing 1,000 bottles per shift, randomly select the first bottle, then every 10th bottle (so $k = 10$). This yields 100 bottles for inspection without requiring a random number table.

**Caveat:** If the production line has a periodic defect occurring every 10th bottle, this method introduces bias. The auditor should verify $k$ does not coincide with any known periodic pattern.

**(a)(iii) Stratified Sampling [1.5 marks]:**
The population is partitioned into **homogeneous subgroups (strata)** based on a known attribute (machine, shift, supplier). A random sample is drawn from each stratum, often in proportion to its size:

$$
n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h}{N}
$$

**TQM Example:** A factory operates 3 machines (A, B, C) producing bearings. Even if Machine A produces 70% of output, the auditor draws 20 bearings from each machine — ensuring all machines are evaluated. This improves precision because within-stratum variability is lower than overall variability.

**(a)(iv) Cluster Sampling [1.5 marks]:**
The population is divided into **heterogeneous clusters** (often geographical or organizational), and **entire clusters** are randomly selected for full inspection. Useful when the population is widely dispersed.

**TQM Example:** A nationwide FMCG company wants to audit cold-chain compliance of its ice-cream distribution. Instead of sampling individual retail outlets across India, it randomly selects 8 out of 80 depots and audits all outlets served by those 8 depots.

---

**(b)(i) Sample Size Calculation [4 marks]:**

**Given:** $N = 10{,}000$, $\sigma = 0.08$ g, $e = 0.01$ g, 95% confidence → $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

**Applying the formula:**

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{e}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{1.96 \times 0.08}{0.01}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{0.1568}{0.01}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = (15.68)^2
$$

$$
n = 245.8624
$$

**Round up:**

$$
\boxed{n = 246 \text{ tablets}}
$$

**Check finite population correction:**

$$
\dfrac{n}{N} = \dfrac{246}{10{,}000} = 0.0246 < 0.05
$$

Correction not required.

---

**(b)(ii) 95% Confidence Interval [4 marks]:**

**Given:** $\bar{x} = 0.502$ g, $n = 246$, $\sigma = 0.08$ g, $z = 1.96$.

**Standard Error:**

$$
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{0.08}{\sqrt{246}} = \dfrac{0.08}{15.6844} = 0.005101
$$

**Margin of Error:**

$$
E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma_{\bar{x}} = 1.96 \times 0.005101 = 0.009998 \approx 0.010
$$

**Confidence Interval:**

$$
CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm E = 0.502 \pm 0.010
$$

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} = (0.492, \; 0.512) \text{ g}}
$$

---

**(b)(iii) Interpretation [2 marks]:**

The design specification is $0.500 \pm 0.030$ g, i.e., the acceptance range is $[0.470, \; 0.530]$ g.

The entire 95% confidence interval $(0.492, \; 0.512)$ **lies well within** the specification range $[0.470, \; 0.530]$. Therefore, **at 95% confidence, the batch meets the weight specification** and can be accepted for release. No corrective action is required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **sampling error**. Differentiate between **sampling error and non-sampling error**. Discuss the **Central Limit Theorem** and its significance in TQM quality inference.

**(b)** An electronic component manufacturer receives a large lot of resistors. A sample of $n = 400$ resistors is tested. The sample mean resistance is $\bar{x} = 102.3$ ohms, and the sample standard deviation is $s = 4.0$ ohms.
   (i) Construct a 99% confidence interval for the true mean resistance.
   (ii) The nominal resistance is 100 ohms with a tolerance of $\pm 5$ ohms. Does the lot meet specification?
   (iii) If the engineer wants a margin of error of $e = 0.5$ ohm at 99% confidence, what sample size is required?

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Sampling Error — Concept [1.5 marks]:**

**Sampling error** is the **difference between a sample statistic and the corresponding population parameter** that arises purely because we are observing only a part of the population, not the whole. It is an inherent, random, unavoidable consequence of sampling.

$$
\text{Sampling Error} = \vert \bar{x} - \mu \vert \quad \text{or} \quad \vert \hat{p} - p \vert
$$

It can be **reduced** (by increasing $n$) but **never eliminated** as long as we sample.

**(a) Sampling Error vs Non-Sampling Error [3 marks]:**

| Aspect | Sampling Error | Non-Sampling Error |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cause** | Random fluctuation due to sample variability | Faulty measurement, bias, frame errors, non-response |
| **Direction** | Random (no systematic tendency) | Usually systematic (one direction) |
| **Reducible** | Yes, by increasing $n$ | Yes, by improving procedures, calibration, training |
| **Eliminable** | No — only reducible | Yes, in principle |
| **Quantification** | Standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ | Bias estimate |
| **Example** | $\bar{x}$ of 30 widgets differs from $\mu$ by chance | Caliper mis-calibrated by $+0.02$ mm shifts every reading |

**(a) Central Limit Theorem & Significance [2.5 marks]:**

**Statement of CLT:** When independent random samples of size $n$ are drawn from *any* population (with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$), the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ approaches a normal distribution as $n$ grows large (typically $n \ge 30$):

$$
\bar{X} \;\dot\sim\; N\!\left(\mu,\; \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

**Significance in TQM:**

1. **Normality Justification:** Quality data (tensile strength, diameters, weights) need not be normally distributed in the population. The CLT allows us to *assume* normality of $\bar{x}$ for inference.
2. **Foundation of Control Charts:** The $\bar{X}$ and $\bar{R}$ charts rely on $\bar{x}$ being normally distributed around $\mu$ with known standard error.
3. **Confidence Intervals & Hypothesis Tests:** All Z-based and t-based procedures are derived from CLT.
4. **Predictable Variability:** Knowing $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ lets engineers quantify uncertainty and design sample sizes precisely.

---

**(b)(i) 99% Confidence Interval [4 marks]:**

**Given:** $n = 400$, $\bar{x} = 102.3$ ohms, $s = 4.0$ ohms, 99% confidence.

Since $n = 400$ is large, we use the Z-approximation (or equivalently $t$ with 399 df, which converges to Z):

**Critical value:**

$$
z_{0.005} = 2.576
$$

**Standard Error:**

$$
SE = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{4.0}{\sqrt{400}} = \dfrac{4.0}{20} = 0.20 \text{ ohms}
$$

**Margin of Error:**

$$
E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE = 2.576 \times 0.20 = 0.5152 \text{ ohms}
$$

**Confidence Interval:**

$$
CI_{99\%} = \bar{x} \pm E = 102.3 \pm 0.5152
$$

$$
\boxed{CI_{99\%} = (101.785, \; 102.815) \text{ ohms}}
$$

---

**(b)(ii) Specification Check [3 marks]:**

The nominal specification is $100 \pm 5$ ohms, i.e., the acceptable range is $[95, \; 105]$ ohms.

The entire 99% confidence interval $(101.785, \; 102.815)$ lies **well within** the specification range $[95, \; 105]$. The lower bound (101.785) is far above 95, and the upper bound (102.815) is far below 105.

**Conclusion:** **The lot meets the resistance specification at 99% confidence level.** The lot can be accepted.

---

**(b)(iii) Required Sample Size [3 marks]:**

**Given:** $e = 0.5$ ohms, $\sigma \approx s = 4.0$ ohms, 99% confidence → $z = 2.576$.

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{e}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{2.576 \times 4.0}{0.5}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = \left(\dfrac{10.304}{0.5}\right)^2
$$

$$
n = (20.608)^2
$$

$$
n = 424.69
$$

**Round up:**

$$
\boxed{n = 425 \text{ resistors}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in Population & Sample questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $z$ with $t$:** When $\sigma$ is given (even if population is small), use $z$. When only $s$ from a small sample is available AND $\sigma$ is unknown, use $t$. Many students wrongly apply $t = 2.0$ when $z = 1.96$ is intended.
> 2. **Forgetting to ROUND UP sample sizes:** Always round $n$ **UP**. Computing $n = 245.86$ and writing $n = 245$ makes the margin of error exceed the requirement — this is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring the Finite Population Correction (FPC):** When $n/N > 0.05$, the SE formula must include the $\sqrt{(N-n)/N}$ factor. Students often skip this and lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Significance level vs Confidence level confusion:** 95% confidence uses $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, NOT $z_{0.05} = 1.645$. The $\alpha/2$ split is mandatory for two-sided intervals.
> 5. **Truncation of negative CI bounds:** When $\hat{p}$ is small, the lower confidence bound can be negative. Always truncate it to 0; never report a negative percentage.
> 6. **"Sample = Sample Size":** Sample is the *set of items selected*; sample size ($n$) is the *count*. Examiners penalize loose terminology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Re-read 24 hours before the exam:**

- [ ] **Population = N, Sample = n.** Parameters = Greek letters ($\mu, \sigma, p$). Statistics = Latin letters ($\bar{x}, s, \hat{p}$).
- [ ] **Five Reasons for Sampling:** Cost, Time, Destructive Testing, Infinite Population, Reduced Fatigue Error.
- [ ] **Probability Sampling Required for Inference:** SRS, Systematic, Stratified, Cluster.
- [ ] **Stratified Sampling** = homogeneous subgroups → higher precision for same $n$.
- [ ] **Cluster Sampling** = heterogeneous clusters → lower precision, cheaper for dispersed populations.
- [ ] **Systematic Sampling Interval:** $k = N/n$. Watch for periodicity bias.
- [ ] **Sample Mean:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$.
- [ ] **Sample Variance:** $s^2 = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ — uses $n-1$ (Bessel's correction).
- [ ] **Standard Error of Mean:** $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ — decreases as $\sqrt{n}$, NOT as $n$.
- [ ] **Sample Size for Mean:** $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{e}\right)^2$. Always **round up**.
- [ ] **Sample Size for Proportion:** $n = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$. Use $p = 0.5$ for maximum sample size when $p$ is unknown.
- [ ] **Confidence Interval for $\mu$:** $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.
- [ ] **Confidence Interval for $p$:** $\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$.
- [ ] **Critical Values:** 90% → 1.645, 95% → 1.96, 99% → 2.576.
- [ ] **FPC Apply Rule:** When $n/N > 0.05$, multiply SE by $\sqrt{(N-n)/N}$.
- [ ] **CLT Statement:** For large $n$ (≥30), $\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2/n)$ regardless of population shape.
- [ ] **Sampling Error** is random and reducible by larger $n$. **Non-sampling error** is systematic and reducible by better procedure.
- [ ] **Destructive tests** (tensile, crash, fire) → sampling is mandatory, not optional.
- [ ] **Stratified Sampling Allocation:** Proportional: $n_h = n \cdot \dfrac{N_h}{N}$.
- [ ] **In TQM** — sampling underpins SPC control charts, acceptance sampling (Z1.4), Six Sigma Measure phase, and reliability life testing.
- [ ] **Watch the units** of $\sigma$, $\bar{x}$, and $e$ — they must be identical.
- [ ] **Always state the interpretation** of every CI in plain English (e.g., "We are 95% confident that the true mean lies between...").
- [ ] **Quotable Definition:** "Sampling is the process of selecting a representative subset of a population to estimate characteristics of the whole population without examining every element."
- [ ] **Quotable Definition:** "A parameter is to a population what a statistic is to a sample — but parameters are unknown constants, while statistics are known random variables."
- [ ] **In the KTU 2024 scheme**, this topic typically carries 7 marks in Part A (definitions + comparisons) and 7–14 marks in Part B (calculations + interpretation).

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Population and Sample**
*PESFT746 — Total Quality Management | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normal Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Curve (Normal Distribution / Gaussian Distribution):**
> The Normal Curve is a continuous, symmetrical, bell-shaped probability distribution function that is fundamentally defined by **two parameters**: the **arithmetic mean ($\mu$)** which locates the centre, and the **standard deviation ($\sigma$)** which controls the spread (dispersion) of the distribution. It is the most critical probability model in **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** and **Total Quality Management (TQM)** because most quality characteristics of manufactured products — such as dimensions, weight, strength, hardness, and tensile properties — tend to follow this distribution when the process is in a state of statistical control.

The mathematical Probability Density Function (PDF) is expressed as:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}$$

where:
- $f(x)$ = probability density at value $x$
- $\mu$ = population mean (location parameter)
- $\sigma$ = population standard deviation (scale parameter, must be $> 0$)
- $\pi \approx 3.14159$ and $e \approx 2.71828$ (Euler's number)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Coffee Shop":**
> Imagine a coffee shop that fills cups throughout the day. If you measure the actual volume (ml) of coffee in, say, 10,000 cups, the cup volumes will not all be exactly the advertised 200 ml — some will have 198 ml, some 203 ml, but **most will be very close to 200 ml**. If you plot a histogram of these measurements, you will see a perfectly bell-shaped curve:
> - **The peak** (tallest bar) sits exactly at 200 ml → this is the **mean ($\mu$)**.
> - **The width** of the bell tells you how consistent the filling process is → this is governed by **$\sigma$**.
> - **Symmetry**: cups just over-filled and just under-filled are equally likely → the curve is perfectly symmetric about the mean.
>
> This bell shape is the **Normal Curve**. It tells quality engineers *how often* a defect will appear and *how predictable* the manufacturing process really is.

## 1.3 Why Normal Curve is the Backbone of TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In TQM, the Normal Curve underpins the following critical tools:
> 1. **Six Sigma Methodology** — process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) rely on the assumption of normality.
> 2. **Statistical Process Control (SPC) Charts** — the $\bar{X}$-chart and $R$-chart use $\pm 3\sigma$ control limits derived from the normal distribution.
> 3. **Acceptance Sampling Plans** — OC (Operating Characteristic) curves are constructed using normal-area integrals.
> 4. **Process Capability Analysis** — the proportion of output within specification limits (USL, LSL) is computed using normal-curve areas.

**Key Standard Metric Highlighted:**
> The constant **$1/\sigma\sqrt{2\pi} \approx 0.3989/\sigma$** is the **maximum height** of the standard normal density curve, occurring exactly at $x = \mu$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Properties of the Normal Curve (Logical Breakdown)

The normal curve possesses **seven classical properties** that every KTU student must memorize:

1. **Bell-Shaped and Unimodal** — The curve has exactly one peak (mode) located at the centre $x = \mu$.
2. **Perfectly Symmetrical** — The left half is a mirror image of the right half about the vertical line $x = \mu$.
3. **Asymptotic to the X-axis** — Both tails approach the horizontal axis but never touch it (theoretically extends to $\pm \infty$).
4. **Mean = Median = Mode** — All three measures of central tendency coincide at the centre.
5. **Total Area = 1** — The entire area under the curve equals unity (100% probability).
6. **Inflection Points at $\mu \pm \sigma$** — The curvature changes from concave-down to concave-up at these two points.
7. **Empirical Rule (68–95–99.7 Rule)** — Approximately **68.27%**, **95.45%**, and **99.73%** of the area lies within $\pm 1\sigma$, $\pm 2\sigma$, and $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean respectively.

## 2.2 Standard Normal Distribution & Z-Transformation

Because every normal distribution has its own $\mu$ and $\sigma$, comparing different processes is difficult. To solve this, we transform any normal variable $X$ into the **Standard Normal Variable $Z$** using the linear transformation:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

After transformation, the standard normal distribution has **$\mu = 0$** and **$\sigma = 1$**, and we use the **Standard Normal Table** (Z-table) to find areas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we need Z-scores in TQM:** Suppose Line A produces shafts with $\mu_A = 10.02$ mm and Line B produces shafts with $\mu_B = 9.98$ mm, each with different $\sigma$ values. By converting both to $Z$, the quality engineer can directly compare the relative position of any single measurement across two completely different processes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Normal PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}$ | Valid for all $x \in (-\infty, +\infty)$; requires $\sigma > 0$ |
| 2 | Standardization (Z-score) | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Resulting distribution: $\mu_Z = 0$, $\sigma_Z = 1$ |
| 3 | Reverse Z-transform | $X = \mu + Z\sigma$ | Used to find raw value from known Z |
| 4 | Empirical Rule — $\pm 1\sigma$ | Area $\approx 0.6827$ (68.27%) | Within $1\sigma$ of mean |
| 5 | Empirical Rule — $\pm 2\sigma$ | Area $\approx 0.9545$ (95.45%) | Within $2\sigma$ of mean |
| 6 | Empirical Rule — $\pm 3\sigma$ | Area $\approx 0.9973$ (99.73%) | Used for SPC control limits |
| 7 | Six Sigma Spread | $\pm 6\sigma$ from mean | Corresponds to $3.4$ defects per million opportunities (DPMO) |
| 8 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Assumes process is centred and normal |
| 9 | Off-Centre Capability | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma},\, \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Accounts for process shift |
| 10 | Tail Probability | $P(X > a) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\Phi(\cdot)$ = standard normal CDF |
| 11 | Symmetry Identity | $P(Z \le -z) = P(Z \ge z)$ | $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Safety Note:** All absolute-value and conditional notations inside the table above use $\vert$ or $\min(\cdot)$ syntax to prevent Markdown table-pipe collisions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In high-volume manufacturing industries (automotive, semiconductor, aerospace), the Normal Curve is the **statistical foundation of Six Sigma quality**. A "Six Sigma process" is engineered so that the specification limits (USL/LSL) sit **$6\sigma$ away from the process mean**, allowing only **3.4 defects per million opportunities** even when the process mean drifts by $1.5\sigma$. This extraordinary defect-reduction capability — derived directly from normal-curve area calculations — is what made Motorola, GE, and Toyota achieve world-class quality status.

## 2.5 Three Important Quality Zones on the Normal Curve

- **Zone A (Central $\pm 1\sigma$):** contains the bulk of conforming output — the "common-cause" zone.
- **Zone B ($\pm 1\sigma$ to $\pm 3\sigma$):** warning region — output is less likely and requires attention.
- **Zone C (beyond $\pm 3\sigma$):** rare event zone — only **0.27%** of output lies here. In SPC, any point falling in this zone signals a **special-cause variation**, triggering investigation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Area Under the Normal Curve Equals 1

We must prove that $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$.

Let $I = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} dx$.

**Step 1.** Apply the substitution $u = \frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}$, so $du = \frac{1}{\sigma} dx$, i.e., $dx = \sigma\, du$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-u^{2}/2}\, (\sigma\, du) \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-u^{2}/2}\, du
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate $J = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-u^{2}/2}\, du$ using the Gaussian integral trick. Consider $J^{2}$:

$$
J^{2} = \left(\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-u^{2}/2}\, du\right)\left(\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-v^{2}/2}\, dv\right)
$$

**Step 3.** Combine into a double integral over the $uv$-plane:

$$
J^{2} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-(u^{2}+v^{2})/2}\, du\, dv
$$

**Step 4.** Switch to polar coordinates: $u = r\cos\theta$, $v = r\sin\theta$, with Jacobian $r$. Then $u^{2} + v^{2} = r^{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
J^{2} &= \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-r^{2}/2}\, r\, dr\, d\theta \\
&= 2\pi \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-r^{2}/2}\, r\, dr
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $w = r^{2}/2$, so $dw = r\, dr$. The integral becomes:

$$
J^{2} = 2\pi \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-w}\, dw = 2\pi \cdot \big[-e^{-w}\big]_{0}^{\infty} = 2\pi(0 - (-1)) = 2\pi
$$

**Step 6.** Therefore $J = \sqrt{2\pi}$, and:

$$
I = \frac{J}{\sqrt{2\pi}} = \frac{\sqrt{2\pi}}{\sqrt{2\pi}} = 1 \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Interpretation:** The total area under any normal curve equals **unity**, confirming that the entire probability mass sums to 1 (100%).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Empirical Rule ($68 - 95 - 99.7$)

We compute the area inside $\pm k\sigma$ using the Z-transformation, $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$.

**Step 1.** For $\pm 1\sigma$:

$$
P(\mu - \sigma \le X \le \mu + \sigma) = P(-1 \le Z \le 1) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1)
$$

**Step 2.** Using $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$:

$$
\Phi(1) - \Phi(-1) = \Phi(1) - [1 - \Phi(1)] = 2\Phi(1) - 1
$$

**Step 3.** From standard normal tables, $\Phi(1) \approx 0.8413$.

$$
2(0.8413) - 1 = 0.6826 \approx 68.27\%
$$

**Step 4.** Repeat for $\pm 2\sigma$: $\Phi(2) \approx 0.9772$, so $2(0.9772) - 1 = 0.9544 \approx 95.45\%$.

**Step 5.** Repeat for $\pm 3\sigma$: $\Phi(3) \approx 0.9987$, so $2(0.9987) - 1 = 0.9974 \approx 99.74\%$.

**Summary Table:**

| Spread | Probability (Area) | % of Output |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| $\mu \pm \sigma$ | $0.6826$ | $68.27\%$ |
| $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ | $0.9544$ | $95.45\%$ |
| $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ | $0.9974$ | $99.74\%$ |

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Z-Score Application

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A piston ring has an inner diameter $X \sim N(\mu = 75 \text{ mm},\, \sigma = 0.02 \text{ mm})$. Specification requires the diameter to be between $74.96$ mm and $75.04$ mm. Find the probability that a randomly selected piston ring **conforms** to specifications.

**Step 1.** Convert lower spec limit to $Z$:

$$
Z_{L} = \frac{74.96 - 75}{0.02} = \frac{-0.04}{0.02} = -2.00
$$

**Step 2.** Convert upper spec limit to $Z$:

$$
Z_{U} = \frac{75.04 - 75}{0.02} = \frac{+0.04}{0.02} = +2.00
$$

**Step 3.** Required probability:

$$
P(74.96 \le X \le 75.04) = P(-2.00 \le Z \le +2.00)
$$

**Step 4.** Using symmetry and Z-tables:

$$
P(-2 \le Z \le 2) = 2\Phi(2) - 1 = 2(0.9772) - 1 = 0.9544
$$

**Step 5.** Interpretation: **$95.44\%$** of piston rings meet the specification; the **defect rate is $4.56\%$**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Probability Patterns

| Pattern Type | Condition | Equivalent Z-form | Probability Method |
|--------------|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Within bounds | $a \le X \le b$ | $z_1 \le Z \le z_2$ | $\Phi(z_2) - \Phi(z_1)$ |
| Exceeds upper | $X > b$ | $Z > z_2$ | $1 - \Phi(z_2)$ |
| Below lower | $X < a$ | $Z < z_1$ | $\Phi(z_1)$ |
| Outside bounds | $X < a$ or $X > b$ | $\vert Z \vert > \vert z \vert$ | $2\big[1 - \Phi(\vert z \vert)\big]$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Normal Curve — Annotated Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Process Input X] --> B[Z Transformation: Z = X minus mu over sigma]
    B --> C[Standard Normal Distribution mu = 0, sigma = 1]
    C --> D{Compare to Limits}
    D --> E[Zone 1: Within plus minus 1 sigma: 68.27 percent]
    D --> F[Zone 2: Within 1 sigma to 2 sigma: 13.59 percent each side]
    D --> G[Zone 3: Within 2 sigma to 3 sigma: 2.14 percent each side]
    D --> H[Zone 4: Beyond 3 sigma: 0.135 percent each tail]
    E --> I[Quality Decision: Conforming Output]
    F --> I
    G --> J[Quality Decision: Marginal Output]
    H --> K[Quality Decision: Defect / Special Cause Alarm]
    I --> L[SPC Control Chart Update]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Trigger Corrective Action in TQM]
    L --> N[Process Capability Cp and Cpk Recalculation]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Continuous Improvement Kaizen Loop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart mirrors how a quality engineer actually uses the Normal Curve in practice. Raw process data is standardized, classified into zones, and converted into actionable quality decisions. Notice how Zone 4 (beyond $\pm 3\sigma$) automatically triggers a corrective action — this is the heart of statistical process control.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Normal-Curve-Based Quality Systems

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Manufacturing Process] --> B[Data Acquisition Sensors]
    B --> C[Sample Mean and Sigma Calculator]
    C --> D[Normality Test Shapiro Wilk]
    D -->|Normal Confirmed| E[Z Score Engine]
    D -->|Not Normal| F[Transformation Engine log or Box Cox]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Probability Lookup Z Table]
    G --> H[Conformance Probability Output]
    H --> I[Six Sigma DPMO Calculator]
    H --> J[Process Capability Cp and Cpk]
    H --> K[SPC Control Limits UCL and LCL]
    I --> L[Quality Dashboard TQM]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Management Decision Support]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This architecture shows how the Normal Curve is not a stand-alone formula but the **central engine** feeding multiple TQM tools (Six Sigma, Capability Indices, SPC Charts) simultaneously. Every block downstream ultimately depends on the validity of the normality assumption verified in block **D**.

## 4.3 Conceptual Visualization — Bell Curve with Six Sigma Spread

```
                       +---------------------------------------------------------------+
                       |                  NORMAL CURVE (μ, σ)                          |
                       |                                                             |
            Height     |                          ___                                 |
                       |                       _.´     `._                            |
            0.4 f(x)   |                    _.´             `_.                      |
                       |                  _.´                   `_.                    |
                       |                _´      +---------+        `_                  |
                       |              _´        |  μ = 0  |          `_                |
            0.2 f(x)   |             /         +---------+           \              |
                       |            /              68.27%                \             |
                       |          _´              within ±1σ              `_          |
                       |        _´                                         `_        |
            0.0 f(x)   |_______/________________________________________________\____|
                       |   -6σ   -4σ   -2σ   0σ   +2σ   +4σ   +6σ                    |
                       |                                                             |
                       |  <--3σ zone-->|<--conform--->|<--3σ zone-->                 |
                       |     0.135% each             99.73% within ±3σ               |
                       +---------------------------------------------------------------+
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Normal Curve. State any four of its important properties.**

**Model Answer:**
The Normal Curve is a continuous, symmetrical, bell-shaped probability distribution defined by the PDF $f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}$, where $\mu$ is the mean and $\sigma$ is the standard deviation.

Four important properties:
1. The curve is **bell-shaped, unimodal and perfectly symmetric** about the mean.
2. **Mean = Median = Mode**, all located at the centre.
3. The **total area under the curve equals 1** (unity probability).
4. The curve is **asymptotic to the X-axis** — tails extend infinitely but never touch the axis.
*(Any four properties from the seven listed in Section 2.1 are acceptable.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the Empirical Rule (68–95–99.7 Rule) with reference to the Normal Curve.**

**Model Answer:**
The Empirical Rule states that for any normally distributed data:
- Approximately **$68.27\%$** of observations lie within **$\mu \pm 1\sigma$**.
- Approximately **$95.45\%$** of observations lie within **$\mu \pm 2\sigma$**.
- Approximately **$99.73\%$** of observations lie within **$\mu \pm 3\sigma$**.

This rule is extensively used in TQM for setting **Statistical Process Control (SPC) chart limits**, typically at $\pm 3\sigma$ from the process mean, since $99.73\%$ of in-control process output is expected to fall within these bounds.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the probability density function (PDF) of the normal distribution and explain its importance in Total Quality Management. *(**7 Marks**)*

**(b)** A process produces resistors with resistance normally distributed having $\mu = 100\,\Omega$ and $\sigma = 2\,\Omega$. Specifications require resistance to be between $96\,\Omega$ and $104\,\Omega$. Compute:
  (i) the probability that a resistor conforms to specification,
  (ii) the process capability index $C_p$,
  (iii) the defect rate per million opportunities (DPMO).
*(**7 Marks**)*

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

The normal PDF arises as the limiting case of the **binomial distribution** when $n \to \infty$ with mean $np$ and variance $npq$. The standard derivation (De Moivre–Laplace Theorem) yields:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}
$$

**Importance in TQM:**
1. Most quality characteristics (length, weight, hardness, tensile strength) of stable processes follow this distribution.
2. It provides the **theoretical foundation for SPC control charts** ($\pm 3\sigma$ limits).
3. It enables **process capability analysis** ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$).
4. It is the statistical basis of the **Six Sigma methodology**.
5. It allows **defect-rate prediction** through normal-curve area calculations.

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Given:** $\mu = 100\,\Omega$, $\sigma = 2\,\Omega$, $LSL = 96\,\Omega$, $USL = 104\,\Omega$.

**(i) Conformance Probability:**

$$
Z_{L} = \frac{96 - 100}{2} = -2.00, \quad Z_{U} = \frac{104 - 100}{2} = +2.00
$$

$$
P(\text{conforming}) = P(-2 \le Z \le 2) = 2\Phi(2) - 1 = 2(0.9772) - 1 = 0.9544
$$

**Conformance probability $= 95.44\%$**.

**(ii) Process Capability Index:**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{104 - 96}{6 \times 2} = \frac{8}{12} = 0.667
$$

**$C_p = 0.667 < 1.0$** indicates the process is **not capable** — the specification spread is too tight for the natural process variation.

**(iii) DPMO Calculation:**

Defect rate per unit = $1 - 0.9544 = 0.0456$, i.e., $4.56\%$.

$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.0456 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 45{,}600 \text{ defects per million}
$$

**DPMO $= 45{,}600$**, which corresponds roughly to **$3.5\sigma$ quality level** (not Six Sigma).

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **Standard Normal Distribution** and the Z-transformation. Show how any normal variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$ can be transformed to $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. *(**7 Marks**)*

**(b)** A bottling plant fills 500 ml bottles. The actual fill volume is normally distributed with $\mu = 502$ ml and $\sigma = 1.5$ ml. A bottle is rejected if it contains less than 498 ml or more than 506 ml.
  (i) Find the percentage of bottles that will be rejected.
  (ii) If the company wants only 1% rejection, what is the maximum allowable $\sigma$ (keeping $\mu$ unchanged)?
*(**7 Marks**)*

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

The **Standard Normal Distribution** is a special case of the normal distribution with mean $\mu_Z = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma_Z = 1$. Its PDF simplifies to:

$$
\phi(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-z^{2}/2}
$$

**Z-transformation derivation:**

Let $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2})$. Define the linear transformation $Z = aX + b$. We require $E[Z] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(Z) = 1$:

$$
E[Z] = a\mu + b = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; b = -a\mu
$$

$$
\text{Var}(Z) = a^{2}\sigma^{2} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; a = \frac{1}{\sigma} \;(\sigma > 0)
$$

Substituting back:

$$
Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

This transformation is **area-preserving** (Jacobian $= 1/\sigma$), so all probability calculations remain valid. The resulting $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ enables the use of universal **Z-tables** regardless of the original units.

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**(i) Rejection Percentage:**

Lower bound: $Z_L = \frac{498 - 502}{1.5} = -2.667$

Upper bound: $Z_U = \frac{506 - 502}{1.5} = +2.667$

Probability conforming:

$$
P(-2.667 \le Z \le 2.667) \approx 2\Phi(2.67) - 1 = 2(0.9962) - 1 = 0.9924
$$

Rejection probability $= 1 - 0.9924 = 0.0076 = 0.76\%$.

**Approximately $0.76\%$ of bottles will be rejected.**

**(ii) Maximum $\sigma$ for 1% rejection:**

1% rejection means $99\%$ conformance, so each tail must carry $0.5\%$, i.e., $\Phi(Z) = 0.99$.

From Z-tables, $Z_{0.99} \approx 2.326$.

Setting the upper limit bound:

$$
Z_{U} = \frac{506 - 502}{\sigma} = 2.326 \;\Rightarrow\; \sigma = \frac{4}{2.326} \approx 1.72 \text{ ml}
$$

**Maximum allowable $\sigma \approx 1.72$ ml** (up from current 1.5 ml, surprisingly allowing slightly higher variation — but only because $\mu = 502$ is offset from centre, giving asymmetric tolerance. Recomputing symmetrically: $\sigma_{\max} = 4 / 2.326 \approx 1.72$ ml.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting symmetry identity:** Many students fail to use $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$ and end up with negative probabilities.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in Z-score:** Always verify that $\mu$, $X$, and $\sigma$ are in the **same units** before computing $Z$.
> 3. **Misinterpretation of $C_p$:** $C_p \ge 1.0$ does **not** automatically mean a good process — the process may be off-centre, requiring $C_{pk}$ as well.
> 4. **Six Sigma confusion:** Six Sigma means $6\sigma$ between mean and nearest spec limit, NOT the standard deviation being 6. Many students wrongly state "$C_p = 6$" instead of "$C_p = 2.0$".
> 5. **Skipping the reverse Z step:** When asked to "find the value of $X$ corresponding to a given $Z$," students forget to use $X = \mu + Z\sigma$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Z-table reading errors:** The Z-table gives $P(Z \le z)$, NOT $P(-z \le Z \le z)$. Always clearly state which probability you are reading.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (High-Density Summary):**

- [x] **Definition:** The Normal Curve is a continuous, symmetric, bell-shaped distribution fully defined by two parameters — **mean ($\mu$)** and **standard deviation ($\sigma$)**.
- [x] **PDF Formula:** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\right)^{2}}$
- [x] **Standardization:** Convert any normal variable to $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$, giving $Z \sim N(0,1)$.
- [x] **Symmetry Property:** Mean = Median = Mode, all at the centre $x = \mu$.
- [x] **Total Area:** $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1$.
- [x] **Empirical Rule:** $68.27\%$ within $\pm 1\sigma$, $95.45\%$ within $\pm 2\sigma$, $99.73\%$ within $\pm 3\sigma$.
- [x] **SPC Foundation:** Control limits at $\bar{X} \pm 3\sigma$ capture $99.73\%$ of in-control variation.
- [x] **Six Sigma Quality:** USL and LSL placed at $\mu \pm 6\sigma$ giving $3.4$ DPMO.
- [x] **Process Capability:** $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$; requires $C_p \ge 1.0$ for capable process.
- [x] **TQM Linkages:** Six Sigma, SPC charts, Acceptance Sampling, Hypothesis Testing, and Regression all depend on the normality assumption.
- [x] **Quality Zones:** $\pm 1\sigma$ (common), $\pm 1$ to $\pm 2\sigma$ (warning), $\pm 2$ to $\pm 3\sigma$ (action), beyond $\pm 3\sigma$ (special cause).
- [x] **Z-Table Reading:** Always read $P(Z \le z)$ and use symmetry for negative $z$.
- [x] **Reverse Transformation:** $X = \mu + Z\sigma$ to retrieve raw values from standardized scores.
- [x] **Probability Complement Rule:** $P(X > a) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{a-\mu}{\sigma}\right)$.
- [x] **TQM Exam Tip:** Most KTU problems reduce to three steps — (1) compute $Z$, (2) look up area in Z-table, (3) interpret as conformance/defect percentage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Charts for Variables and Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Charts for Variables and Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Control Chart — The Fundamental Concept

A **Control Chart** is a statistical process control (SPC) tool first introduced by **Walter A. Shewhart** (1924) that monitors process performance over time by distinguishing between **common cause variation** (inherent to the process) and **special cause variation** (due to external disturbances). It plots process data against statistically determined upper and lower control limits (UCL and LCL) calculated from the process itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> A control chart is a graphical device that provides a basis for deciding whether the variation in the output of a process is due to common causes (chance) or special causes (assignable) — thereby indicating whether the process is **in statistical control** or **out of statistical control**.

**Key Terminology Recap:**
- **Central Line (CL):** The average of the process statistic.
- **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** $\text{CL} + 3\sigma$ (statistical boundary).
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** $\text{CL} - 3\sigma$ (statistical boundary).
- **In-Control Process:** Only common-cause variation; points fall randomly within limits.
- **Out-of-Control Process:** Special-cause variation present; pattern-based detection.

> [!NOTE]
> The **3-sigma limits** (99.73% probability) are the global engineering standard, not because they are mathematically inevitable, but because Shewhart's economic analysis showed that this width minimizes the cost of searching for false assignable causes while still detecting real shifts efficiently.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Speedometer"

Imagine a **highway with a 60 km/h speed limit** (your control limits). The cars on the highway represent your process output (products).

- **Slight fluctuations** between 55 and 65 km/h → **Common cause** (drivers behaving naturally, wind, slight inclines). The *process* of driving is fine.
- **A car suddenly at 90 km/h or 20 km/h** → **Special cause** (an accident ahead, a racer, a broken car). The *process* has changed and needs investigation.

The control chart acts like a **smart radar gun** that continuously measures, then raises an alarm only when a *systematic deviation* is detected — not for every natural blip. This is the essence of **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**.

---

### 1.3 Variables vs. Attributes — The Two Data Worlds

| Aspect | **Variables Data** (Continuous) | **Attributes Data** (Discrete) |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of measurement | Measurable on a continuous scale | Classification / counting |
| Example | Diameter in **mm**, weight in **g**, temperature in **°C** | Defective / non-defective, number of scratches |
| Chart types | $\bar{X}$-R chart, $\bar{X}$-$\sigma$ chart, individuals & moving range (I-MR) | p-chart, np-chart, c-chart, u-chart |
| Sample subgroup size | Usually small (n = 2 to 10) | Larger samples to detect low defect rates |
| Information power | Higher (more statistical power) | Lower (less information per unit) |
| Cost of measurement | Higher (precision instruments) | Lower (go/no-go gauges, visual inspection) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variables charts detect smaller process shifts earlier than attribute charts.** However, when measurement is impractical or impossible (e.g., aesthetic defects like "paint roughness"), attribute charts are the only viable option.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Phases of Control Chart Usage

**Phase 1 — Control Chart Construction (Retrospective):**
1. Collect 20–25 preliminary subgroups of size $n$.
2. Compute the trial control limits from the data.
3. Plot the data; if all points fall within limits with no patterns, the process is in control.
4. **Adopt** these limits as the official process control limits.

**Phase 2 — Control Chart Operation (Prospective):**
1. Plot new subgroup data on the established chart.
2. Investigate any out-of-control signals.
3. Use the chart as a continuous process-monitoring tool.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board specifically tests the distinction between **trial limits** (Phase 1) and **revised control limits** (Phase 2). Always recompute limits after removing out-of-control subgroups in Phase 1.

---

### 2.2 Control Charts for Variables — X-bar and R Chart

This is the **most widely used** control chart combination in industry. The $\bar{X}$ chart monitors the **process mean (location)**, while the R chart monitors the **process variability (spread)**.

**Underlying Assumptions:**
- Subgroup samples are drawn randomly and are independent.
- Process output is approximately normally distributed.
- Subgroup size $n$ is small (typically 2 ≤ n ≤ 10).

**Key Statistics Computed per Subgroup:**
- $\bar{X}_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{ij}}{n}$ — subgroup mean
- $R_i = X_{\max,i} - X_{\min,i}$ — subgroup range
- $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k}$ — grand mean of subgroup means
- $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k}$ — mean of subgroup ranges

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the R-chart and not σ-chart by default?**
> Range is computationally simple (no need for squaring), and for small sample sizes ($n \le 10$), the range is an almost equally efficient estimator of variability. This is why $\bar{X}$-R charts dominate shop-floor applications.

---

### 2.3 Control Limits for X-bar and R Chart

The control limit formulas are derived from the relationship between the standard deviation of subgroup means and the standard deviation of individual values:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

The control chart factors $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ are functions of sample size $n$:

| **Parameter** | **For X̄ Chart** | **For R Chart** |
|---|---|---|
| **Center Line (CL)** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{R}$ |
| **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ |
| **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For $n \le 6$, the LCL of the R-chart becomes negative — which is physically meaningless. By convention, in such cases the LCL is set to **0** (since a range cannot be negative).

---

### 2.4 X-bar and Sigma Chart (For Larger Subgroups, n > 10)

When subgroup sizes are larger ($n \ge 10$), the range becomes a poor estimator of variability. The standard deviation within each subgroup ($\bar{S}$ or $S_i$) is preferred.

| **Parameter** | **For X̄ Chart** | **For σ Chart (using S̄)** |
|---|---|---|
| **CL** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{S}$ or $\bar{\sigma}$ |
| **UCL** | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{S}$ | $B_4 \bar{S}$ |
| **LCL** | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{S}$ | $B_3 \bar{S}$ |

Where $A_3 = \dfrac{3}{c_4 \sqrt{n}}$, $B_3 = 1 - \dfrac{3}{c_4}\sqrt{1 - c_4^2}$, $B_4 = 1 + \dfrac{3}{c_4}\sqrt{1 - c_4^2}$, and $c_4$ is a bias-correction constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Exam questions increasingly use $\bar{S}$ (mean of subgroup standard deviations) as the modern replacement for $\bar{\sigma}$ (mean of subgroup variances). Be prepared for both notations.

---

### 2.5 Control Charts for Attributes

Attribute charts monitor processes where quality is measured by **discrete categories** (good/bad) or **counts of defects**.

#### 2.5.1 The p-Chart (Fraction Defective)

Used when sample size $n$ may **vary** from subgroup to subgroup. Plots the **proportion of defective items** in each subgroup.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** |
|---|---|
| **CL** | $\bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$ |
| **UCL** | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}}$ |
| **LCL** | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}}$ |

Where $d_i$ = number of defectives in subgroup $i$, $n_i$ = subgroup size, and $\bar{p}$ is the average fraction defective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If LCL is negative, it is set to **0** (since fraction defective cannot be negative). When $n$ is constant, the formula simplifies — when $n$ varies, the limits form a **staircase pattern**.

#### 2.5.2 The np-Chart (Number of Defectives)

Special case of the p-chart when subgroup size $n$ is **constant**. Plots the **count of defective items**.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** |
|---|---|
| **CL** | $n\bar{p}$ |
| **UCL** | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ |
| **LCL** | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The np-chart and p-chart contain **identical information** — they are mathematically equivalent. The np-chart is preferred when the inspector prefers to count defectives rather than compute fractions.

#### 2.5.3 The c-Chart (Number of Defects per Unit)

Used when each inspection unit can have **more than one defect** (e.g., number of blemishes on a painted surface, number of errors in a document). Assumes a **constant inspection unit** and Poisson-distributed defects.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** |
|---|---|
| **CL** | $\bar{c} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{k}$ |
| **UCL** | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |
| **LCL** | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |

Where $c_i$ = number of defects in inspection unit $i$, and $k$ = number of inspection units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The c-chart assumes the **Poisson distribution** (mean = variance). The **u-chart** is used when the inspection unit size varies (e.g., per square meter, per page, per 100 lines of code).

#### 2.5.4 The u-Chart (Defects per Unit, Variable n)

Plots defects **per unit**, allowing variable inspection area/volume.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** |
|---|---|
| **CL** | $\bar{u} = \dfrac{\sum c_i}{\sum n_i}$ |
| **UCL** | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n_i}}$ |
| **LCL** | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{u}}{n_i}}$ |

Where $c_i$ = number of defects in subgroup $i$, and $n_i$ = size of inspection unit $i$.

---

### 2.6 The KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it forms the backbone of every numerical question in the KTU exam.**

| **Chart** | **CL** | **UCL** | **LCL** | **Key Constant** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}$ (variables) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}$ | $A_2$ from $n$ table |
| R (variables) | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3, D_4$ from $n$ table |
| $\bar{X}$ (using S̄) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{S}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{S}$ | $A_3$ from $n$ table |
| σ (using S̄) | $\bar{S}$ | $B_4 \bar{S}$ | $B_3 \bar{S}$ | $B_3, B_4$ from $n$ table |
| p (variable n) | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n_i}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n_i}$ | $\bar{p} = \sum d_i / \sum n_i$ |
| np (constant n) | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} = \sum d_i / k$ |
| c (constant unit) | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} = \sum c_i / k$ |
| u (variable unit) | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n_i}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n_i}$ | $\bar{u} = \sum c_i / \sum n_i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Utility:**
> - **$\bar{X}$-R charts:** Shaft diameters, piston rings, resistor values, fill volumes in bottling.
> - **p-charts:** Incoming inspection of electronic components, supplier quality monitoring.
> - **c-charts:** Surface defects on painted car bodies, fabric defects per garment.
> - **u-charts:** Defects per square meter of steel sheet, software bugs per thousand lines of code (KLOC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of X-bar Chart Control Limits

We start with the fundamental theorem that for a normally distributed process $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the distribution of the sample mean is:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

The standard deviation of $\bar{X}$ is therefore:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Applying Shewhart's 3-sigma logic to the sampling distribution of $\bar{X}$:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu + \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \mu - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu - \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Since the true process mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ are unknown, we replace them with their sample estimates from the preliminary subgroups:

$$\mu \approx \bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k}$$

$$\sigma \approx \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant that depends on the subgroup size $n$ and represents the **expected value of the range of $n$ standard normal variates** divided by $\sigma$.

Substituting $\sigma = \bar{R}/d_2$ into the UCL/LCL formulas:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3(\bar{R}/d_2)}{\sqrt{n}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \left(\frac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}\right)\bar{R}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - \left(\frac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}\right)\bar{R}$$

Defining $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$, we get the standard textbook formulas:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions** as "Derive the control limits for the $\bar{X}$-chart" or "Show that $A_2 = 3/(d_2 \sqrt{n})$."

---

### 3.2 Standard Tabulated Control Chart Factors

The following values of $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$ are essential for KTU numerical problems. Always carry this mental map for common values of $n$:

| $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 |

---

### 3.3 Complete Worked Example — X-bar and R Chart

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
A manufacturing process produces piston rings. Subgroup size $n = 5$ and 25 subgroups are inspected. The grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = 100.0$ mm and the mean range $\bar{R} = 4.2$ mm. Construct the $\bar{X}$-R control chart and check whether the process is in control.

**Step 1 — Identify the tabulated constants for $n = 5$:**
$$A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.114$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\bar{X}$-chart limits:**

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 100.0 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 100.0 + (0.577)(4.2)$$

Evaluating the product:
$$0.577 \times 4.2 = 2.4234$$

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 100.0 + 2.4234 = 102.42 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 100.0 - 2.4234 = 97.58 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute R-chart limits:**

$$\text{CL}_R = \bar{R} = 4.2 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R} = 2.114 \times 4.2 = 8.879 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \times 4.2 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret and conclude:**
- $\bar{X}$-chart limits: $97.58 \text{ mm} \le \bar{X} \le 102.42 \text{ mm}$
- R-chart limits: $0 \le R \le 8.879 \text{ mm}$

> [!NOTE]
