## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define Six Sigma. What is meant by 3.4 DPMO?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Six Sigma** is a data-driven quality management methodology aimed at eliminating defects and reducing process variation to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.
> The term "Six Sigma" refers to a process operating within **six standard deviations** between the process mean and the nearest specification limit.
> **3.4 DPMO** means that for every **one million opportunities** for a defect to occur, only **3.4 defects** are produced in the long run. This figure incorporates the **1.5σ mean shift** that real processes experience over time. Thus, a Six Sigma process is virtually defect-free and represents world-class operational excellence. *(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the five phases of the DMAIC methodology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The five phases of DMAIC are:
> 1. **Define** – Identify the problem, customer requirements (CTQs), and project scope using a Project Charter and SIPOC diagram.
> 2. **Measure** – Establish the current process baseline by collecting data on defects, DPMO, and validating the measurement system (MSA).
> 3. **Analyze** – Identify the **root causes** of defects using statistical tools such as hypothesis testing, regression, and Pareto analysis.
> 4. **Improve** – Develop, pilot, and implement solutions that eliminate root causes; optimize using Design of Experiments (DOE).
> 5. **Control** – Standardize the improvements using Statistical Process Control (SPC), control plans, and SOPs to sustain gains.
> *(0.6 × 5 = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the origin, evolution, and statistical foundation of Six Sigma. Discuss the concept of the **1.5σ shift** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** An automotive brake disc manufacturing line produces **5,000 units per day**. Each disc has **12 critical quality characteristics** (opportunities). Daily inspection records **90 defects**. Calculate the **DPMO, first-pass yield, and the corresponding sigma level**. Comment on whether the process qualifies as a Six Sigma process. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]**

1. **Origin & Evolution (3 marks):**
   - Six Sigma originated in **1986 at Motorola (USA)** under engineer **Bill Smith** and CEO **Bob Galvin** to combat mounting quality issues in electronics manufacturing.
   - In **1995, Jack Welch** adopted it at **General Electric (GE)** as a corporate strategy, reportedly saving **$12 billion** in five years and popularising the methodology.
   - From the 2000s onwards, Six Sigma expanded into **service industries, healthcare, BPO, banking, and IT**, later merging with **Lean Manufacturing** to form **Lean Six Sigma**.

2. **Statistical Foundation (2 marks):**
   - The Greek letter **σ (sigma)** denotes the **standard deviation** of a process.
   - A process at "kσ" capability means the **USL − LSL specification window** is **kσ wide on each side** of the mean — i.e., a total width of **6kσ** for a 6σ process.
   - The **Empirical Rule** of normal distribution gives the area within ±kσ.

3. **The 1.5σ Shift (2 marks):**
   - Real processes do not remain perfectly centered; over time, the process mean drifts by an average of **1.5σ** from target.
   - This drift increases the tail defect rate from the theoretical 0.002 DPMO to the **practical 3.4 DPMO** benchmark.
   - The shift is included in the Six Sigma definition to make the standard **realistic and achievable** for long-term operations.
   - **Sketch:** A bell curve centered at μ with LSL and USL at ±6σ, and a shifted mean (μ + 1.5σ) showing the increased tail area outside USL — equivalent to 3.4 DPMO.

---

##### **Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given Data:**

$$
\text{Units} = 5{,}000 \quad ; \quad \text{Opportunities per unit} = 12 \quad ; \quad \text{Defects} = 90
$$

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities:**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 5{,}000 \times 12 = 60{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — DPMO:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{90}{60{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}500
$$

**Step 3 — First-Pass Yield:**

$$
Y = 1 - \frac{90}{60{,}000} = 1 - 0.0015 = 0.9985 = 99.85\%
$$

**Step 4 — Sigma Level (using standard conversion with 1.5σ shift):**

| DPMO | Sigma Level |
|---|---|
| 3.4 | 6.0 |
| 63 | 5.0 |
| 230 | 4.5 |
| 621 | 4.0 |
| **1,500** | **≈ 3.7σ** |

> **Comment:** A DPMO of **1,500** corresponds to a **long-term sigma level of ≈ 3.7σ**. Since 3.4 DPMO (6σ) is the world-class benchmark, the process is **NOT** a Six Sigma process — it is performing at **roughly industry-average quality** and has significant scope for DMAIC-driven improvement.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** Describe the **DMAIC framework** in detail. List the tools used in each phase. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A four-step assembly process has the following step-wise yields: Y₁ = 0.98, Y₂ = 0.96, Y₃ = 0.95, Y₄ = 0.92. Compute the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the **overall defect rate**. If the company operates at **10,000 units per month**, calculate the **monthly defect loss**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]**

**DMAIC Phases and Tools (1.4 marks each):**

| Phase | Purpose | Key Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **DEFINE** | Identify the problem, customer (VOC), CTQs, and project scope | **Project Charter, SIPOC Diagram, VOC, CTQ Tree, Voice of Business (VOB)** |
| **MEASURE** | Establish baseline, validate measurement system, map the process | **Process Map, MSA (Gage R&R), Control Charts, Pareto Chart, Cause-and-Effect Diagram** |
| **ANALYZE** | Identify and validate root causes of variation and defects | **5-Whys, Hypothesis Testing, Regression, ANOVA, FMEA, Correlation Analysis** |
| **IMPROVE** | Develop, pilot, and implement optimized solutions | **Design of Experiments (DOE), Kaizen, Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), Solution Selection Matrix, Piloting** |
| **CONTROL** | Standardize and sustain improvements via SPC and SOPs | **Control Charts, Statistical Process Control (SPC), Control Plan, SOPs, Training, Response Plan** |

> The DMAIC framework is **data-driven, project-based, and statistical**, ensuring that improvements are not based on intuition but on validated evidence.

---

##### **Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given Data:**

$$
Y_1 = 0.98, \quad Y_2 = 0.96, \quad Y_3 = 0.95, \quad Y_4 = 0.92, \quad \text{Units/month} = 10{,}000
$$

**Step 1 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY):**

$$
\text{RTY} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4
$$

$$
\text{RTY} = 0.98 \times 0.96 = 0.9408
$$

$$
0.9408 \times 0.95 = 0.89376
$$

$$
0.89376 \times 0.92 = 0.82226
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{RTY} = 0.8223 \;\; (82.23\%)}
$$

**Step 2 — Overall Defect Rate:**

$$
\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - \text{RTY} = 1 - 0.8223 = 0.1777
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Defect Rate} = 17.77\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Monthly Defect Loss (units):**

$$
\text{Defective Units} = 10{,}000 \times 0.1777 = 1{,}777 \text{ units/month}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Although each step appears "good" (92–98%), the **multiplicative nature** of serial processes creates a hidden loss of nearly **18%** of throughput. This is why RTY — not average yield — is the correct quality metric for end-to-end processes, and why **DMAIC** projects target the *weakest step* to lift overall yield.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do not confuse "6σ" with "6 standard deviations of variation"** — Six Sigma means the spec window accommodates **12σ** of total variation (6σ on each side).
> 2. **Never forget the 1.5σ shift** in long-term sigma calculation — using the theoretical 0.002 DPMO instead of 3.4 DPMO will cost full marks.
> 3. **For multi-step processes, use RTY (product), NOT arithmetic mean** of individual yields — this is a frequently repeated board trap.
> 4. **DPMO formula has a 1,000,000 multiplier** — students often forget the multiplier or divide by opportunities in the wrong order.
> 5. **Cpk uses min of upper and lower, not average** — using the mean value of Cpk upper and Cpk lower is incorrect.
> 6. **DMAIC is a closed-loop**, not a one-time fix — students often describe phases but miss the **sustainment (Control)** element.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024**

- **Definition:** Six Sigma = **3.4 DPMO** = **6σ capability** with **1.5σ shift** (long-term benchmark).
- **Origin:** Motorola, **1986**, **Bill Smith**; popularised by **Jack Welch at GE (1995)**.
- **Methodology:** **DMAIC** — Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control.
- **DPMO Formula:** DPMO = (Defects ÷ (Units × Opportunities)) × 1,000,000.
- **Yield Formula:** Y = 1 − (Defects ÷ Total Opportunities).
- **RTY Formula:** RTY = Y₁ × Y₂ × Y₃ × … × Yₙ (product, not average, for serial processes).
- **Cp** measures **spread capability**; **Cpk** measures **spread + centering**; Cpk = min(upper, lower).
- **6σ process:** Cpk ≥ 2.0; spec width = 12σ; Cp = 2.0.
- **Roles:** Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow Belt → White Belt.
- **Key tools by phase:** DEFINE (Charter, SIPOC, VOC); MEASURE (MSA, Pareto, Control Charts); ANALYZE (DOE, Regression, Hypothesis Testing, FMEA); IMPROVE (DOE, Poka-Yoke, Kaizen); CONTROL (SPC, SOPs, Control Plan).
- **Six Sigma is data-driven, project-based, statistically rigorous, and customer-focused.**
- **TQM is culture-based and broad; Six Sigma is project-based and metric-driven** — they are **complementary, not competing** philosophies.
- **3.4 DPMO is the practical figure**, NOT 0.002 DPMO — the 1.5σ shift is non-negotiable in KTU answers.
- **Six Sigma applications span:** Manufacturing, Healthcare, BPO, Banking, IT, Defence, Aviation.
- **Lean Six Sigma** = Six Sigma + Lean Manufacturing (waste elimination + variation reduction).
- **Six Sigma project lifecycle is closed-loop** — improvement without Control leads to regression of gains.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: New Seven Management Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** New Seven Management Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the New Seven Management Tools

The **New Seven Management Tools (N7)** are a set of qualitative planning and management methodologies developed and formalized by the **Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE)** in **1976–1979**. Unlike the original Seven QC Tools (which deal primarily with numerical/quantitative data and statistical quality control), the N7 tools are explicitly designed to handle **linguistic information, ideas, and qualitative data** during the planning, problem-solving, and decision-making phases of management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The *New Seven Management Tools* (also known as the *Seven New QC Tools*) are graphical and structured techniques used to transform messy, qualitative, language-based data into actionable plans. They support the **planning phase (P)** of the PDCA cycle, where statistical tools of the original Seven QC Tools are typically less effective.

The seven tools in the standard order recommended by JUSE are:

1. **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method / Kawakita Jiro Diagram)**
2. **Interrelationship Diagram (Relations Diagram)**
3. **Tree Diagram**
4. **Matrix Diagram**
5. **Matrix Data Analysis (Matrix Ranking / Principal Component Analysis)**
6. **Arrow Diagram (Activity Network / CPM / PERT)**
7. **Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Planning a Family Wedding":**
> Imagine you are tasked with organizing a large Indian wedding with 500 guests. You have:
> - Hundreds of scattered ideas (catering, music, guest list, budget, venue…)
> - Many conflicting requirements
> - A fixed deadline
> - Many people to coordinate
>
> You cannot solve this with spreadsheets alone — you need **structured thinking tools**. You would:
> 1. **Brainstorm** all ideas (Affinity Diagram)
> 2. **Find cause-effect links** between problems (Interrelationship Diagram)
> 3. **Break goals into sub-goals** (Tree Diagram)
> 4. **Map responsibilities vs tasks** (Matrix Diagram)
> 5. **Prioritize options mathematically** (Matrix Data Analysis)
> 6. **Schedule the critical activities** (Arrow Diagram)
> 7. **Prepare for "what if" failures** (PDPC)
>
> These are *exactly* the N7 Tools applied to a Total Quality Management (TQM) context!

### Why "New" and "Management"?

- The **original seven QC tools** (Pareto, Fishbone, Histogram, Scatter, Control Chart, Flowchart, Check Sheet) are called *statistical* tools — they **analyze numerical data after it is collected**.
- The **new seven tools** are called *management* tools — they **organize thinking and language-based data *before* and *during* action**.
- They are especially powerful in **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**, **policy deployment (Hoshin Kanri)**, and **strategic planning**.

---

## 1.3 Position of N7 in the TQM Framework

| Phase of TQM Activity | Typical Tools Used |
|---|---|
| Planning / Idea Generation | Affinity Diagram, Interrelationship Diagram |
| Goal Breakdown | Tree Diagram |
| Responsibility Assignment | Matrix Diagram |
| Quantitative Prioritization | Matrix Data Analysis |
| Implementation Scheduling | Arrow Diagram |
| Contingency Planning | PDPC |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Fact for KTU Exams:** The N7 tools complement, **not replace**, the original Seven QC Tools. Together they form the **"Seven Old + Seven New"** = **14 Tools of Quality**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tool 1 — Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)

**Origin:** Named after Japanese anthropologist **Kawakita Jiro** (1964).

**Definition:** A technique that organizes a large number of **ideas, facts, opinions, or opinions** into natural groupings based on their **mutual affinity (similarity)**.

**When to Use:**
- When the problem is **vague, unstructured, or language-based**.
- When consensus is needed among a cross-functional team.
- During brainstorming when 50–100+ ideas are generated.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. Form a team (4–8 members).
2. State the issue clearly on a flip chart.
3. Silently write each idea on a **separate card/sticky note** (no discussion).
4. Spread all cards on a wall.
5. **Silently cluster** cards that "feel" related.
6. Create a **header card** summarizing each group.
7. Draw the affinity diagram (header cards + grouped sub-cards).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Grouping is done **silently** and **non-judgmentally** to avoid group-think bias.

**Engineering Utility:** Used in QFD to group customer requirements ("Whats") into natural affinity clusters before building the House of Quality.

---

## 2.2 Tool 2 — Interrelationship Diagram (Relations Diagram)

**Purpose:** To **clarify tangled cause-and-effect relationships** among multiple factors — used when the problem is too complex to be solved by a simple fishbone diagram.

**Construction Logic:**
1. Write each problem/factor inside a **box**.
2. Draw arrows between boxes indicating the **direction of influence** (A → B means A influences B).
3. Identify **drivers** (boxes with many arrows going *out*) and **outcomes** (boxes with many arrows going *in*).
4. Count incoming and outgoing arrows; rank boxes accordingly.
5. The box with the **highest outgoing arrows** is the **root cause / key driver**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Variant — "Arrow Method":** Count arrows and draw a **convergent outline** that encloses the most influential cluster of factors.

---

## 2.3 Tool 3 — Tree Diagram

**Purpose:** To systematically break down a **broad goal/objective** into increasing levels of **detail, sub-goals, and actionable tasks** — also called a **systematic diagram**.

**Structure:** Inverted tree — single root at top, branches divide downward.

**Generic Form:**
$$
\text{Main Goal} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Level 1 Sub-goals} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Level 2 Means} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Level 3 Tasks}
$$

**Two Major Variants in TQM:**
- **Means-End Tree:** Top = goal, Bottom = means to achieve it (used for problem-solving).
- **Why-Why Tree (Goal Tree):** Top = desired outcome, Bottom = actions needed.

---

## 2.4 Tool 4 — Matrix Diagram

**Purpose:** To display the **strength of relationship between two sets of items** (e.g., responsibilities vs. tasks, customer requirements vs. engineering characteristics in QFD).

**Six Standard Matrix Shapes (KTU High-Yield):**

| L-Shape | T-Shape | Y-Shape | X-Shape | C-Shape | Roof-Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 group × 1 group | 2 groups × 1 group | 3 groups × 1 group | 2 groups × 2 groups | 3 groups | "Roof" of QFD House of Quality |

**Symbol Convention (Strength of Relationship):**
- ⊙ or ● = **Strong relationship** (typically weighted **9** or **3**)
- ○ = **Moderate relationship** (typically weighted **3** or **1**)
- △ = **Weak relationship** (typically weighted **1**)

> [!TIP]
> The **House of Quality (HOQ)** in QFD is essentially a combination of an **L-shape Matrix** + a **Roof-shape Matrix**.

---

## 2.5 Tool 5 — Matrix Data Analysis (Matrix Ranking / Principal Component Analysis)

**Purpose:** When the matrix is too large for visual judgment, this tool uses **mathematical/statistical techniques** to prioritize items numerically.

**Common Methods:**
- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** — used most often.
- **Multivariate Analysis.**
- **Quantification Theory Type I (for qualitative data).**

**Output:** A ranked list of items based on their statistical importance.

**Key Idea:** Convert qualitative judgments into numerical priorities using **eigenvalues and eigenvectors** of the relationship matrix.

---

## 2.6 Tool 6 — Arrow Diagram (Activity Network / Network Diagram)

**Purpose:** To plan the **optimal schedule** of a project by showing **activities as arrows** and **events/milestones as nodes**, identifying the **critical path**.

**Two Standard Methods:**
- **CPM (Critical Path Method):** Deterministic activity times.
- **PERT (Programme Evaluation and Review Technique):** Probabilistic activity times.

**Key Time Calculations:**

$$
\text{Expected Time (PERT)}\;=\; \frac{a + 4m + b}{6}
$$

Where:
- $a$ = Optimistic time
- $m$ = Most likely time
- $b$ = Pessimistic time

$$
\text{Variance of Activity} \;=\; \sigma^2 \;=\; \left(\frac{b-a}{6}\right)^2
$$

**Project Duration** = length of the **Critical Path** (the longest path through the network).

**Standard Terminology:**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| EST | Early Start Time |
| EFT | Early Finish Time |
| LST | Late Start Time |
| LFT | Late Finish Time |
| Slack / Float | LST − EST (or LFT − EFT) |
| Critical Activity | Activity with **Slack = 0** |

---

## 2.7 Tool 7 — Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)

**Purpose:** To map out **contingency plans** for every possible failure of a process or plan — it anticipates "what can go wrong" and **pre-decides countermeasures**.

**Two Variants:**
1. **Type A — Successive Type:** Used when goal is known; multiple paths lead to it.
2. **Type B — Contingency Type:** Starts with an initial plan, then branches into "if X fails → take Y action."

> [!NOTE]
> **FMEA Linkage:** PDPC is closely related to **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** — both anticipate failures, but PDPC is more *visual and planning-oriented*, while FMEA is more *quantitative and risk-scoring oriented*.

---

## 2.8 Comprehensive KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **high-yield formula reference** for Arrow Diagram problems (the most calculation-heavy topic among the N7 Tools).

$$
\boxed{\;t_{\text{PERT}} = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{activity}} = \dfrac{b-a}{6}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \sum_{i \in \text{Critical Path}} \sigma_i^2\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Slack}_i = \text{LST}_i - \text{EST}_i\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;P(\text{Project} \leq T_s) = \Phi(Z)\;}
$$

Where $\Phi(Z)$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution function.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Methodological Construction

> [!NOTE]
> Since the N7 Tools are primarily **qualitative management methodologies** rather than numerical physics/math derivations, this section provides **exhaustive step-by-step construction procedures**, worked numerical examples (where applicable), and **analytical comparison matrices** that KTU examiners frequently test.

---

## 3.1 Exhaustive Construction Procedure — Affinity Diagram

**Scenario:** A hospital quality team wants to improve **patient satisfaction**. They collected 24 verbal complaints from patients. Construct an Affinity Diagram.

### Step 1 — Team Formation
Assemble a multidisciplinary team:
- 1 Doctor
- 2 Nurses
- 1 Reception Staff
- 1 Quality Manager
- 1 Patient Representative

**Rule:** All members have equal voice; no seniority bias.

### Step 2 — Problem Statement
Write the issue clearly at the top of a wall/board:
> "How can we improve patient satisfaction in our Out-Patient Department (OPD)?"

### Step 3 — Card Generation (Silent Brainstorming)
Each team member silently writes **one idea per card**. After 30 minutes, **24 cards** are produced. Example cards:

```
Card 1:  Long waiting time
Card 2:  Doctor is rushed
Card 3:  No clear directions
Card 4:  Unclean washrooms
Card 5:  Rude reception staff
... (24 total)
```

### Step 4 — Silent Clustering
The team spreads all cards on the wall. **Without speaking**, they rearrange cards into groups that *feel* related. After 15 minutes, the cluster pattern emerges:

**Group A (Header: "Waiting & Time")** → Cards 1, 6, 11, 17, 21, 24
**Group B (Header: "Staff Behaviour")** → Cards 2, 5, 8, 14, 19
**Group C (Header: "Facilities & Cleanliness")** → Cards 4, 10, 15, 22
**Group D (Header: "Communication & Information")** → Cards 3, 9, 13, 18, 23
**Group E (Header: "Treatment Quality Concerns")** → Cards 7, 12, 16, 20

### Step 5 — Header Card Creation
A new card is placed *above* each cluster summarizing its theme in 2–4 words.

### Step 6 — Diagram Assembly

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│       "How can we improve patient satisfaction in OPD?"      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │            │             │              │
         ▼            ▼             ▼              ▼
   ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐
   │Waiting & │ │  Staff   │ │Facilities│ │  Comm &  │ │Treatment │
   │  Time    │ │Behaviour │ │& Cleanli-│ │Information│ │ Quality  │
   └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘
      ↓↓↓↓↓↓      ↓↓↓↓↓         ↓↓↓↓         ↓↓↓↓↓         ↓↓↓↓
   [Cards 1,6,  [Cards 2,5,  [Cards 4,10, [Cards 3,9,  [Cards 7,
    11,17,21,    8,14,19]     15,22]      13,18,23]    12,16,20]
    24]
```

### Step 7 — Interpretation
The team now has a **structured map of 24 raw complaints → 5 affinity clusters** ready for root-cause analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** A common mistake is to write the *solution* on cards instead of *problems*. The Affinity Diagram is for **problems/facts**, not solutions.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Arrow Diagram (PERT Calculation)

This is the **most calculation-intensive** N7 tool tested in KTU exams.

**Scenario:** A small TQM project has 8 activities with the following estimates (in days):

| Activity | Predecessor | Optimistic (a) | Most Likely (m) | Pessimistic (b) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| B | — | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| C | A | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| D | A | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| E | B | 4 | 5 | 6 |
| F | C | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| G | D, E | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| H | F, G | 1 | 2 | 3 |

### Step 1 — Compute Expected Time for Each Activity

Using the **PERT formula**:

$$
t = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6}
$$

**Activity A:**
$$
t_A = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}
$$

**Activity B:**
$$
t_B = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{1 + 8 + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \text{ days}
$$

**Activity C:**
$$
t_C = \frac{3 + 4(4) + 5}{6} = \frac{3 + 16 + 5}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}
$$

**Activity D:**
$$
t_D = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}
$$

**Activity E:**
$$
t_E = \frac{4 + 4(5) + 6}{6} = \frac{4 + 20 + 6}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}
$$

**Activity F:**
$$
t_F = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}
$$

**Activity G:**
$$
t_G = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}
$$

**Activity H:**
$$
t_H = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{1 + 8 + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 2 — Build the Network & Identify All Paths

From the predecessor table, valid paths from Start to Finish are:

- **Path 1:** Start → A → C → F → H
- **Path 2:** Start → A → D → G → H
- **Path 3:** Start → B → E → G → H

### Step 3 — Compute Duration of Each Path

**Path 1:**
$$
T_1 = t_A + t_C + t_F + t_H = 3 + 4 + 5 + 2 = 14 \text{ days}
$$

**Path 2:**
$$
T_2 = t_A + t_D + t_G + t_H = 3 + 3 + 4 + 2 = 12 \text{ days}
$$

**Path 3:**
$$
T_3 = t_B + t_E + t_G + t_H = 2 + 5 + 4 + 2 = 13 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Critical Path

$$
T_{\text{project}} = \max(T_1, T_2, T_3) = \max(14, 12, 13) = 14 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Critical Path} = \text{A} \rightarrow \text{C} \rightarrow \text{F} \rightarrow \text{H}\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Variance of Critical Path

For each activity on the critical path, compute $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{b-a}{6}\right)^2$:

**Activity A:**
$$
\sigma_A^2 = \left(\frac{4-2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111
$$

**Activity C:**
$$
\sigma_C^2 = \left(\frac{5-3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111
$$

**Activity F:**
$$
\sigma_F^2 = \left(\frac{7-3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444
$$

**Activity H:**
$$
\sigma_H^2 = \left(\frac{3-1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111
$$

**Sum of variances on Critical Path:**
$$
\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_F^2 + \sigma_H^2 = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{4}{9} + \frac{1}{9} = \frac{7}{9} \approx 0.7778
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{0.7778} \approx 0.8819 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 6 — Probability of Completion Within Target

Suppose management requires the project in **$T_s = 15$ days**. Find $P(T \leq 15)$:

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}} = \frac{15 - 14}{0.8819} = \frac{1}{0.8819} \approx 1.134
$$

From standard normal tables:
$$
P(Z \leq 1.134) \approx 0.8717 \;\text{or}\; 87.17\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** There is roughly an **87% chance** the project will finish within 15 days.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — N7 Tools

| Tool | Data Type | Phase | Output | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Affinity Diagram | Qualitative | Idea grouping | Clusters | High (definitions) |
| Interrelationship Diagram | Qualitative | Cause-effect | Ranked drivers | Medium |
| Tree Diagram | Mixed | Goal breakdown | Hierarchical plan | Medium |
| Matrix Diagram | Mixed | Responsibility mapping | Relationship matrix | High (QFD link) |
| Matrix Data Analysis | Quantitative | Prioritization | Ranked list | Low |
| Arrow Diagram | Quantitative | Scheduling | Critical path + schedule | **Very High (numerical)** |
| PDPC | Qualitative | Contingency | Failure-response tree | Medium |

---

## 3.4 Decision Algorithm — Which N7 Tool to Use?

```
Is the problem LANGUAGE-based?
    │
    ├── YES ──► Is it about GROUPING ideas?     ──► Affinity Diagram
    │           │
    │           ├── Is it about CAUSE-EFFECT chains? ──► Interrelationship Diagram
    │           │
    │           └── Is it about CONTINGENCY planning? ──► PDPC
    │
    └── NO (it is GOAL or SCHEDULE oriented) ──►
            │
            ├── Need to break down a GOAL?    ──► Tree Diagram
            │
            ├── Need to map RESPONSIBILITIES?  ──► Matrix Diagram
            │
            ├── Need to PRIORITIZE numerically? ──► Matrix Data Analysis
            │
            └── Need to SCHEDULE activities?    ──► Arrow Diagram
```
