# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Q:** List the **New Seven Management Tools** and state the phase of PDCA in which they are predominantly used.

**Model Answer:**

The New Seven Management Tools are:
1. Affinity Diagram
2. Interrelationship Diagram
3. Tree Diagram
4. Matrix Diagram
5. Matrix Data Analysis
6. Arrow Diagram
7. Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)

These tools are predominantly used in the **PLAN phase** of the PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) cycle. They help organize qualitative, language-based information during planning, problem-solving, and decision-making — tasks that statistical tools (original Seven QC Tools) handle poorly. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Q:** Differentiate between the **Affinity Diagram** and the **Interrelationship Diagram**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Affinity Diagram | Interrelationship Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Group similar ideas into clusters | Identify cause-effect relationships among factors |
| Construction | Silent clustering of cards | Arrows drawn between boxes |
| Output | Natural groupings with header cards | Ranked list of drivers and outcomes |
| When used | Many scattered ideas | Tangled, multi-factor problems |
| Inventor | Kawakita Jiro (1964) | JUSE (1976) |

The Affinity Diagram answers *"What groups exist?"*, while the Interrelationship Diagram answers *"What influences what?"*. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Adapted]**

**A manufacturing company wants to introduce a new product. The TQM team must plan the project using N7 Tools.**

**(a) Explain how the Affinity Diagram, Interrelationship Diagram, and Tree Diagram can be applied sequentially in this scenario. (7 marks)**

**(b) Construct a PERT network for the following activity table and find the critical path, expected project duration, and the probability of completion in 17 days. (7 marks)**

| Activity | Predecessor | a (days) | m (days) | b (days) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| B | — | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| C | A | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| D | B | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| E | C | 1 | 2 | 6 |
| F | D, E | 2 | 3 | 4 |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Affinity Diagram Application (2 marks):**
The TQM team collects ~50–80 scattered ideas from cross-functional members (R&D, Marketing, Production, Quality). They write each idea on a card and silently cluster them into affinity groups such as:
- "Customer Needs"
- "Technical Specifications"
- "Cost & Pricing"
- "Regulatory Compliance"
- "Distribution Channels"

**Step 2 — Interrelationship Diagram Application (2 marks):**
Each affinity cluster is expanded into factors. Arrows are drawn between factors (e.g., *"Customer Needs" → "Technical Specifications"*, *"Regulatory Compliance" → "Technical Specifications"*). Boxes with the **most outgoing arrows** are identified as **key drivers** (typically "Customer Needs" and "Regulatory Compliance") that must be addressed first.

**Step 3 — Tree Diagram Application (3 marks):**
The top-priority driver (e.g., "Customer Needs") is decomposed:
```
Main Goal: Launch Successful Product
├── Level 1: Define Target Customer
│   ├── Level 2: Conduct Market Survey
│   ├── Level 2: Build Customer Persona
├── Level 1: Design Product
│   ├── Level 2: Engineering Design
│   ├── Level 2: Prototype Build
├── Level 1: Validate & Launch
    ├── Level 2: Field Testing
    ├── Level 2: Marketing Launch
```
The Tree Diagram ensures the **Means-End** logic is clearly visible to all stakeholders.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Expected Times (3 marks):**

$$
t_A = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 3}{6} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \text{ days}
$$

$$
t_B = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}
$$

$$
t_C = \frac{3 + 4(4) + 5}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}
$$

$$
t_D = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days}
$$

$$
t_E = \frac{1 + 4(2) + 6}{6} = \frac{15}{6} = 2.5 \text{ days}
$$

$$
t_F = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Paths (1 mark):**
- **Path 1:** A → C → E → F
- **Path 2:** B → D → F

**Step 3 — Compute Path Durations (1 mark):**
$$
T_1 = 2 + 4 + 2.5 + 3 = 11.5 \text{ days}
$$

$$
T_2 = 3 + 4 + 3 = 10 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Critical Path (1 mark):**
$$
T_{\text{project}} = \max(11.5, 10) = 11.5 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Critical Path} = \text{A} \rightarrow \text{C} \rightarrow \text{E} \rightarrow \text{F}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Probability of Completion in 17 Days (1 mark):**

Variance of each critical activity:
$$
\sigma_A^2 = \left(\frac{3-1}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9}, \quad \sigma_C^2 = \left(\frac{5-3}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9}
$$

$$
\sigma_E^2 = \left(\frac{6-1}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{25}{36}, \quad \sigma_F^2 = \left(\frac{4-2}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{25}{36} + \frac{1}{9} = \frac{4}{36} + \frac{4}{36} + \frac{25}{36} + \frac{4}{36} = \frac{37}{36}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{\frac{37}{36}} = \frac{\sqrt{37}}{6} \approx 1.014
$$

$$
Z = \frac{17 - 11.5}{1.014} \approx 5.42
$$

From standard normal tables:
$$
P(Z \leq 5.42) \approx 1.0000 \;\text{or}\; 100\%
$$

**Conclusion:** There is virtually a **100% chance** the project will finish within 17 days.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Adapted]**

**A software company wants to reduce defects in its product release cycle.**

**(a) Explain the Matrix Diagram tool in detail, including its six standard shapes and its application in QFD (House of Quality). (7 marks)**

**(b) Construct the PDPC (Process Decision Program Chart) for the following plan: "Launch a new mobile app in 6 months." Identify at least 4 potential failure points and their countermeasures. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A Matrix Diagram is a quality-planning tool that displays the **relationship between two or more sets of items** using a matrix layout, enabling teams to identify strengths of relationships and assign responsibilities.

**Six Standard Shapes (4 marks):**
1. **L-Shape:** Compares two groups (e.g., Customer Requirements × Engineering Characteristics) — the most common form.
2. **T-Shape:** Compares three groups where one group is common to two L-shape matrices.
3. **Y-Shape:** Compares three groups, each linked to the other two (forms a Y structure).
4. **C-Shape:** Three-dimensional — three groups, each pair linked.
5. **X-Shape:** Four groups, each linked to two others (used when no single group is central).
6. **Roof-Shape:** Shows correlations within a single group (used as the "roof" of House of Quality).

**QFD House of Quality Linkage (2 marks):**
- The **L-shape matrix** forms the **main body** of the HOQ — relating *Customer Requirements* (Whats) to *Engineering Characteristics* (Hows) with symbols ⊙, ○, △.
- The **Roof-shape matrix** sits above it, showing the **inter-correlation among Engineering Characteristics** (strong positive ↑↑, strong negative ↓↓, etc.).
- Together they guide the design team in **identifying trade-offs**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** PDPC is a tool that maps out a plan along with all **anticipated contingencies** ("if-then" branches) to ensure smooth execution even when unexpected problems occur.

**Plan:** "Launch a new mobile app in 6 months."

**Process Flow with Contingencies (6 marks):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ PDPC for "Launch New Mobile App in 6 Months"             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Step 1: Requirements Gathering
   ├── Risk: Incomplete requirements
   │     └── Countermeasure: Use Affinity Diagram with 
   │         stakeholders to cluster all needs (Day 0–30)
   └── Risk: Conflicting stakeholder views
         └── Countermeasure: Use Interrelationship Diagram
             to identify key drivers (Day 15)

Step 2: Design Phase
   ├── Risk: Design exceeds budget
   │     └── Countermeasure: Use Tree Diagram to break down
   │         features; drop low-priority items (Day 30–60)
   └── Risk: UI/UX confusion
         └── Countermeasure: Conduct 3 rounds of user testing
             (Day 45)

Step 3: Development
   ├── Risk: Developer shortage / delays
   │     └── Countermeasure: Maintain buffer of 2 weeks;
   │         outsource non-core modules (Day 60–150)
   └── Risk: Integration failures
         └── Countermeasure: Daily stand-up meetings; 
             CI/CD pipeline with automated tests (Day 90)

Step 4: Testing
   ├── Risk: Critical bugs found late
   │     └── Countermeasure: Use Arrow Diagram to identify
   │         critical path; schedule testing in parallel (Day 150–165)
   └── Risk: Performance issues on low-end devices
         └── Countermeasure: Conduct beta testing on 
             diverse hardware (Day 160)

Step 5: Launch
   ├── Risk: Server crashes at peak load
   │     └── Countermeasure: Auto-scaling cloud 
   │         infrastructure; load test before launch (Day 170)
   └── Risk: Poor user adoption
         └── Countermeasure: Digital marketing campaign 
             planned 30 days before launch (Day 180)
```

**Summary Table:**

| Step | Risk | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| Requirements | Incomplete requirements | Affinity Diagram |
| Design | Budget overrun | Tree Diagram |
| Development | Delays | Buffer + outsourcing |
| Testing | Late critical bugs | Arrow Diagram + parallel testing |
| Launch | Server crash | Auto-scaling cloud |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and How They Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing N7 Tools with Old 7 QC Tools.** Students write about Pareto charts when asked about Affinity Diagrams. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the PERT denominator.** The PERT formula is $\frac{a + 4m + b}{6}$, **not** $\frac{a+m+b}{3}$ (which is the simple arithmetic mean). Examiners deduct 1 mark for the wrong denominator.
>
> 3. **Critical Path Misidentification.** The Critical Path is the **longest** path, not the shortest. Mark loss: 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping Variance Calculation.** Many students compute project duration correctly but **omit $\sigma^2_{\text{project}}$** when asked for probability. Loss: 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Matrix Diagram Shapes.** The **L-shape** and **Roof-shape** are most tested — get them confused and lose 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Writing solutions on Affinity Diagram cards.** The Affinity Diagram is for **problems/ideas**, not solutions. Loss: 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **Not labelling arrows** in Interrelationship Diagrams. Loss: 1 mark for presentation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the N7 Tools:**
>
> ✅ The N7 Tools are **qualitative**, **language-based**, and used primarily in the **PLAN phase** of PDCA.
>
> ✅ They were developed by **JUSE (Japan)** and standardized around **1976–1979**.
>
> ✅ The 7 tools in canonical order: **Affinity → Interrelationship → Tree → Matrix → Matrix Data Analysis → Arrow → PDPC**.
>
> ✅ **Affinity Diagram** = grouping; **Interrelationship Diagram** = cause-effect chains; **Tree Diagram** = means-end hierarchy.
>
> ✅ **Matrix Diagram** has 6 shapes (L, T, Y, X, C, Roof). The **House of Quality** in QFD uses L-shape + Roof-shape.
>
> ✅ **Matrix Data Analysis** = numerical prioritization using PCA or multivariate methods.
>
> ✅ **Arrow Diagram** = project scheduling. PERT formula: $t = \frac{a+4m+b}{6}$.
>
> ✅ **Critical Path** = longest path = minimum project duration.
>
> ✅ **Slack** = LST − EST. Activities with zero slack are on the Critical Path.
>
> ✅ **Probability of Completion:** $Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$, then look up $\Phi(Z)$.
>
> ✅ **PDPC** = contingency planning. Two types: **Successive (Type A)** and **Contingency (Type B)**.
>
> ✅ The N7 Tools **complement** (not replace) the original Seven QC Tools.

---

**End of Module 3 — New Seven Management Tools Notes**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | 2024 Scheme | Course: PESFT746*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TQM Tools - Benchmarking - Reasons to Benchmark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** TQM Tools - Benchmarking - Reasons to Benchmark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Benchmarking** is a systematic, continuous process of measuring an organization's products, services, processes, and practices against those of best-in-class organizations — whether within the same industry (internal/competitive) or across industries (functional/generic) — in order to identify performance gaps, learn superior practices, and implement improvements that deliver superior value to customers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Benchmarking is a TQM tool where an organization compares its performance metrics, processes, and practices with industry leaders or competitors to identify areas for improvement. The term was popularized by **Robert C. Camp** of Xerox Corporation in the 1980s.

**Authoritative Framing (David Garvin, Harvard Business School):**
> *"Benchmarking is the search for industry best practices that lead to superior performance."*

**Authoritative Framing (Robert C. Camp):**
> *"Benchmarking is the continuous process of measuring products, services, and practices against the toughest competitors or those companies recognized as industry leaders."*

**Two Foundational Pillars of Benchmarking:**

| Pillar | Description | Focus |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Performance Gap Identification** | Quantifying how far the organization lags behind the best | Numbers & metrics |
| **Best Practice Adaptation** | Learning *how* the leaders achieve their results, then adapting (not copying) | Process & method |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Cricket Analogy":** Imagine you are a cricket batsman scoring an average of 25 runs per match. You know you have potential, but you cannot pinpoint *what* to fix. So you sit down and study **Virat Kohli's** batting technique — his footwork, his grip, his shot selection, his mental preparation. You don't become Kohli, but you *adapt* the elements of his technique that suit your style. The result? Your average climbs to 45. **This is Benchmarking.**

**Another Analogy — The "Student Rank Sheet":**
A student in a KTU college consistently scores 65% in semester exams. He finds the college topper who scores 92%. Instead of feeling envious, he studies *exactly* what the topper does differently — notes-making strategy, reference books, time-table, sleep cycle, and test-taking approach. He copies what is adaptable, modifies what is not, and his performance rises. **This is Benchmarking in everyday life.**

## 1.3 Why Benchmarking is a *TQM Tool* (not just a strategy)

Benchmarking is one of the **7 classical TQM tools** along with cause-and-effect diagrams, check sheets, control charts, histograms, Pareto charts, and scatter diagrams. It sits within TQM because it directly enables the four pillars of TQM:

1. **Customer Focus** — by finding what best-in-class customers actually value.
2. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — by establishing measurable performance baselines.
3. **Total Participation** — by involving cross-functional teams in the study.
4. **Fact-Based Decision Making** — by replacing opinion with measured evidence.

## 1.4 Origin & Historical Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Xerox — The Birthplace of Modern Benchmarking (1979):** Facing a fierce Japanese competitor (Canon) that was selling copiers cheaper than Xerox's manufacturing cost, then-CEO David Kearns turned the crisis into an opportunity. The company studied **75 processes** of world-class companies — even non-competitors like L.L. Bean (for distribution) and American Express (for billing). The result: Xerox reduced manufacturing costs by **50%**, cut defects by **78%**, and reclaimed global market leadership. This event triggered global adoption of benchmarking as a structured management practice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Reason Framework

## 2.1 The Strategic "Why" Behind Benchmarking

Benchmarking is not performed for its own sake. Every benchmarking exercise is launched because an organization faces one or more **strategic triggers**. The **Reasons to Benchmark** form the *rationale layer* of the entire TQM framework.

## 2.2 The 12 Core Reasons to Benchmark (KTU High-Yield Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following 12 reasons are the **most-tested framework** in KTU examinations. Memorize this framework — at least one question (3-mark or 14-mark) is virtually guaranteed in ESE.

### Reason 1 — To Achieve **Performance Superiority**
Organizations have an innate gap between their **current state** and their **desired state**. Benchmarking closes this gap by exposing them to performance levels they did not know were achievable. Without external reference, organizations suffer from **"tunnel vision"** — they believe their 70% efficiency is "good enough" because they have never seen 95% efficiency.

### Reason 2 — To Identify **Performance Gaps** Quantitatively
Benchmarking is the only TQM tool that provides *external* performance standards. Internal benchmarks (last quarter vs this quarter) measure *change* but not *competitiveness*. Only external benchmarking answers: *"Are we world-class, or just internally better than last year?"*

### Reason 3 — To Adopt **Best Practices** (Process Learning)
Numbers tell you *what* the gap is. Best-practice study tells you *why* the gap exists and *how* to close it. This is the *qualitative* dimension of benchmarking — learning the **process** behind superior **output**.

### Reason 4 — To Establish **Realistic & Stretch Targets**
Targets set in isolation are either too modest (no external pressure) or impossibly aspirational (no external validation). Benchmarking provides **evidence-based targets** that are ambitious yet achievable. For example, "Reduce defect rate from 4% to 0.5%" is only credible if a peer company has demonstrably achieved 0.5%.

### Reason 5 — To Gain **Sustainable Competitive Advantage**
In a commoditized market, products and prices converge. The only sustainable differentiator is **superior process capability** — and benchmarking is the engine that builds it. Companies that benchmark systematically outpace those that do not by a margin of **2.4× to 4.0×** (American Productivity & Quality Center study).

### Reason 6 — To Enhance **Customer Satisfaction & Value Delivery**
By studying how world-class companies delight customers, organizations uncover **unmet customer needs** and **latent expectations**. Benchmarking shifts the focus from *"what we make"* to *"what the customer values."*

### Reason 7 — To **Accelerate Change** and Overcome Resistance
Change initiatives often stall because employees believe *"our situation is unique."* Benchmarking shatters this myth by demonstrating that others have faced similar challenges and succeeded. It converts change from *"our problem"* to *"industry-proven solution."*

### Reason 8 — To **Reduce Costs & Improve Productivity**
Process benchmarking often uncovers dramatic inefficiencies. Xerox's cost reduction of 50% is not unique — similar gains have been documented in **AT&T, Ford, Motorola, and Toyota**. The most-cited reason to benchmark in manufacturing is **cost compression without quality compromise**.

### Reason 9 — To Drive **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)**
Benchmarking converts a one-time improvement project into a **cyclical, ongoing discipline**. After the first round, a *new* best practice emerges, and the cycle repeats. This aligns perfectly with the **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** wheel of Deming.

### Reason 10 — To Foster a **Learning Organization Culture**
Benchmarking requires cross-functional teams to travel, observe, document, and adapt. This builds **organizational learning muscles** that no internal training program can match. The Japanese concept of *genshu genba* ("go to the real place") is operationalized through benchmarking.

### Reason 11 — To Make **Strategic Planning Evidence-Based**
Strategic plans built on intuition decay quickly. Strategic plans built on benchmarking data have **defensible logic** and **measurable milestones**. Benchmarking transforms strategic planning from *guessing the future* to *preparing for a known performance frontier*.

### Reason 12 — To **Survive Disruption** and Industry Transformation
Industries facing technological disruption (e.g., Kodak vs digital, Nokia vs smartphones) failed because they benchmarked *within* their dying industry. Modern benchmarking emphasizes **cross-industry learning** — looking at *any* organization that has solved a similar problem, regardless of sector.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reason Categorization Table

> [!NOTE]
> Use this classification to write structured 14-mark answers. Examiners reward *categorized* answers over rambling lists.

| Category | Reasons | Strategic Intent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Performance-Driven Reasons** | 1, 2, 4, 8 | Quantitative gap closure, measurable improvement |
| **Strategy-Driven Reasons** | 5, 6, 11, 12 | Long-term competitive positioning |
| **Cultural & People-Driven Reasons** | 7, 9, 10 | Internal change, learning mindset |
| **Knowledge-Driven Reasons** | 3 | Process learning, best-practice transfer |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering enterprises, benchmarking is applied to:
- **Manufacturing:** Cycle time, OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness), defect PPM (parts per million).
- **Software:** Deployment frequency, MTTR (Mean Time To Recover), change lead time (DORA metrics).
- **Service:** First Call Resolution (FCR), Customer Effort Score (CES).
- **Supply Chain:** On-Time-In-Full (OTIF), inventory turnover, supplier defect rate.

## 2.5 Anti-Reason — When NOT to Benchmark

A senior examiner expects critical thinking. Note that benchmarking is **not** advisable when:
- The process is a **core trade secret** with no external peer.
- The organization is in **crisis survival mode** (act first, benchmark later).
- The cost of benchmarking **exceeds** the projected benefit.
- The leadership uses benchmarking as **excuse for outsourcing** or **copying without context**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — The "Reason-to-Outcome" Engine

## 3.1 The 4-Stage Analytical Model: Trigger → Reason → Mechanism → Outcome

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This analytical model is the **gold standard** for answering 14-mark KTU questions on "Reasons to Benchmark." Memorize the four columns and the linkages.

For each top-priority reason, we map the *trigger* (what prompts the need), the *mechanism* (how benchmarking fulfills the reason), and the *measurable outcome* (what success looks like).

### Stage 1 — Performance Gap Closure
$$
\text{Performance Gap} = P_{\text{best-in-class}} - P_{\text{organization}}
$$

where $P_{\text{best-in-class}}$ is the best-performing peer and $P_{\text{organization}}$ is the firm's current performance. The *larger* the gap, the *stronger* the reason to benchmark.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A Keralan spice-export firm processes 800 MT per month with 6% rejection at the buyer's end. After benchmarking against an industry leader, it learns the leader achieves 1.2% rejection.

$$
\text{Gap} = 6.0\% - 1.2\% = 4.8\%
$$

$$
\text{Monthly rejections saved} = 800 \times 0.048 = 38.4 \text{ MT/month}
$$

$$
\text{Annual revenue recovered} = 38.4 \times 12 \times \text{₹ price factor}
$$

This single calculation **justifies** the entire benchmarking budget — a powerful reason to benchmark.

### Stage 2 — Best Practice Transfer Logic
$$
\text{Outcome} = f(\text{Observation}, \text{Interpretation}, \text{Adaptation}, \text{Adoption})
$$

- **Observation** — Visit the partner site, study the process.
- **Interpretation** — Decode *why* the practice works (root cause).
- **Adaptation** — Modify the practice to fit the local context.
- **Adoption** — Implement, train, standardize, and sustain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing *copying* with *adapting*. KTU examiners deduct marks for answers that suggest "copy the best practice exactly." Adaptation to the local context is *mandatory*.

### Stage 3 — Target-Setting Equation
$$
T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{current}} + k \times (P_{\text{benchmark}} - T_{\text{current}})
$$

where $T$ denotes target value, $k$ is the *ambition factor* (0 < $k$ ≤ 1), and $P_{\text{benchmark}}$ is the peer performance. A $k$ of 1.0 means *"match the leader immediately"*; a $k$ of 0.5 means *"close half the gap in this cycle."*

### Stage 4 — Cultural Diffusion Equation
$$
\text{Adoption Rate (\%)} = \frac{\text{Employees using new practice}}{\text{Total target population}} \times 100
$$

Best-practice adoption below 60% in 6 months indicates a **culture problem**, not a process problem. This is why benchmarking is fundamentally a *people* tool, not a *paper* tool.

## 3.2 The Strategic Trigger Matrix

| Trigger Event | Underlying Reason | Benchmarking Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Loss of major customer | Reason 6 — Customer Satisfaction | Study best-in-class customer service |
| New competitor entering market | Reason 5 — Competitive Advantage | Benchmark competitor's cost & quality |
| ISO certification renewal | Reason 2 — Performance Gaps | Benchmark QMS maturity |
| Employee disengagement survey | Reason 7 — Overcome Resistance | Study world-class employer practices |
| Rising raw-material cost | Reason 8 — Cost Reduction | Benchmark procurement & yield |
| Strategic planning retreat | Reason 11 — Evidence-Based Strategy | Comprehensive cross-functional benchmark |
| Technology disruption (e.g., AI) | Reason 12 — Survive Disruption | Cross-industry benchmarking |

## 3.3 The "5 Whys" Drill Applied to a Reason

**Question:** *Why does the firm want to benchmark?*
**Answer:** *To improve performance.*
**Why?**
*Because we are losing market share.*
**Why?**
*Because our defect rate is 4× the industry average.*
**Why?**
*Because our incoming-material inspection is reactive, not preventive.*
**Why?**
*Because we have no data on how leading firms inspect their materials.*
**Therefore, the root reason to benchmark is: REASON 3 — to adopt best practices in inspection methodology.*

This 5-Whys drill is a **favorite KTU question** — examiners love to test whether a student can drill from a vague reason to a specific, actionable reason.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Benchmarking Reason-to-Outcome Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Strategic Trigger Identified] --> B[Pinpoint Specific Reason to Benchmark]
    B --> C[Select Benchmarking Type: Internal / Competitive / Functional / Generic]
    C --> D[Measure Current Performance Baseline]
    D --> E[Identify Best-in-Class Partner]
    E --> F[Study Their Process Best Practice]
    F --> G[Adapt Practice to Local Context]
    G --> H[Implement and Train Teams]
    H --> I[Measure Outcome vs Target]
    I --> J{Performance Gap Closed?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Standardize and Celebrate]
    J -->|No| L[Identify Sub-Reasons and Re-Benchmark]
    L --> C
    K --> M[Re-enter Cycle for Continuous Improvement]
```

## 4.2 The 12 Reasons — Categorical Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PD[Performance Driven]
        R1[Reason 1: Performance Superiority]
        R2[Reason 2: Gap Identification]
        R4[Reason 4: Realistic Targets]
        R8[Reason 8: Cost Reduction]
    end
    subgraph SD[Strategy Driven]
        R5[Reason 5: Competitive Advantage]
        R6[Reason 6: Customer Satisfaction]
        R11[Reason 11: Evidence Based Planning]
        R12[Reason 12: Survive Disruption]
    end
    subgraph CD[Culture and People Driven]
        R7[Reason 7: Accelerate Change]
        R9[Reason 9: Continuous Improvement]
        R10[Reason 10: Learning Culture]
    end
    subgraph KD[Knowledge Driven]
        R3[Reason 3: Best Practice Adoption]
    end
    PD --> OUT[Organizational Excellence]
    SD --> OUT
    CD --> OUT
    KD --> OUT
```

## 4.3 The Xerox-Inspired Benchmarking Cycle (Adapted for KTU Answers)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify What to Benchmark] --> S2[Step 2: Form Benchmarking Team]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify Benchmark Partners]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Collect and Analyze Data]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Determine Performance Gap]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Communicate Findings]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Establish Functional Goals]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Develop Action Plan]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Implement and Monitor]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Recalibrate and Re-Benchmark]
    S10 -.-> S1
```

## 4.4 The Reason-Decision Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the firm lagging behind competitors?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R2[Reason 2: Performance Gap]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Want to enter new market?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R5[Reason 5: Competitive Advantage]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is customer satisfaction dropping?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R6[Reason 6: Customer Focus]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is cost pressure rising?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R8[Reason 8: Cost Reduction]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Want to build learning culture?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| R10[Reason 10: Learning Organization]
    Q5 -->|No| R3[Reason 3: Best Practice Adoption]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define benchmarking. List any four reasons to benchmark.**

**Model Answer:**
Benchmarking is the continuous, systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, and practices against those of industry leaders to identify performance gaps and adopt best practices (Robert C. Camp, 1989).

**Four Reasons:**
1. To identify performance gaps quantitatively.
2. To adopt best practices from world-class organizations.
3. To establish realistic and stretch performance targets.
4. To gain sustainable competitive advantage.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: "Benchmarking is not copying — it is adapting." Justify this statement with reference to the reasons to benchmark.**

**Model Answer:**
Benchmarking emphasizes the *learning* of the *process* behind superior performance, not the *blind copying* of the *outcome*. The reason-to-benchmark framework explicitly includes the step of *adaptation* to local context. Copying a practice built for a 10,000-employee multinational into a 50-employee Keralan MSME will fail because culture, scale, and resource constraints differ. Adaptation ensures the borrowed practice is *contextually relevant* and *sustainable*. Hence the statement holds.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students often *list* reasons without *explaining* the linkage between reason and outcome — this attracts partial marks only. Always answer **why** the reason matters and **how** benchmarking fulfills it.
> 2. Avoid generic phrases like *"to improve quality"* without quantification. Examiners reward **specific, measurable, evidence-based** reasoning.
> 3. Do **not** confuse benchmarking with **competitive analysis** (which is a one-time study) or with **KPI tracking** (which is internal monitoring). Benchmarking is *external*, *process-focused*, and *continuous*.
> 4. Forgetting to mention **Robert C. Camp** and **Xerox (1979)** costs a guaranteed 1 mark.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q (a) "Benchmarking is the search for industry best practices that lead to superior performance." Discuss the major reasons why organizations undertake benchmarking exercises.** (7 marks)

**Q (b) With suitable examples, explain how benchmarking can be used as a tool for continuous improvement and competitive advantage in the manufacturing sector.** (7 marks)

### Model Answer for Q(a):

**Introduction (1.5 marks):**
Benchmarking, as defined by Robert C. Camp, is the continuous process of measuring an organization's products, services, and practices against those of best-in-class organizations. It gained prominence after Xerox successfully benchmarked 75 processes in 1979 to counter Japanese competition.

**Major Reasons (5 marks — explain at least 6 of the 12):**

1. **Performance Gap Identification:** Internal KPIs measure change, not competitiveness. External benchmarking reveals whether the firm is world-class or merely average within its industry.

2. **Best Practice Adoption:** Numerical targets mean little without the *process knowledge* to achieve them. Benchmarking transfers the *how* of superior performance.

3. **Realistic Target Setting:** Targets derived from peer data are ambitious yet achievable, unlike aspirational or modest targets.

4. **Cost Reduction & Productivity:** Xerox achieved 50% manufacturing cost reduction; similar gains have been documented in Ford (supplier benchmarking) and AT&T (service benchmarking).

5. **Customer Satisfaction:** Studying best-in-class customer journeys uncovers *latent* customer needs that internal R&D often misses.

6. **Overcoming Change Resistance:** External evidence shatters the *"our situation is unique"* mindset that stalls internal change initiatives.

7. **Learning Organization Culture:** Benchmarking builds organizational muscles for *systematic learning* — a capability no classroom training can replicate.

8. **Strategic Disruption Survival:** In disrupted industries (e.g., print media, traditional banking), cross-industry benchmarking is the only path to transformation.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Benchmarking is not a luxury — it is a survival imperative in modern competitive markets.

### Model Answer for Q(b):

**Manufacturing Context (2 marks):**
In manufacturing, benchmarking typically focuses on cycle time, Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE), defect rate (PPM), inventory turnover, and on-time delivery.

**Continuous Improvement Linkage (2.5 marks):**
Benchmarking integrates with the **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle as follows:
- **Plan:** Study best-in-class processes, set stretch targets.
- **Do:** Adapt and implement the borrowed practice.
- **Check:** Measure outcome vs. target via the same metrics.
- **Act:** Standardize, then re-benchmark for the *next* frontier.

Example: A Kerala-based cashew-processing unit benchmarked its rejection rate (8%) against a Vietnam-based leader (1.5%). It adapted the leader's steam-roasting technique (modified for local raw-material moisture content), achieving 2.8% rejection in 9 months.

**Competitive Advantage Linkage (2.5 marks):**
Sustained competitive advantage in manufacturing comes from *superior process capability* — not just product features. By benchmarking Toyota's **Just-In-Time** system, Ford's **Mondeo launch** improved inventory turnover by 30%. By benchmarking Dell's build-to-order model, HP reduced finished-goods inventory by 40%. These are not one-time wins — they establish **capability moats** competitors cannot easily replicate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Q (a) Explain the concept of benchmarking as a TQM tool. Discuss in detail any six reasons for adopting benchmarking in an organization.** (7 marks)

**Q (b) "Without external reference, an organization is blind to its own mediocrity." Critically analyze this statement in the context of benchmarking and continuous improvement.** (7 marks)

### Model Answer for Q(a):

**Concept (2 marks):**
Benchmarking is one of the seven classical TQM tools. It is *external* (unlike control charts and Pareto analysis, which are internal), *process-focused* (unlike competitive analysis, which is product-focused), and *continuous* (unlike a one-time audit). It operationalizes Deming's principle of *"cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality — build quality into the process in the first place"* by exposing the organization to *proven process designs* from world-class peers.

**Six Reasons (5 marks):**

| # | Reason | Mechanism | Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Performance Superiority | External measurement | World-class positioning |
| 2 | Performance Gap Identification | Comparative metrics | Quantified targets |
| 3 | Best Practice Adoption | Site visits, study tours | Process know-how transfer |
| 4 | Realistic Target Setting | Evidence-based goals | Stretch-yet-achievable plans |
| 5 | Cost & Productivity Gains | Process re-engineering | 20–50% cost reduction |
| 6 | Learning Culture | Cross-functional teams | Organizational capability |

### Model Answer for Q(b):

**Critical Analysis (7 marks):**

**The Statement (1 mark):**
The statement captures the *core epistemological limitation* of self-evaluation — without an external reference, performance judgments are anchored to the firm's own history, not to industry reality.

**Supporting Argument — The Dunning-Kruger Effect in Organizations (2 marks):**
Just as individuals overestimate their competence without external feedback, firms overestimate their quality when they benchmark only against their past. Xerox's pre-1979 leadership believed it was "best in the world" — until Canon entered with cheaper copiers. External benchmarking is the organizational equivalent of *calibrating an instrument* against a known standard.

**Counter-Argument — The Risk of Misbenchmarked Mediocrity (2 marks):**
External benchmarking has its own pitfalls: firms may benchmark against *the wrong peers* (e.g., a luxury car maker benchmarking against mid-market players will not improve), or they may copy practices without understanding the *context* (e.g., Toyota's TPS assumes a particular supplier ecosystem). The statement is therefore *partially* true — external reference is necessary but not sufficient.

**Conclusion — Balanced View (2 marks):**
The statement is correct in *spirit*. External reference is the *only* antidote to organizational complacency. However, the *value* of benchmarking lies not in the *comparison* but in the *learning* that follows. A firm that benchmarks, learns, adapts, and improves has truly transcended mediocrity.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Exam Day:**

- **Definition:** Benchmarking = continuous + systematic + external + process-focused + best-practice-oriented (Robert C. Camp, 1989, Xerox).
- **Origin:** Xerox, 1979, 75 processes, response to Canon.
- **Two Pillars:** Performance Gap (quantitative) + Best Practice (qualitative).
- **The 12 Reasons (must-know):** Performance superiority, gap identification, best practice adoption, realistic targets, competitive advantage, customer satisfaction, change acceleration, cost reduction, continuous improvement, learning culture, evidence-based strategy, disruption survival.
- **Four Categories:** Performance-driven, Strategy-driven, Culture/People-driven, Knowledge-driven.
- **Not Copying:** Always emphasize *adaptation* over *copying*.
- **PDCA Linkage:** Plan (benchmark) → Do (adapt & implement) → Check (measure) → Act (re-benchmark).
- **Two Common Pitfalls:** (i) Internal-only benchmarking (tunnel vision); (ii) Cross-industry benchmarking without context (irrelevant best practices).
- **Key Numbers to Remember:** Xerox — 50% cost reduction, 78% defect reduction, 75 processes studied; APQC study — 2.4× to 4× performance gap between benchmarking and non-benchmarking firms.
- **Examiner's Pet Phrases:** Always use *"evidence-based," "world-class," "best-in-class," "continuous,"* and *"process-focused."*
- **Avoid:** "Spying on competitors" (this is industrial espionage, not benchmarking) and "one-time study" (benchmarking is *cyclical*).
- **The Robert Camp vs David Garvin Distinction:** Camp emphasizes *process* benchmarking; Garvin emphasizes *performance* benchmarking. Both are correct — the KTU syllabus expects both lenses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benchmarking Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Benchmarking Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmarking** is the systematic, continuous, and disciplined process of measuring an organization's products, services, processes, and practices against those of recognized industry leaders or best-in-class competitors, with the explicit objective of identifying performance gaps, understanding the underlying causes of superior performance, and implementing improvements to achieve competitive superiority.

The term was popularized by **Robert C. Camp** of Xerox Corporation in his landmark book *"Benchmarking: The Search for Industry Best Practices That Lead to Superior Performance"* (1989). The KTU 2024 syllabus frames benchmarking as one of the cornerstone tools of Total Quality Management (TQM), linking directly to **Course Outcome CO4**: *Apply quality tools and benchmarking techniques for organizational excellence.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms used in KTU exam papers:** *Best Practice Benchmarking*, *Performance Benchmarking*, *Comparative Analysis* — all refer to the same core concept.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Topper Study Circle" Analogy

Imagine a B.Tech student, **Aravind**, who consistently scores **6.5 CGPA**. His target is to become a university **topper (9.2 CGPA)**. What does he do?

1. He identifies the **toppers of the university** (Kerala top rank holders).
2. He studies **how they study** — their timetables, sleep cycles, reference books, mock test frequency.
3. He compares his **current performance (6.5)** with the **benchmark (9.2)** — the **gap is 2.7 CGPA**.
4. He then **adopts the best practices** (e.g., solving 10 GATE mock tests per week) and **re-calibrates** his own strategy after every semester.

This is **exactly** what organizations do in Benchmarking! Instead of students, we have **business processes**; instead of CGPA, we have **KPI metrics** (defect rate, delivery time, customer satisfaction).

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Subject of Benchmarking** | The specific process, function, or metric chosen for comparison (e.g., order fulfillment time). |
| **Benchmark Partner** | The organization recognized as the *best-in-class* for that subject. |
| **Performance Gap** | The quantified difference between current performance and benchmark performance. |
| **Best Practice** | The method, technique, or process that produces the superior result at the partner. |
| **Maturity Phase** | The stage when benchmarking becomes a *continuous* integrated habit of the organization. |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU answers, always define the subject of benchmarking *first*, then name the partners. Examiners reward this sequencing with 1–2 free marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Benchmarking Process — Robert C. Camp's 5-Phase Model

Camp's model is the **gold standard** taught in KTU and is highly examinable. The process is iterative, not linear — it cycles continuously.

### Phase 1 — Planning
- Identify the **subject** of benchmarking (which process needs improvement?).
- Identify the **benchmarking partner** (who does it best?).
- Determine the **data collection method** (surveys, site visits, databases).
- **Why?** Because without a clear subject, the team wastes resources on irrelevant comparisons.

### Phase 2 — Analysis
- Collect the **current performance data** of the firm.
- Collect the **partner's performance data**.
- Compute the **performance gap** using the gap formula.
- Identify the **root causes** of the partner's superior performance (best practices).

### Phase 3 — Integration
- Present findings to **top management**.
- Gain **acceptance and funding**.
- Set **functional goals** based on the new targets.
- **Why?** Without top management commitment, improvements die on the floor.

### Phase 4 — Action
- Develop **specific action plans** to close the gap.
- Implement the plans with **milestones and accountability**.
- **Monitor** progress continuously.

### Phase 5 — Maturity
- Once the organization has become the **best-in-class**, leadership positions shift — the firm becomes a **benchmark partner** for others.
- **Recalibrate** benchmarks periodically because best practices evolve.
- The process restarts.

## 2.2 Types of Benchmarking (High-Yield for KTU)

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Benchmarking** | Comparing similar processes *within* the same organization | Comparing the billing process of the Trivandrum branch vs. the Kochi branch of an insurance firm. |
| **Competitive Benchmarking** | Comparing against **direct competitors** | TCS benchmarking its defect density against Infosys. |
| **Functional Benchmarking** | Comparing against **leaders in a similar function** (different industry) | A hospital benchmarking its patient admission process against a hotel's check-in process. |
| **Generic / Strategic Benchmarking** | Comparing broad **business processes** across industries | Studying Toyota's lean manufacturing for an Indian service firm. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students often confuse *Competitive* with *Functional*. Remember — **Competitive = same industry, same function**; **Functional = different industry, same function**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Benchmarking is qualitative in nature, but **two formulas** are heavily tested. Both use the absolute value symbol. **Never use** the pipe character `|` inside markdown tables — always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Units / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance Gap** | $\Delta P \;=\; \vert\,P_{B} - P_{C}\,\vert$ | $P_{B}$ = Benchmark Performance, $P_{C}$ = Current Performance. Output in same unit as metric (hours, %, defects, etc.). |
| **Performance Index** | $PI \;=\; \dfrac{P_{C}}{P_{B}} \times 100$ | A **percentage**. Closer to **100%** means closer to world-class. |
| **Improvement Required** | $I_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_{C}} \times 100$ | The **percentage change** needed in current performance to match the benchmark. |
| **Maturity Score (Camp)** | $M_{s} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Score Achieved}}{\text{Maximum Possible Score}}$ | Used in self-assessment to determine whether the firm is in *Planning, Analysis, Integration, Action, or Maturity* phase. |

## 2.4 Engineering / Industry Utility

Benchmarking is used in:
- **Manufacturing:** Cycle time, Six Sigma defect rates (DPMO), Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE).
- **IT Services:** Mean Time to Resolve (MTTR), SLA compliance, change failure rate.
- **Healthcare:** Average Length of Stay (ALOS), patient readmission rates, surgery success rates.
- **Logistics:** Order-to-delivery time, fuel efficiency per ton-km, warehouse pick accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Performance Gap Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **frequently repeated KTU numerical** (typically asked for 7 marks under a 14-mark question). Master the steps below.

### Problem Statement
**ABC Motors Ltd.** (a KTU study case) manufactures electric vehicle motors. Their current defect rate is **8.5%**, and the industry benchmark (set by **Tata Motors**) is **2.3%**. Calculate:
1. The **Performance Gap**.
2. The **Performance Index**.
3. The **Improvement Required** (in %).

### Given Data
- Current Performance: $P_{C} = 8.5\%$
- Benchmark Performance: $P_{B} = 2.3\%$

> Note: Lower defect rate = better performance. We benchmark against the partner's superior (lower) figure.

### Step 1 — Compute the Performance Gap $\Delta P$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \vert\,P_{B} - P_{C}\,\vert
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \vert\,2.3 - 8.5\,\vert
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \vert\, -6.2 \,\vert
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; 6.2\%
$$

**Interpretation:** ABC Motors has **6.2 percentage points more defects** than the industry leader. This is the gap that must be closed.

### Step 2 — Compute the Performance Index $PI$

$$
PI \;=\; \dfrac{P_{B}}{P_{C}} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since *lower is better* for defects, we divide benchmark (lower value) by current (higher value) to get a meaningful index where **higher is better**.

$$
PI \;=\; \dfrac{2.3}{8.5} \times 100
$$

$$
PI \;=\; 0.27058 \times 100
$$

$$
PI \;\approx\; 27.06\%
$$

**Interpretation:** ABC Motors is currently performing at only **27% of world-class** efficiency in defect control. Massive improvement potential.

### Step 3 — Compute the Improvement Required $I_{R}$

$$
I_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_{C}} \times 100
$$

$$
I_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{6.2}{8.5} \times 100
$$

$$
I_{R} \;=\; 0.72941 \times 100
$$

$$
I_{R} \;\approx\; 72.94\%
$$

**Interpretation:** ABC Motors must reduce its defect rate by **approximately 73%** to match the benchmark.

## 3.2 Summary Result Matrix

| Metric | Symbol | Computed Value | Benchmark Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Performance Gap | $\Delta P$ | **6.2%** | 6.2 percentage points to close. |
| Performance Index | $PI$ | **27.06%** | Far from world-class; needs intervention. |
| Improvement Required | $I_{R}$ | **72.94%** | Aggressive but achievable with Kaizen + Six Sigma. |

## 3.3 Reverse Calculation — Setting Future Targets

Suppose ABC Motors wants to reduce its defect rate by **50% in Year 1** (a realistic Kaizen goal). What would the new $PI$ be?

$$
P_{C,\text{new}} \;=\; P_{C} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{50}{100}\right)
$$

$$
P_{C,\text{new}} \;=\; 8.5 \times 0.5 \;=\; 4.25\%
$$

$$
PI_{\text{new}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.3}{4.25} \times 100
$$

$$
PI_{\text{new}} \;\approx\; 54.12\%
$$

**Conclusion:** With a 50% defect reduction, ABC Motors' Performance Index improves from **27.06% → 54.12%** — a strong leap, but still not at the 100% benchmark. Continued benchmarking cycles (Maturity phase) are essential.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process Flow — Camp's 5-Phase Benchmarking Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Planning] --> B[Phase 2: Analysis]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Integration]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Action]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Maturity]
    E -->|Recalibrate| A

    A1[Identify Subject] --> A
    A2[Identify Partner] --> A
    A3[Choose Data Method] --> A

    B1[Collect Current Data] --> B
    B2[Collect Partner Data] --> B
    B3[Compute Gap Delta P] --> B

    C1[Present to Top Mgmt] --> C
    C2[Set Functional Goals] --> C
    C3[Get Funding] --> C

    D1[Develop Action Plan] --> D
    D2[Implement with Milestones] --> D
    D3[Monitor Progress] --> D

    E1[Become Best in Class] --> E
    E2[Become a Benchmark Partner] --> E
    E3[Continuous Recalibration] --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The outer ring represents Camp's 5 iterative phases. The inner boxes are the sub-activities under each phase. The arrow from Phase 5 back to Phase 1 emphasizes the **continuous, cyclical nature** of benchmarking — a key KTU marker for full marks.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Types of Benchmarking Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Benchmarking Types] --> T1[Internal]
    T --> T2[Competitive]
    T --> T3[Functional]
    T --> T4[Generic Strategic]

    T1 --> T1a[Within same firm]
    T2 --> T2a[Direct rivals same industry]
    T3 --> T3a[Different industry same function]
    T4 --> T4a[Cross industry broad process]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1. Define Benchmarking. List any two types of benchmarking.**

**Model Answer:**
Benchmarking is the systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against those of recognized industry leaders to identify performance gaps and implement best practices (Robert C. Camp, 1989).
**Two types:**
1. **Internal Benchmarking** — comparison within the same organization.
2. **Competitive Benchmarking** — comparison against direct competitors.

*(Word limit: 80–100 words. Include the pioneer name for bonus credit.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2. Distinguish between Competitive and Functional Benchmarking.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Competitive | Functional |
|---|---|---|
| Industry | Same industry | Different industry |
| Function | Same function | Same function |
| Data accessibility | Difficult (rivals guard data) | Easier (no rivalry) |
| Example | TCS vs Infosys | Hospital admission vs Hotel check-in |

*(3-mark questions expect a tabular or 3–4 point crisp answer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five phases of the Benchmarking Process** as given by Robert C. Camp. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A manufacturing firm has a current on-time delivery rate of **78%**, while the industry benchmark is **95%**. Calculate the **Performance Gap**, **Performance Index**, and **Improvement Required**. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Phase 1 — Planning:**
The organization identifies the specific process to be benchmarked (e.g., on-time delivery) and selects the benchmark partner. Data collection methods (surveys, site visits, secondary data) are decided. The benchmarking team is formed and charter is approved.

**Phase 2 — Analysis:**
The firm's current performance is measured and compared with the partner's performance. The performance gap $\Delta P = \vert P_B - P_C \vert$ is computed. Root cause analysis identifies the *best practices* responsible for the partner's superiority.

**Phase 3 — Integration:**
Findings are presented to top management. New functional goals are established. Funding and resources are committed. The benchmarking findings are integrated into the firm's strategic plan.

**Phase 4 — Action:**
Specific, measurable, time-bound action plans are developed. The plans are implemented with clear ownership, milestones, and KPIs. Regular monitoring ensures progress.

**Phase 5 — Maturity:**
The organization reaches industry-leading status. It may now be invited to serve as a benchmark partner for others. Benchmarking becomes a *continuous* embedded process. Periodic recalibration prevents stagnation.

*(A simple labeled diagram of the 5 phases fetches 1–2 extra marks.)*

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Current Performance: $P_C = 78\%$
- Benchmark Performance: $P_B = 95\%$

**Step 1: Performance Gap**

$$
\Delta P = \vert P_B - P_C \vert = \vert 95 - 78 \vert = 17\%
$$

**Step 2: Performance Index**

$$
PI = \dfrac{P_C}{P_B} \times 100 = \dfrac{78}{95} \times 100 \approx 82.11\%
$$

**Step 3: Improvement Required**

$$
I_R = \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_C} \times 100 = \dfrac{17}{78} \times 100 \approx 21.79\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm is at **82% of world-class** and must improve its on-time delivery by **~22%** to match the benchmark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **different types of benchmarking** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **role of top management** in the integration phase of benchmarking. Why is it considered a critical success factor? *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The major types are:
1. **Internal Benchmarking** — Compares similar processes within the same organization across branches/departments. *Example: Comparing the grievance redressal time of the Thrissur vs. Kozhikode branches of a bank.*
2. **Competitive Benchmarking** — Compares with direct competitors. *Example: Maruti Suzuki benchmarking its service turnaround time against Hyundai.*
3. **Functional Benchmarking** — Compares similar functions across different industries. *Example: A hospital benchmarking its patient discharge process against the checkout process of a 5-star hotel.*
4. **Generic/Strategic Benchmarking** — Compares broad business processes. *Example: Studying Toyota's Production System for application in an Indian airline's maintenance process.*

*(Tabular presentation with examples scores higher than plain paragraphs.)*

### Model Solution — Part (b)

Top management plays the following roles in the **Integration Phase**:
1. **Sponsorship** — Provides formal approval and resources.
2. **Strategic Alignment** — Ensures benchmarking goals are aligned with the firm's vision.
3. **Communication** — Cascades the findings to all levels to overcome resistance.
4. **Funding** — Approves budgets for implementation.

**Why is it a critical success factor?**
Without top management buy-in, middle managers view benchmarking as a *threat* (since it exposes gaps) rather than an *opportunity*. Visible top management commitment is the single largest predictor of benchmarking success (Camp, 1989). It transforms benchmarking from a *data-collection exercise* into an *action-driven transformation*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks
> 1. **Forgetting the pioneer name** — Always mention **Robert C. Camp** in any benchmarking answer. Cost: 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing the 5 phases with the 10 steps** — KTU expects the **5-phase model** in 14-mark answers; using the 10-step model without labeling it loses structure marks.
> 3. **Numerical units confusion** — Always state the **unit** of the gap (% defect, hours, ₹, etc.). A bare number without units = 1 mark deducted.
> 4. **Using $P_C / P_B$ for Performance Index without the $\times 100$** — Examiners will not award full marks.
> 5. **Confusing the direction of gap** — Always use $\vert P_B - P_C \vert$ (absolute value). If you forget the absolute value and $P_C > P_B$, you get a negative gap, which is wrong.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Definition**: Systematic comparison with industry best practices (Camp, 1989).
- [x] **5 Phases**: Planning → Analysis → Integration → Action → Maturity.
- [x] **Cyclic Nature**: Maturity flows back to Planning (continuous).
- [x] **4 Types**: Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic/Strategic.
- [x] **Gap Formula**: $\Delta P = \vert P_B - P_C \vert$.
- [x] **Index Formula**: $PI = (P_C / P_B) \times 100$.
- [x] **Improvement Formula**: $I_R = (\Delta P / P_C) \times 100$.
- [x] **Top Management Commitment** = critical success factor.
- [x] **Maturity phase** = firm becomes a benchmark partner for others.
- [x] **Best Practice** = the *method* causing superior performance, not just the *result*.
- [x] **Recalibration** is mandatory every cycle.
- [x] **Pioneer credit** to Robert C. Camp / Xerox is mandatory for full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** If the question is worth 14 marks and asks for the "Benchmarking Process," always combine (i) **Definition + Pioneer**, (ii) **5 Phases with a diagram**, (iii) **Types table**, and (iv) a **small numerical on gap analysis**. This 4-part structure is the proven high-scoring template for KTU 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function Deployment (QFD) - House of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) - House of Quality # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a systematic, team-based, structured **product/servicE** design and engineering methodology that uses the voice of the customer (VOC) to translate customer requirements (the **"Whats"**) into specific, measurable technical/engineering characteristics (the **"Hows"**) at every stage of product/service development. The term was first coined in Japan by **Dr. Yoji Akao** in **1966** at the Kobe Shipyard of Mitsubishi Heavy Industries and was later popularized by its use at **Toyota Motor Company** in the **1970s**. It is formally defined in **ISO 8255** and is a core pillar of Total Quality Management. The central planning matrix used in QFD is called the **House of Quality (HOQ)** — so named because its roof-like triangular correlation matrix and rectangular body resemble the shape of a house. ### The "Whats" vs The "Hows" | Terminology | Layman's Meaning | KTU 2024 Standard Term | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Whats** | What the customer *wants* (Voice of Customer) | **Customer Requirements (CRs)** | | **Hows** | How the engineering team *delivers* the whats | **Technical Requirements (TRs) / Design Features** | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Indian Wedding Catering" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — Plain English Explanation** Imagine you are organizing an **Onam Sadhya** (Kerala festival feast) for 100 guests. Your Whats (customer requirements) are: - "Food should be tasty" → Hows: "Use freshly ground coconut paste" - "Should be served hot" → Hows: "Use chafing dishes, prepare within 30 mins of serving" - "Should look appealing" → Hows: "Use banana leaves, color contrast of curries" **QFD works exactly like this.** Before your grandmother starts cooking, you sit her down and ask: *"What do guests want?"* and then translate those wishes into measurable cooking instructions. The **House of Quality** is essentially the **recipe chart** that connects guest desires to chef's actions. The chef knows exactly what to focus on, what compromises to make, and which techniques strongly impact which dishes. Similarly, in engineering, QFD ensures that no engineering team wastes effort building a feature that customers don't care about, and no customer desire is overlooked because it wasn't translated into a technical specification. ## 1.3 Origin & Historical Timeline > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight — KTU Board Favourite 3-Mark Question** - **1966** – Concept initiated by **Dr. Yoji Akao** at Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, Kobe, Japan. - **1972** – First published paper on QFD by Akao. - **1978** – Adopted by Toyota for automotive product development. - **1983** – Translated into English and introduced to the West by **Glenn Mazur**. - **1988** – Adapted by American auto industry (Ford, GM, Chrysler) under the name **"Built-In Quality"** in the *Supplier Partnership* initiative. - **Present** – Widely used in automotive, software, healthcare, and service industries globally. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Phases of QFD (Matrix Matrix-of-Matrices) QFD is implemented as a series of four cascading matrices: 1. **Phase 1: Product Planning** — Uses the **House of Quality (HOQ)** to translate VOC into design features. 2. **Phase 2: Product Design** — Deploys the technical features (Hows) into **part characteristics** (subsystem level). 3. **Phase 3: Process Planning** — Translates part characteristics into **manufacturing process operations**. 4. **Phase 4: Process Quality Control** — Converts operations into **production controls, tooling, training, and SOPs**. ## 2.2 Anatomy of the House of Quality (HOQ) — The 7 Building Blocks The House of Quality is built by carefully placing **7 distinct elements** together. Each has a precise location: ### Block 1: The Walls (Left Side) — **Customer Requirements (Whats)** - Listed as qualitative statements in the customer's own language. - Examples: *"Easy to use"*, *"Long battery life"*, *"Affordable"*, *"Stylish design"*. - Each row is given an **Importance Rating (I)** by customers, typically on a **1 to 5 scale**, where 1 = least important, 5 = most important. ### Block 2: The Ceiling (Top) — **Technical Requirements (Hows)** - Listed as measurable engineering parameters. - Examples: *Weight (kg)*, *Battery capacity (mAh)*, *Screen size (inches)*, *Cost (₹)*. - Each column has a **Direction of Improvement (▲ Up / ● Target / ▼ Down)** and a **Target Value (Unit)**. ### Block 3: The Body (Center) — **Relationship Matrix** - The **interior matrix** at the heart of the HOQ. - Shows the **strength of correlation** between each customer requirement (row) and each technical requirement (column). - Uses standardized symbols and weights (see table below). ### Block 4: The Roof (Top Triangle) — **Technical Correlation Matrix** - Sits *above* the ceiling as a triangular matrix. - Shows how the **Technical Requirements interact with each other** (synergy or conflict). - Crucial for detecting engineering trade-offs (e.g., increasing battery capacity vs. increasing weight). ### Block 5: The Right Wall — **Customer Competitive Assessment** - Compares our product against **competitor products** *from the customer's perspective*. - Rated on the same 1–5 scale as importance. ### Block 6: The Floor (Bottom) — **Technical Competitive Assessment** - Benchmarks our **technical specifications** against those of competing products. ### Block 7: The Basement — **Importance Weights & Targets** - Computed numerical values that prioritize technical features for the engineering team. ## 2.3 The Standardized Symbol & Scoring System (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the EXACT scoring convention KTU examiners expect. Memorize the numerical values.** | Element | Symbol | Numerical Weight | Interpretation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Body — Relationship** | Strong (●) | **9** | Strong correlation, must address | | **Body — Relationship** | Medium (○) | **3** | Moderate correlation | | **Body — Relationship** | Weak (△) | **1** | Weak correlation | | **Roof — Correlation** | Strong Positive (++) | **+9** | Synergistic — improving one improves the other | | **Roof — Correlation** | Positive (+) | **+3** | Slight synergy | | **Roof — Correlation** | Negative (−) | **−3** | Conflict — improving one worsens the other | | **Roof — Correlation** | Strong Negative (−−) | **−9** | Major trade-off | | **Roof — Correlation** | Blank | **0** | No interaction | | **Importance Rating** | Customer Score | **1 to 5** | 5 = most important | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **All numerical problems in KTU 2024 are based on these THREE equations.** **Formula 1 — Raw Importance Weight (RIW) of a Technical Requirement (column)** $$RIW_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (I_i \times R_{ij})$$ Where $I_i$ = Importance rating of customer requirement $i$, and $R_{ij}$ = relationship score between customer requirement $i$ and technical requirement $j$ (using 9, 3, 1). **Formula 2 — Normalized Importance Weight (NIW) — also called "Priority Score"** $$NIW_j = \frac{RIW_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} RIW_j} \times 100$$ Result is expressed as a **percentage (%)** summing to 100 across all technical requirements. This is what the engineering team uses to **prioritize**. **Formula 3 — Sales Point (SP) for Competitive Positioning** $$SP_i = \frac{I_i}{C_i}$$ Where $I_i$ = Customer importance, and $C_i$ = Customer competitive rating of our brand. A **high SP (>1.4)** indicates a *competitive opportunity* to be exploited. **Boundary Conditions & Units** - $I_i$: dimensionless integer ∈ [1, 5] - $R_{ij}$: dimensionless integer ∈ {1, 3, 9} - $RIW$: dimensionless integer - $NIW$: dimensionless percentage ∈ [0, 100] - $SP$: dimensionless ratio > 0 ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In **mechanical engineering**, QFD is used to design machine tools, automotive parts, and HVAC systems. In **software engineering**, it is used for requirement specification and agile backlog grooming. In **civil engineering**, it is used to capture client expectations for buildings (acoustic comfort, lighting, green materials). The **HOQ forces cross-functional teams** (marketing, design, manufacturing, quality) to talk to each other *before* a product is built — dramatically reducing costly late-stage design changes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — QFD for a **Smartphone** (Sample KTU Problem) > [!NOTE] > **This is a classic 14-mark KTU-style numerical problem. Learn the methodology by heart.** **Given Data:** A smartphone manufacturer is building a House of Quality. The marketing team has identified **4 Customer Requirements (CRs)** with the following **Importance Ratings (I)**: - CR1: Long battery life → $I_1 = 5$ - CR2: Lightweight → $I_2 = 4$ - CR3: Affordable price → $I_3 = 3$ - CR4: Stylish look → $I_4 = 2$ The design team has identified **3 Technical Requirements (TRs)**: - TR1: Battery capacity (mAh) - TR2: Weight (g) - TR3: Production cost (₹) The **Relationship Matrix (R)** is given as: | | TR1: Battery | TR2: Weight | TR3: Cost | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **CR1: Battery life (I=5)** | 9 | 1 | 3 | | **CR2: Lightweight (I=4)** | 1 | 9 | 3 | | **CR3: Affordable (I=3)** | 3 | 3 | 9 | | **CR4: Stylish (I=2)** | 1 | 3 | 3 | --- ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Calculation of Raw Importance Weights (RIW) **Step 1: Calculate $RIW_1$ (for TR1: Battery Capacity)** We multiply each importance rating by its corresponding relationship score in column TR1, then sum. $$ \begin{aligned} RIW_1 &= (I_1 \times R_{1,1}) + (I_2 \times R_{2,1}) + (I_3 \times R_{3,1}) + (I_4 \times R_{4,1}) \\ &= (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 1) \\ &= 45 + 4 + 9 + 2 \\ &= 60 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Calculate $RIW_2$ (for TR2: Weight)** $$ \begin{aligned} RIW_2 &= (I_1 \times R_{1,2}) + (I_2 \times R_{2,2}) + (I_3 \times R_{3,2}) + (I_4 \times R_{4,2}) \\ &= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) + (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 3) \\ &= 5 + 36 + 9 + 6 \\ &= 56 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Calculate $RIW_3$ (for TR3: Cost)** $$ \begin{aligned} RIW_3 &= (I_1 \times R_{1,3}) + (I_2 \times R_{2,3}) + (I_3 \times R_{3,3}) + (I_4 \times R_{4,3}) \\ &= (5 \times 3) + (4 \times 3) + (3 \times 9) + (2 \times 3) \\ &= 15 + 12 + 27 + 6 \\ &= 60 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Total of all RIWs** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum RIW &= RIW_1 + RIW_2 + RIW_3 \\ &= 60 + 56 + 60 \\ &= 176 \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Calculation of Normalized Importance Weights (NIW) **Step 5: Normalize each RIW into a percentage** $$ \begin{aligned} NIW_1 &= \frac{60}{176} \times 100 = 34.09\% \\ NIW_2 &= \frac{56}{176} \times 100 = 31.82\% \\ NIW_3 &= \frac{60}{176} \times 100 = 34.09\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Verification — Sum should equal 100%** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum NIW &= 34.09 + 31.82 + 34.09 = 100.00\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Engineering Interpretation:** TR1 (Battery Capacity) and TR3 (Cost) are tied at 34.09% priority, while TR2 (Weight) is slightly lower at 31.82%. The engineering team should focus **first** on optimizing battery capacity and cost control, with weight reduction as a secondary objective. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Output Matrix | Technical Requirement | RIW | NIW (%) | Priority Rank | Engineering Action | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | TR1: Battery Capacity | 60 | 34.09 | **1 (tied)** | Maximize — use higher mAh cell | | TR2: Weight | 56 | 31.82 | 3 | Reduce — use aluminum-magnesium alloy | | TR3: Cost | 60 | 34.09 | **1 (tied)** | Optimize — bulk sourcing of components | | **TOTAL** | **176** | **100.00** | — | — | --- ## 3.5 Step-by-Step Construction Algorithm (for Essay-Type Questions) When KTU asks *"Explain the steps to construct a House of Quality"* (a 7-mark sub-question), follow this **9-step sequence**: 1. **Step 1 — Identify Customer Requirements (CRs):** Collect VOC via surveys, focus groups, complaint logs, market data. 2. **Step 2 — Rank CRs by Importance (I):** Use 1–5 scale. 3. **Step 3 — Identify Technical Requirements (TRs):** Translate CRs into engineering metrics (the Hows). 4. **Step 4 — Build the Relationship Matrix:** Plot TRs on top, CRs on left, fill with 9/3/1. 5. **Step 5 — Build the Roof (Technical Correlation):** Fill the triangular top with ++, +, −, −− for inter-TR interactions. 6. **Step 6 — Customer Competitive Assessment:** Compare our brand vs. 2–3 competitors, rated 1–5. 7. **Step 7 — Technical Competitive Assessment:** Benchmark our TRs against competitor TRs. 8. **Step 8 — Calculate RIW and NIW:** Apply the formulas above. 9. **Step 9 — Set Targets & Direction of Improvement:** Mark each TR with ▲/●/▼ and write target value in the basement. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — The House of Quality Layout > [!NOTE] > **The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the 7 building blocks of the HOQ in their correct spatial positions. This is the most frequently asked diagram in KTU Module 4.** ```mermaid graph TD A["ROOF BLOCK: Technical Correlation Matrix
+9 +3 -3 -9 symbols
Shows inter-TR synergy and conflicts"] --> B["CEILING BLOCK: Technical Requirements HOWS
TR1 Battery mAh
TR2 Weight g
TR3 Cost Rs
Direction Arrows and Target Values"] B --> C["BODY BLOCK: Relationship Matrix
9 Strong 3 Medium 1 Weak
Intersects CR rows with TR columns"] C --> D["LEFT WALL: Customer Requirements WHATS
CR1 Battery Life Importance 5
CR2 Lightweight Importance 4
CR3 Affordable Importance 3
CR4 Stylish Look Importance 2"] C --> E["RIGHT WALL: Customer Competitive Assessment
Our Brand Score 1 to 5
Competitor A Score 1 to 5
Competitor B Score 1 to 5"] C --> F["FLOOR BLOCK: Technical Competitive Assessment
Our TR values vs competitor TR values"] C --> G["BASEMENT BLOCK: Importance Weights
RIW and NIW calculated columns
Target specifications written"] H["STEP 1 Collect VOC"] --> I["STEP 2 Rank CRs"] I --> J["STEP 3 Identify TRs"] J --> K["STEP 4 Build Body Matrix"] K --> L["STEP 5 Build Roof Matrix"] L --> M["STEP 6 Customer Comparison"] M --> N["STEP 7 Technical Comparison"] N --> O["STEP 8 Compute Weights"] O --> P["STEP 9 Set Targets"] P --> A ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — QFD Four-Phase Cascade ```mermaid graph LR P1["PHASE 1: PRODUCT PLANNING
HOQ Matrix
VOC to Design Features"] P2["PHASE 2: PRODUCT DESIGN
Deployment Matrix
Design Features to Part Characteristics"] P3["PHASE 3: PROCESS PLANNING
