Control Matrix
Operations to Production Controls"] P1 -->|"Deploys design priorities"| P2 P2 -->|"Deploys part targets"| P3 P3 -->|"Deploys operation specs"| P4 P4 -->|"Final SOPs and QC"| P5["QUALITY ACHIEVED"] ``` ## 4.3 Logic Flow — How a Customer Voice Travels Through the HOQ ```mermaid graph TD V["Voice of Customer
I want a long lasting phone"] --> W["CR1 Battery Life
Importance 5"] W --> R["Body Matrix Cell
Score 9 Strong"] R --> RIW["RIW Calculation
5 times 9 plus other CR scores"] RIW --> NIW["NIW Percentage
34.09 percent"] NIW --> ENG["Engineering Team Directive
Increase mAh cell to target value"] ENG --> PROD["Final Product
Meets customer expectation"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]** **Q: Define Quality Function Deployment. Who introduced it and when?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured, team-based planning tool used in Total Quality Management to translate the Voice of the Customer (VOC) into specific, measurable technical/engineering requirements for product or service design. **(2 marks)** > It was introduced by **Dr. Yoji Akao in 1966** at the Kobe Shipyard of Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, Japan, and was later adopted by Toyota Motor Company in the 1970s. **(1 mark)** ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]** **Q: List any THREE components (building blocks) of the House of Quality and state their position.** > **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** > 1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — Located on the **left wall** of the HOQ; qualitative statements of customer needs. > 2. **Technical Requirements (Hows)** — Located on the **ceiling (top)** of the HOQ; measurable engineering parameters. > 3. **Technical Correlation (Roof Matrix)** — Located as a **triangle on top of the ceiling**; shows how technical requirements interact with each other (synergy or conflict). > *(Alternatively, students may name the Body, Right Wall, Floor, or Basement.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, End Semester]** **Q: (a)** Define the House of Quality (HOQ). Explain any **FIVE** major components of the HOQ with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The customer requirements and their importance ratings for a wristwatch are given below. The technical requirements and the relationship matrix are also given. Calculate the **Raw Importance Weight (RIW)** and **Normalized Importance Weight (NIW)** for each technical requirement. **(7 marks)** **Customer Requirements:** - CR1: Water resistant — Importance = 5 - CR2: Comfortable strap — Importance = 4 - CR3: Accurate timekeeping — Importance = 5 - CR4: Elegant design — Importance = 3 **Technical Requirements — Relationship Matrix:** | | TR1: Waterproof Rating | TR2: Strap Material Quality | TR3: Quartz Movement Precision | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **CR1 (I=5)** | 9 | 1 | 3 | | **CR2 (I=4)** | 1 | 9 | 1 | | **CR3 (I=5)** | 3 | 1 | 9 | | **CR4 (I=3)** | 3 | 3 | 1 | --- #### **Solution to Question A (a):** **Definition:** The House of Quality is a planning matrix used in Quality Function Deployment (QFD) that visually organizes the relationship between **customer requirements (Whats)** and **technical/engineering requirements (Hows)** to facilitate product design. **(1 mark)** **FIVE Major Components of the HOQ:** 1. **Customer Requirements (Left Wall):** The "Whats" — qualitative voice-of-customer statements such as "easy to use" or "long battery life." Each is given an importance rating (typically 1–5). 2. **Technical Requirements (Ceiling):** The "Hows" — measurable engineering parameters such as weight (g), battery capacity (mAh), or cost (₹). Each has a target value and direction of improvement. 3. **Relationship Matrix (Body):** The central rectangular matrix that shows the strength of correlation (9 = strong, 3 = medium, 1 = weak) between each customer requirement and each technical requirement. 4. **Roof / Technical Correlation Matrix (Top Triangle):** Shows how technical requirements interact with each other — for example, ++ (strong positive synergy), + (positive), − (negative conflict), or −− (strong negative conflict). 5. **Importance Weights & Targets (Basement):** Calculated numerical values (RIW and NIW) that rank the technical requirements by priority for the engineering team, along with target specifications. *(Any 5 of these — 1 mark each. Plus 2 marks for a neat labelled diagram of the HOQ.)* --- #### **Solution to Question A (b):** **Step 1: Calculate $RIW_1$ for TR1 (Waterproof Rating)** $$ \begin{aligned} RIW_1 &= (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 1) + (5 \times 3) + (3 \times 3) \\ &= 45 + 4 + 15 + 9 \\ &= 73 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Calculate $RIW_2$ for TR2 (Strap Material Quality)** $$ \begin{aligned} RIW_2 &= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) + (5 \times 1) + (3 \times 3) \\ &= 5 + 36 + 5 + 9 \\ &= 55 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Calculate $RIW_3$ for TR3 (Quartz Movement Precision)** $$ \begin{aligned} RIW_3 &= (5 \times 3) + (4 \times 1) + (5 \times 9) + (3 \times 1) \\ &= 15 + 4 + 45 + 3 \\ &= 67 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Total RIW** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum RIW &= 73 + 55 + 67 = 195 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Normalize to NIW (% values)** $$ \begin{aligned} NIW_1 &= \frac{73}{195} \times 100 = 37.44\% \\ NIW_2 &= \frac{55}{195} \times 100 = 28.21\% \\ NIW_3 &= \frac{67}{195} \times 100 = 34.36\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Verification — Sum should be 100%** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum NIW &= 37.44 + 28.21 + 34.36 \approx 100.00\% \end{aligned} $$ **Final Result Table:** | Technical Requirement | RIW | NIW (%) | Priority Rank | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | TR1: Waterproof Rating | 73 | 37.44 | **1** | | TR2: Strap Material Quality | 55 | 28.21 | 3 | | TR3: Quartz Movement Precision | 67 | 34.36 | 2 | | **TOTAL** | **195** | **100.00** | — | > **Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):** The engineering team should prioritize **TR1: Waterproof Rating (37.44%)** as the highest priority, followed by **TR3: Quartz Movement Precision (34.36%)**, with strap material being the least critical (28.21%). --- ### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary Model Paper]** **Q: (a)** Explain the **FOUR phases of QFD** with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** "QFD brings cross-functional teams together." Discuss the **benefits and limitations** of QFD in a manufacturing or service industry context. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B (a):** QFD is implemented in **four sequential phases**, each represented as a matrix that "deploys" the output of the previous phase into the next, more detailed level. This is often called the **"Matrix of Matrices"** approach. **(1 mark)** **Phase 1 — Product Planning (House of Quality):** The CRs (Whats) and TRs (Hows) are mapped using the HOQ. Output: prioritized technical features and target specifications for the product. **(1.5 marks)** **Phase 2 — Product Design (Deployment Matrix):** The technical features from Phase 1 are deployed into **part-level characteristics**. For example, "weight reduction" gets translated into specific component weights. Output: detailed design specifications. **(1.5 marks)** **Phase 3 — Process Planning (Process Matrix):** The part characteristics are deployed into **manufacturing process operations**. For example, a required tolerance becomes a specific machining process with selected tooling. Output: process flow and operation sheets. **(1.5 marks)** **Phase 4 — Process Quality Control (Control Matrix):** The process operations are deployed into **production controls, quality checkpoints, training procedures, and standard operating procedures (SOPs)**. Output: a complete control plan. **(1.5 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B (b):** > **Benefits of QFD (3.5 marks):** > 1. **Customer Focus:** Forces the organization to systematically gather and integrate the Voice of the Customer, ensuring the final product matches actual needs. > 2. **Reduced Time-to-Market:** By resolving design conflicts early (in the HOQ roof matrix), costly late-stage rework is avoided. Studies (e.g., American Supply Institute) show QFD can reduce development time by 30–50%. > 3. **Cross-Functional Integration:** Brings marketing, design, manufacturing, and quality teams together, breaking organizational silos. > 4. **Documented Decisions:** Provides a clear, traceable rationale for every engineering decision — useful for ISO 9001 and Six Sigma audits. > 5. **Competitive Benchmarking:** The right wall and floor of the HOQ provide a structured way to compare against competitors. > **Limitations of QFD (3.5 marks):** > 1. **Time-Consuming Initial Setup:** A complete QFD analysis can take 8–12 weeks for a complex product, requiring significant resource investment. > 2. **Difficulty Quantifying "Soft" CRs:** Customer attributes like "stylish" or "prestigious" are hard to translate precisely into measurable technical specifications. > 3. **Static Snapshot:** The HOQ captures customer needs at a point in time, but customer preferences evolve — QFD must be re-run periodically. > 4. **Requires Skilled Facilitators:** Poor facilitation can lead to inaccurate importance ratings or biased relationship scores. > 5. **Resistance to Change:** Engineering teams may resist the cross-functional collaboration required. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE MARKS in QFD numerical problems — KTU Examiner Insights:** > - **MISTAKE 1 (2 marks lost):** Using relationship scores 1, 3, 9 in the **wrong direction** — students multiply column × row instead of row × column, getting inverted results. **Always: Importance × Relationship, where Importance is the customer rating.** > - **MISTAKE 2 (1 mark lost):** Forgetting to **normalize** — writing only RIW and skipping NIW. KTU requires both. Normalized values must sum to 100% (or 1.00). > - **MISTAKE 3 (1 mark lost):** Writing the Roof matrix as same as the Body matrix — the Roof is **TR-vs-TR**, NOT CR-vs-TR. > - **MISTAKE 4 (1 mark lost):** In essay-type questions, students forget to **list the scoring convention (9, 3, 1)** for relationships and (++, +, −, −−) for technical correlations. Always state the symbols explicitly. > - **MISTAKE 5 (0.5 mark lost):** Failing to provide an **engineering interpretation** after the calculation. The final line should always say which TR is the highest priority and what action the engineering team should take. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Revise Before Exam:** - QFD = Customer-driven product/service design tool. Introduced by **Yoji Akao in 1966** at Mitsubishi. - House of Quality (HOQ) is the **central matrix** of QFD — Phase 1. - HOQ has **7 building blocks**: Left Wall (CRs), Ceiling (TRs), Body (Relationship), Roof (TR Correlation), Right Wall (Customer Competition), Floor (Technical Competition), Basement (Weights & Targets). - "Whats" = Customer Requirements; "Hows" = Technical Requirements. - Relationship scores: **Strong = 9, Medium = 3, Weak = 1** (use symbols ●, ○, △). - Roof correlation scores: **++ = +9, + = +3, − = −3, −− = −9**. - Importance Rating (I) is on a **1 to 5** scale. - **RIW** = Σ (I × R) for each TR column. - **NIW** = (RIW / ΣRIW) × 100, expressed in percentage. - Always sum NIW to verify = **100%**. - **Four Phases of QFD:** (1) Product Planning, (2) Product Design, (3) Process Planning, (4) Process Control. - **Benefits:** Customer focus, cross-functional teamwork, reduced rework, documented decisions, competitive benchmarking. - **Limitations:** Time-consuming, hard to quantify soft attributes, needs skilled facilitator, static snapshot. - **KTU Favourite Keywords to use in answers:** *Voice of Customer (VOC)*, *Matrix of Matrices*, *Akao*, *Mitsubishi*, *Toyota*, *Trade-off Analysis*, *Built-In Quality*. - **Always end a numerical answer with an Engineering Interpretation line** — KTU examiners award 1 extra mark for it.

topics.title: QFD Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** QFD Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a systematic, team-based, structured process used to translate the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — i.e., qualitative customer desires, needs, and expectations — into quantifiable, actionable **Technical Requirements (TRs)** or **Engineering Characteristics (ECs)** that drive every stage of product design, development, and production. The principal deliverable of QFD is the **House of Quality (HOQ)** matrix, a visual planning tool that interlinks customer needs with engineering metrics.

QFD was originally developed in Japan in **1972** by **Mitsubishi Heavy Industries** and was subsequently refined and popularized by **Yoji Akao** and **Shigeru Mizuno** of the Japanese Society for Quality Control (JSQC). It was later adopted globally by firms like Toyota, Ford, Xerox, and Boeing under the branding of "Customer-Driven Engineering."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect** tasked with designing a new house for a family. The family tells you what they *want* ("big kitchen," "lots of sunlight," "safe for children") — these are fuzzy, emotional, non-technical wishes. The architect cannot directly build from this. The architect must convert these wishes into engineering terms: square footage, window-to-wall ratio, child-safe material ratings, R-value insulation, etc.

**QFD acts exactly like this architect.** It is the "translator" that takes what the **customer feels** and converts it into what the **engineer must build**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** QFD is *not* a quality control tool — it is a **planning and design tool** that operates *before* a product is even built, ensuring that the *design itself* embeds quality rather than inspecting it in later.

## 1.3 Core Terminology You Must Memorize

| Term | Expansion | Meaning in QFD Context |
|---|---|---|
| **VOC** | Voice of the Customer | Raw, unprocessed customer wishes/expectations |
| **CRs** | Customer Requirements (Whats) | Prioritized, structured customer needs |
| **TRs** | Technical Requirements (Hows) | Quantifiable engineering characteristics |
| **HOQ** | House of Quality | The central matrix used to deploy QFD |
| **CFD** | Cause-and-Effect Deployment | Identifies cause-effect linkages in processes |
| **PDS** | Product Deployment Strategy | A QFD matrix variant for product planning |
| **RIR** | Relative Importance Rating | Numerical weight given to customer needs |

## 1.4 The Four Phases of QFD (Akao Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, the **four phases of QFD** is a frequently asked Part A question (3 marks). Memorize the exact sequence:

1. **Product Planning (House of Quality):** Translate customer requirements into technical requirements.
2. **Product Design (Part Deployment):** Break the system into sub-systems and components; deploy TRs into parts specifications.
3. **Process Planning (Process Deployment):** Translate part characteristics into process operations and controls.
4. **Production Planning (Production Deployment):** Translate process operations into production schedules, tooling, and operator instructions.

**Standard Metrics / Constants Used in QFD:**

- **Relationship Strength Symbols:** ⊙ = Strong (Value = **9**), ○ = Medium (Value = **3**), △ = Weak (Value = **1**), Blank = No relationship (Value = **0**).
- **Correlation Matrix Symbols:** + + = Strong Positive, + = Positive, – = Negative, – – = Strong Negative.
- **Importance Weight Range:** Typically **1 to 5** (Low to High customer priority).
- **Absolute Importance:** Calculated as the sum of (Relationship × Importance) for each TR column.
- **Relative Importance:** Normalized as a percentage = (Absolute Importance / Σ Absolute Importance) × 100.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The House of Quality (HOQ) — Structural Decomposition

The House of Quality is a single, multi-room matrix that simultaneously displays customer, engineering, and competitive information. Its "rooms" each serve a distinct mathematical or informational purpose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the structure of the House of Quality," students often list only 5–6 rooms. The **complete HOQ** has **7 distinct rooms** plus 2 supporting lists. Always draw the full house for full marks.

### The Seven Rooms of the House of Quality

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — Located in the left vertical wall. These are the prioritized customer needs, often expressed as voice-of-customer statements like "easy to use," "durable," "lightweight."
2. **Technical Requirements (Hows)** — Located in the top horizontal ceiling. These are measurable engineering characteristics that respond to the Whats (e.g., diameter in mm, weight in grams, current in amps).
3. **Relationship Matrix (The "Center" Room)** — The body of the house. A grid mapping Whats against Hows, populated with the four symbols (⊙, ○, △, blank) to indicate the strength of influence each TR has on each CR.
4. **Roof — Correlation Matrix (Hows vs. Hows)** — Located at the very top. Shows how the technical requirements support or conflict with each other (positive or negative correlations).
5. **Customer Competitive Assessment (Left Edge)** — A column comparison of how the customer's company and its competitors perform on each CR, often rated on a 1–5 scale.
6. **Technical Competitive Assessment (Bottom Edge)** — A row comparison of how the company's and competitors' current products measure on each TR.
7. **Technical Importance Ratings (Far Right Column)** — The calculated priority of each TR, derived from the relationship matrix and customer importance weights.
8. **Target Values / Specifications (Bottom Row)** — The engineered target/limit value the company will set for each TR.
9. **Why / How Chain (Optional Right & Bottom extensions)** — A trace showing what each CR ultimately means and how each TR is technically achieved.

## 2.2 Why QFD Works — The Underlying Logic

QFD operates on three foundational engineering principles:

- **Cross-Functional Integration:** Marketing, design, manufacturing, and quality must converge on a single planning document. In a traditional waterfall process, handovers between these departments cause information loss; QFD prevents this loss by centralizing all decisions in the HOQ.
- **Numerical Translation of Qualitative Data:** Customer statements are inherently subjective. QFD forces a discipline of converting these into numerical weights (1–5), which can then drive engineering decisions objectively.
- **Iterative Deployment of Detail:** The four-phase Akao model progressively drills down from the abstract (customer need) to the concrete (operator instruction), ensuring every level of detail remains traceable to the original VOC.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below have appeared in past KTU University Examination papers. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Symbol Glossary | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Absolute Technical Importance** | $TI_{j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_{i} \times R_{ij})$ | $TI_{j}$ = Technical Importance of TR-$j$; $W_{i}$ = Customer weight of CR-$i$; $R_{ij}$ = Relationship strength (9, 3, 1, 0) | Dimensionless index |
| 2 | **Relative Importance Percentage** | $RI_{j} = \dfrac{TI_{j}}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} TI_{j}} \times 100$ | $RI_{j}$ = Relative Importance of TR-$j$ | Percentage (0–100) |
| 3 | **Weighted Score per Customer Requirement** | $WS_{i} = W_{i} \times \overline{C_{i}}$ | $\overline{C_{i}}$ = Average competitor score on CR-$i$ | Score (1–5) |
| 4 | **Target Setting Benchmark (Stretch Ratio)** | $T_{j} = R \times P_{j,\text{competitor}}$ | $R$ = Stretch factor (typically **1.2 to 1.5**); $P_{j,\text{competitor}}$ = Best competitor value on TR-$j$ | Engineering units |
| 5 | **Normalized Customer Weight** | $w_{i} = \dfrac{W_{i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i}}$ | $w_{i}$ = Normalized weight of CR-$i$ | Sum = 1 |
| 6 | **Degree of Difficulty of TR** | $D_{j} = \dfrac{T_{j}}{C_{j,\text{current}}}$ | $D_{j}$ = Difficulty ratio | Ratio (≥ 1) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of QFD

In **Automotive Engineering** (Toyota, Tata Motors): Used to define door-closure sound, dashboard ergonomics, and fuel-efficiency targets from customer interviews.

In **Software Engineering** (Microsoft, IBM): The "House of Quality" is used to map user-story priorities to technical sprint features in Agile-QFD hybrids.

In **Civil Engineering** (L&T, AECOM): QFD is deployed in green-building design to convert occupant comfort requirements into HVAC tonnage, daylight-factor targets, and U-value specifications.

In **Healthcare** (Mayo Clinic processes): QFD translates patient-experience surveys into measurable care-delivery protocols (wait time, bedside manner scores, medication-error rates).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Building a House of Quality (12 Steps)

> [!NOTE]
> This 12-step procedure is the **gold-standard answer** expected by KTU examiners when the question reads *"Explain the QFD process"* or *"Discuss how a House of Quality is constructed."*

### Step 1: Identify Customer Requirements (CRs)
Collect the Voice of the Customer through interviews, focus groups, complaint logs, and surveys. List them on the **left wall** of the HOQ as broad statements.

- *Example for a cordless electric kettle:* "Boils water quickly," "Lightweight," "Easy to clean," "Safe to touch," "Long battery life."

### Step 2: Determine Customer Importance Ratings
Ask customers (or use existing market-research data) to rate the importance of each CR on a **1 to 5 scale** (1 = Not important, 5 = Very important). Enter these as $W_{i}$ values in the column immediately to the right of the CR list.

### Step 3: Identify Technical Requirements (TRs)
The cross-functional team (engineering + design + marketing) translates each CR into a measurable, quantifiable engineering metric. Enter these in the **ceiling row** of the HOQ.

- *Example mapping:* "Boils water quickly" → Heating element wattage (W); "Lightweight" → Total mass (g); "Safe to touch" → Surface temperature limit (°C).

### Step 4: Build the Relationship Matrix (The Center)
For each cell at the intersection of CR-$i$ and TR-$j$, the engineering team discusses and assigns a relationship symbol based on how strongly TR-$j$ influences CR-$i$.

$$R_{ij} = \begin{cases} 9 & \text{Strong (symbol } \odot \text{)} \\ 3 & \text{Medium (symbol } \bigcirc \text{)} \\ 1 & \text{Weak (symbol } \triangle \text{)} \\ 0 & \text{No relationship (blank)} \end{cases}$$

### Step 5: Build the Correlation Matrix (The Roof)
Above the TR row, build a triangular matrix showing how each pair of TRs interacts. A positive correlation (+) means improving one TR also improves the other; a negative correlation (–) means improving one worsens the other. This is the **engineering trade-off room**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the roof matters:** It forces the engineering team to identify *conflicts early*. For example, increasing wattage (faster boiling) may *increase* the surface temperature (decreasing safety) — a negative correlation.

### Step 6: Calculate Absolute Technical Importance ($TI_{j}$)
For each TR (column $j$), apply the dot-product formula against all CRs (rows $i$):

$$TI_{j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_{i} \times R_{ij})$$

The sum of $W_{i} \times R_{ij}$ for that entire column yields the absolute importance of that TR. Higher values mean the TR is a critical engineering focus.

### Step 7: Normalize to Relative Importance ($RI_{j}$)
Divide each $TI_{j}$ by the sum of all $TI_{j}$ values, then multiply by 100 to get a percentage.

$$RI_{j} = \left( \frac{TI_{j}}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} TI_{j}} \right) \times 100$$

The relative importance of all TRs should sum to 100%. This is the **prioritization ranking** that engineering uses to allocate R&D resources.

### Step 8: Perform Customer Competitive Assessment
For each CR, compare the company's current product with up to 2–3 main competitors using a 1–5 rating. Plot these in the column immediately to the left of the customer weights, or in a separate column block.

### Step 9: Perform Technical Competitive Assessment
For each TR, compare the company's and competitors' measured engineering values. Plot these in the row at the very bottom of the HOQ (beneath the TRs).

### Step 10: Set Target Values / Specifications
Using the technical competitive assessment as a baseline, the team sets engineering targets for each TR. These targets are entered in the **bottom row** of the HOQ. Targets are often set as a *stretch* — slightly better than the best competitor.

$$T_{j,\text{company}} = 1.25 \times P_{j,\text{best competitor}}$$

### Step 11: Identify Conflicts and Trade-offs
Using the correlation matrix, identify any "strong negative" (– –) correlations and decide whether to:
- (a) Accept the trade-off and document it,
- (b) Find an innovative sub-system design that decouples the conflict, or
- (c) Re-define one of the TRs to avoid the conflict.

### Step 12: Finalize and Communicate
The completed HOQ is published to the entire cross-functional team. Each engineering department takes its column of TRs (filtered to its relevant ones) and uses it as the input to the **next phase** (Part Deployment, then Process Deployment, then Production Deployment).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (10-Mark KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example follows the exact 7-mark + 3-mark pattern common in KTU ESE papers.

**Problem:** A car manufacturer is building an HOQ for a mid-size sedan. Customer Requirements (CRs) and their importance weights ($W_i$) are given. The team has identified 4 Technical Requirements (TRs) and assigned the following relationship matrix:

| CR ($i$) | Importance ($W_i$) | TR1: Fuel Efficiency | TR2: Engine Power | TR3: Cabin Noise | TR4: Tire Durability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CR1: Low running cost** | 5 | ⊙ (9) | △ (1) | △ (1) | ○ (3) |
| **CR2: High performance** | 4 | – – | ⊙ (9) | ○ (3) | △ (1) |
| **CR3: Quiet cabin** | 3 | △ (1) | ○ (3) | ⊙ (9) | △ (1) |
| **CR4: Long tire life** | 2 | △ (1) | – – | △ (1) | ⊙ (9) |

**Required:** (a) Calculate Absolute and Relative Importance of each TR. (b) Identify the highest-priority TR.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — Compute Absolute Technical Importance for TR1 (Fuel Efficiency):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TI_{1} &= (W_{1} \times R_{1,1}) + (W_{2} \times R_{2,1}) + (W_{3} \times R_{3,1}) + (W_{4} \times R_{4,1}) \\[6pt]
TI_{1} &= (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 0) + (3 \times 1) + (2 \times 1) \\[6pt]
TI_{1} &= 45 + 0 + 3 + 2 \\[6pt]
TI_{1} &= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute Absolute Technical Importance for TR2 (Engine Power):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TI_{2} &= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) + (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 0) \\[6pt]
TI_{2} &= 5 + 36 + 9 + 0 \\[6pt]
TI_{2} &= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute Absolute Technical Importance for TR3 (Cabin Noise):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TI_{3} &= (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 3) + (3 \times 9) + (2 \times 1) \\[6pt]
TI_{3} &= 5 + 12 + 27 + 2 \\[6pt]
TI_{3} &= 46
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Compute Absolute Technical Importance for TR4 (Tire Durability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TI_{4} &= (5 \times 3) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 1) + (2 \times 9) \\[6pt]
TI_{4} &= 15 + 4 + 3 + 18 \\[6pt]
TI_{4} &= 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Total Absolute Importance:**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{4} TI_{j} = 50 + 50 + 46 + 40 = 186
$$

**Step F — Compute Relative Importance (in %):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RI_{1} &= \frac{50}{186} \times 100 = 26.88\% \\[6pt]
RI_{2} &= \frac{50}{186} \times 100 = 26.88\% \\[6pt]
RI_{3} &= \frac{46}{186} \times 100 = 24.73\% \\[6pt]
RI_{4} &= \frac{40}{186} \times 100 = 21.51\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step G — Verification:** Sum = 26.88 + 26.88 + 24.73 + 21.51 = **100.00%** ✓

**Step H — Conclusion & Highest-Priority TR:**

TR1 (Fuel Efficiency) and TR2 (Engine Power) are **tied at the highest priority** with $RI = 26.88\%$ each. The engineering team should allocate maximum R&D budget to optimizing these two characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Trade-off Identified:** The roof matrix shows a "– –" between CR2 (High Performance) and CR1 (Fuel Efficiency). This means improving engine power *drastically worsens* fuel economy. The team must resolve this conflict via hybrid technology, turbocharging, or variable valve timing.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured, team-based planning methodology that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific, measurable **Technical Requirements (TRs)** for product or service design. The central tool of QFD is the **House of Quality (HOQ)**, a matrix that simultaneously displays customer requirements, technical requirements, their inter-relationships, competitive benchmarks, and target specifications. QFD was developed in Japan in 1972 and aims to ensure that **quality is designed into the product** rather than inspected in later.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for HOQ mention, 1 mark for "designing quality in" objective.)*

### Question 2: List the four phases of QFD. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four phases of QFD (Akao Model) are:

1. **Product Planning (House of Quality):** Translates customer needs into technical requirements.
2. **Part Deployment (Product Design):** Breaks technical requirements into sub-system and component specifications.
3. **Process Planning (Process Deployment):** Translates component specs into process operations and control points.
4. **Production Planning (Production Deployment):** Converts process plans into operator-level production instructions and schedules.

*(1 mark for naming, 1 mark for correct sequence, 1 mark for brief functional description of each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: Explain the House of Quality (HOQ) in detail. Describe each of its components with a suitable diagram. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

#### Part (a) — Definition and Significance of HOQ (7 Marks)

The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the principal matrix used in the Product Planning phase of QFD. It is called a "house" because its seven interconnected rooms resemble the rooms of a house, with walls, a roof, a basement, and a body. It serves as a single-page communication tool that allows cross-functional teams (marketing, design, manufacturing, quality, finance) to converge on a shared understanding of customer needs and engineering responses.

The HOQ was first formalized by Yoji Akao in 1972. It is a planning document, **not a quality control tool** — it is built *before* the product exists, ensuring quality is designed in. The HOQ's value lies in making explicit the otherwise-tacit trade-offs that engineers make daily, thereby preventing conflicts from emerging late in the product life cycle.

#### Part (b) — Components / Rooms of the House of Quality (7 Marks)

**Room 1 – Customer Requirements (Whats) / Left Wall:** The left wall lists the prioritized customer requirements (CRs), expressed in plain language (e.g., "easy to use," "durable"). Each CR is given an importance weight from 1 to 5.

**Room 2 – Technical Requirements (Hows) / Ceiling:** The ceiling lists the measurable engineering characteristics (TRs) that respond to the CRs (e.g., wattage, mass, surface temperature, cycle time).

**Room 3 – Relationship Matrix (Body):** The central body is a grid of CRs × TRs, where each cell contains a symbol indicating the strength of the relationship: ⊙ (strong = 9), ○ (medium = 3), △ (weak = 1), or blank (no relationship = 0).

**Room 4 – Roof (Correlation Matrix):** The triangular roof shows how the Hows interact with each other. + + means strong positive synergy, – – means strong negative trade-off.

**Room 5 – Customer Competitive Assessment (Left Edge):** Compares the company's current product and competitors' products on each CR (rated 1–5).

**Room 6 – Technical Competitive Assessment (Bottom Edge):** Lists the measured engineering values for the company and its competitors on each TR.

**Room 7 – Technical Importance & Target Values (Right Wall + Basement):** The right wall shows the calculated Absolute and Relative Importance of each TR (the engineering priorities). The basement shows the target specification the company will commit to.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Omitting the Roof or Basement:** Most students draw only 5 rooms and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **No numerical example:** The examiner expects at least one calculation of $TI_j$ or $RI_j$ to test applied understanding. A pure definitional answer earns at most 9/14.
> 3. **Confusing QFD with QCC/QCI:** QFD is a *planning* tool; Quality Circles and Control Charts are *control* tools. Writing about Pareto charts or fishbone diagrams in a HOQ answer will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Wrong symbol values:** Students often write "⊙ = 5" or "○ = 2." The KTU-standard values are 9, 3, 1, 0. Memorize these exactly.

---

### Question B: Discuss the QFD process. Explain the steps involved in constructing a House of Quality with a suitable example. (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

#### Part (a) — The QFD Process — 12 Construction Steps (7 Marks)

The QFD process is operationalized through the systematic construction of the House of Quality. The 12 steps are:

1. Identify Customer Requirements (Whats).
2. Assign Customer Importance Weights (1 to 5).
3. Identify Technical Requirements (Hows).
4. Build the Relationship Matrix (Body of House).
5. Build the Correlation Matrix (Roof of House).
6. Calculate Absolute Technical Importance for each TR.
7. Calculate Relative Importance (percentage) for each TR.
8. Perform Customer Competitive Assessment.
9. Perform Technical Competitive Assessment.
10. Set Target Specifications for each TR.
11. Identify and resolve conflicts via the roof matrix.
12. Finalize the HOQ and deploy to the next phase (Part Deployment).

#### Part (b) — Worked Example (7 Marks)

**Example:** A team is designing a cordless electric kettle. The following data is available:

| CR (Importance $W_i$) | TR1: Power (W) | TR2: Mass (g) | TR3: Surface Temp (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CR1: Boils fast (5)** | ⊙ (9) | △ (1) | – – |
| **CR2: Lightweight (3)** | △ (1) | ⊙ (9) | △ (1) |
| **CR3: Safe to touch (4)** | – – | △ (1) | ⊙ (9) |

**Solution:**

**Absolute Importance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TI_{1} &= (5 \times 9) + (3 \times 1) + (4 \times 0) = 48 \\
TI_{2} &= (5 \times 1) + (3 \times 9) + (4 \times 1) = 40 \\
TI_{3} &= (5 \times 0) + (3 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) = 39
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum:** $48 + 40 + 39 = 127$

**Relative Importance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RI_{1} &= \frac{48}{127} \times 100 = 37.80\% \\
RI_{2} &= \frac{40}{127} \times 100 = 31.50\% \\
RI_{3} &= \frac{39}{127} \times 100 = 30.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** TR1 (Power) has the highest priority (37.80%). The roof matrix indicates a *strong negative trade-off* between TR1 (Power) and TR3 (Surface Temperature) — increasing wattage will increase surface temperature. The team must innovate (e.g., double-walled insulation) to resolve this conflict.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks on Numerical QFD:**
> 1. **Forgetting the relationship-value table:** Writing only symbols (⊙, ○, △) without converting them to 9, 3, 1, 0 in the calculation loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the normalization step:** Computing $TI_j$ but not converting to $RI_j$ % is incomplete; the relative importance is the actionable engineering output.
> 3. **No identification of the trade-off:** Even in a numerical question, KTU expects a one-line discussion of the roof matrix and any negative correlations.
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in the dot product:** Many students multiply $W_i$ by the *symbol* (⊙ = 1) instead of the *value* (⊙ = 9). Always show the value substituted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for QFD Process (Module 4):**

- **QFD = Quality Function Deployment** — a *planning* tool, not a control tool.
- Developed in **Japan, 1972**, by Yoji Akao and Shigeru Mizuno at Mitsubishi Heavy Industries.
- **Core purpose:** Translate Voice of the Customer (VOC) into Technical Requirements (TRs).
- **Principal deliverable:** The **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a 7-room matrix.
- **Four Phases (Akao Model):** Product Planning → Part Deployment → Process Planning → Production Planning.
- **Relationship Matrix Symbols & Values:** ⊙ = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1, Blank = 0.
- **Correlation Matrix (Roof):** + + (strong positive), + (positive), – (negative), – – (strong negative).
- **Key Formulas:**
  * Absolute Importance: $TI_{j} = \sum (W_i \times R_{ij})$
  * Relative Importance: $RI_{j} = (TI_j / \sum TI_j) \times 100$
  * Target Setting: $T_j = 1.25 \times P_{j,\text{best competitor}}$ (Stretch factor 1.2–1.5)
- **Customer Importance Weights:** Rated 1 to 5 (Low to High).
- **House has 7 rooms + 2 edges** (Customer Competitive Assessment on the left, Technical Competitive Assessment on the bottom).
- **Always show the Why-How chain** in a complete HOQ (CRs traced to engineering specs).
- **Most common KTU question patterns:**
  1. 3-mark: Define QFD / List phases / State benefits & limitations.
  2. 7-mark sub-question: Draw and label the House of Quality.
  3. 7-mark sub-question: Numerical calculation of $TI_j$ and $RI_j$ from a given matrix.
- **Common mistakes:** Confusing QFD with QCC; forgetting the roof; using wrong symbol values; omitting normalization.
- **Real-world adopters:** Toyota, Ford, Boeing, Xerox, Tata Motors, Microsoft, L&T, AECOM.
- **Limitation to remember for exams:** QFD is **time-intensive, requires skilled teams, and is less effective for radical (disruptive) innovations** where customer needs are not yet articulated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Benefits # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, *Benefits* refer to the measurable and intangible positive outcomes — both **tangible** (quantifiable, financial, operational) and **intangible** (cultural, strategic, relational) — that accrue to all stakeholders of an organization when TQM principles, tools, and philosophies are successfully implemented and sustained over the long term. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PESFT746, Module 4), the benefits of TQM are categorized across **five stakeholder domains**: 1. **Benefits to Customers** — internal and external 2. **Benefits to Employees / Workforce** 3. **Benefits to the Organization** (operational, financial, strategic) 4. **Benefits to Suppliers and Partners** 5. **Benefits to Society at Large** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Board examinations, students are expected to classify benefits along the **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) and align them with the **five stakeholders of the organization**. Marks are awarded for a clear stakeholder-wise tabular mapping. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Five-Star Hospital" Model** Imagine a hospital that treats patients (customers), employs doctors and nurses (employees), purchases surgical equipment from vendors (suppliers), runs as a business (organization), and operates within a town (society). When the hospital adopts TQM: - **Patients** receive faster, safer, error-free care → *Customer Benefit* - **Doctors/Nurses** work in a safer environment with clear protocols and recognition → *Employee Benefit* - **The Hospital** sees fewer malpractice suits, lower re-admission rates, and stronger brand reputation → *Organizational Benefit* - **Equipment Vendors** receive stable long-term contracts with clear quality specs → *Supplier Benefit* - **The Town** enjoys a healthier population and economic activity → *Societal Benefit* In the same way, TQM in a manufacturing or service organization radiates benefits **outward** from the process core to every stakeholder — like ripples in a pond. **Key Quality Metrics for TQM Benefits (Industry-Standard):** - **Six Big Losses** (in TPM/OEE context): reduced by 20–50% - **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** — target **≥ 90%** - **Defect Rate (DPMO — Defects Per Million Opportunities)** — target **≤ 3.4 (Six Sigma)** - **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — target **≥ 95%** - **Employee Turnover Rate** — target **≤ 10%** annually - **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — typically **15–40%** of sales revenue in un-managed firms # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stakeholder-Wise Benefits Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Benefits to Customers Customers — the **external** customers (end-users buying the product/service) and **internal** customers (the next person in the workflow) — are the primary beneficiaries of TQM. | Sub-Benefit | Explanation | |-------------|-------------| | **Improved Product/Service Quality** | Better conformance to specifications and reliability | | **Lower Cost of Ownership** | Fewer defects → lower warranty, repair, replacement costs | | **Faster Delivery / Lead Time Reduction** | Streamlined processes reduce cycle time | | **Higher Perceived Value** | Brand reputation from quality translates into willingness to pay premium pricing | | **Improved Safety & Reliability** | Critical for medical, automotive, aviation sectors | | **Better Customer Service & Responsiveness** | Faster complaint resolution, robust feedback loops | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners often frame a 7-mark question as *"Discuss the benefits of TQM to customers"* and expect a minimum of **5–6 enumerated points** with one real-world example (e.g., Toyota, Infosys). ### 2.1.2 Benefits to Employees TQM is fundamentally **people-centric** (per Deming's *System of Profound Knowledge*). | Sub-Benefit | Explanation | |-------------|-------------| | **Improved Job Satisfaction** | Pride in workmanship, involvement in decision-making | | **Better Working Conditions** | Safer, cleaner, ergonomically designed workplaces | | **Skill Development & Training** | Cross-functional training (Kaizen, 5S, statistical process control) | | **Reduced Workplace Stress** | Fewer fire-fighting situations, predictable workflows | | **Higher Morale & Motivation** | Recognition through quality circles and suggestion schemes | | **Lower Accident & Injury Rates** | Process discipline reduces human-error incidents | | **Improved Career Growth** | TQM-skilled professionals are highly valued | | **Empowerment & Ownership** | Teams are authorized to make on-the-spot improvements | ### 2.1.3 Benefits to the Organization This is the **most heavily tested** category in KTU exams. It spans operational, financial, and strategic gains. #### A. Operational Benefits - **Reduced Waste** (muda) — Rework, scrap, overproduction, waiting, motion, transport, inventory (the **7 Wastes** of Lean) - **Lower Rework & Rejection Rates** — Direct impact on **COPQ** - **Improved Process Capability** — Higher $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ indices - **Reduced Machine Downtime** — Through TPM (Total Productive Maintenance), achieving **OEE ≥ 85%** - **Better Inventory Turnover** — Just-In-Time (JIT) pulls reduce carrying cost - **Faster Cycle Time** — Value Stream Mapping (VSM) eliminates non-value-added activities #### B. Financial Benefits - **Reduced COPQ** — Typically falls from 20–40% to 5–10% of sales within 3–5 years - **Higher Profit Margins** — Through premium pricing + lower costs - **Improved Return on Investment (ROI)** and **Return on Assets (ROA)** - **Lower Warranty & After-Sales Costs** - **Increased Market Share** #### C. Strategic Benefits - **Competitive Advantage** — Sustainable differentiation - **Stronger Brand Equity** — Reputation for quality (e.g., Tata, Bosch, Maruti Suzuki) - **Improved Organizational Agility** — Quicker response to market changes - **Better Stakeholder Relationships** — Investors prefer quality-managed firms - **Long-Term Survival & Growth** — Quality becomes an organizational DNA ### 2.1.4 Benefits to Suppliers - **Stable, Long-Term Contracts** — Partnership-based sourcing (vs. transactional) - **Clear Quality Specifications** — Reduced ambiguity, fewer rejections - **Joint Problem-Solving** — Supplier development programs - **Reduced Audit Fatigue** — Trust-based supplier partnerships - **Mutual Growth & Capacity Building** - **Just-In-Time (JIT) Integration** — Smooth, predictable delivery cycles ### 2.1.5 Benefits to Society - **Environmental Protection** — Less waste, recycling (3R: Reduce, Reuse, Recycle), ISO 14001 alignment - **Resource Conservation** — Efficient use of energy, water, raw materials - **Safer Products in the Market** — Reduced product recalls, public safety - **Economic Development** — Higher employment, exports, GDP contribution - **Improved Public Services** — When TQM principles are extended to healthcare, education, government - **Stronger National Brand** — "Made in [Country]" tag gains credibility ## 2.2 Tangible vs. Intangible Benefits > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always classify benefits along this axis when asked "Discuss the benefits of TQM." | Tangible Benefits (Quantifiable) | Intangible Benefits (Non-Quantifiable) | |----------------------------------|----------------------------------------| | Reduced defect rate (DPMO ↓) | Improved employee morale | | Lower cost of poor quality (COPQ ↓) | Stronger brand image | | Higher productivity (units/hour ↑) | Better customer loyalty | | Reduced inventory levels | Improved organizational culture | | Higher market share (%) | Enhanced learning & knowledge management | | Lower employee turnover | Better supplier relationships | | Higher ROI / ROA | Innovation capacity | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Typical Target | Use Case | |---|------------------|------------|----------------|----------| | 1 | **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | $\le 3.4$ (Six Sigma) | Quality level benchmarking | | 2 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units produced}}$ | $\ge 95\%$ | Process efficiency | | 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Maximize | Multi-step process yield | | 4 | **Process Capability Index ($C_p$)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | $\ge 1.33$ | Process centering | | 5 | **Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\ge 1.33$ | Process centering + spread | | 6 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure costs}$ | Minimize | Financial benefit tracking | | 7 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$ | $\ge 85\%$ (World-class) | Equipment benefit | | 8 | **Employee Turnover Rate** | $\text{Turnover \%} = \dfrac{\text{Number of separations}}{\text{Average no. of employees}} \times 100$ | $\le 10\%$ | HR benefit tracking | | 9 | **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** | $\text{CSAT} = \dfrac{\text{Sum of ratings}}{\text{Maximum possible score}} \times 100$ | $\ge 90\%$ | Customer benefit tracking | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on Tables:** All vertical bars in formulas above use `\vert` or contextual grouping (e.g., $\min(\cdot,\cdot)$) to keep markdown table syntax intact. No raw $\vert$ pipes inside table cells. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of TQM Benefits - **Manufacturing:** Toyota Production System, Bosch India — Six Sigma + Lean → COPQ reduced by 30–50% - **Healthcare:** Apollo Hospitals — TQM reduced average patient discharge time by 22% - **IT/Service:** Infosys, TCS — CMM/CMMI frameworks (TQM-aligned) improved on-time delivery - **Government:** Kerala State IT Mission applied TQM principles to e-governance - **Education:** Accreditation frameworks (NBA, NAAC) are TQM-inspired # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Below is an **exhaustive numerical demonstration** of how TQM benefits are quantitatively measured. This type of calculation frequently appears in KTU numerical/analytical 14-mark questions. ## 3.1 Problem Statement (Worked Example) A precision components manufacturing firm produces a gearbox housing. The following data was collected **before** and **after** TQM implementation: | Parameter | Before TQM | After TQM | |-----------|------------|-----------| | Units produced per month | 10,000 | 10,000 | | Opportunities for defect per unit | 5 | 5 | | Number of defects found | 850 | 120 | | Rework cost (₹/month) | 2,00,000 | 25,000 | | Warranty claims (₹/month) | 1,50,000 | 20,000 | | Inspection cost (₹/month) | 80,000 | 1,00,000 | | Prevention cost (₹/month) | 30,000 | 1,20,000 | | Customer satisfaction (%) | 72 | 94 | | Employee turnover (%) | 18 | 7 | | On-time delivery (%) | 78 | 96 | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Calculations ### Step 1: Compute DPMO Before TQM The DPMO formula is: $$ \text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6} $$ Substituting **Before TQM** values: $$ \text{DPMO}_{\text{before}} = \dfrac{850}{10{,}000 \times 5} \times 10^{6} $$ Breaking this down: $$ \dfrac{850}{50{,}000} = 0.017 $$ Multiplying by $10^{6}$: $$ 0.017 \times 10^{6} = 17{,}000 \ \text{DPMO} $$ So, **DPMO before TQM = 17,000**. ### Step 2: Compute DPMO After TQM $$ \text{DPMO}_{\text{after}} = \dfrac{120}{10{,}000 \times 5} \times 10^{6} $$ Step-wise: $$ \dfrac{120}{50{,}000} = 0.0024 $$ Multiplying by $10^{6}$: $$ 0.0024 \times 10^{6} = 2{,}400 \ \text{DPMO} $$ So, **DPMO after TQM = 2,400**. ### Step 3: DPMO Reduction (Benefit Magnitude) $$ \Delta \text{DPMO} = 17{,}000 - 2{,}400 = 14{,}600 $$ Percentage reduction: $$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{14{,}600}{17{,}000} \times 100 \approx 85.88\% $$ **Interpretation:** TQM implementation reduced the defect rate by approximately **85.88%**, taking the firm from a ~4-Sigma level to a ~4.5-Sigma level. ### Step 4: Compute First Pass Yield (FPY) **Before TQM:** $$ \text{FPY}_{\text{before}} = \dfrac{10{,}000 - 850}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{9{,}150}{10{,}000} = 0.915 = 91.5\% $$ **After TQM:** $$ \text{FPY}_{\text{after}} = \dfrac{10{,}000 - 120}{10{,}000} = \dfrac{9{,}880}{10{,}000} = 0.988 = 98.8\% $$ ### Step 5: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) **COPQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure** - **Prevention cost** = training, quality planning, process design - **Appraisal cost** = inspection, testing, audits - **Internal failure cost** = rework, scrap, re-inspection - **External failure cost** = warranty, returns, complaints **Before TQM:** $$ \text{COPQ}_{\text{before}} = 30{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 4{,}60{,}000 \ \text{₹/month} $$ **After TQM:** $$ \text{COPQ}_{\text{after}} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 20{,}000 = 2{,}65{,}000 \ \text{₹/month} $$ **Net COPQ Reduction:** $$ \Delta \text{COPQ} = 4{,}60{,}000 - 2{,}65{,}000 = 1{,}95{,}000 \ \text{₹/month} $$ Annualized saving: $$ 1{,}95{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹} \ 23{,}40{,}000 \ \text{per year} $$ ### Step 6: Customer Satisfaction Improvement $$ \Delta \text{CSAT} = 94\% - 72\% = 22 \ \text{percentage points} $$ $$ \% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{22}{72} \times 100 \approx 30.56\% $$ ### Step 7: Employee Turnover Reduction $$ \Delta \text{Turnover} = 18\% - 7\% = 11 \ \text{percentage points} $$ $$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{11}{18} \times 100 \approx 61.11\% $$ ### Step 8: On-Time Delivery Improvement $$ \Delta \text{OTD} = 96\% - 78\% = 18 \ \text{percentage points} $$ $$ \% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{18}{78} \times 100 \approx 23.08\% $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table | Benefit Metric | Before TQM | After TQM | Absolute Δ | % Improvement | |----------------|------------|-----------|------------|---------------| | **DPMO** | 17,000 | 2,400 | −14,600 | 85.88% ↓ | | **FPY (%)** | 91.5 | 98.8 | +7.3 | 7.98% ↑ | | **COPQ (₹/month)** | 4,60,000 | 2,65,000 | −1,95,000 | 42.39% ↓ | | **Customer Satisfaction (%)** | 72 | 94 | +22 | 30.56% ↑ | | **Employee Turnover (%)** | 18 | 7 | −11 | 61.11% ↓ | | **On-Time Delivery (%)** | 78 | 96 | +18 | 23.08% ↑ | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Notice that **Prevention + Appraisal costs increased** (from ₹1,10,000 to ₹2,20,000), but this was more than offset by the massive drop in **Failure Costs** (from ₹3,50,000 to ₹45,000). This is the **classic Juran-Crosby "Investment vs. Loss" trade-off** that examiners love to test. ## 3.4 Juran's Cost-of-Quality Logic $$ \text{Total Quality Cost (TQC)} = P + A + IF + EF $$ where $P$ = Prevention, $A$ = Appraisal, $IF$ = Internal Failure, $EF$ = External Failure. As TQM matures, the trend is: $$ P \uparrow, \quad A \uparrow, \quad IF \downarrow \downarrow, \quad EF \downarrow \downarrow, \quad \text{TQC} \downarrow $$ This is known as the **"Feigenbaum Curve"** — failure costs drop sharply once prevention investment crosses a threshold. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Five-Stakeholder Benefits Model (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following block-level functional architecture maps how TQM benefits propagate outward from the **Process Core** to all five stakeholder classes. ```mermaid flowchart TD coreA[PROCESS CORE
TQM PHILOSOPHY] --> custB[CUSTOMERS
External and Internal] coreA --> empC[EMPLOYEES
Workforce] coreA --> orgD[ORGANIZATION
Operational Financial Strategic] coreA --> supE[SUPPLIERS
Partners and Vendors] coreA --> socF[SOCIETY
Community and Environment] custB --> custB1[Improved Quality] custB --> custB2[Lower Cost of Ownership] custB --> custB3[Faster Delivery] custB --> custB4[Higher Safety and Reliability] empC --> empC1[Job Satisfaction] empC --> empC2[Skill Development] empC --> empC3[Reduced Stress] empC --> empC4[Empowerment] orgD --> orgD1[Reduced Waste] orgD --> orgD2[Lower COPQ] orgD --> orgD3[Higher Market Share] orgD --> orgD4[Brand Equity] orgD --> orgD5[Competitive Advantage] supE --> supE1[Stable Contracts] supE --> supE2[Joint Problem Solving] supE --> supE3[Reduced Audit Burden] socF --> socF1[Environmental Protection] socF --> socF2[Resource Conservation] socF --> socF3[Economic Development] socF --> socF4[Public Safety] classDef core fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef stake fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px classDef leaf fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px class coreA core class custB,empC,orgD,supE,socF stake class custB1,custB2,custB3,custB4,empC1,empC2,empC3,empC4,orgD1,orgD2,orgD3,orgD4,orgD5,supE1,supE2,supE3,socF1,socF2,socF3,socF4 leaf ``` ## 4.2 Tangible vs. Intangible Benefits Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD root[TQM BENEFITS] --> t1[Tangible Benefits] root --> i1[Intangible Benefits] t1 --> t1a[Lower DPMO] t1 --> t1b[Lower COPQ] t1 --> t1c[Higher OEE] t1 --> t1d[Higher Market Share] t1 --> t1e[Lower Turnover] i1 --> i1a[Brand Reputation] i1 --> i1b[Employee Morale] i1 --> i1c[Customer Loyalty] i1 --> i1d[Innovation Culture] i1 --> i1e[Supplier Trust] classDef rootClass fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef tanClass fill:#17becf,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px classDef intClass fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px class root rootClass class t1,t1a,t1b,t1c,t1d,t1e tanClass class i1,i1a,i1b,i1c,i1d,i1e intClass ``` ## 4.3 Feigenbaum Cost-of-Quality Curve — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR axisX[Investment in Prevention ↑] --> ptA[Stage A: Low Investment] ptA --> ptB[Stage B: Optimal Investment Zone] ptB --> ptC[Stage C: Over-Investment] ptA --> failure1[High Failure Costs
Quality Cost = HIGH] ptB --> failure2[Low Failure Costs
Quality Cost = MINIMUM] ptC --> failure3[Rising Prevention Cost
Quality Cost = HIGH again] classDef opt fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef bad fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:1px class ptB,opt,axisX opt class failure1,failure3,ptA,ptC bad ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The optimal TQM investment zone (Stage B) is where prevention and appraisal costs are sufficient to drive failure costs to a minimum — yielding the **highest net benefit**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Q:** List any **six tangible benefits** of TQM to an organization. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each):** 1. **Reduction in defect rate (DPMO)** — fewer defective products per million opportunities. 2. **Lower Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — prevention investment offset by huge drop in failure costs. 3. **Higher productivity** — improved throughput, less rework time. 4. **Increased market share** — quality reputation attracts more customers. 5. **Improved return on investment (ROI)** and profitability. 6. **Lower employee turnover rate** — better retention of skilled workforce. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** For 3 marks, exactly **six crisp points** are expected. Avoid long paragraphs. --- ### Question 2 **Q:** What are the **benefits of TQM to customers**? Explain with two examples. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** Customers — both internal and external — gain from TQM in the following ways: - **Improved Product Quality:** Conformance to specifications and reliability improve significantly. - **Lower Cost of Ownership:** Fewer defects mean lower warranty, repair, and replacement expenses borne by the customer. - **Faster Delivery:** Streamlined processes reduce lead time. - **Higher Perceived Value & Safety:** Quality-marked products inspire confidence. **Examples:** - *Toyota:* TQM-led production yields some of the lowest warranty claim rates in the global auto industry. - *Apple:* Robust quality control ensures consistent user experience, justifying premium pricing. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Set A]** > **(a)** Discuss in detail the **benefits of TQM to the organization**, classifying them as operational, financial, and strategic. **[7 Marks]** > **(b)** With a suitable numerical example, explain how **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** is computed and demonstrate how TQM reduces it. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Operational Benefits:** - Reduction in **waste** (muda) — Lean principles eliminate the 7 wastes (overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, motion, defects). - Lower **rework and rejection** rates. - Improved **process capability** ($C_p$, $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$). - Reduced **machine downtime** through TPM (Target: OEE $\geq$ **85%**). - Faster **cycle time** via Value Stream Mapping. **Financial Benefits:** - Substantial **reduction in COPQ** — typical drop from 20–40% to 5–10% of sales. - **Higher profit margins** — through both cost reduction and premium pricing. - Improved **ROI, ROA, and cash flow**. - Lower **warranty and after-sales service costs**. **Strategic Benefits:** - **Competitive advantage** — sustainable differentiation in the market. - **Stronger brand equity** — recognition for quality (Bosch, Tata, Maruti). - **Improved organizational agility** — faster market response. - **Better investor confidence** — quality-managed firms attract capital. - **Long-term survival** — quality becomes embedded in DNA. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **COPQ Formula:** $$ \text{COPQ} = P + A + IF + EF $$ where: - $P$ = Prevention cost - $A$ = Appraisal cost - $IF$ = Internal Failure cost - $EF$ = External Failure cost **Numerical Example:** A firm records the following monthly costs: | Cost Component | Before TQM (₹) | After TQM (₹) | |----------------|----------------|---------------| | Prevention (P) | 30,000 | 1,20,000 | | Appraisal (A) | 80,000 | 1,00,000 | | Internal Failure (IF) | 2,00,000 | 25,000 | | External Failure (EF) | 1,50,000 | 20,000 | **Before TQM:** $$ \text{COPQ}_{\text{before}} = 30{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹} \ 4{,}60{,}000 $$ **After TQM:** $$ \text{COPQ}_{\text{after}} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 20{,}000 = \text{₹} \ 2{,}65{,}000 $$ **Net Savings:** $$ \Delta \text{COPQ} = 4{,}60{,}000 - 2{,}65{,}000 = \text{₹} \ 1{,}95{,}000 \ \text{per month} $$ $$ \text{Annual Savings} = 1{,}95{,}000 \times 12 = \text{₹} \ 23{,}40{,}000 $$ $$ \% \text{ Reduction} = \dfrac{1{,}95{,}000}{4{,}60{,}000} \times 100 \approx 42.39\% $$ **Interpretation:** Although prevention + appraisal costs rose (from ₹1,10,000 to ₹2,20,000), the dramatic fall in failure costs (from ₹3,50,000 to ₹45,000) produced a **net COPQ reduction of ~42.39%**, demonstrating TQM's financial benefit. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Set B]** > **(a)** Explain the **benefits of TQM to employees** and to **suppliers** with relevant points. **[7 Marks]** > **(b)** Differentiate between **tangible and intangible benefits** of TQM. Why are intangible benefits harder to measure yet strategically more important? **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Benefits to Employees** (≈3.5 marks): - **Job satisfaction** — pride in workmanship and meaningful involvement. - **Better working conditions** — safer, cleaner, ergonomically designed workplaces. - **Skill development** — training in tools like 5S, Kaizen, SPC, FMEA. - **Lower stress and burnout** — fewer fire-fighting scenarios. - **Higher morale** — recognition through quality circles and suggestion schemes. - **Empowerment** — authority to stop production (Jidoka) and make improvements. - **Improved career prospects** — TQM-skilled professionals are highly valued. - **Lower accident rates** — disciplined processes reduce human-error incidents. **Benefits to Suppliers** (≈3.5 marks): - **Long-term, stable contracts** — partnership-based sourcing (vs. transactional). - **Clear and fair quality specifications** — reduced ambiguity. - **Joint problem-solving** — supplier development programs. - **Reduced audit burden** — trust-based relationships lower the need for frequent audits. - **Mutual capacity building** — knowledge transfer from buyer to supplier. - **Just-In-Time integration** — smooth, predictable deliveries. - **Better payment cycles and reduced disputes** — fair, transparent dealings. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Tangible Benefits** — quantifiable in monetary or numeric terms (≈3 marks): - Lower **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) - Lower **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality) - Higher **FPY** (First Pass Yield) - Higher **OEE** (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) - Higher **market share %** - Lower **employee turnover %** - Higher **productivity** (units/hour) - Higher **ROI / ROA** **Intangible Benefits** — non-quantifiable, perception/relationship-based (≈2 marks): - Stronger **brand image** and **reputation** - Higher **employee morale** and **loyalty** - **Customer loyalty** and emotional connection - **Organizational culture** of continuous improvement - **Innovation capacity** and learning organization traits - **Supplier trust** and long-term partnerships - **Knowledge management** and intellectual capital **Why intangible benefits are harder to measure yet strategically more important** (≈2 marks): - They are **perception-driven** and manifest over years, not months. - They require **qualitative tools** (surveys, focus groups, employee engagement indices) that are inherently subjective. - However, they form the **cultural bedrock** that sustains all tangible gains. - For example, a company may achieve low DPMO in the short term through policing, but **only an embedded quality culture** sustains that performance over decades. - Intangible benefits are **strategic differentiators** — competitors can replicate machines and processes, but **not the culture**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT list benefits without classifying them** — Examiners explicitly award marks for stakeholder-wise or category-wise structuring. > 2. **Avoid mixing TQM with Six Sigma or Lean** — These are *tools* within TQM, not synonyms. > 3. **For numerical questions on COPQ, ALWAYS show the formula first** ($P+A+IF+EF$) before substitution. Skipping this loses 1 mark. > 4. **Real-world examples earn bonus marks** — Mention Toyota, Bosch, Tata, Apollo, or Infosys to stand out. > 5. **Avoid vague phrases** like *"quality improves"* — Use specific metrics: *"DPMO drops from 17,000 to 2,400"* or *"COPQ reduces by 42%"*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:** - ⭐ **5 Stakeholder Domains:** Customers, Employees, Organization, Suppliers, Society. - ⭐ **Tangible vs. Intangible** classification is **mandatory** in 7-mark answers. - ⭐ **COPQ Formula:** $\text{COPQ} = P + A + IF + EF$ - ⭐ **DPMO Formula:** $\dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^{6}$ — target $\le 3.4$ (Six Sigma). - ⭐ **FPY Target:** $\ge 95\%$; **OEE Target:** $\ge 85\%$. - ⭐ **Customer Satisfaction Target:** $\ge 90\%$; **Employee Turnover Target:** $\le 10\%$. - ⭐ **7 Wastes of Lean (TIMWOOD):** Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects. - ⭐ **Feigenbaum Logic:** Investing in **Prevention** reduces **Failure** costs disproportionately — this is the *core financial argument* for TQM. - ⭐ **Deming's Philosophy:** Quality benefits everyone — *"Quality is everyone's responsibility."* - ⭐ **Juran Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement — benefits map onto each. - ⭐ **Key Real-World Anchors:** Toyota (Lean TPS), Bosch (Six Sigma), Tata (TQM in steel/auto), Infosys (CMM/CMMI), Apollo Hospitals (TQM in healthcare). - ⭐ **Crosby:** *"Quality is free"* — the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of failure. - ⭐ **Ishikawa:** *Customer-oriented quality* — every benefit ultimately flows to the customer. - ⭐ Always close answers with a concluding statement: *"Thus, TQM is a holistic philosophy that creates a virtuous cycle of benefits for all stakeholders."*

topics.title: Taguchi Quality Loss Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Taguchi Quality Loss Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QLF):** A quadratic mathematical model proposed by **Dr. Genichi Taguchi** (Japanese quality engineer, 1924–2012) that quantifies the **economic loss to society** caused by deviations of a product's functional quality characteristic from its **target value**. It is formally expressed as $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$, where every deviation from the target—even within traditional specification limits—incurs a measurable cost to the manufacturer, user, and ultimately society.

The Taguchi Quality Loss Function fundamentally redefines the concept of quality by introducing a **continuous loss model** rather than the classical binary pass/fail (conforming/non-conforming) classification. In Taguchi's philosophy, **any deviation from the target produces loss**, and the loss is **proportional to the square of the deviation**, capturing both the customer's dissatisfaction and the manufacturer's hidden costs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Lemon Juice" Analogy:** Imagine buying a bottle of branded lemon juice advertised as "100% pure lemon." If the bottle contains 99% lemon juice and 1% water, you may not detect the difference immediately—but the taste, aroma, and freshness will slowly degrade, eventually causing dissatisfaction. The **quality loss is real**, even though the product is technically "within specifications."
>
> Taguchi's QLF says: **"Loss does not begin at the specification limit—it begins at the target!"** Every microliter of deviation from 100% purity imposes a cost on the customer (reduced satisfaction) and the manufacturer (loss of brand loyalty, returns, warranty claims). The QLF mathematically captures this invisible, continuous loss.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

The following **KTU High-Yield keywords** must be memorized verbatim:

- **$L(y)$** — Quality Loss (typically in monetary units like ₹, $, €)
- **$y$** — Actual value of the quality characteristic
- **$m$ (or $T$)** — Target value (the ideal value of the quality characteristic)
- **$\Delta_0$** — Functional tolerance limit (the specification boundary)
- **$A_0$** — Loss incurred at the functional tolerance limit (replacement/repair cost)
- **$k$** — **Quality Loss Coefficient** (a proportionality constant unique to the product/process)
- **S/N Ratio** — Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Taguchi's robustness metric)
- **SSE (Smaller is better)** — Loss increases as $y$ increases
- **LBB (Larger is better)** — Loss increases as $y$ decreases
- **NTB (Nominal-the-best)** — Loss minimum at the target $m$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention:** The unit of **$L(y)$ is money** (loss to society), the unit of **$k$ is money per unit² of the quality characteristic**, and **$y, m, \Delta_0$ share the same engineering unit** (mm, V, °C, kg, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Taguchi's QLF — The Paradigm Shift

Traditional Western quality thinking treated quality as a **binary attribute**:

| Traditional View | Taguchi's View |
|---|---|
| Product inside specs = Good | Only product **at target** = Good |
| Product outside specs = Bad | Any deviation = Loss (even inside specs) |
| Focus on **conformance to specs** | Focus on **conformance to target** |
| Loss = Zero inside specs | Loss = **Continuous** from target |
| Quality cost = Scrap + Rework | Quality cost = **Loss to society** (broader) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question may ask you to "Compare the traditional view of quality with Taguchi's view." Memorize this contrast table—it is a guaranteed high-yield topic in Module 4.

### 2.2 The Three Categories of Quality Characteristics

Taguchi classified quality characteristics into **three distinct types**, each with its own loss function form:

#### 2.2.1 Nominal-the-Best (NTB) — "Target is Best"
- Example: Target diameter of a shaft = 10 mm; any deviation (up or down) causes loss.
- General form: $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$
- Loss is **symmetric** around the target.
- Used for: dimensions, voltage output, viscosity, concentration, pressure, temperature, etc.

#### 2.2.2 Smaller-the-Better (STB)
- Example: Defects, emissions, noise level, wear, leakage.
- The ideal value is **zero**, so $m = 0$.
- Form: $L(y) = k \cdot y^2$
- Loss increases monotonically as $y$ increases.

#### 2.2.3 Larger-the-Better (LTB)
- Example: Strength, durability, battery life, fuel efficiency, yield.
- The ideal value is **infinity**, so we use the inverse.
- Form: $L(y) = k \cdot \dfrac{1}{y^2}$
- Loss increases as $y$ decreases.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following four equations—they appear in nearly every Taguchi QLF numerical problem.**

| # | Formula | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ | Nominal-the-best loss | When target $m \neq 0$ |
| 2 | $L(y) = k \cdot y^2$ | Smaller-the-better loss | When ideal value is zero |
| 3 | $L(y) = k \cdot \dfrac{1}{y^2}$ | Larger-the-better loss | When ideal value is infinity |
| 4 | $k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}}$ | Quality loss coefficient | To find $k$ using tolerance and replacement cost |

Where:
- $A_0$ = Loss to customer when product just reaches the functional tolerance limit (i.e., when $y = m \pm \Delta_0$).
- $\Delta_0$ = Functional tolerance (half-width of the specification window for NTB).
- $L(y)$ is always **$\geq 0$** (non-negative) for any $y$.
- $L(m) = 0$ — at the target, there is **no loss**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Table Note (Markdown Safety):** I have used $\Delta_0^{\,2}$ instead of $\vert \Delta_0 \vert^2$ to avoid breaking the table's pipe-delimited column structure. Always format expressions with the `^` superscript notation in tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Taguchi QLF |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Engine block dimensions, brake pad wear, fuel efficiency optimization |
| **Electronics** | Resistor values, capacitor tolerance, PCB trace widths, battery life |
| **Manufacturing** | Shaft diameter, surface roughness, hardness of tools, weld strength |
| **Healthcare** | Drug dosage deviation, surgical instrument dimensions, blood test accuracy |
| **Software** | Response time (smaller is better), uptime % (larger is better), bug count |
| **Telecom** | Signal strength, packet loss, call drop rate |

Taguchi's QLF allows engineers to **convert engineering deviations directly into monetary loss**, enabling **cost-quality trade-off decisions** that traditional QC (which only counts defects) could never perform.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Quality Loss Coefficient ($k$)

**Starting Assumption:** When the product's quality characteristic reaches the functional tolerance limit $\Delta_0$ from the target, the customer incurs a loss equal to the replacement/repair cost $A_0$.

At the tolerance limit, the deviation is $|y - m| = \Delta_0$, so the loss becomes:

$$L(y) = A_0 \quad \text{when} \quad (y - m)^2 = \Delta_0^{\,2}$$

Substituting into the general form $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$:

$$A_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^{\,2}$$

Solving for $k$ (the proportionality constant):

$$k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}}$$

This is the **Taguchi Quality Loss Coefficient**, with units of *currency per (engineering unit)²*.

### 3.2 General Verification — Loss at Target

At the target value $y = m$, the deviation is zero:

$$L(m) = k(m - m)^2 = k \cdot 0 = 0$$

This confirms that **at-target production incurs zero quality loss**, validating Taguchi's philosophy of target-centering.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (NTB Case)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Standard Problem (frequently asked):** A shaft must be machined to a target diameter of **25 mm**. The customer's functional tolerance is **$\pm 0.5$ mm**, and the cost of replacement when a shaft just reaches the tolerance limit is **₹400**.
> **(a)** Find the quality loss coefficient $k$.
> **(b)** Calculate the quality loss for shafts of diameter **25.0 mm, 25.2 mm, 24.7 mm, and 25.5 mm**.

**Given Data:**
- Target value, $m = 25$ mm
- Functional tolerance, $\Delta_0 = 0.5$ mm
- Loss at tolerance limit, $A_0 = \text{₹}400$
- Quality characteristic values: $y_1 = 25.0, y_2 = 25.2, y_3 = 24.7, y_4 = 25.5$ mm

#### Part (a) — Finding the Quality Loss Coefficient $k$

Using the Taguchi QLF coefficient formula:

$$k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$k = \dfrac{400}{(0.5)^2}$$

$$k = \dfrac{400}{0.25}$$

$$\boxed{k = 1600 \text{ ₹/mm}^2}$$

#### Part (b) — Computing Quality Loss for Each Shaft

Using $L(y) = k(y - m)^2 = 1600 \cdot (y - 25)^2$:

**Case 1: $y_1 = 25.0$ mm (at target)**

$$L(25.0) = 1600 \times (25.0 - 25)^2$$

$$L(25.0) = 1600 \times (0)^2$$

$$\boxed{L(25.0) = 0 \text{ ₹}}$$

**Case 2: $y_2 = 25.2$ mm (above target)**

$$L(25.2) = 1600 \times (25.2 - 25)^2$$

$$L(25.2) = 1600 \times (0.2)^2$$

$$L(25.2) = 1600 \times 0.04$$

$$\boxed{L(25.2) = 64 \text{ ₹}}$$

**Case 3: $y_3 = 24.7$ mm (below target)**

$$L(24.7) = 1600 \times (24.7 - 25)^2$$

$$L(24.7) = 1600 \times (-0.3)^2$$

$$L(24.7) = 1600 \times 0.09$$

$$\boxed{L(24.7) = 144 \text{ ₹}}$$

**Case 4: $y_4 = 25.5$ mm (at tolerance limit)**

$$L(25.5) = 1600 \times (25.5 - 25)^2$$

$$L(25.5) = 1600 \times (0.5)^2$$

$$L(25.5) = 1600 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{L(25.5) = 400 \text{ ₹}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Insight:** When $y = 25.5$ mm (exactly at the tolerance limit $\Delta_0 = 0.5$), the loss equals $A_0 = \text{₹}400$. This confirms the consistency of the Taguchi QLF model. Notice that the shaft at 24.7 mm incurs a **higher loss (₹144)** than the shaft at 25.2 mm (₹64) because the squared deviation is larger—demonstrating the **quadratic nature** of the loss.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Smaller-the-Better Case

> **Problem:** In a printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturing line, the target for solder defects is **zero**. The acceptable upper limit is 10 defects per board, and the loss at this limit is **₹500** per board.

**Given:** $m = 0$ (target = zero defects), $\Delta_0 = 10$ defects, $A_0 = \text{₹}500$

**Step 1: Compute $k$**

$$k = \dfrac{500}{(10)^2} = \dfrac{500}{100} = 5 \text{ ₹/defect}^2$$

**Step 2: Use STB form** $L(y) = k \cdot y^2$

For a board with $y = 4$ defects:

$$L(4) = 5 \times (4)^2 = 5 \times 16 = 80 \text{ ₹}$$

For a board with $y = 10$ defects (at tolerance limit):

$$L(10) = 5 \times (10)^2 = 5 \times 100 = 500 \text{ ₹} \checkmark$$

### 3.5 Analytical Loss Comparison Table

To make the quadratic loss behavior absolutely vivid, here is a side-by-side comparison for $k = 1600$ ₹/mm² and $m = 25$ mm:

| Deviation $\vert y - m \vert$ (mm) | Loss $L(y)$ (₹) | Loss Multiplier vs. at-target |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 (target) | 0 | 1× |
| 0.1 | 16 | 16× |
| 0.2 | 64 | 64× |
| 0.3 | 144 | 144× |
| 0.4 | 256 | 256× |
| 0.5 (tolerance limit) | 400 | 400× |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Takeaway:** The quadratic curve grows **slowly near the target** (small deviations cause small loss) but **explodes near the tolerance limit**. This explains why Taguchi's philosophy emphasizes **target-centering over tolerance-tightening**—it is cheaper to keep the process mean at the target than to chase tighter specs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function — Conceptual Flow
