6. **Review and Update / Archive / Dispose** — periodic review, version control, retention schedule.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Table

| Parameter / Concept | Definition | Unit / Standard | Key Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Documented Information** | Unified term for documents and records (ISO 9001:2015, Clause 7.5) | Conceptual | Replaces "documents" + "records" |
| **Quality Manual** | Top-level QMS document | Pages (20–50 typical) | Approved by Top Management |
| **Procedure Document** | Defines "who, what, when" for a process | Number per process | Required only when necessary |
| **Work Instruction** | Step-by-step task guide | Number per task | Floor-level, operator-facing |
| **Record** | Evidence of activity performed | Retention period in years | Must be retrievable, legible |
| **Master Copy** | Original controlled document | Single source of truth | Held by Document Controller |
| **Issue Number / Version** | Sequential identification of document updates | Integer or decimal (e.g., Rev 1.2) | Tracks revision history |
| **Retention Period** | Time a record must be kept | Years (typically 3–7) | Defined in Document Control Procedure |
| **Distribution List** | Authorized recipients of a document | Personnel / departments | Obsolete copies must be withdrawn |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> In a manufacturing plant, the document hierarchy ensures that an **engineer** writes the process procedure, the **supervisor** approves it, the **operator** uses the work instruction, and the **quality inspector** retains the inspection record — a closed-loop, traceable quality chain.

---

## 2.4 Purpose of Documentation (Six Critical Functions)

1. **Communication** — Transmits intent and information across the organization.
2. **Accountability** — Defines roles, responsibilities, and authorities (RACI matrix).
3. **Consistency** — Reduces process variation; ensures uniform output.
4. **Traceability** — Enables root-cause analysis and product recall.
5. **Training Aid** — Supports onboarding and skill development.
6. **Audit & Compliance Evidence** — Satisfies internal, customer, and third-party auditors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Document Control Workflow (Analytical Walkthrough)

The Document Control Workflow is not a formula but a **sequential logical process**. Below is its step-by-step derivation as expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

**Step 1: Identify the Need for a Document**

A need arises from one of four triggers:

$$\text{Need Triggers} = \{ \text{New Process},\ \text{ISO Clause Gap},\ \text{Non-Conformance},\ \text{Improvement Project} \}$$

**Step 2: Draft the Document**

The drafter (usually the process owner) prepares the document using a **standard template**, ensuring:

- Unique **Document Number**
- **Issue Date** and **Revision Number**
- **Author**, **Reviewer**, **Approver** fields
- **Scope** and **Purpose** statements
- **Page X of Y** footer (to detect missing pages)

**Step 3: Review the Document**

The draft is reviewed by:

- A **subject matter expert (SME)** for technical accuracy.
- A **quality representative** for ISO compliance.
- A **user representative** for practicality.

**Reviewer Feedback Matrix:**

| Reviewer Type | Focus Area | Approval Authority? |
|---|---|---|
| **SME** | Technical correctness | No |
| **QA Manager** | ISO 9001 alignment | No |
| **Process Owner** | Operational fit | No |
| **Top Management** | Strategic alignment | **Yes** |

**Step 4: Approve the Document**

Approval is a **formal sign-off** that authorizes the document for use. It must be by a person with **defined authority** (typically the Process Owner or HOD). Approval is recorded via signature, electronic approval, or workflow tool.

**Step 5: Distribute and Make Available**

Distribution involves:

- Issuing **controlled copies** to authorized users.
- Marking each copy as **"CONTROLLED"** (usually in red).
- Maintaining a **Master Document List (MDL)** or **Document Register**.
- Withdrawing and **destroying** obsolete copies (or marking them as **"OBSOLETE"** in red).

**Step 6: Use, Review, and Update**

Documents are used at the point of operation. Periodic review (typically **annually** or upon process change) ensures continued suitability. Updates follow the same Create → Review → Approve cycle, with the **revision number incremented** (e.g., Rev 1.0 → Rev 1.1).

**Step 7: Retention and Disposal**

Records have a **defined retention period**. After expiry, they are:

- **Archived** for legal/historical reasons, OR
- **Securely disposed** (shredding, electronic deletion) per the disposal policy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip (Frequently tested):**
> The question *"Distinguish between a Procedure and a Work Instruction"* or *"Explain the document hierarchy"* almost always appears. Always present it as a **pyramid / four-tier table**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Documents vs. Records

| Parameter | **Document** | **Record** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Specifies what should be done | Provides evidence of what was done |
| **Lifecycle** | Can be revised, version-controlled | Once created, generally **frozen** |
| **Examples** | Quality Manual, SOP, Work Instruction | Inspection report, Audit report, Training record |
| **Change Control** | Strict — needs approval to change | Should **not** be changed; corrections via line-through + sign |
| **Retention** | Replaced by newer version | Retained for defined period |
| **Auditor's View** | A document tells the auditor *what should happen* | A record tells the auditor *what actually happened* |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Why "Documented Information" Replaced "Documents + Records"

ISO 9001:2008 had **6 mandatory procedures** (Document Control, Record Control, Internal Audit, Non-conforming Product, Corrective Action, Preventive Action) and explicit mention of "documents" and "records."

ISO 9001:2015 replaced both with a single term — **"Documented Information"** — because:

1. **Flexibility** — organizations decide what documentation is needed (no mandated list of procedures).
2. **Technology-neutral** — applies equally to paper, electronic, cloud, video.
3. **Risk-based thinking** — documentation level matches the risk of process failure.
4. **Performance over paperwork** — focus shifts from *having documents* to *achieving outcomes*.

**Logical Equivalence Statement:**

$$\text{Documented Information (2015)} \equiv \text{Documents} \cup \text{Records (2008)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Document Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid

    L1[Level 1: Quality Manual] --> L2[Level 2: Procedures and Process Maps]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Work Instructions and SOPs]

    L2 -.Owned by.-> ProcessOwner[Process Owner]
    L3 -.Used by.-> Operator[Floor Level Operator]
    L4 -.Retained by.-> DocController[Document Controller]

```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Document Control Cycle (6-Step Loop)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify Need] --> S2[Step 2: Draft Document]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Review by SME and QA]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Approve by Authority]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Distribute Controlled Copy]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Use Review Update Archive]
    S6 -.Periodic Review Trigger.-> S1
    style S1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000

    style S6 fill:#9bc2e6,stroke:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Documentation Flow in a QMS

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input[External Inputs: ISO 9001 2015, Customer Requirements, Regulatory Norms] --> Policy[Quality Policy and Objectives]
    Policy --> Manual[Quality Manual Level 1]
    Manual --> Proc[Documented Procedures Level 2]
    Proc --> WI[Work Instructions Level 3]
    WI --> Oper[Operational Execution at Point of Use]
    Oper --> Rec[Records Generated as Evidence Level 4]
    Rec --> Analysis[Data Analysis and CAPA]
    Analysis --> Improve[Continual Improvement PDCA]
    Improve -.Updates.-> Manual
    Rec -.Audit Evidence.-> Audit[Internal and External Audit]
    Audit -.Non Conformance.-> Analysis
    style Input fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#000
    style Policy fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Manual fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Proc fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style WI fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    style Oper fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Rec fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Analysis fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000
    style Improve fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style Audit fill:#404040,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The **hierarchy pyramid** shows *static structure* (what exists), while the **control cycle** and **functional architecture** show *dynamic flow* (how documents move through the system). Together, they give the complete KTU-exam picture of documentation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define "Documented Information" as per ISO 9001:2015. How is it different from the old terminology of "documents" and "records"?**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** As per **ISO 9001:2015, Clause 7.5.1**, *Documented Information* is the information required to be controlled and maintained by an organization, and the medium on which it is contained. It can be in any format — paper, electronic, cloud, or video — and from any source.

**Difference from Old Terminology (1.5 Marks):**
- In **ISO 9001:2008**, the standard separately mandated *"documents"* (e.g., Quality Manual, 6 mandatory procedures) and *"records"* (objective evidence).
- In **ISO 9001:2015**, both are unified under the single term **"Documented Information"**, providing flexibility, technology-neutrality, and risk-based documentation.

---

### **Q2. List any six purposes of documentation in a Quality Management System.**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):** Any six of the following:

1. **Communication** of intent and requirements.
2. **Accountability** through defined roles and responsibilities.
3. **Consistency** in process execution, reducing variation.
4. **Traceability** of products, processes, and decisions.
5. **Training aid** for new and existing employees.
6. **Audit and compliance evidence** for internal and external auditors.
7. **Continual improvement** through data analysis and CAPA.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every 14-mark Module question offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q(A)(a).** Explain the **four-tier document hierarchy** in a Quality Management System with a neat sketch. Differentiate between a **procedure** and a **work instruction**.

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The **four-tier document hierarchy** (also called the *Document Pyramid*) organizes QMS documentation from broad policy at the top to objective evidence at the base.

**Tier 1 — Quality Manual (2 Marks):**
- Top-level document defining the **scope, Quality Policy, and Quality Objectives** of the QMS.
- Describes how the organization meets each ISO 9001:2015 clause.
- Approved by **Top Management**.
- Typical length: 20–50 pages.

**Tier 2 — Documented Procedures / Process Maps (1.5 Marks):**
- Define *who* does *what*, *when*, and *where* for each major process.
- Examples: *Procedure for Handling Customer Complaints*, *Procedure for Internal Audit*.
- Owned by **Process Owners**.

**Tier 3 — Work Instructions / SOPs (1.5 Marks):**
- Step-by-step, **task-level** instructions for operators.
- Examples: *How to calibrate a micrometer*, *How to inspect a weld*.
- Format often includes photographs, decision flowcharts, and warning notes.

**Tier 4 — Records / Forms (2 Marks):**
- **Objective evidence** that activities were performed.
- Examples: Inspection reports, training records, calibration certificates.
- Retained for a defined period; should not be altered after creation.

**Procedure vs. Work Instruction (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Procedure | Work Instruction |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Process-level: *what* and *who* | Task-level: *how*, step-by-step |
| Audience | Process owners, managers | Operators, floor staff |
| Detail level | Moderate | Very detailed |

*(Examiner tip: Always present a sketch of the pyramid — it fetches easy marks.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q(A)(b).** Describe the **six-step Document Control Cycle** as required by **ISO 9001:2015 (Clause 7.5.3)**. Why is uncontrolled documentation considered worse than no documentation?

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** ISO 9001:2015 Clause 7.5.3 mandates that documented information be controlled to ensure its **availability** and **suitable protection** from loss of confidentiality, improper use, or loss of integrity.

**The Six-Step Document Control Cycle (5 Marks):**

1. **Create / Identify Need** — A need is identified due to a new process, regulatory change, or audit finding.
2. **Review** — The draft is reviewed by subject matter experts and the QA team for adequacy, accuracy, and ISO compliance.
3. **Approve** — Authorization by a competent authority (e.g., Process Owner or HOD) before issue.
4. **Distribute / Make Available** — Controlled copies issued to authorized users; obsolete versions withdrawn.
5. **Use** — Document is followed at the point of operation.
6. **Review and Update / Archive** — Periodic review; outdated versions archived or securely disposed.

**Why Uncontrolled Documentation is Worse (1 Mark):**
- Users may rely on **obsolete versions**, leading to wrong actions.
- Creates **legal liability** (e.g., wrong safety procedure followed).
- Causes **audit non-conformances**, risking ISO 9001 certification.
- Erodes **trust** in the QMS among employees and customers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing only "Create, Distribute, Use" — this is **incomplete**. You must include **Review, Approve, and Update/Dispose** steps for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *Document Control* with *Record Control*. Records are **controlled** but should be **frozen** (not revised).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not quoting the **ISO 9001:2015 clause number** (7.5.3). Examiners reward clause citations.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping the **Master Document List / Document Register** mention. Always state that a **Master Document List (MDL)** must be maintained.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q(B)(a).** What is a **Quality Manual**? Explain its **contents, purpose, and the process of its preparation and approval** in an organization implementing ISO 9001:2015.

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The **Quality Manual** is the top-level document of a QMS that describes the scope, exclusions (if any), Quality Policy, Quality Objectives, and the interaction of processes within the QMS. It is the *flagship document* of the organization.

**Contents of a Quality Manual (2.5 Marks):**
1. Title page and table of contents.
2. Scope of the QMS (Clause 4.3).
3. Exclusions and their justification.
4. Quality Policy (Clause 5.2).
5. Quality Objectives (Clause 6.2).
6. Description of QMS processes and their interaction (process map).
7. Reference to documented procedures.
8. Roles, responsibilities, and authorities (Clause 5.3).
9. Revision history and approval signatures.

**Purpose (1.5 Marks):**
- Acts as a **single point of reference** for the entire QMS.
- Communicates the organization's **quality intent** to internal and external stakeholders.
- Serves as a **guide for auditors** during certification audits.
- Provides a **framework for new employees** to understand the QMS.

**Preparation and Approval Process (2 Marks):**
1. **Initiation** — Top Management assigns the QA Manager to draft the manual.
2. **Drafting** — Process owners contribute content; QA Manager consolidates.
3. **Review** — Reviewed by Top Management and legal/regulatory experts.
4. **Approval** — Signed by the **CEO / Managing Director** (highest authority).
5. **Issue and Distribution** — Issued as **Rev 1.0** with controlled copies distributed.
6. **Periodic Review** — Reviewed at least **annually** or upon major changes.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q(B)(b).** Compare and contrast **Documents and Records** in a QMS. Why did **ISO 9001:2015** merge them under the term **"Documented Information"**? List any **four benefits** of this change.

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Document | Record |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Specifies what should be done | Provides evidence of what was done |
| Lifecycle | Can be revised | Frozen after creation |
| Examples | Quality Manual, SOP | Inspection report, Audit report |
| Change Control | Strict approval needed | Not changed; corrections by line-through + signature |
| Retention | Replaced by new version | Retained for defined period |

**Why ISO 9001:2015 Merged Them (2 Marks):**
- The old standard's separate treatment of "documents" and "records" caused **duplication** in clauses (e.g., Clause 4.2.3 and 4.2.4 in ISO 9001:2008).
- Both serve the common purpose of providing **controlled, retrievable information** for the QMS.
- A unified term reflects the **digital age** where the distinction between a "document" and a "record" is blurred (e.g., an electronic form filled in is both).

**Four Benefits of the Merger (2 Marks):**
1. **Flexibility** — Organizations decide what documentation is needed; no rigid list of 6 mandatory procedures.
2. **Technology-neutral** — Applies equally to paper and electronic formats.
3. **Risk-based** — Documentation level is proportional to process risk.
4. **Reduced bureaucracy** — Focus shifts from paperwork to outcomes and performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Saying *"ISO 9001:2015 removed documentation requirements."* This is **WRONG**. Documentation is still required — only the **label** changed.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *Clause 7.5.1* (general), *7.5.2* (creating and updating), and *7.5.3* (control). Quote the **correct clause** for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not mentioning the **Master Document List (MDL)**. It is a frequent 2-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- ✅ **Documented Information** = ISO 9001:2015 term replacing "documents + records" (Clause 7.5.1 to 7.5.3).
- ✅ **Four-tier hierarchy**: Quality Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.
- ✅ **Quality Manual** is the *top-level* document, approved by **Top Management**.
- ✅ **Procedure** = *what* and *who*; **Work Instruction** = *how*, step-by-step.
- ✅ **Records** are **objective evidence** — they are **frozen**, not revised.
- ✅ **Master Document List (MDL)** must be maintained; obsolete copies withdrawn.
- ✅ **Six steps of Document Control**: Identify → Draft → Review → Approve → Distribute → Use & Update.
- ✅ **Clause 7.5.2** = Creating and updating documented information.
- ✅ **Clause 7.5.3** = Control of documented information (availability + protection).
- ✅ Documentation supports the **PDCA cycle** by providing the **Plan** (manual/procedures) and **Check** (records).
- ✅ Common exam keywords: *traceability, accountability, audit evidence, master copy, controlled copy, retention period, version control*.
- ✅ **Three purposes** of any document: *communicate intent, ensure consistency, provide evidence.*
- ✅ **One mnemonic** to remember the pyramid: **"M-P-W-R"** → Manual, Procedure, Work-instruction, Record.
- ✅ **Risk-based thinking** dictates *how much* documentation is needed — more risk = more documentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Auditing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT746 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Auditing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quality Audit** is a **systematic, independent, and documented process** for obtaining **audit evidence** and evaluating it objectively against predefined **audit criteria** to determine the extent to which the audit criteria are fulfilled. This definition is consistent with **ISO 19011:2018** — the international standard that provides guidelines for auditing management systems.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, Quality Auditing is treated as the **verification backbone of Total Quality Management (TQM)**. Without audits, the PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) cycle has no "Check" arm, and the TQM philosophy collapses into wishful thinking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (Write this verbatim for 2-mark answers):**
> *"A Quality Audit is a structured, impartial, and evidence-based review of a quality system, process, or product conducted to verify conformity with standards, specifications, or contractual requirements, and to identify opportunities for continual improvement."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your mother comes to inspect your hostel room once a month. She:
1. **Announces the visit** in advance (auditor arrives with a plan).
2. **Walks around with a checklist** — bed made, floor clean, no unwashed dishes, no expired food (audit criteria).
3. **Picks up evidence** — opens the fridge, checks the dustbin (objective evidence).
4. **Notes deviations** — "The dustbin is overflowing!" (non-conformance).
5. **Asks for corrective action** — "Clean it within 2 days and send me a photo" (follow-up).

That is **exactly** what a Quality Auditor does inside a factory or service organization — only with more paperwork, statistical sampling, and a formal report.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Every Quality Audit (Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Independence** — The auditor must be free from bias and the activity being audited.
> 2. **Evidence-Based** — Findings must be supported by documents, records, or observable facts — never opinions.
> 3. **Systematic Process** — Audits follow a defined methodology (ISO 19011), not ad-hoc judgment.

## 1.3 Why Quality Auditing Matters in TQM

- **Verification of Conformity** → Confirms whether stated quality standards are actually being met on the shop floor.
- **Identifies Gaps & Risks** → Exposes non-conformances *before* the customer finds them.
- **Drives Continual Improvement (Kaizen)** → Each audit finding is a *seed* for a PDCA improvement cycle.
- **Builds Stakeholder Confidence** → Customers, regulators, and certification bodies rely on audit reports.
- **Legal & Contractual Defense** → In disputes, audit records serve as objective proof of due diligence.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** Quality Audit is the *only* mechanism in TQM that provides an **independent** check on whether the documented QMS (Quality Management System) is actually being followed in practice — bridging the **"say-do gap"**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Quality Audits (KTU Frequently Asked)

### A. By **Who Performs the Audit** (Three-Party Model)

| Type | Also Called | Performed By | Typical Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Party Audit** | Internal Audit | The organization itself (own staff) | Self-assessment, ISO clause compliance, pre-cert check |
| **Second-Party Audit** | Supplier Audit | Customer (or on their behalf) | Evaluate supplier capability before contract award |
| **Third-Party Audit** | Certification / External Audit | Independent certification body (e.g., TÜV, BIS, BSI) | Issue ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 / ISO 14001 certificate |

### B. By **What is Being Audited**

| Type | Focus | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Audit** | Conformance of finished product to specifications | Inspecting 50 sample bolts for dimensional accuracy |
| **Process Audit** | Whether the manufacturing process is capable and controlled | Checking whether CNC calibration is done as per SOP |
| **System Audit** | The entire QMS against a standard (e.g., ISO 9001) | Clause-by-clause audit of the QMS manual |
| **Compliance Audit** | Adherence to regulatory/legal requirements | Pollution Control Board norms, Factories Act |
| **Service Audit** | Quality of intangible services | Mystery shopper in a bank branch |

## 2.2 The Audit Process (ISO 19011 Six-Stage Model)

1. **Initiating the Audit** — Define audit scope, objectives, criteria, and feasibility.
2. **Preparing Audit Activities** — Document review, audit plan, checklist development, resource allocation.
3. **Conducting the Audit** — Opening meeting → on-site evidence collection → closing meeting.
4. **Preparing Audit Report** — Document findings, conclusions, and recommendations.
5. **Completing the Audit** — Distribute report, retain records per document control.
6. **Audit Follow-up** — Verify implementation of corrective actions; close out the audit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** Students often confuse "auditing" with "inspection" or "testing". The difference:
> - **Inspection** → Examines a *product*.
> - **Testing** → Measures a *characteristic* of a product.
> - **Auditing** → Examines a *system or process* against criteria.

## 2.3 Audit Findings — The Four Outcome Categories

Every piece of audit evidence is classified into one of four buckets:

| Finding Type | Definition | Typical Mark in KTU |
|---|---|---|
| **Conformity (C)** | Evidence shows the requirement is met | Positive observation |
| **Observation (O)** | Minor concern that could become a problem | Worth 1–2 marks |
| **Minor Non-Conformance (NC-M)** | Isolated lapse; does not indicate systemic failure | Often asked as 7-mark question |
| **Major Non-Conformance (NC-Ma)** | Systemic failure, absence of control, or breakdown of a clause | Asked as 14-mark question |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Auditing is largely qualitative**, but a few quantitative ratios are frequently tested. The most important is the **Audit Effectiveness Ratio (AER)**.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Audit Effectiveness Ratio (AER)** | $AER = \dfrac{N_{VR}}{N_{T}} \times 100\,\%$ | $N_{VR}$ = Verified Recommendations, $N_{T}$ = Total Recommendations. Higher % = stronger follow-up culture. |
| 2 | **Sampling Size for Product Audit** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$ | $Z$ = Z-score, $p$ = expected defect rate, $e$ = margin of error |
| 3 | **Non-Conformance Rate (NCR)** | $NCR = \dfrac{N_{NC}}{N_{A}} \times 100\,\%$ | $N_{NC}$ = number of NCs, $N_{A}$ = total audit opportunities (sample) |
| 4 | **Audit Coverage** | $Coverage = \dfrac{A_{audited}}{A_{total}} \times 100\,\%$ | What % of the auditable scope was actually examined |
| 5 | **Auditor Independence Index** | $II = 1 - \dfrac{C_{conflict}}{C_{total}}$ | Closer to 1 = higher independence; 0 = fully compromised |
| 6 | **CAPA Closure Time** | $T_{close} = T_{verify} - T_{raised}$ | Average time to close corrective & preventive action requests |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pipe Safety Reminder:** The LaTeX delimiter `|x|` (absolute value) inside a table row will break the markdown renderer. **Always** write absolute values as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in tables.

## 2.5 Roles & Responsibilities in an Audit

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Auditee** | Person/department being audited; provides access, records, and cooperation |
| **Lead Auditor** | Manages the audit team, prepares the report, presents findings |
| **Auditor** | Collects evidence objectively against the checklist |
| **Technical Expert** | Provides specialist knowledge (e.g., welding, software) |
| **Guide** | Escorts the audit team, helps locate documents/people |
| **Sponsor / Client** | Initiates the audit, defines scope, receives the report |

## 2.6 Principles of Auditing (ISO 19011 — Frequently Asked in KTU)

The **Seven Auditing Principles** are:
1. Integrity
2. Fair Presentation
3. Due Professional Care
4. Confidentiality
5. Independence
6. Evidence-Based Approach
7. Risk-Based Approach

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing**: ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 audits for automotive suppliers.
- **Healthcare**: NABH audits for hospitals.
- **IT/Software**: CMMI and ISO 27001 audits.
- **Education**: NAAC accreditation audits for universities.
- **Environment**: ISO 14001 EMS audits for pollution control.
- **Food Safety**: FSSAI / ISO 22000 audits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Determining the Audit Sample Size

In a **product audit**, the auditor must statistically justify the sample size. The derivation uses the standard normal approximation for a proportion.

### Step 1 — Define the parameters
- $N$ = population size (lot size, e.g., 5,000 units).
- $p$ = expected (or historical) proportion of non-conformances, e.g., $p = 0.05$.
- $e$ = allowable margin of error, e.g., $e = 0.02$.
- $Z$ = Z-score for the desired confidence level.

### Step 2 — Confidence level mapping
For **95% confidence** (most common KTU exam value), the Z-score is:

$$Z_{0.95} = 1.96$$

For **99% confidence** (industrial audits), $Z_{0.99} = 2.576$.

### Step 3 — Apply the sample-size formula
The standard sample size for a proportion is:

$$n_{0} = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$$

### Step 4 — Correct for finite population
If the sample is more than 5% of the population ($n_{0}/N > 0.05$), apply the **finite population correction (FPC)**:

$$n = \dfrac{n_{0}}{1 + \dfrac{n_{0} - 1}{N}}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example
Given: $Z = 1.96$, $p = 0.05$, $e = 0.02$, $N = 5000$.

Substitute into the formula:

$$n_{0} = \dfrac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot (1 - 0.05)}{(0.02)^2}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$(1.96)^2 = 3.8416$$

$$0.05 \times 0.95 = 0.0475$$

$$3.8416 \times 0.0475 = 0.182476$$

Compute the denominator:

$$(0.02)^2 = 0.0004$$

Therefore:

$$n_{0} = \dfrac{0.182476}{0.0004} = 456.19$$

Round up to the next integer: $n_{0} = 457$.

Check finite population correction:

$$\dfrac{n_{0}}{N} = \dfrac{457}{5000} = 0.0914 > 0.05$$

So apply FPC:

$$n = \dfrac{457}{1 + \dfrac{457 - 1}{5000}} = \dfrac{457}{1 + 0.0912} = \dfrac{457}{1.0912} = 418.78$$

Round up: $n = 419$ units to be sampled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for the KTU exam:** *The auditor must inspect a minimum of 419 units from a lot of 5,000 to be 95% confident that the estimated defect proportion is within ±2% of the true proportion.*

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Audit Effectiveness Ratio (AER) — Worked Numeric

The **AER** measures how well an organization actually implements the auditor's recommendations.

### Step 1 — Define variables
- $N_{T}$ = total number of recommendations raised across all audits in a year.
- $N_{VR}$ = number of those recommendations that were *verified* as implemented within the agreed timeline.
- $N_{PR}$ = number of recommendations *partially* implemented.
- $N_{NR}$ = recommendations *not* implemented.

### Step 2 — Consistency check
The recommendations must sum to the total:

$$N_{T} = N_{VR} + N_{PR} + N_{NR}$$

### Step 3 — Compute AER
Strict (binary) version — only fully implemented ones count:

$$AER_{strict} = \dfrac{N_{VR}}{N_{T}} \times 100\,\%$$

### Step 4 — Lenient (weighted) version
Assign partial credit, e.g., $w_{P} = 0.5$ for partial, $w_{VR} = 1.0$ for verified:

$$AER_{weighted} = \dfrac{(1.0 \times N_{VR}) + (0.5 \times N_{PR}) + (0 \times N_{NR})}{N_{T}} \times 100\,\%$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Example
Suppose $N_{T} = 50$, $N_{VR} = 30$, $N_{PR} = 12$, $N_{NR} = 8$.

Consistency check:

$$50 = 30 + 12 + 8 \quad \checkmark$$

Strict AER:

$$AER_{strict} = \dfrac{30}{50} \times 100\,\% = 60\,\%$$

Weighted AER:

$$AER_{weighted} = \dfrac{(1.0 \times 30) + (0.5 \times 12) + 0}{50} \times 100\,\%$$

$$= \dfrac{30 + 6}{50} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{36}{50} \times 100\,\% = 72\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A *weighted* AER of 72% is a healthy figure for a mature QMS. Anything below 40% in either metric indicates a **systemic failure in the follow-up stage** — a major KTU exam point.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: The PDCA-In-Audit Workflow (Process Matrix)

| Stage | Input | Process | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P — Plan** | Audit programme, prior reports | Define scope, criteria, schedule, team | Approved Audit Plan + Checklist |
| **D — Do** | Audit Plan | Opening meeting → document review → field evidence collection → closing meeting | Audit Notes + Collected Evidence |
| **C — Check** | Audit Notes | Classify findings (C / O / NC-M / NC-Ma); grade | Draft Audit Report |
| **A — Act** | Draft Report | Issue final report → initiate CAPA → follow-up verification | Closed Audit + Improvement Actions |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Audit Selection Logic

| Situation | Recommended Audit Type |
|---|---|
| New supplier, no prior record | Second-party supplier audit |
| Customer complaint about a batch | Product audit + Root-cause analysis |
| Want ISO 9001 certification | Third-party certification audit by accredited body |
| Internal gaps in QMS documentation | First-party system audit (internal) |
| Regulatory compliance check (e.g., fire safety) | Compliance audit |
| Pre-shipment quality verification | Product / source inspection audit |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Killer:** If a question describes a scenario and asks "what type of audit would you recommend?", always justify the choice with **three reasons** referencing *scope*, *independence*, and *cost-effectiveness*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Quality Audit Process Flow (ISO 19011 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Initiate Audit] --> A2[Step 2: Prepare Audit Activities]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Conduct On-Site Audit]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Prepare Audit Report]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Complete the Audit]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Audit Follow-Up]
    A6 --> A7{NCs Closed?}
    A7 -- No --> A8[Re-Verify in Next Cycle]
    A8 --> A2
    A7 -- Yes --> A9[Audit Closed Successfully]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Three-Party Audit Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Quality Audit Universe]
    A0 --> B1[First Party Internal]
    A0 --> B2[Second Party Supplier]
    A0 --> B3[Third Party Certification]

    B1 --> C1[Self-assessment of QMS]
    B1 --> C2[Clause-by-clause internal review]

    B2 --> C3[Pre-contract supplier evaluation]
    B2 --> C4[Ongoing supplier monitoring]

    B3 --> C5[ISO 9001 / IATF 16949 certification]
    B3 --> C6[Regulatory compliance verification]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Audit Findings Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Audit Evidence Collected]
    D1 --> D2{Criteria Met?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[Conformity]
    D2 -- Partial --> D4[Observation]
    D2 -- No --> D5{Isolated or Systemic?}
    D5 -- Isolated --> D6[Minor Non-Conformance]
    D5 -- Systemic --> D7[Major Non-Conformance]
    D6 --> D8[Corrective Action Request]
    D7 --> D8
    D8 --> D9[CAPA Implementation]
    D9 --> D10[Follow-up Verification]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Audit Lifecycle & Document Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E1[Audit Programme Annual Plan] --> E2[Specific Audit Plan]
    E2 --> E3[Audit Checklist]
    E3 --> E4[On-Site Evidence Collection]
    E4 --> E5[Working Papers and Notes]
    E5 --> E6[Draft Audit Report]
    E6 --> E7[Closing Meeting]
    E7 --> E8[Final Audit Report]
    E8 --> E9[CAPA Log]
    E9 --> E10[Follow-up Audit]
    E10 --> E11[Audit Closure Record]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for KTU:** The diagram visually maps every document a quality auditor handles in a year. If asked "list the outputs of an audit", you can derive the answer directly from this architecture — **Audit Plan, Checklist, Working Papers, Report, CAPA, Follow-up Report, Closure Record**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between First-Party, Second-Party, and Third-Party Quality Audits with one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Audit Type | Performed By | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Party** | The organization itself (own trained staff) | Self-assessment of QMS; continual improvement | Internal ISO 9001 audit by an in-house auditor |
| **Second-Party** | Customer or on their behalf | Evaluate capability of a supplier before/after contract | A car manufacturer auditing its Tier-1 brake supplier |
| **Third-Party** | Independent certification body (CB) | Issue/withhold certification to an international standard | TÜV SÜD conducting ISO 14001 EMS certification audit |

> *Award 1 mark per correct row with example.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List any three principles of auditing as per ISO 19011:2018."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Integrity** — The auditor must be honest and ethical in all dealings.
2. **Independence** — The auditor must be free from bias and conflicts of interest.
3. **Evidence-Based Approach** — Conclusions must be based on verifiable evidence, not assumptions.

*(Other acceptable principles: Fair Presentation, Due Professional Care, Confidentiality, Risk-Based Approach.)*

---

## **Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format**

> **Common Stem:** *"As the newly appointed Quality Manager of an automotive component manufacturing firm (certified to IATF 16949:2016), answer any **ONE** of the following:"*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Design a complete internal quality audit programme for the production shop floor, including the stages, frequency, and resources required. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the differences between a Minor Non-Conformance and a Major Non-Conformance, with two real-world examples of each. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Internal Audit Programme Design (7 marks)

**1. Audit Scope and Objectives (1 mark)**
- Scope: All CNC turning, heat treatment, and assembly cells on the production shop floor.
- Objectives: (i) Verify conformance to IATF 16949 clauses 7.1.5 and 8.5.1; (ii) Identify risks to product safety; (iii) Drive continual improvement.

**2. Audit Frequency and Criteria (1 mark)**
- Each cell audited **at least once per year** (IATF 16949 requirement).
- High-risk cells (welding, heat treatment) audited **twice per year**.
- Trigger-based audits if any customer complaint or process parameter drifts beyond Cpk < 1.33.

**3. Auditor Selection and Independence (1 mark)**
- Auditors must be **trained and qualified** (minimum 16 hours audit training + 3 supervised audits).
- They must **not audit their own work**.

**4. Audit Plan and Checklist (1 mark)**
- Annual programme → specific audit plan 2 weeks before → checklist covering IATF clauses, process FMEAs, control plans, and operator competency.

**5. On-Site Execution (1 mark)**
- Opening meeting → document review (PFMEA, control plan) → field walk-through → witness 2–3 process steps → closing meeting.

**6. Reporting and Follow-up (1 mark)**
- Report issued within 7 working days; CAPA raised within 14 days; follow-up verification within 60 days.

**7. Records and Management Review (1 mark)**
- Audit reports, CAPA logs, and AER metrics fed into the **Management Review Meeting** as a key input.

#### Model Solution — (b) Minor vs Major Non-Conformance (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
- **Minor NC** — An isolated lapse or single observed non-fulfilment of a requirement that does **not** indicate a systemic breakdown.
- **Major NC** — A systemic failure, absence of a required control, or any situation that raises significant doubt about the QMS's ability to meet customer or regulatory requirements.

**Minor NC — Examples (2.5 marks, 1.25 each):**
1. One operator's training record missing a single refresher signature, while the rest of the team's records are complete.
2. A single calibration label on a torque wrench is smudged and not legible, though the wrench itself is within calibration date.

**Major NC — Examples (2.5 marks, 1.25 each):**
1. The firm has **no documented PFMEA** for a safety-critical welding process, indicating the entire risk-assessment control is missing.
2. Customer-complaint analysis is **never performed** — i.e., the CAPA process for complaints is absent, indicating systemic failure of clause 10.2 of ISO 9001.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the complete Quality Audit Process as per ISO 19011:2018 in six stages. (7 marks)**
**(b) A company conducted 75 internal audits in a year, raising 240 recommendations. Of these, 150 were fully implemented, 60 were partially implemented, and 30 were not implemented. Calculate the strict and weighted Audit Effectiveness Ratio (AER) and interpret the results. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Six-Stage Audit Process (7 marks)

1. **Initiating the Audit** (1 mark) — Establish audit feasibility; appoint the lead auditor; define scope, criteria, and objectives in writing.
2. **Preparing Audit Activities** (1.5 marks) — Document review, on-site visit logistics, audit plan, and checklist development; resource allocation.
3. **Conducting the Audit** (1.5 marks) — Opening meeting → evidence collection (interviews, observation, document sampling) → findings recorded → closing meeting.
4. **Preparing Audit Report** (1 mark) — Document findings, conclusions, recommendations, and follow-up date; reviewed and approved before issuance.
5. **Completing the Audit** (1 mark) — Distribute report to sponsor, auditee, and management; archive records per the document-control procedure.
6. **Audit Follow-up** (1 mark) — Verify implementation of corrective actions; if not effective, raise a new finding or escalate.

#### Model Solution — (b) AER Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $N_{T} = 240$ recommendations
- $N_{VR} = 150$ verified
- $N_{PR} = 60$ partially implemented
- $N_{NR} = 30$ not implemented

**Step 1 — Consistency check (1 mark):**
$$150 + 60 + 30 = 240 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Strict AER (3 marks):**

$$AER_{strict} = \dfrac{N_{VR}}{N_{T}} \times 100\,\% = \dfrac{150}{240} \times 100\,\%$$

$$= 0.625 \times 100\,\% = 62.5\,\%$$

**Step 3 — Weighted AER (2 marks):** Using weights $w_{VR} = 1.0$, $w_{PR} = 0.5$, $w_{NR} = 0$:

$$AER_{weighted} = \dfrac{(1.0 \times 150) + (0.5 \times 60) + (0 \times 30)}{240} \times 100\,\%$$

$$= \dfrac{150 + 30 + 0}{240} \times 100\,\%$$

$$= \dfrac{180}{240} \times 100\,\% = 75\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
A *strict* AER of **62.5%** and a *weighted* AER of **75%** indicate a **moderately mature follow-up culture**. The 30 unimplemented recommendations should be re-escalated; the company should target ≥ 80% strict AER in the next audit cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Audit with Inspection** (loses 1 mark) — Inspection checks a *product*; Audit checks a *system/process*.
> 2. **Omitting the Consistency Check** (loses 1 mark) — Always show that $N_{VR} + N_{PR} + N_{NR} = N_{T}$ before computing AER.
> 3. **Using $|x|$ inside table cells** (loses presentation marks) — Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`.
> 4. **Forgetting Auditor Independence** (loses 1 mark) — Every audit answer must mention that the auditor must not audit their own work.
> 5. **Vague "Audit is important" answers** (loses 2 marks) — Always back statements with **ISO 9001 / ISO 19011 clause numbers** where possible.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ **Definition** — A Quality Audit is a *systematic, independent, documented* evaluation of evidence against criteria.
- ✅ **Three-Party Model** — First (internal), Second (supplier), Third (certification) — memorize with examples.
- ✅ **Four Audit Types by Subject** — Product, Process, System, Compliance/Service audits.
- ✅ **ISO 19011:2018 Six Stages** — Initiate → Prepare → Conduct → Report → Complete → Follow-up.
- ✅ **Four Finding Types** — Conformity, Observation, Minor NC, Major NC.
- ✅ **Seven Auditing Principles** — Integrity, Fair Presentation, Due Professional Care, Confidentiality, Independence, Evidence-Based, Risk-Based.
- ✅ **Key Formulae** — $n_0 = \dfrac{Z^2 p(1-p)}{e^2}$; $n = \dfrac{n_0}{1 + (n_0 - 1)/N}$; $AER_{strict} = \dfrac{N_{VR}}{N_T} \times 100\,\%$.
- ✅ **Auditor Independence** — Auditors **cannot** audit their own work; qualified with training + supervised audits.
- ✅ **PDCA Inside Audits** — Plan the audit → Do on-site → Check the report → Act via CAPA follow-up.
- ✅ **Audit ≠ Inspection ≠ Testing** — Memorize this distinction; it's a guaranteed 1–2 mark question.
- ✅ **Output Documents** — Audit Plan, Checklist, Working Papers, Audit Report, CAPA Log, Follow-up Report, Closure Record.
- ✅ **Always Cite ISO 19011:2018** in KTU answers; it carries academic weight with the examiner.
- ✅ **In Markdown Tables, never use `|x|`** — always use `\vert x \vert` to keep the table renderer happy.

> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When answering a 14-mark audit question, structure your answer in **five clear sections** — (1) Definition, (2) ISO reference, (3) Stages, (4) Roles/Findings, (5) Example/Interpretation. Examiners reward **structured, clause-referenced** answers, not essays.
